actions.thought: I am holding 7c 3s in UTG position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker (7-3 offsuit). Given my early position with 5 players left to act behind me, I have no fold equity to steal the blinds and very poor playability post-flop. The standard and only correct play here is to fold immediately.

In 6-max No-Limit Hold'em, King-Queen offsuit (KQo) is a strong opening hand from Middle Position, especially after the Under-the-Gun (UTG) player has folded. Opening for 3x the big blind (300 chips) is a standard raise size that balances building a pot with a strong range and folding out weaker hands to gain position or win the blinds immediately.

Preflop in the cutoff with 6s7d offsuit against a middle position raise to 300. This is a weak, offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability postflop. It plays poorly against a typical MP opening range (pairs 88+, broadway hands, suited connectors). Position helps slightly, but the pot odds (450:300 ~1.5:1) require too much equity, and we'll often be dominated or floating OOP multiway potentially. Deep stacks early in tournament; standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

T8o on the button facing a 3x raise from middle position. While I have position, T8o is a marginal hand that doesn't play well against a MP opening range. Cold calling also leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the blinds. Hand 1 of 150 - no need to force marginal spots. Folding is disciplined here.

Facing a middle-position raise to 300 while I'm in the small blind with Td2s (unsuited 10-2) — a very weak, easily dominated hand — I should not invest another 250 chips to see a flop out of position. There's also the risk of further action from the big blind. With deep stacks early in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is correct. Folding preserves stack and avoids playing a marginal hand OOP against an opener.

KJs (suited) is strong enough to continue versus a middle-position 3bb open. Calling keeps ranges wide but leaves us out of position in a single-raised pot; 3-betting wins the pot immediately some frequency, denies equity to hands like Axs/QTs/low pairs, and sets up a favorable SPR when we flop top pair/strong draws. With no reads and deep stacks, a standard BB 3-bet size is ~3.5–4x the open to pressure the opener’s range. I’ll 3-bet to 1000 and proceed cautiously versus a 4-bet.

FiveTwo's 3-bet from the Big Blind to 1000 is a standard response to my Middle Position open. While KQo is vulnerable to a premium 3-betting range (like QQ+, AK), I am getting decent pot odds to continue (700 to call into a 1350 pot) and I will have the benefit of position throughout the rest of the hand. Folding KQo in this spot is too conservative for 6-max play, and 4-betting would likely overplay the hand. I will call to see a flop and use my positional advantage to navigate the post-flop play.

As the preflop 3-bettor, I retain a range advantage on A-high boards (I have many strong Ax and overpairs), and a small c-bet pressures Flash’s non-A hands (small pairs, suited connectors, some broadways) while denying equity. KhJh has a gutshot to the Q plus backdoor hearts/overcard potential, so it’s a reasonable low-risk bluff candidate at a small sizing. If I get raised, I can comfortably fold; if called, I can continue on good turns (Q/K/J/heart) and often shut down on blanks.

FiveTwo has led for a small sizing (approximately 23% of the pot) on a board that contains an Ace, a Ten, and a Nine. After 3-betting pre-flop, this range frequently includes strong Aces, sets, or high pairs like JJ. With KQo, I have a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway nuts (any Jack) and I am in position. The immediate pot odds for calling 600 to win a total pot of 3,250 are approximately 18.5%, which is very close to the raw equity of hitting the gutshot. When factoring in the implied odds of winning a larger pot if I hit my straight on the turn or river, and the fact that I am in position to navigate later streets, calling is the most profitable play. Folding is too conservative against this sizing, and raising is unnecessarily risky against a range that hits this board well.

After 3-betting pre and c-betting flop, Flash’s call on A-T-9 heavily concentrates him on Ax, Tx, and some sticky pairs/draws. The 5s turn doesn’t improve my hand (still only a gutshot to Q for Broadway), and K/J overcards are rarely clean versus his continuing range. This is also not an ideal combo to barrel as a bluff (I don’t block Ax and I don’t pick up additional equity like a spade draw), so firing again risks getting called too often by Ax/Tx and burning chips early. Best line is to check and realize equity; versus a meaningful turn bet I’ll usually fold, and if it checks through I can consider value/bluffing rivers that favor my range (Q in particular).

FiveTwo's check on the turn after leading the flop suggests either pot control with a marginal hand or a surrender. With my gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and King-high, checking behind is the most prudent play. It allows me to realize my equity and potentially hit my straight on the river for free. Betting now risks a check-raise that would force me to fold my equity, and it is unlikely to fold out a hand like an Ace or a medium pair that FiveTwo might be checking for control.

River K gives us a medium-strength hand (pair of kings) but it’s a bad value-betting candidate: Flash’s flop call in a 3-bet pot is heavily weighted to Ax, Tx, and strong draws like QJ (now a straight). If we bet, we get called/raised by many better hands (Ax, QJ, KQ, two pair) and are unlikely to be paid often by worse (mainly some Tx that may still just check back). Checking keeps our showdown value intact and also allows Flash to bluff missed draws; we can then decide based on sizing.

On this river, the King of Clubs improves my hand to a pair of Kings with a Queen kicker. The board is Nine of Spades, Ace of Diamonds, Ten of Hearts, Five of Spades, and King of Clubs. FiveTwo, who 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop, has now checked both the turn and the river. While the pair of Kings is a decent showdown hand, it is still losing to any Ace (which is a significant part of a 3-betting range). If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I will almost certainly be called or raised by better hands like an Ace or a set. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value and potentially win the pot against hands like Jack-Jack, Queen-Queen, or missed draws that FiveTwo might have been 3-betting with.
I am UTG with T2o, which is a very weak hand. In a 6-max game, I should be playing a tight range from early position. T2o has no value here and should be folded immediately.

Pocket Aces is the best starting hand in hold'em. UTG folded, so the pot is unopened. In middle position with deep stacks in a tournament, a standard raise of 3x the big blind (300) builds the pot for value while maintaining fold equity against speculative hands behind. Limping is too passive and invites multi-way pots; folding is -EV. This size discourages weak calls while allowing set-miners or strong hands to continue.
62 offsuit is a trash hand with no playability. Facing a 3x raise from middle position, this is a clear fold. No reason to defend with such weak holdings.

I’m on the button with Q♦J♦ facing a 3bb (300) open from middle position. QJ suited is a strong, positionally advantaged hand — good playability and blockers to broadway hands. I want to take the initiative, isolate the opener, and reduce the chance of a multiway pot, so I 3-bet. I’ll make it a standard-sized 3-bet to 1,000 total (about 3.3x the open): big enough to generate fold equity and discourage the blinds from calling cheaply, but not so large as to overcommit. Plan: if called, use position to c-bet on favorable flops and play postflop aggressively; if faced with a sizable 4-bet, fold QJs (it’s behind a polarized 4-bet range).

Facing an UTG+1 open and a button 3-bet, J7s is too weak to cold-call out of position with the big blind still to act. Even with decent raw equity, our equity realization will be poor OOP, and we’re at risk of getting squeezed or forced into tough postflop spots in an inflated pot. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here with no meaningful blockers and limited playability if called. Best decision is to conserve chips and fold.

I am holding Pocket Nines (9c 9d) in the Big Blind. The action involves an open raise from Middle Position to 3bb and a 3-bet from the Button to 10bb. This indicates a strong range from the Button. Cold calling a 3-bet out of position with 99 is generally -EV due to the lack of initiative, being out of position, and the risk that the original raiser (Elon) may 4-bet. Furthermore, the implied odds for set mining are insufficient (calling 900 to win against stacks of ~9000 offers 10:1, while 15-20:1 is preferred). Folding is the prudent play to preserve stack depth early in the tournament.

Pocket Aces remain the nuts preflop facing a button 3-bet, which has a wide range including Ax hands, broadway, suited connectors, and bluffs. Early in the tournament with deep stacks (~97BB), a 4-bet extracts maximum value from worse hands like AK/AQ/KQ while maintaining fold equity against bluffs and speculative hands. Calling induces multi-street playability but keeps the pot smaller; we prefer to build it now with AA. Standard sizing ~3x the 3-bet to 3000 total commits appropriately without overexposing our stack. Blinds folded, heads-up dynamics favor aggression.

I’m on the button with Q♦J♦ and I 3-bet to 1,000 to take initiative. Elon responded with a large 4-bet to 3,000, so I must call 2,000 into the current 4,150 pot (calling would make the pot ~6,150) — I need roughly 32.5% equity to break even. Elon’s sizing and position after my 3-bet is polarizing and likely value-heavy (AA/KK/QQ/AK with only occasional bluffs). QJs is often dominated or has poor equity vs that range (roughly ~20–30%), and a raise to 5,000 would overcommit me and invite a shove. Consistent with my preflop plan to fold QJs to a sizable 4-bet, I will fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

AK suited is a premium hand from UTG in a 6-handed game. Standard play is to raise to build the pot, take initiative, and narrow the field. Previous hands show opens to 300 are standard. Raising to 3x BB (300) is appropriate with deep stacks (~100BB effective). No strong reads yet, but this hand plays well post-flop in position or as aggressor.
T2o is a very weak hand. Facing a UTG raise of 3x, I have no reason to get involved with this trash. Easy fold.

I’m in the cutoff with 6♥6♦ facing an UTG open to 3bb (300) and ~90bb effective stacks. Pocket sixes are a classic set‑mining hand — with these depths I prefer to call rather than make a marginal 3‑bet. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves my implied odds to hit a set, and keeps position on later streets vs the opener. A small reraise to 500 risks a 4‑bet and puts me in a tough spot; if there’s a sizeable 3‑bet/4‑bet I’ll fold. I’ll call and evaluate on the flop.

KQo is strong on the button, and the UTG open plus a cold-call creates a great squeeze spot: we have position, can often win the pot preflop, and when called we isolate weaker ranges while denying equity to the caller. Flat-calling invites the blinds in and plays a dominated/high-variance multiway pot with an offsuit broadway hand. I’ll 3-bet to a size that pressures both players and discourages the blinds from coming along; if UTG 4-bets, we can comfortably fold this hand.

I am holding T8o in the Small Blind facing significant action: a UTG open to 300, a CO call, and a Button 3-bet to 1500. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against a 3-bet and potential 4-bets from the original raiser. The pot odds do not justify calling with an unsuited connector gap hand that is likely dominated. Conserving chips is the only logical play here.

Holding 9d 3d in the Big Blind, I am facing a significant raise to 1500 from FiveTwo (Button) after an UTG raise from Elon and a call from Minni. Although the hand is suited, 9-3 is extremely weak and will be played out of position against potentially three opponents if Elon or Minni call. The risk-to-reward ratio for calling 1400 chips with such a marginal hand is poor. I will fold and preserve my stack.

AK hearts suited is a premium hand, top of my UTG range and crushes Button's likely polarized 3-bet range after a CO caller (QQ+, AK, bluffs/squeezes). Folding is too tight early in tournament with deep stacks. Calling OOP risks tough post-flop spots with merged ranges and no initiative. 4-betting is optimal: takes control, builds pot when ahead, applies pressure. Sizing to ~3x the 3-bet (4500 total) balances value and fold equity without overcommitting (fits stacks: mine 10.8k, villain 6.85k). Previous action sizes support this (e.g., Hand 2 3k over 1k).

I called the UTG open intending to set‑mine, but the button 3‑bet to 1.5k and Elon then 4‑bet to 4.5k puts me in a spot I should not stretch into with 6♥6♦. I must call 4,200 more into a 6,450 pot, which requires roughly 39% equity (4200/(6450+4200) ≈ 39.4%). Pocket sixes only hit a set by the river about ~19% (flop ~11.8%), and against a 4‑better’s likely range (high pairs/AK) I’m behind most of the time. Implied odds are limited here (calling commits a big chunk of my stack and the pot will likely be multiway), and a 5‑bet shove would be too thin and dominated by value hands. Folding preserves chips for better spots, so I’ll fold.

After squeezing preflop with KQo, facing a UTG 4-bet to 4500 from a stack that will have only ~2150 behind is a clear indicator of a value-heavy, committed range (typically QQ+/AK and maybe a little wider). Calling 3000 requires ~32% equity, but KQo is usually dominated and performs poorly versus that range, and the resulting SPR is so low that we’ll be forced into high-variance all-ins without enough equity or fold leverage. Shoving is even worse because he’s never folding getting great odds. Best play is to cut losses and preserve the remaining stack.
T7o from UTG is a clear fold. This hand is too weak to open from early position - it's dominated by better tens and has poor playability postflop. I'll wait for a better spot.

I’m in middle with JcQc (QJ suited). Pot is 150, 100 to call and the hand is unopened (Claude folded). QJs is strong and plays well post-flop, so I want to open-raise to take the initiative, build the pot, and reduce multiway action. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (total 300) — large enough to discourage callers/squeezes but small relative to our ~86BB stack. If called I’ll use position to c-bet on favorable flops and re-evaluate vs aggression; I’ll fold to a large 3-bet out of position unless I have a specific read or the sizing makes calling correct.

Minni’s 3bb open from middle position is likely fairly strong, and Q6s is dominated by many of their continuing Qx hands. Calling invites tough spots with reverse implied odds and we still have the button and blinds behind who can squeeze. A 3-bet bluff with Q6s is too thin given stack depths and opener’s likely continue range. Best EV is to conserve chips and wait for better suited/connected holdings in position.

I am on the Button with Ah 4h facing a standard 3x open raise from Minni in Middle Position. A4s is a strong candidate for flat-calling in position. It has excellent playability post-flop due to nut flush and wheel straight potential, but it is often dominated by the opener's Ax range, making a 3-bet slightly risky against an MP open (though viable as a bluff). Given the deep stacks (~98 BB) and early stage of the tournament, calling allows me to realize my equity in position without inflating the pot unnecessarily or facing a potential 4-bet that would force me to fold my equity. I will call to see a flop.

I have pocket Jacks in the small blind, which is a premium hand. There has been an open to 300 from middle position and a call from the button. Playing JJ multi-way out of position is difficult, so I will 3-bet (squeeze) to 1200. This size is designed to either win the pot pre-flop or isolate a single opponent, reducing the likelihood of overcards outdrawing me on the flop.

8h5d is a complete trash hand with no playability, straight draw potential, or suited/connectors value. Facing a 3-bet from Flash (SB) after Minni's open and Pro's call, this is a clear fold. Multiway pot potential with poor odds (1900 pot to call 1100, but equity <10% vs ranges). Preserve stack early in tournament (hand 4/150). Flash shown aggression previously (Hand 1 squeeze/call, Hand 2 fold). No reason to speculate.

I opened to 300 with QcJc and the button called; the small blind 3-bet to 1200. Calling costs 900 more into a ~1900 pot, so I need ~32% equity to break even. QJ suited has good post‑flop playability and — crucially — I will still have position on the 3‑bettor after the flop, which makes a flat-call the preferred line: it lets me realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and use position to apply pressure on favorable boards. Folding is too tight with this hand and a deep stack early in the tourney; 4‑betting to 2100 is too thin (I don’t have the value hands to do it for value and QJs is marginal as a 4‑bet bluff given stack depths and the risk of a shove). Plan: call, see the flop, c‑bet when the texture and equities favor me, and fold or pot‑control when I’m out of position/dominated or face heavy resistance.

I am facing a squeeze to 1200 from the Small Blind (Flash) and a call from the original raiser (Minni). I have already invested 300 chips. The total pot is currently 2800 (1200 Flash + 1200 Minni + 300 Me + 100 Dead BB). I need to call 900 more to see the flop, offering me pot odds of approximately 24% (900/3700). Although the SPR will be low (~2.3), A4s plays well in multi-way pots due to its nut flush and straight potential. Being on the Button grants me the advantage of acting last post-flop, allowing me to realize equity or bluff effectively if checked to. My hand is too weak to 4-bet for value and 4-betting as a bluff has low success probability against two opponents who have shown strength. Folding is too tight given the excellent price and position. Therefore, calling is the most +EV play.

I have an overpair (JJ) on a very favorable 9-high flop. The board contains two clubs, so while it is relatively dry, there are some flush draws possible. Since I was the pre-flop 3-better, this board texture hits my range well, and I likely have the best hand against Minni and Pro. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 1,600 (roughly 43% of the pot) to extract value from 9x, TT, 88, and flush draws, while also protecting my hand against overcards.

Call 1600. I have QcJc on 9c 2h 3c — a very strong flop: a four-card club flush draw plus two overcards. I have 9 club outs + 6 non-club Q/J outs = 15 outs (≈32% to hit on the turn, ≈54% by the river). Calling 1600 into a 5300 pot requires ≈23% equity to break even, so this is +EV. Flash 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet here; his range includes value but also bluffs and hands without clubs. I'm in position, so calling lets me realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and see how the button reacts. Raising is a reasonable semi‑bluff but would bloat the pot and risk running into a stronger club or a re‑raise. Plan: call now; if I hit a club or pair I will press for value, if I miss I'll proceed cautiously (check/call or fold to heavy turn aggression) and re-evaluate.

I hold Ah 4h on a board of 9c 2h 3c. I have a gutshot straight draw (need a 5), a backdoor flush draw, and an overcard. The pot odds are attractive at roughly 19% (calling 1600 into 8500), but the situation is precarious. The SB (Flash) 3-bet pre-flop and is c-betting into two opponents, signaling strength (likely overpairs or strong draws). Minni's call in the middle indicates they likely have a piece of the board or a flush draw, removing outs or dominating my equity. My Ace outs are likely dominated by the 3-bettor's range (AK/AQ), and the 5c is a dirty out that completes the flush. With an SPR of roughly 1.2 if I call, I will be committed to a shove on the turn without a made hand. Given the dirty outs and reverse implied odds in a multi-way pot, preserving my stack is the better long-term play.

The turn card (2c) is highly unfavorable for my pocket Jacks. It completes the club flush and pairs the board. Since I do not hold a club, I have no insurance against a completed flush. Minni called a 3-bet pre-flop and a significant flop bet, suggesting a range that includes strong top pairs, overpairs, or flush draws that have now hit. With the pot already at 6,900 and Minni having only 5,850 behind, a bet here effectively commits us. I will check to control the size of the pot and see how Minni reacts. If she checks back, I can re-evaluate on the river; if she bets, I will have to consider if my overpair is still good against her range.

I have QcJc and the turn (9c 2h 3c 2c) completes my club flush. Flash (the SB) was the preflop 3‑bettor and c‑betted the flop, then checked the turn — a line that often contains missed overcards, single pairs, or turn‑pairs/pot‑control hands rather than the nut club or a full house. With a made flush I should be extracting value and charging any weaker clubs, 9x/3x pairs, and redraws while protecting against giving a free river. I’ll bet for value about half the pot to maximize calls from worse hands but avoid overcommitting if I face a large raise. Bet 3000 (~0.45 pot); if I get a big raise I’ll re‑evaluate (large raises will usually represent A/K‑clubs or full houses and will be treated cautiously).

The turn card, the 2 of clubs, is a highly unfavorable card for my pocket Jacks. It completes the club flush and also pairs the board. While I have an overpair, I do not hold a club, meaning I have no insurance or redraw potential if Minni has completed a flush. Minni called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the flop, suggesting her range is quite strong, likely consisting of high pairs, sets, or flush draws. When I checked the turn to control the pot, her 3,000 bet (nearly half the pot) signals significant strength or an attempt to capitalize on the scare card. Given the board texture and the fact that I am out of position with no clubs in my hand, I am likely either way behind a flush or a set, or in a marginal spot against a hand like A-9 or 10-10. Considering the tournament's early stage, I will fold to preserve my stack rather than committing a large portion of it on a board where my hand has become a bluff-catcher.

UTG in a 6‑hand game with 2♠3♠ is too weak to open. This hand has low absolute value, lots of reverse implied odds and will be played out of position against several players. Limping (call 100) just invites raises and multiway pots where my hand rarely realizes equity; raising to 200 is leaking chips with a hand I don’t want to build a pot with. Table history shows active preflop aggression, increasing the chance of isolation/3‑bets. With 127BB I can wait for much better spots — fold and preserve the stack for positionally profitable hands.

J2o is too weak to open from middle position in 6-max: it’s heavily dominated by opponents’ continuing ranges and plays poorly postflop, especially with tougher positions (CO/BTN) left to act who can 3-bet or isolate. Limping is also unprofitable as we realize equity poorly and invite multiway pots. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff with King-Five offsuit (K5o). This is a weak hand that typically falls outside of a standard opening range from this position. While late position allows for stealing the blinds, the players behind me (Flash on Button, Elon in SB) have shown aggression in previous hands (3-betting and squeezing). Opening this hand risks getting 3-bet or playing a dominated pot out of position against the Button. Folding is the prudent and standard play here.

I am on the button with Queen-Five offsuit. While I have the best position, this hand is too weak to open for a raise, especially with Elon in the small blind who has shown significant aggression in previous hands (4-betting in Hand #2 and Hand #3). Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

Folded to me in the small blind with Q8o, a playable stealing hand against the big blind (Claude, who has been folding preflop in prior hands and likely defends wide but folds often). Pot odds and position postflop make this a standard spot to raise for a steal. Deep stacks (130BB effective), early in tournament, maximize fold equity while maintaining initiative. Raising to 2.5x BB (250 total) is standard sizing for SB steal: small enough to steal frequently but balanced. Limp is poor as it plays out of position OOP with no initiative; fold too nitty missing dead money.

4c6s is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. Facing a min-raise from SB, I'm getting 2.3:1 odds which isn't great for this hand. 46o plays poorly postflop and will be dominated often. Folding is the disciplined play here.

6-max UTG with K7o is too weak to open: it’s heavily dominated by the continuing ranges (KQ/KJ/KT, AK) and plays poorly multiway when we limp. With ~69bb effective, there’s no need to force marginal spots this early; folding preserves stack and avoids tough postflop decisions out of position.

I hold Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o) in Middle Position. This is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With players left to act behind me and no chips invested in the pot other than the ante (if any, though none listed here), the only correct strategic play is to fold immediately to preserve my stack of 86.5 BBs for better opportunities.

I am in the Cutoff position with King-Ten suited (Kd Td). This is a strong opening hand, especially after two players have already folded. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially win the blinds or play the hand with positional advantage against the Button or Blinds.

Q9s is a strong suited connector on the button facing a cutoff open-raise from an aggressive player (Flash). Position is excellent, equity realization is high postflop with flush and straight potential. Pot odds are favorable (calling 300 into 750, ~4:1, needing ~20% equity, which Q9s achieves vs. wide CO range). Calling keeps the pot small, allows blinds to act (potential multiway pot where hand plays well), and exploits position. 3-betting is possible as a bluff but risks bloating pot OOP vs. calling station; folding too tight early with deep stacks. Prefer calling to realize equity over 150-hand tournament.

AJh is a premium hand in the small blind. Flash raised to 300 from CO and Elon called on BTN. With two players in the pot, I should 3-bet for value rather than just calling. AJs plays well heads-up and can extract value from worse aces and broadways. A standard 3-bet size here is around 3.5-4x the original raise, so around 1100-1200.

I'm in the big blind with 7♦8♣ facing a small‑blind 3‑bet to 1,100 after a 300 CO open and a button call. The pot is 1,800 and I must call 1,000 more (total committed 1,100), which gives ~36% break‑even — with deep stacks (~126 BB) and good implied equity from a connected hand, this is worth defending. A 4‑bet is unappealing (no blockers, poor fold equity), so I will call to see a flop and proceed cautiously: fold to a large 4‑bet, otherwise continue only with strong connections (open‑enders, two pair or better, or favorable board texture/top pair with decent kicker).

I started the action with a raise to 300 holding Kd Td. After a 3-bet to 1100 from Claude in the Small Blind and a cold-call from Minni in the Big Blind, the pot has grown significantly. I need to call 800 more to stay in the hand. The pot odds are approximately 3.5 to 1, and King-Ten suited is a very strong hand that plays well in multi-way pots due to its straight and flush potential. Given the stack depths and the price, calling is the most profitable play to see a flop and evaluate further action.

Facing a SB 3-bet squeeze after calling a CO open, now with Q9s suited on the button in a growing pot. Pot odds are excellent (800 into 3600, ~18% equity needed), and Q9cc has strong implied odds, playability, flush/straight potential multiway, and position to realize equity postflop. SB 3-bet range polarized (value QQ+/AK, bluffs); BB flat and CO call suggest speculative/medium strength hands where Q9s performs well (35%+ equity 3-way vs typical ranges). Raising risks bloating pot OOP for opponents or isolation unnecessary; folding too tight early with deep stacks (eff ~7k) and 150-hand tourney. Call to control pot size, exploit position, and play speculative hand profitably.

I whiffed this flop completely with AhJh on 8dTs6d. I have two overcards but no pair, no diamond draw, just backdoor hearts. With 3 opponents in a 4400 pot, c-betting into this coordinated board is risky - it hits their calling ranges (pairs, sets, straight draws, diamond draws). Best to check and see a free card, hoping to hit an A or J on the turn.

I hold 7♦8♣ on a flop of 8♦ T♠ 6♦ — middle pair (pair of eights) with a weak kicker. This is a 4‑way pot and the board has overcards (T) and diamond cards, so many opponents' ranges include hands that currently beat me (top pair, overpairs, strong draws). I have only a backdoor diamond (needs two cards) and a one‑card straight draw to the 9 plus a few outs to two pair/trips (~9 outs ≈ ~35–36% to improve), so my hand is vulnerable. Against multiple players I have poor fold equity, so betting here would bloat the pot and likely get raised by better hands. I’ll check to control the pot, see a turn, and only invest more if I improve or get good price to continue; I’ll fold to large aggression unless the turn gives a clear improvement (7/8/9 or favorable action).

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker and have a strong flush draw (King-high). The pot is already quite large (4400) due to the pre-flop action. Since the Small Blind (Claude) and Big Blind (Minni) have checked, I should take the lead. Betting here serves two purposes: extracting value from weaker pairs or draws and protecting my hand against various straight draws (like 7-9 or 5-7). A bet of approximately half the pot is appropriate given the board texture and my strong equity.

Qc9c holds a gutshot straight draw (7 for T-9-8-7-6), queen overcard, and backdoor club flush draw on a drawy 8d-Ts-6d flop. Pot odds are strong (2200 to call into 6600, requiring ~25% equity), and the hand likely realizes 30%+ against Flash's polarized donk-bet range after flatting a 3-bet preflop (draws like diamond flush draws, 97/75 straight draws, mid-pairs, bluffs). Position on the button allows postflop control and equity realization in a 4-way pot where blinds checked (Claude's 3-bet may have missed, Minni speculative). SPR low (~1:1 after call), but favorable as queen-high has showdown value and turn hits make strong hands. Folding is too tight early (hand 6/150, deep stacks ~100bb+); raising risks commitment with marginal equity and unchecked blinds behind. Call to exploit position, pot odds, and playability.

I have AhJh on 8dTs6d - complete whiff with just two overcards. Flash bet half pot and Elon called, showing strength. I need 20% equity to call (2200 into 8800). With ~6 outs to overcards that may not even be good on this coordinated board (sets, two pair, diamond draws possible), I only have ~12% equity. Minni still behind me. Clear fold.

I hold 7♦8♣ on 8♦ T♠ 6♦: middle pair (eights) with a weak 7‑kicker, a backdoor diamond possibility and a gutshot to the 9. Flash bets 2,200 into a pot of 8,800 and Elon calls. Calling 2,200 would mean putting up 22 BB for only ~20% pot‑odds (2,200 to win 11,000). Although I have ~9 outs to improve (~35% to hit by river) that is versus a single hand; multiway and the likely ranges here (top pair tens, overpairs, strong draws) reduce my real equity and I can still be crushed even when I improve. Raising is unattractive — I have poor blockers and little fold equity in a 3‑way spot. Per my preflop/flop plan to check‑control and fold to significant aggression, the correct, risk‑averse play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I have top pair (Tens) with a King kicker and a King-high flush draw (Kd Td on a 8d-Ts-6d-8h board). The 8h on the turn pairs the middle card of the flop. My stack is 5450 while the pot is 8800, creating a low stack-to-pot ratio. Checking here allows me to evaluate Elon's reaction. Since he has been aggressive in previous hands, checking may induce a bluff from his straight draws (79, 57) or lower diamond draws. Given my top pair and significant flush-draw equity, I am likely committed to the hand, but checking is a safer way to navigate the turn against a potentially improved range or to trap a bluff.

Turn board pairs the 8h, bricking my gutshot straight draw (to the 7) and backdoor flush draw, leaving queen-high with no pair or strong draw on a paired, draw-heavy board (open-ended straight draws, diamond flush draws possible). Heads-up vs Flash after blinds folded post-flop; he donk-bet flop polarized (sets like 88/T8/68, two-pair, top-pair good kicker, combo draws like J9dd/97dd/75dd, bluffs like Adx/KQs), but checking turn suggests weakness (missed draw, marginal made hand without value, or trap with monster). Pot 8800, SPR low (~0.6 for him with 5450 behind, higher for me at 9800), to-call 0 checked to me in position. Betting risks raising from better (sets/two-pair) or folding out worse (missed draws/ace-high bluffs) while committing further with thin value; queen-high has ~25-35% equity vs his range but poor realization if raised. Checking controls pot, induces bluffs on river (paired board caps strong hands), realizes showdown value cheaply against his likely capped range (no AA/KK likely after preflop action), and preserves stack early in tournament (hand 6/150, deep effective stacks). Preflop call and flop call were correct for playability/odds/position; now pivot to passive line to extract max EV.

The river Ace of Clubs is a significant scare card. My pair of Tens, which was top pair on the flop, has now been downgraded to second pair, and I missed my King-high flush draw. Given the action, Elon could easily have floated the flop with an Ace-high flush draw or a straight draw that includes an Ace. Since I am first to act on this street, checking is the most prudent move. It allows me to see how Elon reacts; if he checks back, I might win with my Tens against a missed straight or lower flush draw. If he bets, I will have to evaluate based on the size, but betting myself into a possible Ace or better hand is not ideal given my stack size relative to the pot.

Qc9c holds queen-high on a paired 8s board with an Ace river, facing Flash's check after he donk-bet flop (polarized: draws/TPGK/sets) and checked turn/river. His check-check post-flop suggests capped range (missed diamond/straight draws like J9dd/97dd/75dd, weak pairs like 77/55/JT, A-high bluffs without Ac, or traps with monsters but unlikely given action). Queen-high has solid showdown equity (~40-50% vs. this range) and blocks Qx. Pot odds irrelevant at check; betting thin risks folding out worse (missed draws/A-high) or facing raises from 88/T8/86/A8/T6 (low frequency), while SPR low (~1:1 for me). Checking realizes equity, induces any weak calls if he wakes up (rare), controls pot, and preserves deep stack early (hand 6/150, ~100bb). Consistent with passive pivot post-brick turn as planned.

I am holding 2h 5c in the UTG position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unsuited, disconnected, low ranks). Being out of position with a trash hand makes this a trivial decision. There is no strategic justification for entering the pot.

I am in Middle Position with Jack-Seven offsuit (J7o). This is a weak starting hand with poor equity and post-flop playability. Given that there are still four players to act behind me, including the blinds, and I am in an early-middle position, J7o does not meet the requirements for a standard opening range. I will fold to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

Cutoff position after two early folds, giving strong stealing opportunity. 8sTs is a premium suited connector (~12-15% of hands), excellent for playing in position postflop with flush and straight potential. Deep stacks (98BB) favor raising for value, fold equity, and building pot. Standard open size 3x BB (300 total) consistent with prior opens, builds pot without overcommitting. Limping weak, folding too tight this early in tournament.

I have Ah2c on the button facing a 3x open from Elon in cutoff. A2o is marginal but I have position and the ace provides some value. Calling is reasonable here - I can play postflop in position and see if I connect. 3-betting would be too aggressive with this hand, and folding gives up too much value from the button with position.

I'm in the small blind with Th7s facing a cutoff open to 300 and the button call (pot 750, I must call 250). This will be at least a three-way pot and I'll be out of position against both opponents, making a marginal hand like T7s difficult to play postflop. Calling for ~250 into 750 (~3:1) requires ~25% equity vs two ranges, which T7s rarely has here; 3-betting to isolate would be thin and commit more chips into a hostile spot. With deep stacks and many hands left, folding and waiting for a better spot is the +EV choice.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action preflop (200 to win 750), and K2 suited has enough equity/playability to defend the big blind in a multiway pot. A squeeze here would bloat the pot out of position with a dominated king and poor realization when called, so the higher-EV line is to take the odds and see a flop.

We have bottom pair plus a strong king-high diamond flush draw on Qd-7d-2s. In a 3-way pot out of position versus the preflop raiser, leading isn’t ideal because it bloats the pot while we’re still vulnerable and can get raised into tough spots. Checking keeps ranges wide, lets Elon c-bet frequently, and preserves the option to continue by calling or occasionally check-raising depending on sizing and who continues (multiway reduces fold equity, so we don’t need to force action immediately).

Flop of 7d 2s Qd completely misses my 8sTs suited connector. No pair, no straight draw (gutshot at best with T98x unlikely), weak backdoor spade flush draw and minor straight potential. Board favors opponent ranges with Qx, diamond flush draws, or low pairs. Facing two opponents (FiveTwo checked BB, Claude behind on button), betting as a bluff risks too much pot (950) into multiway with deep stacks and position disadvantage on Claude. Checking preserves stack, allows free card for backdoors or turn improvement, and controls pot. Fold equity low post-check, but can fold to Claude's bet. Continuation betting too thin early in tournament against calling stations like FiveTwo (history of big calls/raises). Optimal check to realize equity or escape cheaply.

I flopped bottom pair with ace kicker (A-high). Both opponents checked to me - Elon was the preflop raiser and checked, showing weakness. This is a good spot to take a stab at the pot with position. A small bet around 1/3 pot will fold out overcards and test if anyone has a queen or better. My pair of 2s beats complete air.

Claude’s 350 stab into 1300 is very small, giving us an excellent price (350 to win 1650) with bottom pair plus a strong diamond flush draw. Raising here in a 3-way pot risks bloating the pot OOP and getting forced into high-variance decisions if Claude 3-bets or continues only with strong Qx/sets, while calling keeps his bluffs/worse pairs in and also allows Elon to overcall with dominated draws/pairs (increasing our implied odds when we hit diamonds). With our equity and positional disadvantage, the higher-EV, lower-variance line is to peel and play turns (apply pressure on diamond turns / good barreling cards; continue vs reasonable sizing on blanks).

Flop completely misses my 8sTs: no pair, no strong draw, only weak backdoor spade flush draw and negligible straight outs. Preflop I raised as suited connector for playability, but checked flop realizing no equity. Claude's button bet into two checkers shows strength (likely Qx, flush draw, or probe), and FiveTwo's call from BB adds value to their range (pairs, draws). Multiway pot with 1650 already and getting 350 more, but my equity is <10% against their ranges; pot odds don't justify a float. Folding preserves stack early in tournament (hand 7/150, ~95BB deep) for better spots. No reason to bluff-raise or call out of position on Claude.

Turn brings the 4th diamond and we make a K-high flush. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot and we don’t want to check and let Claude take a free showdown with Qx/7x that will often check back on a scary 4-flush board. Leading also targets value from worse made flushes (any lower diamond combo), pairs/sets that picked up a diamond, and occasional stubborn Qx with a diamond. I’ll choose a medium sizing to get called by worse while not overinflating the pot against the main hand that beats us (A-high diamond flush).

I have bottom pair (deuces) with ace kicker but no diamond. The turn brought the 5d, putting three diamonds on board. FiveTwo donk-betting 900 into me after just calling my flop bet is concerning - this could be a made flush, a strong queen with diamond draw, or a semi-bluff. With just bottom pair and no redraw on this wet board, I'm likely behind when facing aggression. Calling 900 to potentially face another bet on the river with a vulnerable hand isn't profitable here.

I am in the UTG (Under The Gun) position with 8h 4d. This is an extremely weak hand to play from the earliest position at a 6-max table. Standard poker strategy dictates folding such hands to avoid being out of position and dominated by stronger holdings from opponents behind me. There is no reason to enter the pot with this hand.

Preflop, folded to me in middle position with Jd8d suited connectors. This is a playable hand for a raise in 6-handed play, especially deep-stacked. Standard 3x BB raise to 300 to steal blinds or build pot with position advantage postflop. Folding too tight, limping weak. Opponents deep, my aggressive image from prior hands supports wide raising range.
92 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from middle position, this is an easy fold. No reason to get involved with such a marginal holding.

On the button with A♥J♠ facing a standard MP open to 300 (pot 450). This is a strong button spot — AJo is a good 3-bet candidate: it has value vs the opener’s range and plays well in position. I want to apply pressure, isolate the opener, and pick up fold equity against the blinds. I’ll 3-bet to a size that’s big enough to get respect but leaves plenty of chips to play postflop (raise to 1,200 total). If Elon folds I take down the pot; if he calls or the blinds come along I keep positional advantage and can c-bet or realize equity. If Elon 4-bets large (committing a big portion of effective stacks) I’ll strongly consider folding AJo since it’s often dominated by 4-bet ranges.

TT is strong versus a Button 3-bet range, but a cold 4-bet from the small blind at ~78bb effective tends to create a very low SPR if called, effectively forcing us to stack off postflop with a medium overpair that’s in rough shape versus the part of Minni’s range that continues (JJ+/AK-heavy) and also exposes us to a back-raise from Elon. Calling keeps dominated hands (AQ/KQ, suited broadways, smaller pairs) in, preserves stack depth for postflop edge, and avoids turning TT into a thin/awkward 4-bet stack-off spot OOP. We’ll proceed with a controlled 3-bet pot and evaluate flops (happy on low boards, cautious on A/K/Q boards).

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 4c, a very weak offsuit hand. Facing an open raise to 300, a 3-bet to 1200 from the Button, and a cold call from the Small Blind, the action is heavy. Even though I am getting decent pot odds, my hand has very low equity against the ranges of a 3-bettor and a cold caller, and I would be playing a bloated pot out of position with a trash hand. Folding is the only correct play.

Preflop, I opened to 300 from middle position with Jd8d suited connectors, a standard play in 6-handed deepstack. Minni 3bets to 1200 from the button (wide range possible, but polarized), and FiveTwo flats 1150 OOP from SB (suggests decent strength like pairs, suited broadways, or suited connectors). Pot is 2800, calling 900 OOP multiway requires ~25% equity, but J8s plays poorly postflop OOP vs these ranges (dominated often, hard to realize equity). 4betting as bluff is risky with no blockers to strong hands and deep stacks; better to fold speculative hand and preserve 9200 stack for premium spots. Opponents aggressive but this spot too tough.

We’re OOP in a 3-bet pot on K-8-3 rainbow holding TT (a medium underpair). This board heavily favors Minni’s continuing range (lots of AK/KQ, KK, AA, and higher pairs like QQ/JJ), so leading doesn’t accomplish much and risks bloating the pot when we’re often behind. Checking keeps our range protected (we can have strong hands like AK/KK/88), controls SPR, and lets us respond based on her c-bet sizing (potentially continue versus small bets, fold versus heavy pressure).

I was the preflop 3‑bettor from the button with A♥J♠ and the SB (FiveTwo) called. The flop 8♠ K♣ 3♦ is very dry and was checked to me. My hand has an ace that is ahead of a lot of the SB’s calling range (many broadways, A‑x, and weak holdings) but no pair and no real draw. I want to apply pressure to fold out unpaired hands and small pockets while keeping the pot manageable against a sticky caller. With a pot of 2,800 a ~1/3 pot c‑bet (~1,000) is the right sizing: it preserves fold equity, extracts value from worse A‑x, and avoids building a bloated pot with a marginal hand. If I face a large check‑raise I’ll fold; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (bet for value if I improve to a pair, otherwise control the pot).

Minni’s 3-bet range smashes K-8-3, so we shouldn’t bloat the pot with a raise when most of the continuing range is Kx/overpairs. However, this is a very small c-bet (1000 into 3800), giving us excellent immediate odds and allowing us to realize equity (set outs + some showdown value versus hands like AQ/AJ/QJ that range-bet). Plan: call once and proceed cautiously—fold to large turn pressure on bad cards (A/Q/K, heavy sizing), continue on more neutral turns versus reasonable sizing.

Turn brings the Ace, which is one of the worst cards for TT versus Minni’s 3-bet/c-bet range: it adds a lot of strong Ax (AK/AQ/AJ) and improves her bluffs into higher-equity hands, while our hand is now just a marginal underpair with very limited improvement (mostly 2 outs to a set). With SPR ~1, leading (or check-raising) would mostly get action from hands that crush us and rarely folds out the Ax portion. Best line is to check, keep the pot from inflating, and evaluate her sizing—planning to continue only versus very small bets and otherwise fold.

I have A♠J♠ (top pair, good kicker) after the turn A♣. I 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the dry K‑8‑3 flop; the SB (FiveTwo) called both streets, which is consistent with a range of Kx, medium pocket pairs, some A‑x, or speculative hands. He checked the turn, showing weakness or pot‑control. This is a clear value‑bet spot: I beat Kx, most pocket pairs, and worse A‑x hands. I want to extract value without over‑committing or folding out thin callers, so I’ll bet ~1/2 pot to target those hands and deny equity to unlikely backdoor draws. If he check‑raises big I’ll strongly consider folding (turn check‑raise polarizes to sets/two‑pair), but if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and continue for thin value on safe cards.

Turn A♣ is a very bad card for TT in a 3-bet pot on K-8-3: it heavily improves Minni’s range (AK/AQ/Ax bluffs now become top pair, plus she still has AA/KK and Kx). Against a turn betting range here, TT has very little real equity (mostly 2 outs) and we’re not a good bluff-catcher because we lose to all her value and also to hands like QQ/JJ if she’s betting them for protection.

Pot odds are ~25%, but calling also leaves us with a tiny stack behind and an awkward river where she can jam and we’re forced into a dominated bluff-catch or a late fold after putting in more chips. Raising/shoving has poor fold equity versus the Ax/Kx-heavy portion that barrels this card. Best play is to cut losses and fold now.

UTG with 2d5c offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands. No straight potential, no flush potential, dominated in most scenarios. Position is terrible for speculative hands. Deep stack tournament play requires folding trash UTG to avoid difficult post-flop spots and preserve stack for premium hands. Pot is just blinds, no reason to enter voluntarily.
62 suited is a junk hand from middle position. Not worth calling 100 to enter the pot with this weak holding.

Pocket jacks in the cutoff is a clear open-raising hand: strong, wants to take the initiative, and benefits from isolating the button/BB rather than seeing a multiway flop. There's been no raise yet and the pot is only the blinds (150), so a standard 3x BB open to 300 builds the pot, provides fold equity, and keeps control of the hand while leaving plenty of stack depth to maneuver postflop. I will raise to 300; if I face a medium 3-bet I can call and play the flop, and I’ll re-evaluate against a very large 3-bet or shove.

On the button versus a cutoff open, A7o is a reasonable candidate to apply pressure rather than flat. Calling invites the blinds (especially a deep BB) to squeeze and puts us in dominated-ace spots postflop; 3-betting leverages position, uses our ace blocker to reduce her strongest continues (AK/AQ/AA), and should pick up the pot immediately at a decent frequency given typical CO opening ranges. With our stack (~56bb), we’ll keep the sizing controlled and fold comfortably to a 4-bet.

I hold 9sTs in the Small Blind. The action facing me is a raise from the Cutoff to 300 and a 3-bet from the Button to 900. Cold calling a 3-bet from the Small Blind with a suited connector is generally a losing play due to being out of position against a strong range and the possibility of the original raiser 4-betting or the Big Blind squeezing. The pot odds do not justify the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage. Folding is the standard and correct decision here.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Four offsuit. The action has been a raise from Minni (CO) and a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo (BTN). K4o is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially when I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents. It's an easy fold to conserve my stack for better spots.

I opened CO to 300 with JJ and the button responded with a small 3-bet to 900. JJ is too strong to fold and I don’t want to play a large pot out of position passively. The 900 sizing indicates a fairly wide, potentially exploitable range from the shorter-stacked Button (4750). I’ll 4-bet to 3000 — large enough to get fold equity, isolate the button and make postflop play simpler (if he calls the effective stacks become shallow ~1750), but not commit my entire stack. If he jams I’m comfortable calling with JJ; if he calls we proceed OOP with a strong hand and a big pot. Raising only to the minimum (1500) is too weak and a flat-call gives him the initiative and cheaper flops.

We 3-bet A7o as a blocker/position pressure play, but facing the 4-bet to 3000 with only 4750 total, continuing is bad. A call leaves ~1750 behind (very low SPR), so we’ll be essentially committed on most flops while often dominated by Minni’s value-heavy 4-bet range (AQ+/AK, big pairs). Jamming doesn’t generate meaningful fold equity either because she’d only need to call 1750 more and will continue with virtually all hands she 4-bet. Pot odds on a call (~34%) aren’t met versus a typical 4-bet range. Best play is to cut losses and fold.

JTs suited is a solid opening hand from UTG - good playability with straight and flush potential. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB. If I get 3-bet heavily I can fold, but this hand plays well postflop and has good equity against most calling ranges.

Claude opened UTG to 250 in a 6-handed game. I have Ac8c — a suited Ace with decent postflop play but often dominated by stronger Aces in an UTG range and I’m out of position to players yet to act. A tiny reraise to 400 gives little fold equity and a larger 3-bet is too loose vs an UTG opener. Folding is too tight given the hand’s equity. I’ll call 250 to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and see a flop where I can continue with an Ace or strong club draw; I’ll be prepared to fold to heavy preflop 3-bet sizing or large postflop aggression without improvement.

K2o is heavily dominated and plays poorly multiway, especially versus an UTG open plus a caller. With only ~47.5bb, squeezing here is too loose and risks getting flatted or 4-bet by stronger ranges; calling invites tough postflop spots with reverse implied odds. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

I am on the Button with K9o. Claude opened from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range (AQ+, 88+, KQs, etc.), and Minni flat-called in MP, likely with a pocket pair or suited connector. K9o performs poorly against a strong UTG opening range and is often dominated by hands like KQ, KJ, or AK. Although I have position, the multi-way nature and reverse implied odds make this a losing call. A squeeze is also risky given the UTG strength. Folding is the most prudent long-term play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The action opened with an UTG raise to 250 followed by a call. Queen-Four is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against an UTG opening range. There is no reason to risk chips with such a low-equity hand when I will be playing the rest of the hand out of position against two opponents.

AhJd is a strong premium hand, roughly top 5% equity, with excellent playability from the big blind. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (250, 2.5BB) and a flat call from Minni, plus folds behind including the SB, the pot is 650 and I only need 150 to call, offering great implied odds. However, being OOP multiway with AJ offsuit calls for aggression to isolate against Claude's likely strong UTG range (e.g., pairs, broadway) where AJ dominates many holdings like AQo, KQs, AJs. A 3-bet also applies pressure and builds the pot when ahead. Sizing to 900 (about 3.6x the open, pot-committing lightly) balances value, fold equity, and my 91BB stack while respecting villain stacks (Claude 79BB, Minni deep). Calling risks multiway OOP domination scenarios; folding is too tight early in tournament.

JTs suited is too strong to fold facing a standard 3-bet to 900. I'm getting ~2.2:1 pot odds with a hand that plays well postflop. We have great implied odds with straight/flush potential. Calling keeps Minni in potentially, giving us multiway action where our suited connectors excel. 4-betting would be too aggressive OOP with this hand.

Fold. Elon’s 3-bet to 900 and Claude’s cold-call behind him indicate real strength; this is exactly the heavy 3-bet spot I planned to give up on when I called the 250. Calling 650 more into ~2100 only gets ~24% pot equity requirement, and A♣8♣ is frequently dominated by stronger Aces and broadway hands in these two ranges. I’d be out of position and facing difficult multiway postflop decisions, and a 4-bet to isolate would be committing too many chips with a marginal hand. Preserve the stack and wait for a clearer spot.

Heads-up OOP on a 6-7-8 rainbow flop after 3-betting preflop with AJo. This board heavily favors Claude's UTG 3-bet calling range (sets like 66/77/88, two-pair like 67/78, straights like 59/T9, strong pairs/draws). I have ace-high with no pair, no straight draw (J is live but not connected), no flush draw (backdoor diamond/spade irrelevant). Equity is low (~20-30% vs range). Betting risks check-raise or calls; c-bet sizing tough on coordinated board, low fold equity. Checking realizes equity by showdowning vs missed overpairs/draws if villain checks back, or easy fold to bet. Pot odds good now (2100 into 8300 stack), preserve stack early in tournament (hand 10/150). No need for spewy bluff OOP.

I have JTs on 8-7-6 rainbow board. This gives me a gutshot straight draw (need a 9 for T-high straight) plus two overcards and backdoor spade flush draw. Elon 3-bet pre and checked this connected flop, suggesting either missed overcards (AK/AQ) or a trapping range. With 6-7 outs of equity and fold equity against his air, a small semi-bluff bet makes sense to take down the pot or charge his draws while building the pot for when I hit.

Facing a 900 bet into a ~2100 pot (now 3000 total) OOP on a highly coordinated 6-7-8 rainbow flop with AhJd (ace-high, no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw, only speculative backdoors). My preflop 3-bet range included AJ as a value-ish hand, but this board smashes Claude's tight UTG calling range: sets (66/77/88), two-pair (67/68/78), straights (T9/59), overpairs (AA/KK rare), combo draws. Equity <25% vs his range; pure bluff-catch or fold equity play needed. Calling OOP realizes minimal equity, vulnerable to turn barrels, poor implied odds deepstacked early (hand 10/150, 83BB). Raising as semi-bluff has low fold equity vs sticky villain post my check; commits ~20% stack for thin equity. Folding preserves stack for better spots, as previous check was to pot control/realize equity selectively but villain's bet polarizes to value we beat rarely (overpairs). Consistent with GTO: fold marginal ace-high to c-bets on drawy boards OOP.

UTG in a 6-max game with T♣5♠ (offsuit) is too weak to play. This hand is often dominated by broadways and has very poor equity in multiway pots; with five players left to act I’d be out of position against likely raises. Limping (calling 100) invites multiway action where T5o performs poorly, and open-raising to 200 is outside a reasonable UTG opening range. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

UTG has already folded and we’re facing only the blinds (50/100). With 9♠? actually 9h2d offsuit in middle position, this is far too weak to open-raise profitably and too dominated to limp/call and realize equity multiway. Preserving ~47.5bb and avoiding tough postflop spots vs strong/aggressive players behind (Button/Blinds) is best.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Five offsuit (A5o), and action has folded to me. While standard charts often suggest opening any Ace from the Cutoff to steal the blinds, the table dynamics suggest caution. Specifically, the player in the Small Blind (Elon) has shown significant aggression, including multiple 3-bets and 4-bets in the first 10 hands. Additionally, the Button (Flash) is the chip leader and capable of applying pressure. A5o is a marginal hand that plays poorly against 3-bets and is easily dominated post-flop. Given my tight image so far, a raise might get respect, but the risk of facing a re-raise from the aggressive blinds outweighs the potential reward of stealing the 1.5bb pot. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand or a better spot to attack.

I am on the button with Ace-King offsuit (AKo), which is a top-tier starting hand. Since everyone folded to me, I should raise to build the pot and potentially take the blinds, or play a strong hand in position against the blinds. A standard 3x big blind raise is appropriate here.

83o is a complete trash hand with no playability, connectivity, or high card strength. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has a wide range but still much stronger than mine, especially out of position in the SB. Pot odds are poor (calling 250 into 450 is ~35% equity needed, but this hand has negligible equity against any raise range). Folding preserves stack for better spots. No history suggests Flash is super weak here.

Q6o is a weak hand to defend from the BB against a button raise. While I'm getting decent pot odds (200 to win 450), I'll be out of position with a marginal holding. Q6o is near the bottom of any reasonable defending range. Better to fold and save chips for stronger spots.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 2♠3♥ offsuit is far too weak to open or limp profitably. Calling 100 would put us in multiway, out of position versus stronger ranges, and we have poor playability and reverse implied odds. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am in Middle Position with Pocket Nines (9c 9d), a strong pair that sits well within a standard opening range. The player Under the Gun (FiveTwo) has folded, leaving the pot unopened. Given the stack depths (85BB effective) and the aggressive nature of the opponents observed in previous hands (frequent 3-betting), opening the pot is the standard play to build value and deny equity to overcards. I will raise to 250 (2.5BB), which is a balanced sizing that offers a good price on a steal while limiting losses if I face a re-raise from the aggressive players behind me.

I have AQo in the Cutoff, which is a premium starting hand. Pro raised from Middle Position to 250. I will 3-bet to 750 to take the initiative, isolate the raiser, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. 3-betting also makes it harder for the Button and Blinds to enter the pot with speculative hands.

Suited KJo is a strong hand on the button, crushing typical open-raising and 3-betting ranges from Pro (passive-tight) and Flash (aggressive). Pro's middle position raise is wide, Flash's cutoff 3-bet over it suggests light aggression based on history (frequent raises and calls). Excellent squeeze spot: 4-bet to isolate, fold out Pro (often folds to aggression), pressure Flash, and blinds likely fold. Call realizes equity but invites multi-way pot OOP for others; fold too tight early tournament. Size to ~3x the 3-bet (2250 total) for value, fold equity, and pot-building while leaving ~6000 behind (73bb effective post-raise). Maximizes EV in deep-stack tournament.

T7o is a weak hand facing a 4-bet. There's been a raise, 3-bet, and now a 4-bet to 2250. I'd need to call 2200 with a hand that has very poor equity against 4-bet ranges. Easy fold - this hand plays terribly in a 4-bet pot out of position.

I have 4♠5♦ (offsuit) — a weak, easily dominated hand. The action shows Pro opened, Flash 3-bet, and Elon 4-bet to 2,250. The pot is 3,400 and calling 2,150 requires ~38.7% equity to break even, which 4-5 offsuit does not have vs a 4-bet/3-bet range (likely premium pairs and big broadways). This will also very likely be multiway and I'll be out of position postflop, greatly reducing implied odds. A raise/5-bet bluff is too risky here (Elon still has chips to call and original players may respond). Preserve my 14,900 stack for better spots — fold.

I opened to 250 with Pocket Nines, faced a 3-bet to 750 from the Cutoff, and now face a cold 4-bet to 2250 from the Button. A cold 4-bet indicates an extremely strong range (likely QQ+, AK), against which 99 performs poorly (roughly 20-50% equity). The cost to call is 2000, and with the Button having only ~3750 behind, I do not have the implied odds to set mine (requiring ~15-20:1, getting ~3.5:1). Additionally, the original 3-better is still left to act behind me. Continuing here would be losing chips in the long run. The prudent play is to preserve my healthy stack of ~80BB.

I originally 3-bet to 750 with AQo, but Elon (the Button) has now 4-bet to 2,250. This is a very strong signal, especially in a tournament setting. Elon has a relatively short stack of 6,000. If I call, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand with a significant portion of my stack committed. Given that Elon is 4-betting into two players (myself and the original raiser Pro), his range is likely extremely narrow, consisting of premium hands like AK, QQ, KK, or AA. AQo performs poorly against this range. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am in UTG position with J5o (Jack of Spades, Five of Clubs). This is a statistically very weak hand with poor playability and low equity, especially from early position where I would be out of position against any callers. The standard and optimal play is to fold immediately to conserve chips.

I am in the Hijack position with Ace-Six offsuit. While an Ace is always tempting, A6o is a relatively weak hand to open with in a 6-max game, especially with active players like Elon and Minni still to act behind me. Standard opening ranges usually exclude offsuit Aces below A8 or A10 from this position. Given my healthy stack and the potential for a 3-bet from the late positions, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Preflop in cutoff with two folds ahead. 72h is a weak suited hand with poor playability and low equity against calling ranges. BB has 46BB and may defend wide, SB deep. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in early tournament phase. Tight-aggressive approach prioritizes premium hands.

On the button with 23o - one of the worst hands in poker. Everyone has folded to me, which is a good steal spot, but 23o is too weak even for a button steal. FiveTwo is short-stacked and might look to reshove, and Minni is the big stack who can defend profitably. If I raise and get called, I'll play terribly postflop. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Heads-up vs the big blind with 7♠5♦ — a weak, unsuited hand and I’ll be out of position postflop. The cost to see a flop is small (50 more chips to make my total commitment 100) and gives me the chance to hit a cheap flop or fold to aggression. I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand and risk a 3-bet/all‑in from a shorter-stacked BB, so I’ll just complete to see a flop. If the BB raises significantly I’ll fold; if he calls we proceed cautiously and will only continue with clear improvement or good draws.

Minni’s limp from the small blind caps their range and invites us to take initiative. AJo plays very well heads-up and suffers if we let them realize equity cheaply with lots of dominated Ax/Jx and suited connectors. Raising builds a value pot versus worse aces/jacks, generates folds from junk, and gives us betting lead postflop while keeping sizing reasonable at ~4.5bb effective.

I completed to 100 to see a flop cheaply, but FiveTwo’s raise to 450 is a meaningful reraise. With 7♠5♦ offsuit I’m out of position and have very poor equity and fold equity; calling 350 to play a bloated pot OOP is a losing proposition and a 3-bet bluff is too thin against a shorter-stacked, aggressive BB. Preserve my healthy stack and fold to this raise.

I am in early position (UTG) with 2s 4d, which is an extremely weak hand. It has virtually no showdown value and poor playability. Standard poker strategy dictates folding such hands from this position to avoid getting into difficult spots with inferior equity.

K7o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor postflop playability, dominated by better kings and vulnerable to suited/connected hands. In middle position, 6-handed, with aggressive opponents like FiveTwo and Minni behind who frequently raise, folding avoids difficult multiway pots or tough postflop decisions. Early in tournament (hand 14/150), stack at 94BB is deep; preserve for premium spots.
A7 suited in the cutoff with two folds ahead. This is a solid opening hand - suited ace with some playability. Standard raise to 250 (2.5x) to isolate and take initiative.

Fold. 7♣3♠ is a very weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with almost no post‑flop playability. Claude opened to 250 from the cutoff (2.5x BB). There are two players (SB FiveTwo and BB Pro) left to act who are likely to defend, which would make this a multiway pot and further destroy my equity. Calling 250 into a 400 pot or attempting a light 3‑bet bluff would risk chips with little chance to realize equity or generate reliable fold equity. I'm on the button so there will be better spots to exploit — preserve the 14,800 stack for those opportunities.

We’re in the small blind, out of position versus a cutoff open. 9♣2♣ is too weak to defend here: it’s dominated when we make a pair, has poor playability OOP, and calling invites the big blind to come along (or squeeze), creating tough multiway spots with low equity. A 3-bet bluff with this hand is also unnecessary at ~48bb effective given our poor blockers and limited postflop realization. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with KdQs facing a standard 2.5x open from the Cutoff (Claude). The Small Blind has folded. My hand is strong enough to 3-bet for value, but calling is a very standard and profitable line that keeps the opponent's range wide, including hands I dominate (like KJs, QJs, weaker Kx/Qx), and minimizes the risk of facing a 4-bet which would force a fold. The price is good: I need to call 150 to win a pot of 400, offering me excellent pot odds to realize my equity out of position. I will call and defend my blind.

I have flopped the nuts (Broadway straight) with KdQs on an AsJsTd board. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, Claude. The board texture is extremely wet and interacts heavily with the Cutoff's opening range (sets, two pairs, top pairs, flush draws). It is highly likely that Claude will make a continuation bet given this texture. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out his bluffs and might freeze up his marginal hands. Checking allows me to induce a bet, after which I can decide whether to check-raise to build the pot immediately (charging flush draws) or check-call to trap. Therefore, checking is the standard and most profitable line to start the post-flop action.

I flopped top pair with A7h on Js-As-Td. This is a wet board with spade flush draws and straight draws (KQ makes the nuts). Pro checked to me. I should c-bet for value and protection against the many draws. Betting around 60-70% pot is appropriate - enough to charge draws but not overcommit with a weak kicker.

I have flopped the Broadway straight (the current nuts) on a wet board (As Js Td) while holding Kd Qs. My hand is extremely strong, but the board texture is dynamic with flush draws and pair-board potential. I hold the Qs, which blocks some of the opponent's flush draw combos, and the Kd, which blocks the straight split. The opponent c-bet 350 into 550. Raising here (check-raising) would extract value from sets (JJ, TT) and two pairs (AJ, AT), but it risks folding out weaker top pairs (Ax) and pure bluffs which I want to keep in the pot. Since I am in position relative to the action sequence for the next street (I act first, but calling closes the action now), calling allows me to conceal the strength of my hand. A call keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing him to barrel again on the turn with bluffs or value bet worse hands. Given we are deep-stacked, I can look to build the pot on later streets or if the turn is safe.

I hold the nut straight (KdQs on AsJsTd7d). The turn card 7d introduces a second flush draw (diamonds) in addition to the spades. Having check-called the flop, leading out now (donk betting) is generally suboptimal as it often folds out bluffs and weak hands that might otherwise bet. The standard and most profitable play against a pre-flop raiser who c-bet the flop is to check again. This gives the opponent a chance to fire a second barrel with strong hands (sets, two pair) or semi-bluffs (flush draws), or even pure bluffs. If he bets, I can check-raise for value and protection against the draws. If he checks back, I will look to bet the river for value. My hand is strong enough that I want to maximize the pot, and checking induces the most action from an aggressive opponent.

Turn 7d improved me to two pair (Aces and Sevens). Board is still draw-heavy with spade flush draw and straight possibilities (KQ, Q9). Pro check-called flop and checked again - likely on a draw or weaker ace. Need to bet for value and to charge draws. Betting around 65% pot.

I hold the current nut straight (KdQs) on a highly dynamic board (AsJsTd7d) that now features two flush draws and potential full house outs for the opponent. The opponent has fired a second barrel of 800 into a pot of 1250 (approx 64% pot), indicating strength (sets, two pair, strong draws) or a committed semi-bluff. While calling is a safe option to keep bluffs in, the wetness of the board makes 'slow-playing' risky; many river cards (any spade, any diamond, board pair) could freeze the action or downgrade my hand to a bluff-catcher. Furthermore, we are deep-stacked, and simply calling leaves the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) over 2 for the river, making it harder to get all chips in. By check-raising the turn to 2400, I accomplish three things: 1) I extract maximum value from hands that will call (sets, two pair, combo draws), 2) I charge draws a premium price while I am definitely ahead, and 3) I manipulate the pot size to create an SPR of less than 1 for a comfortable river shove. This line maximizes my expectation of winning the opponent's entire stack.

Pro check-raised my turn bet from 800 to 2400. This is a strong line that could indicate: sets (JJ/TT), straights (KQ), strong two pair (AJ/AT/JT), or semi-bluffs with combo draws (KQs/Q9s of spades). My A7 for two pair is decent but vulnerable. I beat weaker two pairs and draws, but lose to sets, straights, and better two pairs. Pot odds are good (~3.8:1), and folding two pair seems too weak here. Calling to control pot size and evaluate river makes sense rather than raising and potentially getting it in bad against hands that dominate me.

I hold the Broadway straight (KdQs) on a board of AsJsTd7d5s. The river 5s completes the spade flush draw, which is a significant scare card given the opponent called my large check-raise on the turn. The opponent's range includes sets, two pairs, straights, and flush draws. Betting (likely an all-in given the SPR < 1) is problematic because the opponent will likely fold hands I beat (sets, two pair) and call with hands that beat me (flushes). Betting effectively turns my straight into a bluff or a thin value bet that isolates me against a stronger range. Checking is the superior option as it allows me to potentially realize my equity at showdown if the opponent checks back worse hands, or induce a bluff. If the opponent bets, I will have a decision to make, but leading out provides little value.

River 5s completes the spade flush. Pro check-raised me big on the turn then checks the river when the flush completes. This is interesting - if Pro had a flush draw, they'd likely bet for value now. The check suggests either: (1) a hand like AJ/AT/JT scared of the flush, (2) a set/straight now worried, or (3) a bluff that gave up. My two pair is decent but there are many hands that beat me (better two pair, sets, straights, flushes). Betting for value is thin - I'd mainly get called by better. Checking back to realize my showdown value is the correct play here.

Pocket eights is a strong premium hand from UTG in 6-handed play. Standard strategy is to open-raise for value against a calling range that includes worse pairs, suited connectors, and broadways that we dominate. Raising 3x the big blind (300) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multi-way pots where sets are vulnerable. Folding is too tight preflop. Opponents have shown aggression in past hands, but our hand plays well postflop. Deep stacks (94bb) allow for flexibility.
K3o is a weak hand facing an UTG raise. With 4 players behind me and an early position raiser showing strength, this is a clear fold. No need to defend with such a marginal holding.

Facing an UTG open to 300 with 4♣6♣ in the cutoff, this is a weak, low-suited connector that plays poorly out of position against an early opener. The pot is only 450 and calling 300 gives marginal immediate pot odds but poor implied odds versus an UTG raising range. There are still the button (shorter, likely to shove or isolate) and two big stacks in the blinds who can exert pressure — calling risks multiway pots and difficult postflop decisions OOP. 3-betting as a bluff is too thin here versus an UTG range and I don't have enough equity to call profitably. Preserve my stack for better spots and fold.

Button vs an UTG 3bb open, Td6d is a bit loose but has decent playability (suited, can make strong draws) and we have position to realize equity. A 3-bet bluff here is unattractive with a middling suited hand that blocks nothing and a 48bb stack—getting 4-bet puts us in a bad spot and bloats variance. Calling keeps ranges wider, lets us outplay postflop, and we can comfortably fold to heavy pressure on unfavorable textures.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 7c (76 offsuit), facing a raise from UTG (Elon) to 300 and a call from the Button (FiveTwo). The pot odds are roughly 25% (calling 250 to win a total pot of 1000), but I am in the worst position at the table against a strong UTG opening range and a Button caller. 76o is a weak hand that suffers from significant reverse implied odds, especially in a multi-way pot out of position. It rarely flops well enough to continue profitably. Folding is the clearly superior play to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind holding 3c-4h, which is an extremely weak hand. Elon raised from UTG, showing significant strength, and FiveTwo called on the button. While the pot odds are tempting (200 to call into a pot of 750), this hand plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position against two opponents. It's better to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

Pocket eights on a Kd9s6s flop is a marginal made hand heads-up OOP against a button calling range that includes many kings, nines, suited broadways, and flush/straight draws. The board is extremely draw-heavy with two spades (flush draw) and potential straight draws (78o/s, J10o/s, 75s, etc.). Betting risks raising into better hands or getting check-raised off equity, while check-calling induces bluffs from missed draws and controls the pot size. FiveTwo's button call preflop is wide but caps stronger pairs somewhat; checking balances our range and exploits aggression shown in prior hands (e.g., Hand 1, 7). With deep stacks (94bb), we preserve fold equity later and realize equity by check-calling medium bets, folding to large overbets indicating strength. Plan: check-call small/medium bets, fold to large ones, check-raise all-in if villain shows weakness.

We have a weak made hand (bottom pair) with some modest backdoors, but betting into an UTG range on K96ss risks getting check-raised off our equity and bloating the pot when we’re often behind Kx/overpairs/9x. Since Elon checked, we can take the free card in position, keep the pot controlled, and re-evaluate turns where we improve (6/T/diamond) or where his range stays capped and he checks again (then we can stab).

Pocket 8s remains a marginal holding OOP heads-up on a K-high, draw-heavy board (6s Kd 9s Jc) after a check-check flop. The turn Jc furthers straight draws (T7, 78) and improves Jx or QJ hands in villain's wide button calling range, while completing no flush. Villain's flop check-behind suggests missed draws, weak pairs (77-55, Ax suited), or traps with top pair; however, their history shows selective aggression (e.g., Hand 7 turn bet, Hand 1 continuation). Betting risks inducing stronger hands (Kx, sets) or check-raises, bloating the pot with a hand that wants to realize equity cheaply.
