Checking maintains pot control, invites bluffs from missed draws (spades, 78, T7), balances our checked range (draws, weak pairs), and allows cheap realization of showdown value. With villain's shorter stack (45bb) and pot SPR ~10:1, we can call medium bets profitably while folding to large overbets signaling strength. Plan: check-call small/medium villain bets exploiting their aggression, fold to 3x+ pot, check-fold river if checked through unless obvious weakness.

Elon has checked flop and turn after opening UTG, which caps his range heavily toward give-ups (AQ/AJ without a spade, some underpairs) and medium-strength hands that want pot control. Our 6x is a weak showdown hand but we also picked up a gutshot to the 8, and we benefit a lot from denying equity and avoiding tough river spots where he can stab. In position, a delayed stab is credible because our range contains plenty of Kx/Jx/sets that would take this line. I’ll use a medium sizing that pressures his ace-high and underpairs, while keeping my risk controlled so I can comfortably fold to a check-raise.

Pocket 8s remains a strong but vulnerable one-pair hand OOP on this draw-heavy board (6s Kd 9s Jc) after check-check flop. Villain's small turn bet (450 into 1200, ~38% pot) after checking flop behind suggests thin value (KJ, QJ, T9, mid-pairs like 77-TT), probe bets, or bluffs from missed draws (spades, 78, T7). Their button calling range preflop includes these, and history shows selective aggression (e.g., Hand 1, 7, 14). Pot odds are excellent (450 to win 1650, ~3.6:1), and we have decent equity (~30-40% vs polarized range). Calling realizes equity cheaply, maintains pot control, balances our range (blocks sets, can have draws), and sets up fold/call river based on action. Raising risks bloating pot OOP to worse or getting called by better; folding is nitty given price and villain's tendencies. Effective SPR post-call ~1.7:1 with villain's 45bb stack, allowing profitable calls to medium river bets if checked through.

Pocket 8s is a marginal one-pair, no-kicker hand OOP heads-up on a scary, draw-heavy board (6s Kd 9s Jc Qc) that completes some straight draws like T8 (8-9-T-J-Q) and improves Qx/Jx to two-pair or trips. Preflop: villain wide button call vs UTG raise includes Ax, Kx suited, suited connectors (T8s fits perfectly as spade or club draw preflop), broadway. Action: check-check flop (villain weak/missed), my check-turn 450 bet-call (~75% pot, thin value/polarization probe after check-back), now river Qc is a bad card for our hand, bringing villain's possible straight (T8), QJ two-pair, KQ top two, while missing spades/clubs flush draws. First to act OOP on river with vulnerable medium-strength: betting thin value risks raises from better (Kx, straights, sets from 66/99/KK) or folds from worse, bloating pot badly (SPR post-bet low with villain 40bb). Checking induces bluffs from missed draws (spades, gutshots like 75/87), realizes showdown vs thin value we beat sometimes (77-55, weak Ax), balances checked range (missed draws, weak pairs blocking sets), exploits villain's selective aggression (turn bet after flop check-back suggests range including bluffs/value). Pot odds excellent if bet-into; villain 40bb effective, so can call <50% pot profitably vs polarized range (~35% equity). Plan: check-call small/medium river bets (probe thin Kx/Jx), fold to large (nuts), check behind if weak. Consistent with prior check-call discipline, deep stack flexibility, villain history (e.g., Hand 7 turn bet after checks, Hand 1 cont bets). Maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

River Qc completes our 9-T-J-Q-K straight (K-high). Elon’s line (UTG open, check flop/turn, call a small delayed turn stab, check river) is consistent with a lot of bluff-catchers (Kx, Qx like AQ, Jx), some two-pair that improved (KQ/QJ), and occasionally a ten for a chop. The main hand that has us crushed is specifically AT for Broadway, which can plausibly arrive this way, so I don’t want to overbet and isolate myself vs that portion. I’ll go for a medium value size that gets paid by one-pair/two-pair and chops, while keeping room to fold if he springs a big check-raise that’s heavily weighted to AT.

Pocket 8s OOP facing 900 (35% pot) river bet into 2550 after check-check flop, my check-call turn 450, and scary Qc river on draw-heavy 6s-Kd-9s-Jc-Qc board. Pot odds excellent (~26% equity needed). Villain's button call vs UTG raise: wide (suited Ax/Broadway/connectors like T8s/T9s for straight, spade/club draws, mid-pairs TT-77, weak Kx). Flop check-back: caps strong K9/sets, favors missed draws/weak pairs (e.g., Axs spades, 87s, QJs). Turn Jc bet small (~38% pot): thin value (Jx draw complete, KJ) or probe/semi-bluff; call induces. River Qc bricks spades but completes straights (T8/T9/AT for 9TJQK), improves Qx/KQ to two-pair/top-two, clubs draw. However, villain ~30% equity vs our 8s: beats bluffs (missed spades/gutshots like 75s/107s), weak Ax/77-55, thin K9/KT, loses to KQ/KJ/QQ/JJ/T8/straights/sets. History: FiveTwo selective aggression (Hand1 cont bets, Hand7 turn bet after checks, Hand14 turn aggression) but check-backs weak; smaller turn bet then 35% river consistent with polarization including bluffs post-check. Deeper stack (86bb eff) allows profitable call (SPR ~2 post-call vs villain 31bb); calling realizes cheap equity, balances range (checked medium pairs block sets), exploits tendencies. Fold too tight given price/history; raise poor (thin value OOP risks calls by better/folds worse, low SPR). Call maximizes EV, pot control.
4s9c is a very weak hand, especially from UTG position. No connectivity, no suited cards, no high cards. Easy fold.

I have A♦8♣ in middle position with four players yet to act (Cutoff FiveTwo 6600, Button Pro 11250, SB Flash 13350, BB Elon 7650). Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call. This is a hand worth opening — strong ace but a mediocre kicker that I don’t want to see multiway for free. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300) to take the initiative, isolate the blinds or thin the field and avoid being dominated by stronger Aces in a limp pot. Plan: if I get one caller I’ll c-bet favorable ace/two-tone flops and proceed cautiously on wet boards; fold to a large shove/3‑bet (big 3‑bets > ~3x my open) because the kicker is vulnerable. A 300 open is a balanced, low-risk way to build pots and steal blinds from these stacks.

Minni opens 3x from middle position and we’re in the cutoff with 6c2c. This hand has very weak high-card value, poor realization versus an MP range, and calling invites tough squeeze spots from Button/Blinds with our stack not deep enough to justify loose suited-trash speculation. It also has no relevant blockers to make a profitable 3-bet bluff. Best EV is to preserve chips and wait for better steal/defend opportunities in position.

I am on the Button with J9o, facing a 3BB open raise from Minni in Middle Position. Minni is the chip leader and has shown aggression in previous hands (3-betting and 4-betting). J9o is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by an MP opening range (AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, etc.). Although I have position, the hand lacks the playability and implied odds to profitably call a 3x raise against a strong range, and calling invites squeezes from the blinds. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind holding King-Five offsuit. Minni opened with a raise to 300 from middle position. K5o is a weak hand to play out of position against a range that likely consists of broadway cards and pairs. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff with this hand is not ideal. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in the big blind facing a raise to 300 from Minni in middle position after UTG folded. My hand is 3c8s, a complete trash hand with no playability, low cards, offsuit, and disconnected. Minni's opening range from MP is strong (pairs, broadway, suited connectors, etc.), and 38o has very low equity against it (~20-25% at best). Pot odds are 450 to call 200 (2.25:1), requiring ~31% equity, which I don't have. No position postflop, stack 76.5BB is deep enough to wait for better spots. Clear fold to preserve stack.

UtG with J♠ T♥ (Jack-Ten offsuit) in a 6-handed game — playable, connected hand with good postflop playability and decent showdown value. With deep stacks (~150BB) and the table tending to open to ~3x, I want to take the initiative and avoid a multiway pot where JTo loses equity. Standard 3xBB isolation raise to 300 is size-consistent, gives fold equity, and keeps postflop options open. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, play straightforward heads-up, and fold to very large 3-bets or re-evaluate based on opponent and sizing.

Minni opened UTG to 3bb, which is typically a stronger range in a 6-max lineup. Ts3o plays very poorly versus an UTG opening range, is dominated when it makes a ten, and has no suited/connected properties to realize equity. With multiple players left to act (CO/BTN and blinds) and no positional advantage postflop, calling is -EV and bluff-3betting is unnecessary with this hand profile. Best decision is to fold and preserve stack.

I hold Pocket Deuces (2c 2s) in the Cutoff facing a raise to 300 (3BB) from Minni in UTG. While my stack (112.5 BB) is deep enough to technically set-mine (following the Rule of 20), several factors suggest folding is the better option. First, an UTG opening range is typically strong, often dominating small pairs. Second, there are three players left to act behind me, including aggressive opponents like Elon who have shown a willingness to 3-bet/squeeze (e.g., Hand #12). Pocket 2s have extremely poor post-flop playability if a set is not hit immediately, and calling invites a squeeze that would force a fold. Given the position and the potential for action behind, folding is the disciplined, low-variance play.

I am on the button with 9c 2h, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG. Given the strength of my hand and Minni's early position raise, there is no justification for continuing in this pot. I will fold.

4d5h is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability from the small blind, out of position against an UTG raise from Minni who has shown aggression in prior hands. Pot odds are approximately 1.8:1 (450:250), but this hand has low equity against a strong UTG range (dominated often, few straight draws), minimal fold equity if raising, and poor implied odds postflop OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a long tournament.

I have 7-3 offsuit in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300. This is a very weak hand with poor playability. UTG raises typically represent strong ranges, and 73o has minimal equity and plays poorly postflop. Even getting ~2.25:1 odds, this is a clear fold.

UTG in a 6-max lineup still plays as an early position open, and T9 offsuit is too vulnerable to domination (TJs/QTs/AT, etc.) and plays poorly versus 3-bets and multiway calls. With no need to force thin EV spots at 66bb effective, I’ll avoid the reverse-implied-odds situation and wait for a better open.

I hold 2s 6c in Middle Position, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker (unsuited, unconnected, low cards). With a healthy stack of over 100BB early in the tournament (Hand 18/150), there is absolutely no strategic incentive to play this hand. Several players are left to act behind me. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Cutoff with Jh8d. Two players have folded, and it is my turn to act. This hand is significantly below the opening range for this position, as it lacks high-card value and strong connectivity. With three players yet to act, including the button and the blinds, there is a high probability of being faced with a 3-bet or being out of position post-flop with a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

On the button facing an unopened pot after three folds (UTG, MP, CO), I have excellent position. J7s is a strong suited hand for button opens: good playability, straight potential, flush draw potential, and dominates worse hands while having equity against better ones. Raising steals blinds frequently (SB and BB stacks suggest they defend but not always), builds pot OOP advantage postflop, and deep stacks (76BB) reward position. Standard open ~3x BB (300 total). Folding too tight; limping passive. Raise to 300.

I have 3s5h offsuit in the SB facing a button raise to 300. This is a very weak hand - bottom of the deck offsuit. Even with reasonable pot odds (250 to win 450), 35o plays terribly post-flop and is not worth defending against a standard button open. Clear fold.

Elon (button) opened to 300. I’m in the BB with A♦6♥. Pot is 450 and I need 200 more to call, so my total commitment after calling would be 300. A6o is a reasonable defend vs a single BTN open — pot odds (~30.8%) justify a call and the hand plays well enough postflop. A min 3-bet to 500 would be tiny and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand that’s often dominated. Plan: call, see the flop; proceed aggressively if I hit an Ace or a strong draw, otherwise use pot control and fold to heavy pressure.

I have A♦6♥ on 4♦ K♦ K♥ — the nut-diamond draw plus Ace-high. Pot is 650 and I’m out of position to Elon (button), who opened preflop and can easily have Kx (which now makes trips) or many broadways and suited Aces. Because the board is king-paired, building a big pot OOP with just a draw is risky: trips/full houses are in his range. I’ll check to control the pot and induce action. If Elon bets a reasonable size (roughly up to ~2/3 pot, about 400), I’ll call — I have ~10 diamond outs (~21% on the turn, ~38% by the river) and that price + implied odds justifies calling. I will fold to a large check-raise or an over-aggressive shove that commits too much, since those lines are consistent with trips/full houses. If he checks back I get a free turn to realize my outs. Check/call keeps options open and maximizes expected value with this semi-bluff draw out of position.

Post-flop on a paired Kings board (Kd Kh 4d) with Js7h, I have complete air: no pair, no draws, just Jack-high with no blockers to villain's likely range. Minni called a button raise from BB pre-flop, so range includes Kx, pocket pairs, suited connectors/draws. OOP check is standard and weak; c-betting into this board risks calling from value or draws while I have zero equity. Checking preserves my 73BB stack, takes free card (turn OOP first again), and avoids bloating pot OOP with no showdown value. No fold equity strong enough vs. defender, board favors value over bluffs. Long-term tournament play prioritizes not spewing chips with trash.

Check. I have A♦6♥ on 4♦ K♦ K♥ 8♠ — essentially Ace‑high with a very remote backdoor diamond possibility (you would need both remaining diamonds on turn+river; ~4% to make a flush). Elon checked the flop, so his range is wide and not necessarily strong, but the paired kings make trips/full houses possible and I don’t want to build a big OOP pot with a marginal hand. Pot control and free-card equity are correct: check to see the river for free and fold to heavy turn aggression. If Elon bets a small amount on the turn I can call for showdown value and the chance to catch an ace; I will fold to large pressure that strongly represents kings/sets. Checking preserves my 14,800 stack and keeps options open.

On this turn board of Kd Kh 4d 8s, I hold Js7h for pure air: jack-high with no pair, draw, or blockers to villains' strong hands. Minni, OOP in BB, called a button raise preflop (wide range: pairs, suited connectors, Ax, Kx) and checked flop and turn, indicating possible medium strength, weak hands, or traps but likely not monsters given passivity. After checking flop myself (standard with air), a bet now risks villain calling with better (KQ, 88-sets unlikely, but any K beats us) or raising, while fold equity is low on paired board favoring value-checks. Pot is small (650 vs 73BB stack), so realize zero equity for free river card in position; pot control OOP for villain; preserve stack for better spots. Bluffing here spews chips tournament-long term vs defender who checked twice. Check behind optimal.

River: 4♦ K♦ K♥ 8♠ 2♥; my hand A♦6♥. The backdoor diamond missed — I only have Ace‑high. Elon opened preflop then checked flop and turn, a passive line that suggests weakness (or a rare trap), but there’s a real chance he has a king or a stronger made hand that beats me. There’s very little value to betting (few worse hands will call) and my bluff equity is thin (I block some Ax combos with the A♦ and his passive line reduces fold frequency). Pot is small relative to stacks, so the risk/reward favors checking to showdown. Action: check.

River board Kd Kh 4d 8s 2h pairs top with low cards and a possible backdoor heart flush. Holding Js7h for pure Jack-high air, no pair, no completed draw. Minni defended BB preflop (wide: Ax, pairs, suited connectors) and checked flop/turn/river, suggesting medium strength, showdown value, or weakness, but unlikely to fold to a bet on this paired board after passivity. Pot 650 vs my 7300 stack (73BB); deep tournament (hand 18/150) favors checking behind in position: realize equity for free if villain air, pot control, preserve stack for premium spots. Bluff fold equity low vs defender with showdown; betting spews value. Optimal check.

I am in UTG position with Ad 5h (A5o). In a 6-handed game, A5o is generally considered too weak to open from Under the Gun. The hand suffers from poor playability post-flop, domination issues against calling ranges (which often include better Aces), and lack of flush potential compared to A5s. With aggressive players behind like Elon and Minni who have shown willingness to 3-bet or play aggressively, opening this marginal hand creates a negative expected value situation. The standard and disciplined play here is to fold.

I am in middle position with Q8o. This hand is too weak to open under normal circumstances, especially with five players remaining and aggressive opponents behind me. My stack is healthy at 133.5 big blinds, and I should focus on playing higher-quality hands in better positions. Standard 6-max opening ranges for middle position typically do not include Q8o.

Preflop in cutoff with folds from UTG and MP, facing unraised blinds (pot 150, to call 100). T5s is a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential. Excellent position to steal blinds or play postflop profitably against button (Claude) and blinds. Standard open-raise size around 2.5x BB to 250, given typical sizing in history (250-300). Builds pot, applies pressure, and leverages position. Deep effective stacks support speculative hands.

Q4o is a weak hand even on the button. Facing Elon's raise from cutoff, I don't have enough equity to profitably call or 3-bet. Q4o plays poorly postflop and I'd often be dominated when hitting a queen. Standard fold.

I'm in the small blind with 7♥8♠ facing a cutoff open to 250 (pot 400) — I must call 200. 7-8 offsuit is a marginal but playable connector: heads-up it has decent equity and with deep stacks (~154bb) there are good implied odds if I flop a draw or pair. Folding is too tight given the price; 3-betting with an unsuited connector is suboptimal (a min 3-bet to 400 is too small to credibly isolate and a larger 3-bet would be risking chips with a speculative hand). I will call to see a flop, plan to proceed cautiously out of position (fold to big 3-bets preflop, and on the flop only continue with a pair/strong draw or check/control against heavy aggression).

We’re getting an excellent price (150 to win ~600+) and close the action in the BB. A♣9♣ plays well multiway with suited nut-flush potential and decent top-pair value, while a squeeze would bloat the pot OOP versus a CO opener plus an SB cold-caller who can continue a lot. Take the cheap defend and realize equity.

I have 7♥8♠ on A♥ T♣ 7♦ — middle pair (sevens) with a weak eight kicker and I'm out of position. Pot is small (750) and there's no bet to call. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot when I'm often behind to any A‑x or T‑x in the raiser’s range and vulnerable to a raise; the thin value I can get from worse hands doesn’t justify building a big pot. I’ll check for pot control and to let the preflop raiser act first. Plan: call a small/medium continuation bet to extract value from worse pairs and draws, fold to a large raise, and re-evaluate on the turn if checked through.

Multiway (CO raiser + SB caller) on an A-high board strongly favors the preflop raiser’s range. With A9 (top pair, weak kicker, only backdoor clubs), donk-betting risks bloating the pot OOP and getting raised/continued by better Ax and strong draws, while folding out the hands we actually dominate. Checking keeps the pot manageable, protects our checking range, and lets CO c-bet his bluffs and value hands; we can then respond based on sizing and action.

Flop Ah Tc 7d is rainbow, ace-high board. I raised preflop from cutoff with T5s suited connector and hit middle pair (tens) with a weak 5 kicker. Multiway pot with SB (Minni, caller from blind) and BB (FiveTwo, defender). Both checked to me first to act. My range includes top pair Ax, sets, etc., so continuation bet represents strength, denies equity to overcards/draws, extracts value from weaker Ax, worse pairs, or air. Equity solid (~35-45% multiway assuming ranges). Pot 750, bet 40-50% pot for value/protection without overcommitting. Check would pot control but misses value and fold equity. Deep stacks support standard c-bet.

I have middle pair (7♥8♠) on A♥ T♣ 7♦, out of position to the cutoff opener. Elon c-bet 400 into a ~750 pot (pot now 1150). Folding here is too tight — my hand has showdown value and some backdoor/improvement potential. Calling 400 risks 400 to win 1550, so I need ~25.8% equity to break even; against a realistic CO c-bet range (AT, A‑x, some T‑x, broadways, and bluffs) my pair should have ~30–40% equity and good implied odds with deep stacks (~152 bb). Raising OOP with a marginal pair and weak kicker is thin value and likely to get me in trouble versus Ax/Tx. I will call to control the pot, keep worse hands and draws in, and re-evaluate on the turn — fold to a large raise unless I improve to two pair/trips.

We have top pair with a weak kicker in a 3-way pot OOP. Raising mostly isolates ourselves versus better Ax and strong value/draws, while folding out the hands we actually beat (Tx, 7x, floats) and bloats the pot in a reverse-implied-odds spot. Calling is getting a strong price (400 to win 1950 once we call) and keeps CO’s c-bets wide while we realize equity; we can re-evaluate on the turn and play more defensively versus larger barrels or heavy action from SB.

I have middle pair (7♥8♠) with a weak kicker on A♥ T♣ 7♦ Kc and I’m out of position to the original raiser (Elon). The King on the turn helps a lot of the cutoff’s likely range (Kx, some two‑pairs, Ax combos and T‑x), so my hand is still often behind. The pot is modest (1950) and there’s no bet to call. Betting OOP with a marginal pair here is thin value and will simply bloat the pot or get me raised off my hand by better hands; checking preserves pot control and allows me to keep worse hands and bluffs in. Plan: check the turn, and on the river call small/medium bets as a showdown catcher but fold to large raises unless I improve to two pair/trips.

Turn Kc gives us a big equity hand: top pair (A9) plus the nut flush draw (Ac-high). In a 3-way pot OOP, leading tends to bloat the pot and invites raises from better Ax/AK while folding out the weaker hands we want to keep in (Tx, 7x, worse draws). Checking keeps CO’s range wide (lets him continue barreling bluffs/value), protects our checking range on this texture, and we can comfortably continue versus most reasonable bets with our strong draw + showdown value.

Turn board brings the Kc, pairing no immediate board but adding straight possibilities (QJ) and a club flush draw threat (Tc-Kc). I hold middle pair (Ts) with a weak 5s kicker plus a live spade flush draw (8 outs). Multiway pot with two checkers ahead who called flop c-bet; their ranges include Ax (likely top pair), weaker pairs, draws, or floats. My preflop raise + flop bet range is strong (Ax, sets, etc.), so I rep well. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws and missing value from worse Ax/Tx/77. Semi-bluff bet extracts value from weaker hands, protects against draws, and leverages fold equity given their check-check. Pot 1950, bet ~60% pot (1200) balances value/protection without overbetting deep stacks. River flush/straight potential justifies building pot.

I’m out of position with 7♥8♠ on A♥ T♣ 7♦ K♣ — middle pair with a weak kicker. Elon (CO) opened preflop, c‑bet the flop and now fires a sizable second barrel (1200 into a ~1950 pot). Calling 1200 to win 3150 requires ~28% equity (1200 / (3150+1200) ≈ 27.6%). My chance to improve on the river is small (~8 outs → ~17% to hit two‑pair/trips), and versus his turn‑betting range (Ax, Kx, Tx, two‑pairs and some value hands) my overall showdown equity is likely well below the break‑even threshold. Raising OOP with a marginal pair is thin — it risks a re‑raise or commits chips when I’m usually dominated. Given the large second barrel and three‑way dynamic, this is a fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

We’re getting a very good price: 1200 to win 3150 (~27.6%). Our hand is strong enough to continue because we have (1) top pair and (2) the nut flush draw (9 clean club outs on an unpaired board), plus some additional equity versus his one-pair hands when we spike a 9.

Raising is unattractive here: when we raise, we mostly fold out the weaker parts of his range (bluffs and dominated Kx) and get action from the hands that have us in bad shape (AK/AT/sets/QJ). Calling keeps his barreling range widest, preserves our implied odds (river SPR will be low enough to stack him when we hit clubs), and lets us make a disciplined decision on the river if we brick and face a big shove.

Plan: call now; on a club river we can usually check-jam or lead jam for value, and on bricks we lean toward check/fold versus large polar bets while still considering bluff-catch vs smaller sizing.

River Jh is a very dynamic card that improves CO’s double-barrel range a lot (AQ/QJ/KQ make broadway; AJ/AT/AK/KT/KJ are strong value). Our Ac9c is just one pair with a weak kicker and no flush possible (only two clubs on board), so a lead doesn’t get called by many worse hands and can get raised/jammed by the range that has us crushed. Checking keeps our showdown value intact and gives villain the chance to check back bluffs/missed hands; if he bets small we can consider a bluff-catch, but versus a big bet/jam we can comfortably fold.

On the river with the board Ah Tc 7d Kc Jh, I hold Ts5s for middle pair (tens) with a poor kicker and a missed spade flush draw. The pot is 4350, and FiveTwo (BB, stack 4750) has checked after calling my flop c-bet (400) and turn barrel (1200). My preflop raise and two continuation bets represent a strong range: top pair Ax, two-pair/combos, sets, or straights. The river Jh completes the broadway straight for any Qx (10JQKA), but also doesn't complete clubs (TcKc) or hearts significantly. FiveTwo's BB defending range multiway pre, then calling two streets OOP includes Ax (top pair), Tx/Kx worse than mine unlikely, draws that missed, or monsters. Checking river behind realizes equity safely with showdown value against bluffs/floats/weaker Ax (if any), avoids value-owning by better hands like Ax/set/Qx if he calls a bet, and pot-commits risk with my stack (5450) nearly even to pot size. Betting thin value risks getting snapped by Ax or monsters while folding out bluffs; EV-maximizing play is check given the check to me and history of FiveTwo's calling station tendencies (e.g., Hand 15). Preserves stack for future spots in tournament.

I am UTG with Jack-Four offsuit. This is a very weak hand and is not within a standard opening range for this position in a 6-max game. Playing such hands out of position often leads to difficult post-flop situations and unnecessary chip loss. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

UTG (Flash) has folded, leaving a clean pot of 150 (blinds). A9o is a profitable opening hand from middle position in a 6-handed game, with good ace-high equity, playability postflop, and fold equity against the remaining players. Limping is weak and invites multiway pots; folding is too tight given position and hand strength. Standard open-raise size of 3x the big blind (300) balances value and pot-building while maintaining fold equity against button, blinds, and cutoff. My stack of 54.5BB supports this aggression without overcommitting.

KJo in the cutoff facing a standard 3x open from Elon in MP. This is a solid hand that plays well in position. I could 3-bet for value/isolation, but flatting is also fine here to keep the pot manageable and play postflop in position. Calling 300 to see a flop.

I’m on the button with 8♠9♣ facing an open to 300 and one caller. With deep stacks and position on both opponents, this hand has good postflop playability (connectivity, one spade) and the pot odds to call are attractive (300 into 750). A tiny squeeze to 500 would be too weak to fold out callers and a larger reraise unnecessarily bloats the pot with a marginal holding. I’ll take the flop in position, proceed cautiously postflop (check/call or probe-bet only with strong connections), and fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3bb open plus two flats. Although the price (250 more) is tempting, 3♦4♦ realizes equity very poorly out of position in a multiway pot: we’ll often make weak pairs/low straights that are dominated, and we’re vulnerable to the big blind squeezing which would force us to forfeit the dead money. With no good squeeze candidate ourselves and plenty of better spots to invest chips, folding preserves stack EV.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Deuces (2c, 2h). Facing a raise to 300 from Elon and two callers (Claude and Minni), the pot is currently 1,050. I need to call 200 more to see the flop. This gives me direct pot odds of 5.25:1, which is very favorable. My main goal with pocket deuces is to 'set mine' (flop a set of three of a kind). The odds of flopping a set are approximately 7.5:1. Given the multi-way action and the stack depths of my opponents (Elon ~51bb, Claude ~57bb, Minni ~145bb), the implied odds are sufficient to justify the call. I will call the additional 200 to close the action and look to hit a set on the flop.

I hold pocket deuces on a board of Q-T-3. With three opponents in the pot, including the pre-flop raiser, it is highly likely that at least one of them has connected with this board (Top pair, middle pair, or straight draws). My underpair has very little equity and no draw. Being out of position, leading out would be a mistake. The standard play is to check and fold to any aggression.

Multiway pot (1250) on a QTs3 rainbow board after preflop raise with A9o (ace-high, no pair or strong draw). As preflop raiser in early position, a continuation bet maintains aggression, represents strength, and has good fold equity against calling ranges that likely missed (e.g., suited connectors, pocket pairs below Q). Board has some straight draw potential (J9, K9), but low coordination and my ace-high blocks top pairs. Opponents (Claude, Minni, Pro) have shown passive tendencies (check-calls in history). Half-pot bet (600) balances value/protection while keeping pot manageable with deep stacks and position disadvantage. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or check-raises; betting exploits initiative.

I have KJs on a 3c Qd Ts board - giving me an open-ended straight draw (any A or 9 gives me the nut straight). Elon bet 600 into 1250, pot is now 1850 and I need 600 to call. Getting ~3:1 pot odds with ~31% equity to hit my straight by the river. This is a profitable call with good implied odds if I hit. Still have Minni and Pro behind, so I'll just flat to keep the pot manageable and see if I can hit my draw cheaply.

I’m on the button with 8♠9♣ on a Q♦ T♠ 3♣ flop. Elon (preflop raiser) bets 600 and Claude calls. I have a J-gutshot (4 outs) plus three 8s and three 9s = ~10 outs to improve on the turn (~21% to hit). Calling 600 into a ~2450 pot requires ~19.7% equity, so my raw equity to improve slightly exceeds the price. I also have position and reasonable implied odds against shorter stacks, and I can fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss. Raising as a semi-bluff into two callers is too risky here, so I’ll call to realize equity and reevaluate on the turn.

I hold pocket Deuces (2c, 2h) on a flop of 3c-Qd-Ts. I checked, the original raiser bet 600, and two other players called. I have a weak underpair with only 2 outs to improve to a set, which is roughly 4% equity on the turn. Facing a bet and two calls, it is virtually certain that I am beaten by pairs of Queens, Tens, or even Threes. Getting 5:1 pot odds is not enough to justify chasing a 2-outer out of position against three opponents. I must fold.

Turn board brings the 6s, completing spade flush draws and adding straight draw possibilities (e.g., 97, 85). My Ad9s now has ace-high showdown value plus a strong spade nut flush draw (9 outs, ~19% equity to river alone). Pot is 3050 with two callers (Claude and Minni) who showed strength by calling flop c-bet multiway. However, their ranges likely include Qx, Tx, mid-pairs, and missed draws that fold to further aggression. History shows passive check-calls from them, but also some aggression. As preflop raiser acting first, checking risks giving free cards to villain draws or inducing check-raises from made hands. Betting maintains initiative, gets value from worse aces/draws/pairs, protects equity, and applies pressure deep-stacked. Half-pot bet (1500) balances thin value, fold equity, and pot control while leaving room for river maneuvers (stack ~4550 post-bet). Larger bet risks overcommitting without nuts; smaller invites floats. This exploits my equity and opponents' tendencies for stack growth.

I have KJo with an open-ended straight draw (any A or 9 gives me the nut straight). Elon bets 1500 into 3050, pot is now 4550. I need 1500 to call, getting about 3:1 odds. With 8 outs, I have ~17% to hit on the river - direct odds don't quite justify the call, but Elon only has ~1550 behind and Minni may come along too. My implied odds are decent if I hit the nuts. Folding a nut draw getting 3:1 seems too tight. Calling to see the river.

I have 8♠9♣ on 3♣ Q♦ T♠ 6♠. On the turn I have 14 raw outs (4 Js and 4 sevens complete straights; plus three other 8s and three other 9s to make a pair) ≈ 30% to hit the river. Pot is 6050 and it costs 1500 to call, so I need ~20% to break even — the raw odds favor a call. Calling preserves position, realizes equity, and keeps my options open on the river (I can fold second-pair type rivers to heavy action or extract value when I hit a straight). A raise here is too thin: it bloats the pot with a drawing hand, will either shove Elon (short) and create an awkward SPR or simply get called by Claude; folding gives up +EV equity. I’ll call 1500 and re-evaluate on the river — fold to large bets if I only pair, value/raise if I complete the straight or get a clear read and action.

River 9d pairs my 9s for third pair (9s) with Ad kicker on a coordinated board (3c-Qd-Ts-6s-9d) featuring possible straights (78x), top pairs (Qx, Tx), overpairs, and sets within villains' calling ranges. Both Claude and Minni called a large turn bet (1500 into ~4500 pot) multiway after preflop and flop action, indicating strong ranges: likely Qx, Tx, mid-to-high pocket pairs (88+), and formerly strong draws (spades) that missed the river flush completion. My Ad9s now holds showdown value against pure air or unpaired hands but is crushed by top pair+, straights (e.g., 78x for 6789Ts), and two pairs; equity likely <35% vs. their combined ranges. Pot is 7550 (75BB) with my stack at 3050 (30BB), making any bet (up to all-in) a thin value/pressure play with poor risk-reward if called by better. Checking first exploits opponents' passive check-call histories (Hands 1,6,14,15,18,19 show frequent river check-downs), induces bluffs from missed draws, controls pot size, and realizes equity cheaply at showdown with ace-high backdoor or pair outs. Folding bets post-check preserves stack for better spots in early tournament phase (Hand 20/150). Aggression earlier built the pot; now pot control maximizes EV long-term.

I made my straight! K-J with Q-T-9 on board gives me the nut straight (K-Q-J-T-9). Elon checked to me. I need to bet for value. Elon only has 3050 left, and Minni is behind with a big stack. I'll bet around 3500 to extract value from Qx, two pair hands, or lower straights like J8. This sizing puts Elon effectively all-in if he calls and may get Minni to come along.

I paired the river (9♣9♦) but it’s a very marginal pair with an 8 kicker on Q–T–3–6–9. Pot is 11,050 and Claude’s raise is 3,500, so I need ~24% equity to justify a call (3,500 / (11,050+3,500)). Claude’s line (call flop, call turn, river raise) looks value-heavy — slowplayed two‑pair/sets or top pair (Qx, TT, Q9, etc.) are much more likely than bluffs given the board and his earlier passive calls. My hand is behind most of those hands and only beats rare worse pairs or unlikely bluffs, so I estimate my equity vs his raising range is well below the break‑even threshold. A raise is nonsense (no fold equity and it bloats the pot with a weak kicker). I’ll fold and preserve ~28% of my stack I’d otherwise risk calling a marginal spot.

Facing Claude's river shove of 3500 (leaving 150) into 11050 pot after my check, with 3050 to call (all-in). Pot odds ~3.6:1 require ~22% equity. Pair of 9s + Ad kicker beats bluffs/smaller pairs but loses to Qx/Tx (top pair), 78 (nuts straight 6-7-8-9-10), two pairs/sets in Claude's strong calling range (multiway preflop call, flop/turn calls vs my aggression). Board coordinated with straight/straight draw potential; river 9d improves some but pairs low card. Claude's history shows passive calls but value-oriented aggression (e.g., Hand 10,14); unlikely pure bluff given pot size/tiny remainder. Equity likely <22% vs polarized value-heavy shove range. Early tournament (Hand 20/150, 30BB effective), preserve stack for premium spots over speculative hero-call risking elimination.

UTG with J3o, a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low high card, and no suited or connected potential. Standard tight play from early position requires folding trash like this to avoid difficult postflop spots with a short stack of ~30BB. No reads from prior hands suggest loosening up here, and preserving stack for better spots is key in tournament.
3h 8c is a very weak hand - unsuited, poorly connected, no high cards. In middle position with 4 players left to act behind me, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such poor equity.

Raise to 300. I’m in the cutoff with KTh (King-Ten of hearts), a strong suited broadway that plays well as a steal and postflop. Pot is 150 and it’s folded to me — calling 100 is passive and gives the button and blinds good odds to see a flop. A 3x open to 300 buys fold equity, charges speculative hands, and keeps the initiative while leaving plenty of stack behind (I still have deep stack post-raise). Size is standard: large enough to punish limpers/callers but not so large it invites unnecessary 3-bet pressure.

Plan: If called, play aggressively on favorable flops (c-bet often with backdoor hearts and top-pair/strong draws), and proceed cautiously on dry boards. If I face a large, polarized 3-bet from aggressive opponents (Flash/Pro), I will usually fold KTo; I may call smaller 3-bets to leverage position and postflop skill. Overall raising here maximizes EV in this spot.

Minni appears to be opening fairly wide from late position, and on the button I get maximum positional advantage and good immediate pot odds (300 to win 450). Q8o isn’t strong enough to 3-bet profitably here (poor playability in a 3-bet pot and can get 4-bet pressured), but it’s reasonable as a flat to realize equity in position. I’ll proceed with a call and play controlled postflop—avoid stacking off with dominated one-pair Qx, and be ready to fold if the blinds squeeze large.

I hold Ten-Four offsuit (T4o) in the Small Blind. The action facing me is a raise from the Cutoff (Minni) to 300 and a call from the Button (FiveTwo). Although the pot odds are roughly 3:1, my hand is extremely weak and unconnected. Furthermore, if I call, I will be playing the entire post-flop portion of the hand out of position against two opponents who have shown strength (raise and call). There is also the threat of the Big Blind squeezing or overcalling behind me. T4o is not a defensible hand in this spot; folding is the only standard play.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey suited. The Cutoff raised to 300 and the Button called. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 750, giving me nearly 4-to-1 odds. Ace-Trey suited is a strong hand that plays well in multi-way pots, especially when looking for nut flush draws or hitting an Ace. Closing the action with a call is the most efficient play here to see a flop for a small price.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-High (As3s). The flop is 9c-6h-2d, which is a fairly dry board. I have one overcard (the Ace) and a backdoor spade draw. The pot is 950 and I am currently first to act post-flop. Since there was a pre-flop raiser (Minni) and a caller (FiveTwo), I will check to the aggressor to see how the action develops. This is a standard defensive play with Ace-high on a board that doesn't significantly help my hand.

I opened to 300 with K♥T♥ and the flop 9♣6♥2♦ is very dry. I have two overcards and a backdoor heart — decent equity against typical calling ranges (small pairs, broadways, suited connectors) and good fold equity as the preflop aggressor. Both opponents checked, so a continuation bet should take down many unmade hands and charge any backdoor draws. I choose a ~60–65% pot c-bet to apply pressure but leave plenty of stack behind: 600 into a 950 pot. If I face a large raise I will fold KTo; if I get called I'll continue on favourable turns (K/T/heart) and exercise pot control on blanks.

Multiway flop (CO c-bet into BB + BTN) on 9-6-2 rainbow, Q8o has very little equity: no pair, no flush draw, and only thin backdoor straight possibilities. While the sizing (600 into 1550) gives decent immediate odds, our realization is poor because many turns don’t improve us, Minni can barrel frequently, and the BB can continue/raise, squeezing our positional advantage. With minimal backdoors and low bluffing prospects in a multiway pot, the best EV is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-High (As3s) on a 9c-6h-2d board. I checked initially, and Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) bet 600 into a pot of 950. FiveTwo folded. I have one overcard (the Ace) and a backdoor spade draw. While Ace-high has some showdown value, a 600-chip bet into 950 is quite large (nearly 2/3 pot) on such a dry board, suggesting strength or a high-equity semi-bluff. Calling 600 to win 1550 doesn't offer great direct odds given my weak equity. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.
97 offsuit from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak hand with poor playability and I'm first to act with 5 players behind me. No reason to open this from early position.

Q♦T♦s is a strong, playable mid‑position opening hand: suited, connected and good postflop playability. I have ~130bb so I should take the initiative and thin the field. A standard 3x open to 300 gives fold equity, discourages limp‑calling and keeps the pot controllable multiway. Players behind include FiveTwo (CO) and Pro (BTN) who can defend or squeeze, Flash (SB) who will defend some, and Elon (BB) who is short (~29bb) and may shove — QTs has reasonable equity vs a shove, and I can call if necessary. Versus a large 3‑bet out of position I’ll proceed cautiously and often fold. Open-raise to 300.

Minni is opening to 3bb from middle position and we’re in the cutoff with K3o. Even if Minni’s range is wide, K3 offsuit is heavily dominated by their Kx/Qx/Ax continues, plays poorly postflop, and doesn’t make a profitable flat given the players behind (BTN and blinds can squeeze). A 3-bet bluff here is too loose and unnecessary with deep enough stacks. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with Qc 9d. The player in Middle Position (Minni) has raised to 300 (3bb). Minni has been active recently. Q9 offsuit is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by the opening range of a competent player from middle position (hands like KQ, AQ, QJ, A9, T9s). Although I have position, calling a 3x raise with Q9o is generally -EV due to reverse implied odds and lack of playability. 3-betting as a bluff is risky given the opponent's activity level. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 6-max game. Minni opened from middle position. While calling is an option, playing out of position for the rest of the hand is difficult. A 3-bet allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or thin the field. Additionally, Elon in the Big Blind is very short-stacked (29.5 BBs), and a raise puts maximum pressure on him. I will raise to 1100 to discourage a call and define ranges.

Short stack of ~30BB in BB facing a SB 3-bet to 1100 (11BB) over an MP open to 300. J9o is a marginal speculative hand at best, offsuit with mediocre high card strength and straight potential, but terrible out of position. Pot odds ~1.5:1 direct (1500 pot, 1000 to call), needing ~40% equity heads-up, which J9o lacks vs a polarized 3-bet range (AA-JJ, AK, some bluffs). Multiway risk with Minni still to act worsens it. No strong blockers or fold equity for a 3-bet squeeze (min to 1900 commits ~60% stack). Preserve stack for better spots as shortest stack in tournament.

I opened to 300 with Q♦T♦ from MP and Flash (SB) has 3‑bet to 1,100 (I need 800 more). QTs has good post‑flop playability (flush/straight/broadway potential) and I’ll have position on later streets against the SB. The 3‑bet sizing is not huge relative to stacks, so I prefer to keep the pot controllable and realize equity rather than folding or polarizing with a 4‑bet. Plan: call to see a flop, continue with top pairs and strong draws, and fold to extreme pressure when completely missed. Total commit to the pot (including my prior 300) = 1,100.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the initiative and the range advantage on this board. A Jack-high board like J-5-2 is generally favorable for my perceived range, which includes hands like AA, KK, QQ, and AJ. Although my specific hand (King-Queen) missed the flop, a small continuation bet (C-bet) of approximately one-third of the pot puts pressure on my opponent's middle pairs (like 66-TT) and high-card hands that missed (like AQ or AK). This size allows me to take down the pot immediately or define Minni's range without overcommitting if she has a strong Jack or a draw.

Flop is 5c 2d Jd and I hold Q♦T♦. I have a very strong drawing hand: two overcards plus a four‑to‑a‑diamond flush (9 clean diamond outs) and additional backdoor/broadway outs. Flash 3‑bet preflop and now makes a small c‑bet (800 into a 3,100 pot ≈25%), so his range is fairly wide and this is a good spot to realize equity. Pot odds on the call are ~800/(3100+800)=~20.5%, while my flush draw alone is ~35% to hit by the river (and total improvement odds are higher when you include Q/T pair outs, though those can be dirty). I also have position postflop, so calling keeps the pot controllable, lets me see a free turn card, and preserves fold equity if I hit. Raising as a semi‑bluff is possible but would bloat the pot without a made hand and likely get called/raised by many of his defended hands. Plan: call the 800, re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to heavy pressure if I miss; press when I complete or make a strong pair). Total commit = 1,100 preflop + 800 call = 1,900.

The 2c on the turn is a relatively neutral card that doesn't change the board texture significantly, but it does mean that any pair Minni holds has now become two pair (with the board). Since Minni called a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, her range is likely centered around Jx hands (AJ, KJ, QJ), mid-pocket pairs (TT, 99, 88), or diamond draws. With just King-high, a second barrel is unlikely to fold out a Jack or a medium pair. By checking, I am looking to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card. If she checks back, I still have some showdown value against her draws and can potentially hit a King or Queen on the river to take the lead.

I check. Board is 5♣ 2♦ J♦ 2♣ and I hold Q♦ T♦. I missed a pair but have a four‑to‑a‑diamond flush (9 clean outs) so my chance to complete on the river is ~9/46 ≈ 19.6%. Villain (Flash) 3‑bet pre, made a very small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn — that line reads as a wide, often medium/weak range or pot control rather than a strong locked hand. With only ~20% to improve and both stacks healthy (pot = 3,900; I’m not priced in to call any turn raise), the +EV play is to take the free card and keep the pot controlled rather than bloating it with a semi‑bluff now. If I hit the flush on the river I can extract value; if he bets the river and I miss I can fold without having risked more on the turn. If he checks through the river I can barrel for value/bluff as appropriate. So I check and re‑evaluate on the river.

On the river, the board is J-5-2-2-T. After my continuation bet was called on the flop and both players checked the turn, the pot remains sized at 3900. My King-high has very little showdown value, but it does beat missed diamond draws like 9d8d or 8d7d. Betting here as a bluff is risky because Minni's range likely contains several Jack-x hands or middle pocket pairs (66-99) that might not fold to a single river bet after the turn went check-check. By checking, I'm opting for a cheap showdown, hoping my King-high might occasionally be the best hand against a busted draw or that I can minimize my losses if she has a made hand.

Check. On the river I have Q♦T♦ on 5♣ 2♦ J♦ 2♣ T♥: that gives me a pair of tens with a queen kicker (second pair). Villain (Flash) 3‑bet preflop, made a tiny flop c‑bet and then checked both turn and river — a line consistent with a wide/mid strength pot‑control range (missed draws, medium pairs, some Jx, some bluffs). Hands that clearly beat me are Jx, any 2x (trips/fuller combos), or two‑pair combos (JT); hands I beat are pure bluffs, busted draws and worse Tx kickers. A river bet would only get value from rare worse T‑hands and will often be called (or raised) by better hands, so betting is thin and risky. Checking to showdown realizes equity without bloating the pot or turning marginal spots into big losses. With pot = 3,900 and my stack healthy, the lowest‑variance +EV line is to check and show down.

UTG in a 6-max spot with K♦8♠ — an offsuit king with a weak kicker and poor postflop playability out of position. The table shows several aggressive openers, so I'm likely to face a raise or 3-bet; K8o is often dominated by better Ks and is hard to play OOP deep-stacked. The pot is only 150 and calling 100 or min-raising to 200 to try and steal is unnecessarily risky given this hand. Preserve the stack and wait for a better opening hand — fold.

Unopened pot after UTG folds and we’re in MP with AJo offsuit (strong enough to open for value and as a blocker-heavy hand). With playable stacks behind (and a short SB who may reshove), I want to take initiative, build the pot with the stronger part of my range, and deny equity to the blinds. A 3x open is consistent with table sizing and pressures the blinds while keeping my range uncapped.

I hold [8c Td] (Ten-Eight offsuit) in the Cutoff. An opponent in Middle Position (FiveTwo) has opened the pot with a raise to 300. T8o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by a standard opening range from middle position (which includes better Tens, pairs, and stronger connectors). Calling would be loose and unprofitable, especially with the Button and Blinds still to act behind me who could squeeze. There is no strategic advantage to playing this hand in this spot.

I am holding Ace-Trey offsuit on the button. While I have the benefit of position, a middle position raise to 3x from FiveTwo usually indicates a range that dominates my weak Ace. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a weak kicker, and 3-betting as a bluff is risky against an active player like FiveTwo. Given the stack sizes and the stage of the tournament, I will opt for a disciplined fold and wait for a stronger hand to commit chips.

98dd is a strong suited connector with excellent playability, straight and flush potential, and good equity against an MP raiser's calling range. FiveTwo min-raised from MP after UTG fold, suggesting a wide range. BTN and CO folded, isolating the pot. In SB, standard is to 3-bet or fold speculative hands OOP rather than flat calling to avoid difficult post-flop spots. 3-betting to 900 (3x the raise) offers high fold equity against FiveTwo's likely continuation range and BB squeeze potential, while keeping pot manageable with my 29bb stack. If called, we hit flops well and can continue with initiative. This applies pressure and builds stack early.

Q2o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. Elon is short-stacked (2050) which means he's likely committed with his 3-bet and has a strong range. Calling 800 out of position with Q2o has poor implied odds and we'll be dominated by most of his value range. Easy fold.

Elon’s small 3-bet from the SB with only ~2k total looks like a value/commit setup more than a 3-bet/fold. If I 4-bet jam to 2050, he’s getting a great price to call (needs ~28%) and will continue with essentially his entire 3-bet range, so I’d be taking AJo into a mostly strong range (AQ+/TT+) with poor fold equity.

Calling 600 is a much better deal: I’m in position, the pot will be ~1900 and his remaining stack will be ~1150 (very low SPR), so I can play it as a near-preflop all-in decision—continue comfortably when I connect (top pair+ / strong draws) and fold when I whiff and he shoves. The immediate pot odds (600 to win ~1900) plus position make the flat-call the highest-EV option.

Post-flop on 5h6s6d board, heads-up vs FiveTwo who called my pre-flop 3-bet. My 9d8d gives a strong nut flush draw (10 outs) plus gutshot straight draw to 7 (4 outs, one overlapping), totaling ~13 outs or 47% equity vs a typical calling range. The paired board hits my 3-bet range (overpairs, Ax6x, sets) and disguises my draw. Pot is 1900, my stack 2050 (~20BB effective). Shoving all-in applies maximum pressure OOP, denies equity to his floats/Ax/weak pairs, gets value from worse made hands like 66/56/TT+, and fold equity is high against his MP calling range post-3bet (likely QQ+, AK, suited connectors that missed). Checking risks giving free cards to draws or letting him realize equity cheaply; betting smaller leaves me committed with shallow stack OOP. Standard semi-bluff jam builds stack aggressively in tournament.

We’re facing Elon’s all-in continuation bet for 2050 into ~3950. Calling 2050 to win ~6000 requires ~34% equity.

On 6-6-5 rainbow(ish), our AdJc is just ace-high with no pair and only very thin backdoors (backdoor diamonds). Versus a typical SB 3-bet range that takes this low-SPR jam line (TT+ and big Ax like AQ/AK, with some AJs/KQs at best), our equity is usually in the ~15–25% band: we’re crushed by overpairs and dominated by AQ/AK on this board, and we don’t have enough outs.

Given the poor equity relative to pot odds and no leverage left (villain is already all-in), the highest-EV play is to preserve stack and fold.

UTG in 6-max with 56o is too weak to open. It makes low/fragile pairs and non-nut straights, and being out of position versus multiple aggressive players (Elon/Flash/Claude) leads to tough reverse-implied-odds spots. With ~78bb effective, there’s no need to speculate from the worst seat—just preserve stack and wait for better UTG holdings.

I am in Middle Position (Hijack) with King-Four suited (K4s). This hand is generally too weak to open profitably from this position in a 6-handed game, as it falls outside standard opening ranges (typically K9s+ or K8s+). Furthermore, aggressive players like Elon (Button) and Flash (Cutoff) remain behind me, increasing the likelihood of facing a 3-bet which would force a fold. With no previous action to attack and deep stacks, preserving chips by folding is the prudent play.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Nine offsuit. While this is a semi-coordinated hand, it is generally considered too weak to open from this position in a 6-max game, especially with an aggressive short-stack like Elon on the button and active players in the blinds. T9o does not play well against a 3-bet, and my goal is to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

AdJd is a premium hand from the button in an unopened pot. Excellent position, high equity against calling ranges, suited for flush potential and straight draws. Standard to raise for value and to thin the field. Raising to 3x the BB (300) is a balanced size used frequently in prior hands, builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against blinds. My 40BB stack supports postflop play. No specific villain reads indicate changing this.

K5o in the small blind facing a button raise. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Elon has been active with raises. K5o doesn't have good playability postflop - it's not suited, has poor connectivity, and will often be dominated when I hit. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I’m in the big blind with J♠4♦ facing a button open to 300. Folding is too tight here given the cheap price to defend — I only need to call an extra 200 into a 450 pot (pot after my call will be 650), so I need roughly 30.8% equity. Elon has been opening frequently and widely from the button, so a flat call is justified to realize equity and defend the blind. I’ll call and play the flop cautiously out of position: check and fold to heavy aggression unless I pair my jack or pick up strong draw/board texture that justifies continuing. If Elon 3-bets big preflop I’ll fold — J4o is not a hand I want to play raised or as a 3-bet bluff OOP.

I defended the blind with J♠4♦ preflop because Elon opens wide from the button, but on the 3♣-2♣-7♦ flop I have no pair, no club, and no real draw. I’m out of position and the pot is modest (650) — best line is to control the pot and see a free turn. I’ll check and plan to fold to significant pressure unless I pair my J or pick up a strong draw (or Elon blatantly overbets very small). Checking preserves stack and realizes showdown equity without bloating the pot with a very weak hand.

Flop is 3c 2c 7d, a low coordinated board with two clubs presenting flush draw and wheel straight draw possibilities. Minni checked from BB after calling my button raise pre-flop, which suggests weakness or trapping with a hand like a low pair, pocket pair below 7, or draws. My AdJd has ace-high with two live overcards (A and J), backdoor diamond flush draw, and backdoor straight potential. No made hand but huge semi-bluff equity: 6 outs to top pair or better (12% turn), plus fold equity against Minni's wide calling range. Position on button allows control. Standard continuation bet to take it down now, deny equity to her draws/improve hands, and build pot if called. Sizing 2/3 pot (approx 400 into 650) balances value from overpairs she might call with and protection. History shows Minni calls stations but folds to aggression when behind (e.g., Hand 20). Stack depths support (36BB effective). Checking risks giving free cards to draws that beat ace-high.

I defended the blind preflop against Elon's wide button open, but on 3♣-2♣-7♦ with J♠4♦ I have no pair, no club, and essentially no shooter draws — just a very weak backdoor possibility. Elon bets 400 into the 650 pot (pot 1050), so calling 400 would require ~27.6% equity (400 / 1450). Out of position against a frequent, aggressive opener my hand has far less than that equity and little post‑flop playability. Raising as a bluff is too risky with no fold equity against his likely calling/shoving range, and calling invites larger barrels or a shove from a shorter-stacked opponent. Better to conserve my deep stack for profitable spots. I fold.

I am in UTG position with 7d Th, which is a very weak hand (7-10 offsuit). In a 6-max game, the UTG opening range should be tight, typically comprising the top 15-20% of hands. 7To is far outside this range. With 5 players left to act behind me, the likelihood of someone having a better hand is high, and playing this hand out of position post-flop is difficult and unprofitable. Therefore, the only correct strategic move is to fold immediately.

I am holding Ace-Trey offsuit (A3o) in Middle Position. In a 6-max game, A3o is generally too weak to open from this position, especially with active players like Elon and Claude behind me. Raising here often leads to difficult situations post-flop with a weak kicker or facing a 3-bet from the late positions. Folding is the standard disciplined play to maintain my stack for better opportunities.

Cutoff position with folds ahead, no raises or limps. K9o is within a standard opening range from late position due to king high, playability postflop, and position advantage. Players have been aggressive but no action yet. Standard open to 3x the big blind to build pot and take initiative. Limping is suboptimal; folding too tight for stack preservation over 150 hands.

Holding 9s2d on the button facing Elon's raise to 300. This is a very weak hand - 92 offsuit has poor equity and playability. Even with position, this hand is too weak to defend against a raise. Easy fold.

Fold. J♥2♥ is a weak, mostly dominated hand and I’m out of position in the small blind facing a 3BB cutoff open. Calling 250 into a 450 pot would make the pot 700, so I’d need ~35.7% equity (250/700) just to break even against the raiser — far more than J2s typically has versus a cutoff opening range. The big blind (7750) is likely to defend, creating a multiway pot OOP where this hand plays very poorly. A min-squeeze to 500 is risky (invites a reraise or a call and still leaves me OOP). With a healthy 14.7k stack I’ll preserve chips and fold to wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3x CO open from Elon with T2s in the BB, calling needs decent equity and playability, but this hand is heavily dominated by his Tx and broadways and performs poorly out of position (reverse implied odds when we flop a ten). Against a likely tighter-than-average opening range (and with no compelling squeeze dynamics), this is a low-EV defend. I’ll preserve chips for better BB defense candidates and better 3-bet bluffs.

I am holding K2o in UTG position. This is a very weak starting hand and does not play well out of position or in multi-way pots. Given the table dynamics and my stack size, folding is the standard and most prudent play.

Flash has folded UTG, so it's folded to me in middle position with 9sTs suited, a strong speculative hand with good playability, straight and flush potential. Standard to open raise here to steal the blinds or build a pot in position against the blinds. My stack of 4450 is decent, and opponents behind have larger stacks but this is a profitable spot. Raising to 3x the big blind (300 total) is standard sizing for early position opens in deepstack play.
Q4o is a weak hand in the cutoff facing a middle position raise. Not worth calling or 3-betting - easy fold.

I’m on the button with J♦3♦ — a weak but suited hand. Elon opened to 300 from middle; I have deep stack (14,700) and great position so I can realize equity postflop. A raise here (to 500) would be a thin isolation and risks a fold from better hands or a shove/call from opponents; J3s is not strong enough to 3-bet as a bluff against a sane opener. Calling 300 keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a flop in position, and fold cheaply if I miss or proceed aggressively if I hit. I’ll call.

We’re in the small blind, out of position, facing an open to 300 and a flat from the button. 8♠3♥ has very poor playability and realization multiway: we’ll frequently flop dominated pairs/weak draws and have to navigate OOP vs two ranges that contain many better 8x/3x and broadways. Even getting a decent price (250 more) isn’t enough when our implied odds are bad and reverse implied odds are high. No squeeze candidate either. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 8d, facing a raise to 300 from Elon in middle position and a call from Minni on the button. The pot is 750, and I need to call 200 to see the flop, offering me pot odds of roughly 21%. However, 84o is one of the worst hands in poker with very poor playability and equity, especially out of position against two ranges that likely dominate me. Even with the attractive price, the reverse implied odds and difficulty of realizing equity make this a clear fold.

Flop is 6c-5s-7h, giving me the nut spade flush draw (9 outs) plus a gutshot straight draw to the 8 (4 outs, though some overlap), plus two overcards for gutshots/pairs, totaling a very strong semi-bluff hand with ~30% equity against most calling ranges and huge potential. I raised pre-flop and Minni called from the button with a wide range (suited connectors, broadway, Ax, etc.), many of which I dominate or have live draws against. Board is draw-heavy (straight/flush possibilities), so betting builds the pot when ahead, charges opponent's draws, and denies equity. Checking risks giving free cards to worse hands or letting Minni bluff. Half-pot bet of 400 sizes well: cheap for calls, protects my hand, and commits ~10% of my stack while leaving fold equity. Pot odds favor aggressive play to maximize EV in this deep-stack spot.

Flop 6c-5s-7h, I hold J♦3♦. Elon (preflop opener) c-bets small (400 into ~1150). I have a 4-card gutshot to the 4 (4 outs) and backdoor diamond possibilities — combined chance to improve by river is roughly in the high-teens/low-20s percent. Pot odds to call 400 to win ~1550 require ~25.8% equity, so pure draw equity is a bit short. However I'm on the button (in position) and facing a single, small c-bet; calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves fold/raise options on the turn, and allows me to realize equity or seize initiative if a favorable card appears. A raise as a bluff is too risky with J3s (vulnerable to a shove or calls by better hands), and folding is overly tight given my positional advantage and reasonable implied odds. Plan: call the 400, see the turn, proceed only with a good card (4, a diamond, or a Jack) or leverage any opponent weakness.

On the turn Kc, the board is 6c-5s-7h-Kc. I still have the nut spade flush draw (9 outs), a gutshot straight draw to the 8 (4 outs, and 8s makes straight flush), and the ten for overcard potential. Equity remains strong against Minni's calling range (~35-40% equity likely). The king brings a possible top pair for her or club flush draw, but my range as preflop raiser and flop barrel includes strong hands like sets, two pairs, and overpairs that beat many of her calls. Minni flat-called flop from button, which could be draws, floats, weak pairs, or made hands like 88-JJ, 98, 45, A-high draws. Betting continues aggression OOP, denies equity to her draws (e.g., 4x, 8x, clubs), builds the pot when ahead, and has fold equity against floats/marginal hands. Checking risks giving a free river card where she improves or bets me off the hand. Deep stacks (my 3750 vs her 14000, pot 1550), so ~2/3 pot bet of 1000 commits ~27% of stack but leaves maneuverability, pressures her range effectively. This maximizes EV in a spot where semi-bluffing is +EV.

Fold. I called the flop with J♦3♦ to see a cheap turn, but the K♣ on the turn removes my backdoor diamond plan and doesn’t help my hand. After the turn I have only ~6 raw outs (four 4s for the straight + two Jacks) ≈ 13% to improve on the river. The pot is 2,550 and I must call 1,000, so the break-even equity is ~1,000/(2,550+1,000) ≈ 28%. My ~13% is far below that, so a call is -EV. A raise as a bluff is also unattractive: Elon has shown strength (turn raise) and a short stack (2,750) makes him likely to shove or call, leaving me with poor fold equity and bad showdown equity if called. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots in position.

2s4d is one of the worst possible starting hands, offsuit low cards with no connectivity or playability. UTG in a 6-handed game, first to act preflop with deep stacks (~53BB). Standard tight play: fold immediately to avoid playing a trash hand out of position. Preserve stack for premium opportunities in this long tournament.
QTo in middle position is a playable hand. With Elon folding UTG, I have a decent opportunity to open raise. This hand plays well and I can take initiative. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB).

Fold. 3♦2♣ is a very weak, offsuit hand with almost no playability vs an open from middle position. Claude's raise to 250 is a normal opening size; calling 250 into a 400 pot would require roughly 250/(400+250) ≈ 38% equity to break even on the call — far more than 3-2o has vs a raiser’s range. If I call I'll often be out of position and face action from the button and blinds (possible 3-bets or multiway pots) where this hand performs poorly. With a 14,000 stack (140BB) early in the tournament, there's no need to gamble with junk — fold and wait for a better spot.

Button vs a 2.5x MP open, A8s is strong enough to apply pressure in position. The suited ace plays well postflop with nut-flush potential and good blocker effects (reduces AA/AK/AQ in Claude’s range), making it a profitable 3-bet that can win preflop often and gives us initiative when called. I’ll size to a standard in-position 3-bet that discourages blinds from coming along and sets up clean c-bet/jam lines if needed.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen-Seven suited. The action involves an initial raise from middle position by Claude and a 3-bet from the Button by FiveTwo. Facing a raise and a re-raise while out of position is a very strong signal to fold, as my hand is significantly dominated by the ranges of both opponents. The pot odds are poor and the risk is high given the remaining action behind me (the Big Blind and the original raiser). Folding is the only standard play.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. There has been an open raise from Claude and a 3-bet from FiveTwo. Given the strength of the action and the poor quality of my hand, there is no reason to commit any more chips to this pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Facing a 3-bet from the button with QTo out of position. This is a marginal spot - I have pot odds of about 2:1, but QTo doesn't play well OOP against a 3-bet range. FiveTwo will have position on me for all streets. While the 3-bet sizing is small, continuing with QTo here leads to difficult postflop decisions. This is a fold - better to wait for stronger spots.
AKo is a premium hand in UTG. Standard open raise to 2.5x BB. This hand plays well against 3-bets and has good equity against calling ranges.

Claude opened UTG to 250 in a 6‑max game, which represents a relatively strong early‑position range. 5♦6♦ is a small suited connector that plays poorly out of position against an UTG open — low showdown value, lots of overcards, and significant reverse implied odds. Calling 250 (more than half the current 400 pot) would be too expensive for a speculative holding and invites multiway action or a squeeze from players behind. 3‑betting as a bluff is too thin here versus an UTG opener. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude is opening UTG, so his range is relatively strong. 8c3c is a very weak suited hand with poor connectivity and dominated flush potential; even with position, it won’t realize equity well and can get squeezed by the Button/blinds. A bluff 3-bet from CO versus UTG is also too ambitious with this blocker-less holding. Best play is to conserve stack and fold.

I hold Pocket Queens (Qs Qc) on the Button, a premium hand. Claude raised to 250 (2.5bb) from UTG. To extract maximum value and avoid a multi-way pot which lowers the equity of my overpair, I should 3-bet. A call would allow the blinds to enter cheaply. I will 3-bet to 800, which is roughly 3.2x the opening raise, a standard size when in position to isolate the original raiser and build the pot with a strong hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. Claude (UTG) raised to 250 and Pro (Button) 3-bet to 800. T6o is a very weak hand and does not play well against the likely strong ranges of an UTG opener and a button 3-better. I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the only sensible move here.

Qh8h is a speculative suited connector with some playability for straights and flush draws, but facing an UTG raise from Claude (likely strong range) and a button 3-bet from Pro (could be a squeeze or polarized strong), this hand lacks the equity and blocking value to justify continuing OOP. Pot odds are 700 to call into 1200 (about 37% pot), but postflop OOP against two aggressive players with position on me (Pro has button), the implied odds aren't great given my 5200 stack. Folding preserves chips for premium spots in this multi-player tournament to maximize stack over 150 hands.

AKo is a premium hand that plays very well against 3-bet ranges. Pro's 3-bet to 800 from the button could be a wide stealing range. I should 4-bet for value here - calling would leave me out of position with a hand that plays better with initiative. A standard 4-bet sizing is around 2.2-2.5x, so around 1800-2000.

I hold Pocket Queens on the Button. After 3-betting to 800, the UTG player (Claude) has 4-bet to 2000. This action typically indicates a very strong range (JJ+, AK). While QQ is a premium hand, 5-bet shoving here likely only gets called by hands that are flipping (AK) or crushing me (KK, AA), making it a high-variance move that isolates against the top of the opponent's range. I have position and the pot odds to call are favorable (calling 1200 to win a total pot of roughly 4150 requires ~29% equity; against a tight range of KK+, AK, I have ~43% equity). Calling allows me to evaluate the flop in position and navigate post-flop with a low stack-to-pot ratio.

I 4-bet with AKo and got called by Pro. The flop is 5h4c4h - completely missed my hand. Pro's calling range for a 4-bet is likely strong pairs (JJ+) or AK. Against this range, my AK high is behind all their pairs and they won't fold. The pot is 4150 and we both have ~8800 behind (SPR ~2:1). C-betting here accomplishes little - I won't fold out better hands. Checking allows pot control and I can catch an A or K on the turn to improve, or take a free showdown path.

In this 4-bet pot, the SPR is low (~2.1). The board [5h 4c 4h] misses the typical high-card 4-betting range (AK) but connects with overpairs (AA, KK). My opponent, the pre-flop 4-bettor, has checked to me. If I bet, I likely fold out hands I am crushing (AK) and isolate myself against hands that beat me (AA, KK) which might look to check-raise all-in. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, keep his bluffing range (AK) in play for the turn, and avoid facing a difficult decision against a check-raise. I have strong showdown value and position, so realizing my equity passively on the flop is the prudent play.

Turn 9s doesn't change much. Pro checked behind on the flop which is interesting - with a strong overpair like QQ+, they'd often bet for value/protection. Their check suggests either a medium pair pot-controlling (JJ, TT) or possibly AK like me. Either way, I'm likely behind any pocket pair. Betting accomplishes nothing - I won't fold out better and might get raised. Continue pot control and look to hit an A or K on the river or get to a cheap showdown.

In a 4-bet pot, ranges are extremely narrow (typically JJ+, AK). My opponent (UTG) has checked the flop and now the turn on a board of 5-4-4-9. This line is consistent with two main hand classes: AK giving up/pot-controlling, or AA/KK trapping/exercising caution. If I bet, I likely fold out the hands I am beating (AK) and only get action from hands that crush me (AA, KK). There is very little value to extract from worse hands. By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid facing a difficult check-raise, and keep his bluffs or AK-high in his range for the river. I have strong showdown value with Queens and position, making checking the optimal low-variance play to realize my equity.

River Ace is great - I now have top two pair (Aces and Fours). Pro's line of calling 4-bet then checking flop and turn suggests medium pairs (JJ, TT) pot-controlling or possibly AK/AQ. With the Ace hitting, I can now value bet. If Pro has JJ/TT they might hero call, if AQ they'll pay off, if AK we chop. Betting around 1/2 pot for thin value.

Pre-flop was a 4-bet pot (UTG vs Button), defining extremely strong ranges (JJ+, AK). The flop and turn were checked through, which is consistent with the opponent holding AK (missing) or AA/KK (pot controlling/trapping). The River brings an Ace, which is arguably the worst card for my Pocket Queens. This card connects perfectly with the AK portion of the opponent's range that checked the flop and turn. The opponent leads for half-pot (2000), a line heavily indicative of value (AK or AA). It is highly unlikely the opponent is value betting worse (KK, JJ) on this scare card, and bluffs are scarce in a UTG 4-bet range. I am beating almost nothing that bets here. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

5♦8♣ UTG is a very weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and I'm first to act against five players behind. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see a flop — limping or raising here just creates multiway pots where my hand has very little equity and I’ll be out of position post‑flop. With a healthy 14000 stack I should preserve chips and wait for a much stronger, positionally favorable spot. Fold.

We’re first in after UTG folds, facing only the BB. 8♥3♥ is too weak to open from middle position: it makes dominated pairs and weak top pairs, and its straight/flush potential doesn’t compensate without late-position leverage. Limping also invites squeezes and puts us in tough multiway spots. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better open/steal candidates from later positions.

I hold 7s 8d in the Cutoff position. The action has folded to me. My hand is an unsuited connector with low equity and playability. Although I am in late position, 78o is generally too weak to open-raise profitably, as it plays poorly against calling ranges and cannot withstand a 3-bet. With aggressive players still to act in the blinds and on the button, the most prudent and standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with 2s 5s. While it is a suited hand, it is extremely weak. Both players in the blinds (Elon and Claude) have shown significant aggression in recent hands.
