Claude, in particular, has been very active and just won a large pot. Attempting a blind steal with the bottom of my range into two aggressive opponents is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop in the small blind with A8o after all players folded to me. This is a standard spot to raise for a steal or to play a strong ace-high hand postflop. A8 has good equity against the big blind's wide defending range (top 40-50%), ace blocker reduces likelihood of AA or AK, and position advantage. My stack is 51.5BB, shortest at table, so applying pressure to deeper BB. Raising to 3BB total (300) is a balanced size to get folds or build pot profitably.

Facing a min-raise from Elon in SB with J4o in BB. Getting 2:1 pot odds (call 200 into 400). J4o is weak and plays poorly out of position, but the pot odds are decent. Elon has been aggressive preflop throughout. I'll call and see a flop - can fold to aggression if I miss.

Flop is Js Td Ts, a paired board with a spade flush draw possibility. Facing Claude's BB calling range vs my SB steal raise (A8o has ~45-55% equity preflop vs typical wide defend), this board heavily favors his range: he has Tx (top pair/top kicker or better/trips), JT (two pair), suited connectors/spades for draws frequently. My Ad8s is ace-high with no pair/draw, vulnerable OOP first to act. C-betting thin for value/protection risks check-raises from strong hands and free cards are cheap. Pot control and realizing equity by checking back for showdown or check-calling/raising light is better; induces bluffs from missed draws. Stack depths allow fold equity later if needed. Standard check OOP with marginal ace-high on villain-heavy board.

I flopped top pair (Jacks) with the board showing Js-Td-Ts. Elon checked to me. I have two pair (Jacks and Tens) but weak kicker. This is a decent spot to bet for value - I beat all missed hands and some weaker Jx. Elon checking suggests he may have whiffed. I'll bet around half pot to get value from worse hands while keeping the pot manageable with my mediocre kicker.

Facing a small 300 bet into a 900 pot after my check on Js Td Ts flop with Ad8s (ace-high, backdoor spade flush draw with 8s, no strong made hand or draw). Claude's BB defending range vs my SB steal has strong advantage here: frequent Tx for trips/top pair, JT two-pair, sets, strong spades/Jd for equity. However, small bet size (1/3 pot) screams thin value from medium strength or probe/induce, also catches bluffs/draws from his wide range (spade draws like KsQs, Q9s, straight draws like Q9/KQ). Pot odds are excellent (~4:1 direct to win 1200 pot post-call), and my 49BB stack can afford the 300 commitment (6% stack) with implied odds if I hit A/spade/backdoors. Folding ace-high OOP wastes equity against his polarized betting range where I beat bluffs/missed draws at showdown. Raising has fold equity but risks villain's strong hands check-raising pre-turn or continuing lighter; better to pot control by calling, see turn for free cards or cheap realization. Table dynamics: Claude aggressive (e.g., Hand 14 bet/called raise on draw-heavy board, Hand 28 river bluff), so he may overbet value or bluff weak. Call keeps pot small OOP, realizes equity profitably.

Turn card Ac pairs the board's top card and perfectly hits my Ad8s, giving me top pair aces with an 8 kicker on a Js Td Ts Ac board (paired tens, spade flush draw). Previously ace-high on a villain-favored flop where I checked and called Claude's small 300 bet into ~900 pot, which pot-controls while realizing equity OOP against his BB defending range (Tx trips/TP, JT 2P, strong draws, Ax, bluffs). The Ace is a great card for my SB stealing range (frequent Ax like A8s), less so for his BB range which has fewer Ax (defends wide but not super strong Ax always). Claude's small flop bet likely thin value (Tx, Jx, strong draws) or probe; he can have worse Ax, missed draws turning into bluffs, or weaker pairs. Betting for value now extracts from his worse Ax (A-rag without 8+ kicker), inferior pairs/draws, while protecting against spade rivers. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or check-raise from monsters (sets/trips). Pot 1200, my 4600 stack (~46BB), his 12350 deep. Bet 800 (~2/3 pot) balances value/protection, gets calls from worse (AJ-A9o/s, Tx below trips), folds worse, sets up river. Table dynamics: Claude aggressive (Hand 14 turn bet/called raise on dryish board, Hand 28 river bluff vs Pro), so likely calls light or raises monsters. This builds pot profitably with TP good enough ~70-80% time vs his range.

I have Jacks and Tens with a weak kicker. The Ace on the turn is concerning - Elon check-called flop then leads out 800 when the Ace hits. This could indicate he has an Ace (now beating me with AA-TT two pair) or was slow-playing a Ten. However, it could also be a bluff or semi-bluff with spades. Pot odds are 2.5:1 (800 to win 2000). My hand is still decent but vulnerable. Folding two pair seems too weak, raising makes no sense. I'll call and evaluate the river.

River 2d is a complete blank that doesn't complete any obvious draws (spades were the main one, straight draws like Q9 improved minimally if at all, no flush). Board: Js Td Ts Ac 2d remains paired tens with top pair aces possible. My Ad8s gives top pair aces with 8 kicker, ahead of weaker aces (A9o-A2o without T/J), unpaired jacks, naked tens (but tens are trips actually—holding any T gives trips, which is strong). Stronger hands in Claude's range: AT (aces + trips), AJ (aces + jacks = TPTK effectively), JJ (FH), TT/AA (FH). However, his BB defending range vs my SB steal includes many Ax that hit the turn ace (A-rag calls flop small bet as probes/value, continue calling turn), JT/9T draws that bricked or turned into weak pairs, and bluffs. I bet turn 800 as value/protection with the ace hitting my range strongly; his call indicates medium strength or floats (doesn't scream monsters, which might raise). Checking risks free showdown with worse Ax or giving him a chance to bluff, but with position, he can value bet thin. Value betting extracts from weaker Ax, Jx, missed draws turning into calls. Pot 2800, bet ~2/3-3/4 pot (1800-2100) balances value/protection, gets called by AQo-ATo-ish (worse kickers), Jx, while setting up folds from air if needed. My remaining stack 3800 allows this without overcommitting (~18bb bet). Table image: Claude calls light/sticky (Hand 14 called turn raise OOP with possible draw, Hand 20 river value), so likely calls worse. Maximizes EV by charging.

I have Jacks and Tens (two pair) with a 4 kicker. Elon's betting line is concerning: checked flop, then led out when the Ace hit on turn, now betting 2000 on river (leaving only 1800 behind). Any Ax beats me (Aces and Tens > Jacks and Tens), any Ten beats me with trips. His line strongly suggests he hit the Ace on the turn. While pot odds are ~2.4:1 (need ~29% equity), his value-heavy line doesn't suggest many bluffs. Missed spade draws don't typically lead turn then barrel river on a brick. Folding my weak two pair here.

6-handed but still UTG and out of position the rest of the hand. Js5d offsuit is too weak: it’s frequently dominated when we make a jack, has poor straight/flush potential, and plays badly versus 3-bets and multiway pots. With 81bb effective we don’t need to take thin, high-variance spots—better to preserve stack for stronger opens/steal positions.

I hold J7 offsuit in Middle Position. This is a statistically weak hand that falls well outside a standard opening range for this position (typically top 20% of hands). With active players behind me and no strategic reason to bluff or loosen my range this early in the tournament with 87 BBs, the correct play is to fold and preserve chips.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Two players have already folded before me, and I am in the Cutoff position. There is no strategic reason to enter the pot or attempt a bluff with this hand against three players still to act. Folding is the only sensible move.

KJo is a strong hand from the button with folds to me. Position is excellent, and blinds may defend wide but I have great playability and equity. Standard to raise for value and isolation. Raising to 3x BB (300) is appropriate given stack depths and opponents' tendencies to call or 3-bet occasionally from blinds. Avoids limping which invites multiway pots.
8-6 offsuit is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Out of position with a marginal hand that doesn't play well postflop. Easy fold.

Facing a 300 button open while I'm on the big blind with T♠9♥. I need to put in 200 more into a 450 pot (pot odds ≈ 2.25:1 — need ≈30% equity). T9o has good flop playability against a wide BTN opening (straight and two‑pair potential) and is worth defending from the BB rather than folding. A tiny 3‑bet to the stated 500 minimum is too small to generate meaningful fold equity and will often leave me OOP with a marginal hand if called; a larger 3‑bet is unnecessary and unnecessarily bloats the pot. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position, and fold to a substantial 4‑bet. Total to put in = 300 (100 already posted + 200 call).

Check. On A♣-4♦-3♦ with T♠9♥ I have no pair, no diamond/flush draw, and essentially no meaningful straight draw. I’m out of position versus the preflop raiser (Elon), who will c‑bet this Ace‑high, two‑tone flop very often and will be ahead with many continuing hands (Ax, pocket pairs). I only have ~6 clean outs to pair (~24% to hit by the river) and would be paying to chase a weak one‑pair equity out of position. A bluff‑raise here is too risky without fold equity or blockers. Default, lowest‑variance line is to check and fold to a standard continuation bet; if Elon checks back I can realize equity and reevaluate on the turn.

The flop of 4d-3d-Ac completely misses my KJo. I have king-high with only a weak backdoor diamond flush draw using the Jd. Minni's BB calling range versus a button raise includes Ax (which crushes me), suited diamonds for flush draws, broadway cards, pairs, and suited connectors that could have straight draws (e.g., 5-2 for wheel). The board is coordinated and scary, favoring her range. Pot is 650, my stack 6300, so risk/reward for a c-bet bluff is poor—she can call or raise wide with equity. In position, checking back is best: pot control with a bluff catcher at best, realize any thin equity on turn cheaply, and avoid bloating pot OOP if I improve or fold easily to aggression. Consistent with long-term EV in tournament.

I’m OOP with T♠9♥ on A♣‑4♦‑3♦‑4♥. I have no pair, no diamond draw and only 6 clean outs to a pair (6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river). Elon opened BTN preflop but checked the flop, which often indicates pot‑control with Ax or medium pairs — hands that will call a turn bet rather than fold. Betting into that range OOP on a paired board is high variance and unlikely to fold enough of his range; if called I’m left barreling a marginal hand. With a healthy stack and only one card to come, the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check and try to see the river for free. If Elon bets the river I’ll fold most realistic bet sizes given my poor equity; if he checks back I’ll showdown or reassess on the river.

Board is 4d-3d-Ac-4h. I have KsJd: king-high, no pair, gutshot backdoor straight draw with J, backdoor diamond flush draw. Complete air postflop. Minni checked flop and turn from BB, indicating possible weakness but her range still favors Ax, pocket pairs, diamond draws that bricked, or weak broadway. Pot 650, stacks deep (~60BB effective). In position after double check, best to check back for pot control, realize equity cheaply, and showdown king-high vs her possible worse hands (e.g., non-Ax broadway, missed draws). Bluffing risks bloating pot OOP for her with Ax/ships, poor equity/call-down vs value, and fold equity uncertain given her passive history (calls light but folds to turns/rivers sometimes). Consistent with prior flop check plan. Maximize EV by avoiding unnecessary risk this spot.

River: board A♣-4♦-3♦-4♥-3♣, my hand T♠9♥ — I have no pair and no blockers to A/4/3. Elon was the preflop raiser but checked both flop and turn, which is consistent with pot‑control Ax or a medium pair; it’s also possible he’s slow‑playing a very strong hand (4x/3x). On this double‑paired, highly coordinated river my bluff equity is thin and a bet is unlikely to fold enough of his calling range to be +EV, while exposing me to a check‑raise or a call when I have virtually no showdown value. With no bet to face, the lowest‑variance +EV line is to check and go to showdown; if Elon bets I’ll fold most realistic sizes given my poor equity.

River 3c pairs the 3d, creating possible trips for 3x hands or completing gutshots/straight draws like A5/A2/52, but my KsJd remains king-high with no improvement. Minni's BB calling range vs button raise still favors Ax (beats me), pairs (now some two-pair or trips possible), bricked diamond draws, or weak broadway. Her triple check suggests possible weakness (missed draws, trap with monster unlikely given passivity), but showdown value uncertain. Pot 650, deep stacks; bluffing risks vs her calling Ax/pairs/low pairs, low fold equity from history (passive calls). Checking back realizes equity, controls pot, consistent with checked-down pot plan from flop/turn. EV+ to showdown king-high vs possible Qx/Jx/air.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with King-Seven suited (K7s). This is a 6-handed table with deep stacks (I have ~87.5 BB). From UTG, K7s is generally below the threshold for a profitable opening raise. It is easily dominated by hands like KQ, KJ, KT, and Ace-King, and playing out of position post-flop with a weak kicker is difficult. Given there are no antes to fight for and I have 5 players left to act behind me, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

K2s is a marginal hand that is generally too weak to open from middle position in a 6-max game. While it has flush potential, the risk of being dominated by better Kings or facing three-bets from active players like Elon and Minni makes this a clear fold to maintain stack integrity for better opportunities.

Preflop in cutoff position with folds from UTG (Pro) and middle position (Flash). Hand is Qh4c offsuit, which is too weak for an open raise even in late position for a blind steal. Standard cutoff opening range includes stronger queens like QJo+ or suited connectors, but Q4o has poor playability postflop, low equity against calling ranges, and dominated often. With deep stacks (66BB effective), better to preserve stack for premium hands rather than risk entering a pot with trash. Folding is disciplined and maximizes long-term EV in tournament.
On the button with 47o after 3 folds. Perfect steal spot - only blinds left to act. This is a standard button steal opportunity even with a weak holding. Raising to 2.5x to take down the blinds.

Facing a standard button open to 250 while I'm in the small blind with 4♦6♠. This is a very weak, offsuit, poorly connected hand that will be out of position against the raiser (and likely the big blind if they defend). Calling 200 more to see a flop is negative EV — hand is frequently dominated and has poor postflop playability. With a deep stack and plenty of better spots in position, I fold and preserve chips.

Button open range is wide and JTs plays very well as a BB 3-bet candidate: it has strong playability when called, blocks some continues (Jx/Tx broadways), and benefits from taking initiative out of position while denying equity to his weaker opens. At ~80bb effective, a 3-bet to ~4x pressures his range without bloating the pot too much; if he 4-bets, we can comfortably fold this combo.

Attempted a button steal with 47o, but FiveTwo 3-bet from the BB to 950. With this trash hand, I have no business calling a 3-bet - I have poor equity against any reasonable 3-betting range and 47o plays terribly postflop. Easy fold.

In a 6-handed game, KJo is at the threshold of a standard opening range from UTG. I'm raising to 300 to take the initiative and define the strength of my hand. While the table has shown significant pre-flop aggression, I need to maintain a balanced opening frequency and avoid playing too passively. If I encounter a 3-bet from players in late position or the blinds, I will re-evaluate based on their sizing and previous tendencies.

AQo is a premium hand that plays well multi-way or heads-up. Facing a standard UTG open from Flash (300 into 450 pot), who has shown aggression but folds to pressure in spots (e.g., Hand 4, Hand 12). In middle position with deep stacks (66BB effective), 3-betting isolates the raiser, denies equity to callers behind (Claude, Minni active), and builds the pot with our strong range advantage. AQo dominates much of UTG range (AJs+, KQs, etc.). Sizing to 900 (3x) is standard, balanced, and leaves fold equity while committing ~14% of stack if called. Calling risks multi-way pot OOP; folding too tight early in tournament.

Q8o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet from Elon after Flash's initial raise. I'm in the cutoff and would need to call 900 with a hand that plays poorly against 3-betting ranges. Q8o lacks the equity and playability to profitably call here - easy fold.

Fold. Facing an UTG open and a 3-bet to 900, my J♦4♦ on the button is a weak, often-dominated hand and has poor playability postflop. The pot is 1,350 and calling 900 would require ~40% equity to break even, which J4o does not have versus a tightening 3-bet range. Raising as a bluff is unnecessary with deep stacks and two blinds still to act — I can wait for a much better spot on the button. Preserve my 13,975 stack and avoid a marginal spot out of position postflop.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open and a 3-bet from Elon, with the big blind still to act behind us. 9h5h has poor playability in a 3-bet pot out of position: we’re frequently dominated when we make a pair, and our suited/straight potential won’t realize well versus strong, high-card ranges and higher SPR constraints. A cold 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here (no blockers, risk of getting jammed on by the shorter stack, and UTG can continue). Best stack-preserving decision is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am facing a UTG open raise to 3bb and a cold 3-bet from Middle Position to 9bb. My hand, QJo, is significantly dominated by the ranges of both opponents, particularly the 3-bettor who is showing strength against a UTG open. Additionally, I would be playing out of position against strong ranges. The pot odds do not justify a call given the reverse implied odds and low equity (~25-30%) against a standard 3-betting range. Folding is the only viable option.

I opened KJo from UTG which is already at the bottom of my opening range. Elon has 3-bet to 900. KJo is significantly dominated by a standard 3-betting range (AK, AQ, AJ, and pairs like JJ+) and playing it out of position for the rest of the hand is disadvantageous. Given Elon's frequent aggression, I could potentially be ahead of some bluffs, but the lack of playability and potential to be dominated makes a fold the most prudent move to preserve my stack for better spots.

72s is one of the weakest possible starting hands, especially from UTG in a 6-handed tournament. It has poor playability post-flop, low equity against calling ranges, and no strong suited connector value. Folding preserves my stack (7075, decent depth) for better opportunities in later position or with premium hands. Opponent stacks suggest no immediate pressure to widen range here. Long-term EV maximization requires discipline pre-flop.

Pocket deuces in middle position with 112 BB stack. Small pair is playable here - I'll open raise to 2.5x for set mining potential. If 3-bet, I can fold. If called, looking to hit a set on the flop which has about 12% chance but offers great implied odds.

Claude opened to 250 from MP (≈2.5bb). I’m in the cutoff with K♥3♣ — a weak, easily dominated hand with poor playability out of position. Calling 250 into a 400 pot requires ~38.5% equity; versus a typical MP open (lots of Kx, Ax, broadways and pairs) K3o rarely has that and will often be crushed or in awkward postflop spots. A min‑3bet to 400 has almost no fold equity and a larger 3bet would be risking too much with a poor kicker. With the button and blinds yet to act and deep stacks, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold preflop.

Claude is opening from middle position (2.5x). With K5 offsuit on the button, I’m frequently dominated by his Kx/Qx/Ax range, and the hand has poor playability even in position. A 3-bet bluff isn’t attractive here because I don’t have strong blockers beyond a single K and I’d be risking ~900–1000 to win 400 without expecting enough folds. Best EV is to let it go and preserve stack for better steal/iso spots.

I hold A4o in the Small Blind facing a 2.5x open from Claude in Middle Position. Claude has shown a tendency to open strong hands (QQ, KQs, KJs) from early/middle positions. A4o is often dominated by his opening range (better Aces, pairs) and plays poorly out of position. While I get decent direct pot odds to call, realizing that equity out of position is difficult, and the Big Blind is still left to act. A 3-bet bluff is possible but risky against a tight-aggressive opener with a large stack. The most prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit. Claude has raised to 2.5x from middle position, and everyone else has folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop, with the pot already at 400. This gives me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1. While King-Eight offsuit is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend the big blind against a single open raise from middle position given these odds. I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, but the price to see a flop is too good to fold.

I defended the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit. The flop of Ace-Jack-Seven is quite unfavorable for my hand, as it hits many of the hands in a middle-position raiser's range (Aces, Jacks, and broadway cards). I have no direct pair or draw. My best course of action is to check and see how the aggressor, Claude, decides to play the board. I am prepared to fold to most continuation bets.

Missed my set with pocket 2s on A-J-7 board. However, this is a good c-bet spot as the Ace-high board heavily favors my preflop raising range. Flash checked to me, so I'll make a small continuation bet (~50% pot) to take it down or get information. If called or raised, I'll give up.

I defended the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit and the flop came Ace-Jack-Seven, which misses my hand entirely. This board texture is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser, Claude, who likely has many Ace-x and Jack-x hands in his range. I have no pair and no draw, and with a bet of 275 into a 550 pot, I do not have the necessary equity or favorable implied odds to continue. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here.
72 offsuit in UTG position - this is the worst starting hand in poker. Easy fold.

Fold. 5♥2♣ is the weakest offsuit hand — no real equity and terrible playability. I'm in middle position with the cutoff, button and both blinds yet to act, so any call will likely be a multiway pot out of position and a raise-to-steal is a marginal bluff that will frequently run into better hands or a 3-bet. Pot is small relative to my 13,975 stack; there will be plenty of better spots to invest chips. Preserve stack and fold.

We’re in the cutoff with AA and action has folded to us, so this is a mandatory open for value. Raising builds a pot versus the blinds’ wide defending ranges and also sets up profitable 4-bet/get-in lines if the button or blinds 3-bet. I’ll use a standard-ish sizing that I’d also use with other opens here to avoid telegraphing strength and keep dominated hands (Ax, broadways, pairs) continuing.

I am on the Button with QJo facing a 3x open raise (300) from the Cutoff (FiveTwo). While QJo has decent playability in position, it is often dominated by the opener's value range (AQ, AJ, KQ, KJ) and faces reverse implied odds. The 3x sizing makes a call less attractive due to pot odds and the risk of a squeeze from the blinds. A 3-bet is possible as a bluff but risky against an aggressive opponent. Folding is the standard disciplined play here to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind holding 7c 8c, which is a strong suited connector. FiveTwo raised to 300 from the Cutoff. With a stack of 10,400 and getting nearly 3:1 pot odds (250 to call into a 450 pot), this is a standard call to see a flop. While I will be out of position post-flop, the potential of suited connectors in a single-raised pot is high enough to justify the investment. I also have to consider Elon in the Big Blind, but calling allows me to realize my equity cheaply.

Preflop in the big blind with 9h8s offsuit, facing a cutoff raise to 300 by FiveTwo and a small blind call by Flash, making it multiway if I call. My hand is marginal with some straight potential but offsuit, no suited connectors, and poor implied odds multiway. Pot odds are favorable (700 to 200, ~3.5:1), but FiveTwo's cutoff range is wide but strong enough, Flash's call suggests speculative hands, and 98o doesn't play well postflop OOP against these players based on history where FiveTwo raises frequently but pots aggressively. Stacks are deep (~70BB), but folding preserves stack for better spots in this early tournament stage to maximize long-term EV.

I have flopped middle pair (eights) on a King-high board. As the Small Blind and facing the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo), checking is the standard and most prudent play. This allows me to see if FiveTwo continues with a bet and helps me gauge the strength of their hand without inflating the pot unnecessarily while out of position.

Flop is fairly dry (Kd-3c-8h, no flush draw). As the preflop raiser with AA, we have a large range advantage and want to start extracting value from Kx, 8x, stubborn pocket pairs (QQ–44), and some floats, while denying cheap overcard equity. A small c-bet keeps worse hands in and lets us comfortably continue versus calls; if we get check-raised, we can evaluate based on sizing/line but AA is strong enough to continue versus many SB check-raise ranges here.

I have middle pair (8s) on a dry board of K-3-8. FiveTwo's small bet of 250 into a 700 pot suggests a continuation bet that could come from a wide range of hands, including air, marginal pairs, or a King. Given the generous pot odds (nearly 4:1), I have a clear call to see how the turn develops. I'm not looking to bloat the pot further by raising out of position, but folding here would be far too tight given my equity against their likely range.

I have middle pair (eights) on a board of Kd-3c-8h-Ts. The Ten on the turn is a semi-coordinated card but doesn't change the absolute strength of my hand significantly. As I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo, the most standard and disciplined play is to check. This allows me to control the pot size and see how FiveTwo responds. If they check back, I can likely take my hand to showdown. If they bet, I will have to evaluate the bet size and the likelihood that my pair of eights is still the best hand.

Turn Ts doesn’t meaningfully change the texture for us: we still have a strong overpair on a mostly dry K-8-3 board, and Flash’s flop check-call range contains plenty of hands that can continue versus another value bet (Kx, Tx like KT/QT/JT, 8x, and some stubborn pocket pairs). Betting also denies free equity to random overcards/gutshots. I’ll barrel a bit over half-pot to keep worse hands calling while still building a pot for a solid river value bet. If Flash check-raises, I’ll evaluate sizing—small raises can be continued against, but big raises polarize toward sets/two-pair.

I have middle pair (eights) on a board of Kd-3c-8h-Ts. FiveTwo has fired a second barrel of 650 into a 1200 pot. While the Ten is an overcard to my pair, it is unlikely to have improved many hands in a Cutoff opening range that weren't already ahead, though it does pick up some equity for hands like QJ or J9. I am getting roughly 2.85:1 on a call (650 to win 1850). While being out of position is a disadvantage, folding a middle pair to a standard second barrel would be too tight and exploitable. I will call to see the river and evaluate based on the final card and FiveTwo's subsequent action.

On the river, the board is Kd-3c-8h-Ts-9s. My pair of eights, which was middle pair on the flop, has now become a relatively weak hand as the board has become more coordinated (9, 10, and K are all present). A straight is now possible with any Jack (QJ was already possible on the turn). Given that I have been check-calling to control the pot and evaluate the strength of FiveTwo's range, checking is the most logical move here. I am out of position and my hand is primarily a bluff-catcher at this point. I want to see if FiveTwo checks behind for a cheap showdown or if they fire a third barrel, which would likely force a fold from my marginal pair.

River is a marginal spot for a thin value bet. After SB check-calls flop and turn on a fairly dry K-8-3 texture, their range is heavily weighted toward Kx (which has us beat) plus some slowplayed sets/two-pair and a smaller portion of Tx/8x/pocket pairs. The 9s also completes some 2-pair (K9/T9) and the QJ straight, increasing the density of hands that will happily call/raise versus a third barrel. Since there are very few natural missed draws to bluff-catch and a bet is unlikely to get paid often by worse (and never folds out better), checking back maximizes EV by taking showdown with AA and avoiding paying off Kx+.

6-max UTG with K♥9♣ is marginal but openable — I want the initiative and to thin the field. Standard open size of ~2.5–3x BB accomplishes that, gives fold equity against the blinds and keeps weaker hands from seeing cheap flops. With a deep ~140BB stack I can still maneuver postflop, but I will be cautious vs a sizable 3‑bet (will usually fold K9o OOP to large 3‑bets from the blinds; may call small 3‑bets depending on sizing and opponent). Plan: raise to take the pot heads‑up or play predictable c‑bet lines on favourable flops and pot‑control on dangerous boards.

UTG open to 3bb from Minni represents a relatively strong range. K3o is heavily dominated (by Kx, Ax) and plays poorly postflop, especially with several players left to act behind who can 3-bet or call and put us in tough spots. With ~96bb effective, there’s no need to defend this hand; folding preserves stack and avoids negative-EV dominated situations.

I am in the Cutoff with Ad 6h (A6o). The player in UTG (Minni) has raised to 300. Although Minni has been active, an UTG opening range typically includes strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) and pocket pairs, all of which dominate or perform well against A6o. Calling is risky due to reverse implied odds (if an Ace hits, I am likely kicked) and the threat of a squeeze from the aggressive players yet to act (Flash on Button, Elon in SB). 3-betting is too loose with this holding against an UTG range. The disciplined and correct play is to fold.

I am on the button with pocket sixes. Minni raised from UTG, which generally indicates a strong range. Pocket pairs play very well in position for set-mining. Given the stack sizes (approx. 90bb deep), I have excellent implied odds to call the 300 and see if I can hit a set on the flop. If I miss, I can easily evaluate the situation based on the board texture and Minni's aggression. 3-betting here would likely only be called by better hands or force out hands I'm ahead of, so calling is the most profitable play.

4d Ts is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind facing an UTG raise from Minni (likely strong range) and a button call from Flash. Pot odds are about 3:1 (250 to call into 750), but post-flop OOP with this hand offers little equity against villain ranges and potential BB action. Standard fold to preserve stack in a long tournament.

I have Ah2h in the BB facing a minraise from UTG and a call from the button. Suited ace has good playability and I'm getting excellent pot odds (200 to win 750 = 3.75:1). Easy call to see a flop with potential to hit a flush or top pair.

Flopped the nut flush draw with Ah2h on Th-Qd-4h. I have 9 outs to the nuts. Out of position against UTG raiser and button caller. Checking makes sense here - I can see a free card or call a reasonable bet if they lead out. No need to lead into two opponents when I can't rep much on this board.

I opened UTG with K♥9♣ but I missed this flop — Th Qd 4h gives me no pair, only K‑high and a very slim backdoor heart equity. It’s three‑way (Flash on the button + Claude in the BB), and both callers’ ranges include many Qx/Tx, broadways and pocket pairs that have me dominated or better equity. Multiway bluff c‑bets are much less effective and the table history shows these opponents frequently check‑call rather than folding to small aggression. Pot is small relative to our deep stacks, so there’s no need to force the issue. I’ll check to control the pot, fold to any meaningful bet, and re‑evaluate on the turn — consider a delayed bluff only if the turn meaningfully helps my range or if I see a clear opportunity to take it away.

On this flop of Th-Qd-4h, my pocket sixes are now an underpair to the two overcards. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor from UTG, checked, which could indicate she missed the board with high cards like AK or AJ, or is playing a medium pair cautiously. Claude also checked from the big blind. While I have position, betting here into two opponents doesn't achieve much; I'm unlikely to fold out a Queen or a Ten, and I don't have enough value to build a pot. Checking back is the most prudent play to keep the pot small and see a free turn card, potentially hitting one of my two remaining sixes or reaching a cheap showdown if the action remains passive.

I have the nut flush draw (Ah2h) on Th-Qd-4h-3c. Turn was a blank. Both opponents checked the flop, showing weakness. I should bet here as a semi-bluff - I can win the pot now or have 9 outs (~18%) to the nuts on the river. Betting around 2/3 pot to apply pressure.

I opened UTG with K♥9♣ and checked the flop to control the pot. The turn (3♣) doesn't improve me — I have only K‑high and a very remote runner‑runner heart possibility. Claude leads for 600 into a 1,550 pot (~38% pot). That sizing looks like a probe/thin value bet; his betting range will include many Qx/Tx/pairs that currently beat me, but 600 is a small price to see the river and gives me ~21% pot‑odds to continue. Against a table that often check‑calls, calling lets me float and try to take the pot away on the river or fold if Claude shows strength. Raising here would be risky and inconsistent (I checked the flop) and would likely commit me out of position to better hands. Plan: call the turn (600), see the river, then either fold to a strong bet or bluff/lead if the river and opponent action give a feasible opportunity to steal.

On the turn, Claude (Big Blind) has led for 600 into the pre-flop aggressor and myself after the flop checked around. Minni (UTG) called this bet. This sequence suggests that at least one of my opponents has a piece of the board (likely a Ten, a Queen, or a draw). My pocket sixes are an underpair to the two overcards on the board (Queen and Ten) and have very little equity to improve (only two remaining sixes). Given that I am facing a bet and a call, it is highly probable that my hand is currently worst. There is no reason to put more chips into the pot with a marginal hand that has almost no chance of being the best at showdown without improving significantly. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

River bricked - 8s doesn't complete my flush draw. I have Ace high which is essentially no showdown value. Minni called my turn semi-bluff of 600, indicating they likely have at least a pair (Q or T). Bluffing again into a player who already called one street would be risky and expensive. I'll check and give up on this pot.

I have K♥9♣ and missed the board (T♥ Q♦ 4♥ 3♣ 8♠) — no pair, no backdoor. Claude led small on the turn (600 into ~1,550) — a probing/thin‑value sizing — then checked the river, which is either weakness or pot‑control. This spot gives me a shot to bluff, but the table and Claude’s lines show a high tendency to check‑call; a profitable river bluff would require a sizable fold frequency (roughly 25–40% depending on sizing). I don’t block many of his likely calling hands and have no showdown value, so risking chips here is low EV. I’ll check back, preserve my stack, and avoid creating a marginal OOP bluff against a check‑calling opponent.

6-max UTG with AKo at ~97bb is a mandatory open for value and initiative. Raising (instead of limping) isolates the blinds, can win the pot pre, and sets up profitable continuations on many boards. Use a solid but not bloated sizing that still applies pressure and keeps our range protected vs 3-bets.

I am holding 9h 5d in Middle Position, facing an opening raise to 250 from the Under-The-Gun player (FiveTwo). 95 offsuit is a very weak hand with extremely low equity, making it unplayable against an early position raise, especially with several players left to act behind me. There is no strategic merit in calling or bluff-raising here. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately to conserve chips.

I am holding 7s 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. FiveTwo has opened from UTG with a raise to 250. Even with position, 73o has no playability against an early position opening range. Folding is the only sensible move here to preserve my stack.

62o is one of the worst hands in poker: offsuit, disconnected low cards with minimal playability or showdown value. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression but often with reasonable hands preflop. Pot odds to call 250 into 400 (~1.6:1) are poor for speculative hand like this. Even on the button, folding preserves stack for better opportunities. No reason to raise bluff. Deep stack (69BB), plenty of hands left in tournament.

I have T9o in the small blind facing a 2.5x UTG raise to 250. This is a marginal hand but playable given the price. I need to call 200 into a 400 pot (3:1 odds). T9o has decent playability - can make straights and two pairs. The main concern is being OOP, but at this price I'll call and see a flop. If Minni squeezes, I can fold.

FiveTwo opened to 250 UTG and the SB cold-called, so this will be at least a three‑way pot. I have 5s4s — a small suited connector with good multiway playability and strong implied odds. The pot is 600 and I only have to add 150 more (total 250) to continue, giving roughly 20% pot odds, which makes a call cheap and correct here. 3-betting with 5-4s out of the big blind into an UTG range is too thin (I’ll be out of position and often dominated), so I’ll just call and see a flop. Plan: continue with pairs, two pair, straights or flush draws; fold to heavy aggression if I miss and face strong pressure.
Flop is Ah 8h 8s and I have T9o - completely whiffed. No pair, no meaningful draw. Best to check and see what happens. Will likely fold to any significant aggression.

Board is A♥ 8♥ 8♠ and I hold 5♠4♠ — no pair, no heart backdoor is irrelevant, essentially air. This is a multiway pot (UTG raiser + SB + me), so bluffing into two opponents on a paired board with an Ace is a poor +EV line: FiveTwo’s UTG open includes many Aces and strong hands, and both opponents checking the flop could be slowplaying or exercising pot control. I have good stack depth relative to the pot and no equity to continue as a bluff, so I’ll conserve chips and check. Plan: fold to meaningful flop/turn aggression unless I improve to at least a pair or get a very cheap probe bet I can call to see the next card; be prepared to attempt a later bluff only if the table gives strong weakness reads.

Multiway on Ah-8h-8s, AdKc is top pair/top kicker but the board is paired, so ranges are fairly condensed and raises skew toward trips/full houses. Still, both blinds can have plenty of worse Ax (A2–AQ), pocket pairs, and heart draws that will continue versus a small c-bet. I want to extract value and deny a free card to hands like QhJh/KhQh while keeping the pot manageable and not overexposing myself to a check-raise range that’s often very strong here. A ~1/3 pot bet accomplishes this and keeps my range balanced on this texture.

I have T9o on Ah 8h 8s - completely missed. No pair, no flush draw (no hearts), no straight draw worth mentioning. FiveTwo's 1/3 pot c-bet could be wide, but I have zero equity and no way to improve. Folding is the clear play.

Board: A♥ 8♥ 8♠. I have 5♠4♠. I checked the flop and FiveTwo (UTG opener) bets 250 into a 1000 pot; SB folded, so it’s heads‑up. To call 250 I’d need roughly 250/(1000+250+250) ≈ 16.7% equity to break even. My hand has virtually no showdown value and very poor equity versus an UTG continuation range (lots of A‑x, 8x, overpairs and some strong broadways). I only have remote runner‑runner/spade/straight outs and no meaningful blockers to A or 8. Raising OOP as a bluff is too thin — I’ll often be called or 3‑bet by better hands. With deep stacks but a clearly dominated hand, the +EV line is to fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I am holding Ten-Seven offsuit (T7o) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a statistically weak hand with very little playability, especially from early position where I have to act first and potentially face raises from five other players. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand 100% of the time in this spot to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in middle position with Kd Qs, a strong broadway hand. Since the action has folded to me, I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to take control of the pot and narrow the field. KQo is well ahead of most random hands and plays well against common calling ranges from the blinds.

74o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. In cutoff position facing a standard open-raise to 3x from middle position (Flash), who has shown aggression in past hands but often with playable ranges. My stack is deep at ~69BB, but this hand has zero playability post-flop, dominated equity against any reasonable raising range, and no fold equity if I 3-bet bluff. Pot odds are poor (~1.5:1), and speculative calling leads to difficult spots. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Long-term goal is stack maximization over 150 hands; avoid spew.

J8 suited on the button facing a single raise from middle position. This is a playable hand in position. I'll call to see a flop with position advantage rather than 3-bet with a marginal holding. J8s has good playability with flush potential and some straight possibilities.

I have A♦7♠ in the small blind facing a raise to 300 and a button call — a multiway, out-of-position spot. A7o is playable but marginal to 3-bet; with deep stacks there's no need to isolate or get sticky out of position. Calling 250 more (300 total) keeps the pot manageable, lets me use my Ace blocker postflop, and gives reasonable pot odds to see a flop. Plan: call to 300 total, fold to any sizable 3-bet, and then proceed only with clear equity or position-based opportunities (bet/raise when I pair the Ace or pick up strong draws; otherwise control pot size).

We’re getting a very good price closing the action: 200 to win a 1000 pot (~20% immediate odds) in a multiway spot. 9h6h is too weak to squeeze from the BB into an opener plus two callers (we’ll get called and play bloated OOP), but it has enough suited/playability and implied odds to defend profitably at this price. Plan postflop: proceed on boards that give strong draws/two pair+ potential; otherwise check-fold versus pressure.

Check. This is a multiway flop (preflop raiser + button + BB) and I have top pair with a very weak kicker (A♦7♠) on A♣-8♦-J♣. Out of position and with two clubs on board, my hand is vulnerable to better Ax, two-pair/sets and club draws, so I want to control the pot rather than build it. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in, lets the aggressive preflop raiser c-bet often (which I can call for pot control), and avoids getting raised into difficult decisions OOP. Plan: call reasonable single bets (roughly up to ~1/3–1/2 pot, ~400–600) to see a turn, fold to large/polarized raises, and if action checks through take a free turn to re-evaluate (bet for thin value only if I improve or the turn is a safe spot).

Flop A♣ J♣ 8♦ is very favorable for the preflop raiser and the callers’ ranges (Ax, broadways, strong club draws). With 9♥6♥ we have no pair, no flush draw, and only very thin backdoor straight potential, so a donk-bluff into three opponents is unlikely to generate enough folds and just builds a pot OOP. Best is to check and be prepared to fold versus meaningful pressure, continuing only if we improve strongly on the turn.

I am in a 4-way pot on an Ace-high board with two overcards to the middle of the board and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). While I was the pre-flop aggressor and can represent the Ace, betting into three opponents on this texture is risky as the likelihood of someone having an Ace or a strong draw is high. Checking here allows me to see a turn card for free, control the size of the pot, and potentially realize my equity if a Ten falls. In multi-way pots, a more conservative approach with marginal equity is generally preferred.

I flopped two pair (Jacks and Eights) on Ac 8d Jc. This is a strong hand that needs protection - there's a club flush draw on the board. Everyone checked to me, so I should bet for value and to charge draws. Betting around 2/3 pot (~800) is appropriate to extract value and deny equity to flush draws.

Flop: I hold A♦7♠ on A♣-8♦-J♣. This is top pair with a very weak kicker, out of position in a multiway pot (Flash and FiveTwo checked). Claude bets 800 into a 2000 pot (≈40% pot). Pot odds to call are ~800/(2000+800)=28.6%. My Ace blocks a fair number of strong A× combinations, and a call here keeps worse Ax and many Jx/bluff combos in the pot while controlling the size. Raising OOP with a weak kicker into multiple players is too thin and will often get me in trouble against better Aces or sets. Plan: call the 800 to see a turn, re-evaluate on the next street, and fold to a sizable raise (I’ll concede if Claude shows much stronger aggression — e.g., a raise to 1600+). If the turn is a safe card and action checks, I can extract thin value or check for showdown on scary turns (clubs or high cards that complete draws).

We have 9♥6♥ on A♣ J♣ 8♦ with no pair, no club draw, and only very thin backdoor/runner-runner straight/flush possibilities. Facing an 800 bet into 2800 and a call in a multiway pot, our realized equity will be far below the ~22% needed to continue (we’ll often face more heat on turns and won’t get paid enough when we spike a 7/T/heart runout). A check-raise bluff is also unattractive here: there’s already a caller, the bet is small (often inducing calls), and Flash still has to act behind us. Best play is to preserve chips and fold.

On this Ace-high board, my KQo has a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). Claude bet 800 into the 1200 pot from the button, and Minni called from the small blind. The pot currently stands at 2800 (pre-flop 1200 + Claude 800 + Minni 800), and it costs me 800 to call. This gives me pot odds of 800/3600, or about 22%. My chance of hitting a Ten on the turn is only about 9%. Given the multi-way action and the high likelihood that at least one of my opponents holds an Ace (especially Minni after calling), my King or Queen overcards are likely not clean outs. Chasing a four-outer against two opponents in this spot is mathematically unfavorable, so I will fold.

I improved on the turn — I have A♦7♠ and the 7♥ pairs my 7, giving me two pair (Aces and Sevens) heads-up vs Claude (the flop bettor). Hands that currently beat me are A8/AJ two-pair combos, sets (88, JJ, 77) and the turned straight (T9); however many of Claude’s flop-calling/betting hands (weaker Ax, Jx, and club draws) are behind or drawing. Checking would give free cards to club/straight draws and allow weaker Ax to realize equity cheaply. Betting accomplishes two goals: extract value from worse Aces and Jx and charge/price out flush/straight draws. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (1400 into 2800) — large enough to deny/protect against many draws and get called by dominated Ax and Jx, but small enough to keep worse hands in. If Claude raises big I’ll re-evaluate (two pair is strong vs his range but I’ll be cautious vs large, polarized action). Action: bet 1400.

I have two pair (Jacks and Eights) on Ac 8d Jc 7h. Minni check-called flop then led turn for 1400. This line often indicates a draw that wants to control the price, a medium ace, or occasionally a slow-played monster. With my strong holding and blockers to JJ/88, I'm likely ahead of most of their range. The club flush draw is still out there - I should raise for value and protection. Raising to ~3200 puts pressure on draws and extracts value from worse hands like Ax or one pair.

I hold A♦7♠ — two pair (Aces and Sevens) on A♣-8♦-J♣-7♥. I bet the turn 1,400 to charge draws and extract value; Claude shoved up the aggression and raised to 3,200 (1800 more to me). Claude’s line (calling preflop, betting the flop, now raising the turn) is weighted toward strong value hands (AJ, A8, 88, JJ, 77 and the occasional T9 straight), but he will still have some bluffs/weaker Ax and Jx in his range. Calling 1,800 into a 7,400 pot is cheap (~20% required equity) and two pair has very good showdown value versus his likely range. Raising again would commit a lot more and often puts me in a miserable spot against his value combos (and could fold out worse hands that would call). Folding two pair here is too weak. Plan: call to see the river and re-evaluate — call small/medium river bets for value, but be prepared to fold to a polarized shove if the line/size looks like a very narrow strong-value range.

I have A♦7♠ — two pair (Aces & Sevens) on A♣‑8♦‑J♣‑7♥‑9♠. Claude’s line (flop c‑bet, then a sizable turn raise vs my protect bet) is weighted toward strong value hands: AJ, A8, sets (88, JJ, 77) and some made straights (T9/98). Many of those hands beat me and a lead here risks folding out worse A/J hands while getting raised/leveled by hands that are already ahead. The river 9 also completes the T9 straight and pairs up a card that can help Claude, so the street is somewhat hostile. Checking keeps control, realizes the hand’s reasonable showdown equity, and lets me call small/medium bets for value while folding to a polarized shove. With the pot at 9,200 I’d rather see a river bet and call modest sizes (~~1/3 pot) than bet and get raised off or turned away from value. Action: check and call small/medium river bets; fold to a large polarized shove.

River 9s doesn't complete obvious draws besides T8 straight. Minni check-called flop, led turn, called my raise, then checked river - this line caps their range at medium strength hands. With two pair (J8) I should value bet. Minni likely has Ax or missed clubs. A moderate bet around 1/3 pot should get called by worse hands while keeping the pot manageable if I'm beat.

I have A♦7♠ — two pair (Aces and Sevens) on A♣-8♦-J♣-7♥-9♠. Claude’s line (flop c‑bet, then raising my turn protect bet) is weighted toward very strong value (AJ, A8, sets 88/ JJ/77, some T9 straights) but will still include some worse A‑x, Jx and occasional bluffs. His river sizing (2,800 into a 12,000 pot, ~23% pot) is small and looks like a thin value/blocked bet or a probe — not a polarized shove. Folding two pair to such a small bet surrenders value; raising OOP would commit a lot of chips and often run into hands that already beat me. Calling 2,800 risks 2,800 to win ~14,800 (pot after call), so I need ≈19% equity — well within two pair’s equity against a range that includes bluffs and worse Ax/Jx. Plan: call the 2,800 to reach showdown, but fold to a large shove above this sizing if Claude polarizes further.

I am dealt Ace-King offsuit (AKo) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a premium hand in a 6-max game and requires a strong opening raise to build a pot and reduce the number of opponents. My stack is healthy at approximately 86 big blinds. Looking at the table dynamics, players like Claude and Minni have been active and aggressive, but AKo is well ahead of their general ranges. I will follow my established opening size of 300 (3x the big blind) to maintain consistency and value.

T8s is a suited connector with some playability for implied odds and multiway pots in position, but facing an UTG raise from Flash in middle position, the range is strong (likely 22+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways). Calling risks playing OOP potentially multiway against multiple players behind (Claude cutoff, Minni button with deep stack), leading to difficult postflop decisions with a marginal hand. Pot odds are poor (300 to call into 450 pot, ~40% equity needed but vs strong range we have ~30-35% equity heads-up, worse multiway). Raising is not justified as we're often dominated. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament setting with 112 hands left; discipline maximizes EV long-term.
93o is a very weak hand. Flash opened from UTG which typically shows strength. With players still to act behind me and no real playability with this hand, folding is the clear choice.

Button with Jh9s (J9 offsuit). Flash raised UTG to 300 — a fairly strong opening range — but I have position and a playable hand. Calling 300 is cheap relative to my 20.5k stack (~1.5%) and lets me realize equity and use position post-flop. Folding is too tight; a min-reraise to 500 is too small to isolate and will often invite a 3-bet from the blinds or the opener. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — play aggressively if I connect (pair, two‑pair, straight/river draws); be cautious in multiway pots and fold to sizable resistance. If faced with a large 3-bet preflop (>>~1,000–1,200), I will fold this marginal offsuit hand.

We’re in the small blind, out of position versus an UTG open and a button flat. K6o is heavily dominated by both players’ continuing ranges (KQ/KJ/KT, better kings, broadways, suited aces) and plays poorly multiway from the SB. The price is tempting, but the implied reverse implied odds are bad and we’ll realize equity poorly OOP. No profitable 3-bet bluff profile here either given UTG strength + button call. Best is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Deuce suited (Qh2h). Flash raised from UTG to 300, indicating a strong range, and Minni called on the Button. The Small Blind folded. Although I am getting favorable pot odds of 3.75 to 1 (calling 200 to win a pot of 750), my hand is very weak and easily dominated. Playing Q2s out of position against two opponents, one of whom has a strong UTG opening range, will make it difficult to realize my equity. The potential for flush draws exists, but often I will be unsure of my standing or dominated by better Queens or flushes. Discretion is the better part of valor here; folding preserves my stack for better spots.

The flop of 4d-8s-7d is unfavorable for my UTG range, as it connects well with the Button's likely calling range of medium pocket pairs and suited connectors. With Ace-King high and a backdoor nut flush draw, I have some equity, but betting into a sticky and deep-stacked opponent like Minni on this board texture is risky. I will check to control the size of the pot and see how she reacts, hoping to catch an Ace or King on the turn or potentially see a free card if she checks behind.

I'm on the button with Jh9s facing a single UTG opener on 4d-8s-7d. I missed but have position and his UTG range will miss this flop a fair amount. With a small stack-to-pot commitment advantage I should try a low-frequency, small continuation bet to take the initiative and deny equity to missed broadway hands and floats. Bet ~1/3 pot (250) to fold out many hands that missed and keep the pot small if called. If he calls I will pot-control on the turn (check and re-evaluate) and fold to significant aggression unless I pair or pick up a real draw; if he raises big here I will fold this marginal hand.

On a board of 4d-8s-7d, the texture heavily favors the Button's calling range, which contains many more middling connectors, suited gappers, and pocket pairs than my UTG opening range. Minni's bet of 250 into a 750 pot is a small, probing bet, but my Ace-King has no direct draws and very poor playability out of position. Even if I hit an Ace or King on the turn, I could easily be behind to a straight (56) or two-pair (87). Given the coordination of the board and my lack of even a backdoor flush draw, the most disciplined play is to fold and avoid a potentially expensive guessing game on later streets.

2c6h is an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no high card strength, and minimal straight potential. As UTG in a 6-handed game, the position is vulnerable to multi-way pots and raises from behind. Standard play is to fold trash to preserve stack for premium hands. Opponent stacks are deep enough that limping or raising this would be exploitable and -EV. Prioritize tight-aggressive strategy to maximize long-term stack growth over 150 hands.
J5 offsuit is a weak hand from middle position with 4 players still to act behind me. Not a profitable open or call spot - easy fold.

Cutoff open with Ac9h is a standard steal/attack: I have an Ace blocker and good post‑flop playability in position. With healthy stacks and only the blinds to act, I want to apply pressure and narrow ranges, so I’ll open to ~3x the big blind. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold (A9o is vulnerable vs polarized 3‑bet ranges); if called I’ll use position to c‑bet on dry flops and pot‑control on Ace‑high or coordinated boards.

Minni is opening fairly actively from late position and we have position on the button. QTo is strong enough to defend, but it plays better as a flat than a 3-bet here: if we 3-bet, we mostly get action from a tighter, stronger continuing range and bloat the pot with an offsuit hand that can run into dominated top pairs. By calling we keep in worse Qx/Tx and suited connectors, realize equity well in position, and keep the pot manageable while still having good postflop leverage.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo), a premium hand. Minni, the chip leader who has been opening frequently, raised to 300 from the Cutoff, and FiveTwo flat-called on the Button. This is a prime spot for a 'squeeze' play. FiveTwo's flat call often caps their range, and Minni's range is likely wide. Playing AQo passively out of position in a multi-way pot is suboptimal due to its lack of playability on many textures and the risk of domination if I just call and let the Big Blind in. By 3-betting (squeezing) to 1200, I can isolate the loose opener, deny equity to the button and big blind, and take down the dead money (750 chips) immediately or play a heads-up pot with the betting initiative. The sizing of 1200 (4x the open) is standard for this situation.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Four offsuit. The action has been a raise from Minni (CO) to 300, a call from FiveTwo (BTN), and a 3-bet squeeze from Pro (SB) to 1200. K4o is far too weak to call a 3-bet, especially out of position against potentially three opponents. Pro's range for squeezing from the small blind into two players is generally quite strong. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

I opened CO to 300 with A♣9♥ which is a standard steal. Button called, then the small blind (Pro) 3‑bet to 1,200 — a fairly large, polarized sizing. The pot is 1,900 and it costs me 900 to continue (~32% pot odds). A9 offsuit is vulnerable vs a polarized 3‑bet (value range like QQ+/AK dominates me) and with the button already in, this will often be a multi‑way pot that further reduces my equity and postflop maneuverability. Pro has shown willingness to make sizable, value‑oriented raises, so I give him a tighter/polarized 3‑bet range here. A call would leave me playing a marginal hand out of position against a strong range; a 4‑bet bluff is thin and unnecessary with deep stacks and many better spots to apply pressure later. I’ll conserve chips and fold.

Pro’s small blind squeeze after the cutoff open + my button flat is typically a fairly strong, linear range (lots of AQ+/KQ, big pairs, and some suited broadway bluffs). With QTo offsuit I’m dominated too often when I make top pair, and the resulting SPR if I call is very low (about ~2), which reduces my ability to maneuver postflop and increases the chance I’m forced into thin/losing stack-off spots. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive because Pro’s continuing range versus a 4-bet will be strong and I’d be risking too much with limited fold equity. Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack.
54 offsuit in UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest hands in poker, and from early position with 5 players behind me, there's no value in playing this.

J♠8♠ is a playable, suited hand with good postflop equity but not a premium. With deep stacks (≈207 BB) and no raises yet, I want to take the initiative and avoid limp-heavy, multiway pots that reduce my fold equity. A standard 3x open (300 total) is the right mix — it’s large enough to narrow the field and gain fold equity against the blinds but keeps the pot manageable if called. If I face a significant 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold vs a large 3-bet; consider calling vs a small 3-bet to play postflop in position). Postflop plan: c-bet when I hit or when board texture favors a semi-bluff, play straightforward when checked to, and use position to extract value or fold to strong resistance.

Facing a 3bb open from a large-stack MP, 4♠2♠ is too weak to defend profitably: it has poor raw equity versus a reasonably strong opening range, is frequently dominated when it makes a pair, and the call is further penalized by the squeeze risk from the Button and blinds behind. With no need to take a marginal, high-variance spot, I preserve stack and wait for better steal/3-bet candidates.

I hold 9d 6s on the Button, which is a very weak off-suit hand. Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from Middle Position. Despite having position, my hand has very poor playability and equity against an opening range. There is no strategic merit in calling or 3-betting with this holding at this stage. Folding is the only standard play.

I am holding King-trey offsuit in the small blind. Minni, who is the chip leader and has been quite active, raised to 300 from middle position. K3o is a weak hand to play out of position against a raise, especially against a deep stack. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack for better spots.

Facing an isolation raise to 300 from Minni in middle position after folds around. Pot is 450, calling 200 more (total 300 committed) offers excellent implied odds with ~3.5:1 direct odds. K9o is a standard BB defending hand vs MP open: decent high-card strength, blocks KK/AA/KQ, and equity vs typical 15-20% MP range (~42% equity raw). OOP but deep stacks (68BB), will realize well when hitting TP/TK or draws. Folding too tight in BB; 3-bet risks vs aggressive Minni (20k stack) with marginal hand. Plan: call, check/call/lead flops with hits, fold to c-bets on misses.

Heads-up OOP vs Minni's MP raise after defending wide pre-flop as planned. Flop Qd8hTc completely misses my K9o: no pair, no straight draw (Q-T-8 board doesn't connect with K9), no flush draw, pure king-high air. Her range hits this board decently (Qx, Tx, 88+, AJs+, broadways) while also having straight draws (J9, 97). As per pre-flop plan, this is a miss so check-fold to her likely c-bet (high frequency from her history). No fold equity to donk OOP with zero equity/showdown; preserve stack for better spots deep in tournament. Her massive 20k stack adds no pressure to bluff.

I opened to 300 preflop and got one caller (heads-up). The flop Q♦ 8♥ T♣ gives me middle pair (8s) with a Jack kicker and a gutshot to a 9. This is a hand with decent showdown value but vulnerable to Qx/Tx and overpairs — with position and initiative I should c-bet to extract value from worse pairs and charge/force folds from many missed hands in the BB’s wide calling range. I’ll make a half-pot continuation bet (325) — large enough to get folds and charge draws, but small enough to keep worse hands and floats in. Plan: if called, control the pot on the turn and re-evaluate; fold to a large raise since my hand is often behind vs strong resistance.

Facing Minni's flop c-bet of 325 into 975 (~1/3 pot) on Qd8hTc board with Kd9h (king-high, no pair, no straight draw as K doesn't connect to Q-T-8 rainbow board, gutshot unlikely vs her range, backdoor FH poss but negligible). Her MP open range (~15-20%: Qx+, T9s+, 88+, AJs+, broadways) hits this coordinated board medium strength frequently (top pair Qx/Tx, sets, str draws J9/97, OESD J9). As BB defender OOP with marginal K9o preflop (defended for implied/multiway poss, now heads-up), this is a textbook fold spot per plan: zero showdown equity, <30% equity vs her polarized c-bet range (value + bluffs, but bluffs less on this wet board). Pot odds ~3:1 but reverse implied terrible if called and she checks back worse or bets turn; folding preserves 6625 stack (66BB deep) for superior spots vs her 20k monster but no need to bluff-catch air. Minni's history shows aggression but c-bets flops consistently; check-fold optimal to avoid spew.

2♦5♥ offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands and should be folded UTG in a 6-handed game. I'm first to act with a 150 pot and 100 to call (min-raise to 200). Calling out of position or open-raising this hand is negative EV: it has very poor equity against any reasonable calling/raising ranges and will be dominated postflop. Several opponents have shown early aggression, so risking chips with a junk hand is unnecessary. With a large stack I should conserve chips and wait for a much better spot in position with a real hand. Fold.

With 6-handed action folded to me in middle position, Ah6d is good enough to open at a mixed frequency: it has decent high-card value, an ace blocker vs opponents’ 3-bets, and plays better with initiative than as a limp/call. I’ll use a standard small open to pressure the blinds and keep risk controlled; if faced with a significant 3-bet from later position, I can comfortably fold this offsuit A-x.

I hold 7c 6h in the Cutoff facing a raise to 2.5 BB from FiveTwo in Middle Position. 76 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly against a standard MP opening range. Even though I have position on the opener, there are three players left to act behind me who could squeeze. The hand lacks sufficient equity and playability to justify a call or a bluff 3-bet. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am on the button with 6s 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from middle position. Even though I have the positional advantage, this hand is not strong enough to call a raise or to attempt a 3-bet bluff against a middle-position open. Folding is the only sensible play to preserve my stack.

KQs Kd is a strong offsuit broadway hand with good equity, top pair/top kicker potential, and straight potential. Facing an isolation raise from FiveTwo in middle position after UTG fold, with button folded, this is a good spot to 3-bet for value and to take initiative OOP. FiveTwo has shown aggression in history but also folds to pressure. Sizing to 900 (3.6x the raise) balances value and fold equity while keeping pot manageable with ~65BB stacks. Calling OOP is suboptimal with this hand; folding too tight.
T2o is a very weak hand facing a 3-bet. I'd need to call 800 into a 1250 pot, and I'll be out of position with a hand that has terrible playability. Easy fold.

Elon’s small-blind 3-bet to 900 is a normal sizing and, even with position and an ace blocker, Ah6d performs poorly versus a typical SB 3-bet range (dominated A-x, reverse implied odds when we make top pair). The price (650 to win 1250) looks tempting, but A6o won’t realize equity well and can lead to expensive second-best spots. A 4-bet bluff would need a very high fold frequency and is risky at this stack depth. Best is to stick to the plan and preserve stack.

6-handed but still first to act with a weak offsuit 8-5. From UTG this hand performs poorly: it’s frequently dominated when it makes a pair, has limited straight potential, and limping invites multiway pots with bad playability and reverse implied odds. With ~92 BB effective there’s no need to force action—best is to preserve stack and wait for better open candidates.

I hold KQo in Middle Position with 92 BBs. After the UTG player folds, I have a strong broadway hand suitable for opening. KQo plays well as an opener to thin the field and build a pot. A standard open raise is the best play here to deny equity to random hands in the blinds and gain initiative. I will raise to 2.5x BB (250), which is a standard size in this tournament structure.

I am holding Queen-Jack suited (Qh Jh) in the Cutoff, which is a very strong broadway hand with excellent post-flop playability. Pro raised to 2.5bb from Middle Position. While I could 3-bet to take initiative, calling is a very standard and effective play here. It allows me to see a flop with a hand that flops well while keeping the pot size manageable and maintaining position on the initial raiser for the rest of the hand. If Elon (Button) or the blinds call, the hand still performs well in multi-way pots due to its straight and flush potential.

Pocket kings is one of the best starting hands in poker, especially on the button with position on two players. Pro raised from middle position (likely a wide range including suited connectors, Ax, broadway, pairs), and Flash flat-called from cutoff (could be speculative hands or medium strength). A 3-bet is standard here to build the pot, deny equity to worse hands, and isolate against their ranges. Sizing to 1000 total (4x the open raise, considering the call) puts good pressure while leaving room for further action post-flop. Effective stacks allow this without overcommitting. Folding or calling would be too passive with this premium.

Pocket 7s facing a 3-bet to 1000 from Elon on the button. With 5300 stack and needing to call 950, I'm getting about 2.7:1 pot odds. While set mining typically wants 15-20x implied odds, there's potential for more callers (Pro/Flash already in), which improves implied odds. If we hit our set, we can extract significant value. 77 doesn't play well post-flop without hitting, but the multiway nature and decent pot odds make calling reasonable for set mining.

I’m in the big blind with pocket deuces facing a button 3‑bet to 1,000 after a 250 open and a call. I’ve already got 100 in; calling 900 (1000 total) is cheap relative to the pot (pot 2,600) and my 20,925 stack, so this is a set‑mining spot vs. one (or two) opponents with ~60bb effective stacks. Immediate pot odds (~2.9:1) aren’t enough on their own, but implied odds with these stack depths justify a call; a 4‑bet or shove with 22 is a leak and folding loses profitable set‑mining opportunities. Plan: call now, proceed cautiously on the flop (check‑fold to heavy action on unfavorable boards, extract value or slowplay if I flop a set).

I opened to 2.5bb from MP with KQo and faced a call, a 3-bet from the button, and two cold calls from the blinds. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 750 to win a pot of over 4000), KQo performs poorly in multi-way 3-bet pots. I am likely dominated by hands like AK, AQ, KK, or QQ which are well within the ranges of the 3-bettor or the cold-callers. Additionally, playing out of position relative to the 3-bettor and having the initial caller (Flash) still to act behind me creates a spot with significant reverse implied odds. The disciplined play is to release the hand rather than committing to a low SPR pot with a easily dominated holding.

I am holding QhJh in the Cutoff. After my initial call of Pro's raise, Elon 3-bet to 1000 from the Button. Both blinds (Claude and Minni) cold-called the 1000, and Pro folded. The pot currently stands at 3500, and it costs me 750 more to call. I am getting roughly 4.6-to-1 on a call (17.6% equity needed). QhJh is an excellent hand for multi-way pots because it flops well-defined draws and strong top pairs. Given the massive pot odds and the high implied odds if I hit a straight or flush against three opponents, calling to see a flop is the most profitable decision. Raising further (4-betting) would be too risky against multiple opponents who have shown strength by calling a 3-bet.

I have pocket 7s on a 4c Js 5d board in a 4-way pot. My pair is only an overpair to the 4 and 5, but underpair to the Jack. In this multiway pot, someone likely has a Jack or better. Betting into 3 opponents with a vulnerable middle pair is risky - I'll mostly get called or raised by hands that beat me. Better to check and see how the action develops. If checked through, I may have the best hand. If someone bets, I can evaluate based on sizing.

I called the 3‑bet preflop as a set‑mining spot. The flop (4c Js 5d) missed my 22 — I have only an underpair and no meaningful draw, and there are multiple players who put in big preflop action (the original 3‑better on the button plus callers). In a multiway pot vs strong preflop ranges, betting as a bluff is low EV (will often be called or raised) and slowplaying is impossible because I don’t have a made hand to extract value. I want to control the pot size and see how the players who showed strength react. Standard, low‑risk line: check and fold to significant aggression; consider calling a very small probe if the price is right, but not lead or raise here.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen kicker (Qh Jh) on a 4c-Js-5d board. This was a 3-bet pot pre-flop with four players, which is a very heavy-weight situation. Both the Small Blind (Claude) and Big Blind (Minni) have checked. Since Elon was the pre-flop aggressor (3-better) and has position on me, checking is the standard and most strategic play. It allows me to see how the original raiser acts and prevents me from bloating the pot against potentially stronger ranges (like AJ, AA, KK, or QQ). My hand is a strong 'catch-all' that can comfortably call a continuation bet from the Button, but it is not strong enough to lead out into three opponents in a 3-bet pot.

Pocket Kings remain very strong post-flop on this 4c-Js-5d board, an overpair to the jack-high texture with no flush draws completed and limited straight draws (like 36s or 23). Multiway pot after our preflop 3-bet, but all three opponents (Claude SB, Minni BB, Flash CO) checked, indicating weakness in their defending ranges, which likely include Ax suited, lower pairs, suited connectors that missed, and some Jx. In position on the button, this is a prime spot for a continuation bet to extract value from top pair hands (Jx), weaker pairs, and any draws while protecting our hand and denying equity to floaters. Pot is 4250, betting around half-pot (2100-2200) balances value and fold equity without overbetting into deep Minni stacks, keepingSPR favorable (~2:1). Checking would be too passive, risking free cards to overcards/draws and missing value. Standard play to fire here with premium strength.

Facing a 2200 bet from Elon (who 3-bet pre) into 3 opponents on J54 board. My 77 is a weak underpair to the Jack. Elon's aggression into multiple players suggests strength - likely an overpair or Jx. Calling commits half my stack (2200 of 4350) with a hand that's rarely good and has only 2 outs when behind. Getting ~3:1 isn't enough when my equity is so poor against his value range. Plus Minni/Flash still to act behind. Disciplined fold.

I set‑mined preflop with 22 and planned to check the flop and fold to significant aggression. The flop (4♣ J♠ 5♦) missed me completely. Elon (who showed preflop strength) now bets 2,200 into a 6,450 pot — calling would cost 2,200 to win 8,650 (pot odds ≈ 25.4%). My chance to improve to a set by the river is only about 8.4% (2 outs over two streets), well below the break‑even. This is also a multiway spot and Elon’s line is consistent with a strong/value‑heavy range; calling just to chase a set here is negative EV and leaves me vulnerable to turn pressure or a shove. I preserve my deep stack for better set‑mining or value spots and fold.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen kicker (Qh Jh) on a relatively dry board (4c-Js-5d). After three players checked to the pre-flop aggressor (Elon), he placed a continuation bet of 2200 into a 4250 pot. With both blinds folding, it is now head-up. While Elon's 3-bet range pre-flop is strong and contains hands like AA, KK, QQ, and AJ, folding top pair here would be too exploitable. The pot odds (2200 to win 8650) are approximately 3.9-to-1, meaning I need about 20% equity to justify a call. My hand currently beats his potential bluffs or semi-bluffs (AK, AQ, KQ) and some worse Jacks (KJ, JT). Given the low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR), I am likely committed to the hand, but calling is the most appropriate action to evaluate the turn and keep his bluffs in.

I have significantly improved my hand on the turn, hitting three-of-a-kind (trips) with a Queen kicker. The board is now 4c-Js-5d-Jd. Given that Elon was the pre-flop 3-bettor and fired a large bet on the flop, his range is heavily weighted towards overpairs like AA, KK, and QQ, or high-card hands like AK/AQ. There is only one Jack left in the deck (Jc), making it highly improbable that he has me beat with a hand like AJ. With the pot at 8650 and Elon having only 3775 remaining, the stack-to-pot ratio is extremely low (0.4). By checking, I am playing my strong hand deceptively, allowing him to continue his aggression with overpairs or bluffs. I am fully committed to the pot and will call any bet he makes or move all-in if the action returns to me.

Pocket Kings remain an overpair on a paired Jack board (Js/Jd/5d/4c), which improves our equity against bluffs and medium-strength hands while blocking sets of kings. Flash's line—flat preflop vs 3-bet multiway, check-call large flop cbet on jack-high dry board, then check turn—suggests a capped range: likely top pair (AJ/KQ/JT), stubborn overpairs (TT/99/88), or bricked draws that check-folded would have done already. The jack pairing is good for thin value against worse (e.g., non-jack pairs, ace-high), as villains without J often can't fold. Checking back risks giving free river cards or uncontested pot from weak holdings, missing value in position. Pot 8650, my remaining stack 3775 (SPR ~0.44:1), shoving commits fully but extracts max from calling range while denying equity. Flash has 5000 left; he'll call worse often enough for +EV. Standard value shove with premium strength deep in tournament.

I have improved to three-of-a-kind (trips) with a Queen kicker. The board is 4c-Js-5d-Jd. Elon, the pre-flop 3-bettor, has moved all-in for 3775 into a pot of 8650.
