My hand is extremely strong in this context. While Elon's 3-bet range includes hands like AA, KK, QQ, and AJ, I am currently beating all his overpairs and bluffs. The only realistic hand that beats me is AJ (or KJ, though less likely to 3-bet), as JJ for quads is highly improbable with only one Jack remaining in the deck. Given the pot odds (3775 to win a total pot that will be over 16,000) and the strength of my hand, this is an automatic call. I am committed to the pot and have the best hand the vast majority of the time.

I am in UTG position with J8o (Jack of Spades, Eight of Clubs) in a 5-handed game. My stack is roughly 90 big blinds. J8o is a very weak hand that is not suitable for opening from early position, even at a short-handed table. There is no strategic merit in limping or raising with this hand given the stack depths and positional disadvantage. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I am holding 7-3 offsuit in UTG+1, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given my early position and the lack of any potential for this hand to improve significantly or dominate others, the most mathematically sound and disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Th9h on the button is a solid hand - suited connector with good playability. With only the blinds left to act after two folds, this is a great spot to raise for value and position. Standard button open to 2.5x.

Facing a button raise to 250 while I’m in the small blind with J♥5♣. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more. J5o is a weak, easily dominated hand with poor playability out of position against a button raiser (and I’ll still have the big blind acting after me). With deep stacks I don’t need to gamble with a marginal hand — 3-betting is a thin bluff and calling puts me in tough multi-street OOP spots against likely c-bets/3-bets. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position.

Button opens to 2.5bb and it folds to us in the BB. Ks3s is a standard defend: we’re getting a good price (150 to win 400) and the hand has decent playability/realization with suitedness and a king. A 3-bet here would be a fairly thin bluff versus a 41bb stack that can jam/call, putting us in awkward spots OOP, so the higher-EV line is to defend and play postflop.

We’re heads-up OOP versus the Button raiser on a K♣T♠7♣ flop with K♠3♠ (top pair, weak kicker, no club). This is a classic medium-strength hand that doesn’t love building a big pot via donk-betting: worse hands often fold, better Kx continues, and we give draws a clean pricing decision. Checking keeps our range protected (we have plenty of strong hands in BB defend), allows Claude to c-bet a wide range (including bluffs and worse Tx/7x), and lets us respond based on his sizing—primarily check-calling reasonable bets to realize equity and control pot size against a short-ish 41bb stack.

I flopped middle pair with Th on a Ts7cKc board. FiveTwo checked to me. I have position and a decent made hand. The board has a club flush draw. I should bet for value and protection - around 65% pot to charge draws and get value from worse pairs like 7x or underpairs.

Claude c-bets 350 into 900 (~39% pot) on Kc Ts 7c after we defend BB with K♠3♠. This is a standard check-call: we have top pair and are well ahead of his air and many Tx/7x hands, while a raise mostly gets action from better Kx (KQ/KJ/KT) and strong draws, and risks facing a jam from his ~37.5bb stack in a spot where our kicker is poor and we have no club to reduce his flush-draw continues. Calling keeps his bluffs in, controls pot size OOP, and lets us re-evaluate on turns (especially clubs, broadways, and runouts that increase his value density).

Turn Q♦ is a key range card for the Button: it improves a lot of his continuing flop bets (KQ/QT/QJ/AQ) and also completes J9 to a straight. With K♠3♠ (top pair, weak kicker, no club), our hand is now more of a bluff-catcher/medium-strength showdown hand that doesn’t want to inflate the pot OOP versus a ~3 SPR stack.

Leading here doesn’t deny much (his club draws and strong made hands continue anyway) and risks getting raised/jammed by a value-heavy range where we’re in bad shape. Checking keeps our range protected (we can still have strong hands like KQ/sets/straights), allows him to keep bluffing, and lets us respond based on sizing (generally continue versus small/medium bets, get cautious versus large/shove lines).

Turn brought Qd making board Ts7cKcQd. Now there's a broadway-heavy board where any J makes a straight. I have middle pair with Th and a gutshot to the nut straight (J gives me AKQJTs). FiveTwo called flop and checked again. With a medium-strength hand on an increasingly coordinated board, checking back for pot control makes sense. I can still catch value or bluff-catch on the river.

River is the 2♣, completing the front-door club flush while not improving our K♠3♠. Given the turn went check-check (a lot of his medium-strength hands/pot-control lines), our hand is now primarily a bluff-catcher with a very weak kicker and no club blocker. Betting is unlikely to get called by much worse (many Tx/7x/weak Qx fold, and better continues include stronger Kx, straights like J9, and now any made flush), and it risks value-owning ourselves or getting raised off our equity. Checking keeps the pot controlled and lets us realize our showdown value versus his missed bluffs/air, while allowing us to respond if he chooses to stab.

River completed the club flush (Ts7cKcQd2c). I have middle pair with tens. FiveTwo has check-called flop and checked turn/river. Their line suggests either a marginal made hand or a missed draw. My pair of tens has showdown value but the board is very dangerous - flush possible, J makes a straight. Betting thin for value risks only getting called by hands that beat me (any K, Q, flush, straight). Better to check back and show down my medium-strength hand.

I am in the UTG position with 5d 9s, which is a very weak starting hand. In a 5-handed game, this hand is well below the range of hands that should be played from early position. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
T2o is a very weak hand in UTG+1 position. With only 37.5 BBs and such poor holdings, this is a clear fold. No reason to get involved with this hand from early position.

I'm on the button with K3o (K♥3♣). UTG and UTG+1 folded, so only the SB and BB remain — a great spot to open-steal. K3 is marginal but acceptable on the button; I want initiative and to make the blinds pay to see a flop. I'll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300) to apply pressure and isolate. If I face a sizable 3-bet I'll fold this holding; if I'm called I'll C-bet on favorable textures and play cautiously when I miss.

Button is opening a wide range at 3bb, and we’re out of position in the SB. 6♠5♠ is a good candidate to 3-bet as a semi-bluff: it has solid playability when called (suited, connected, can make strong disguised hands), generates fold equity versus Minni’s steals, and helps deny Pro in the BB a cheap overcall/squeeze spot. If Minni 4-bets large, we can comfortably fold.

I am in the Big Blind with K3o. The Button opened to 300 and the Small Blind 3-bet to 1200. This is a significant show of strength or a squeeze play. My hand is extremely weak (King-high, poor kicker, offsuit), and I would be cold-calling a 3-bet out of position, potentially sandwiched if the Button calls. The pot odds do not justify a call, and the hand has very low playability. Folding is the only standard and correct play.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♥3♣ and FiveTwo (SB) responded with a 3-bet to 1,200. That means I must call 900 more into a 1,600 pot — I need roughly 36% equity to justify a call. K3o is badly dominated by the SB’s 3‑betting range (Kx, Ax, broadways, pairs) and has poor playability out of position. A 4‑bet bluff is possible only with much stronger blockers (A/K) or a polarized range; K3 is a weak candidate and risks a costly 5‑bet or being called by better Kx/AK. I’ll fold, preserve a deep ~197BB stack, and wait for a better spot to use my position/stack advantage.

J9o from UTG is too weak to open. With 37.5BB stack and being one of the shorter stacks at the table, I should play tight from early position. J9o is outside a standard UTG opening range - folding here.

T8o is a marginal but playable hand in a 5-handed game — my opening ranges should be much wider than at a full table. Folding or limping here surrenders initiative and invites multiway pots out of position (button acts after me), so I want to take the lead and build fold equity. A standard 3x sized open (300 total) balances getting the blinds to fold while keeping the pot controllable if I get called. Plan: if I get one or two callers I'll continuation-bet appropriate flops (any ace/ten/club/connected textures I can continue on); if I face a sizable 3-bet I will likely fold T8o (or detach and re-evaluate vs a small 3-bet depending on sizing and villain). Stack is deep enough that a 300 open is a small, high-expected-value investment to either take the blinds or play a defined pot.

Minni’s 3bb open from UTG+1 (5-handed) represents a reasonably strong/competent range, and 8h5d offsuit has poor equity and realization even on the button (dominated when we make a pair, few strong draws). The price isn’t good enough to justify a defend versus this sizing, and a 3-bet bluff is unnecessary with stacks deep and better candidates available. Preserve stack and wait for higher EV spots in position.

I hold A6o in the Small Blind facing a 3bb raise from Minni in UTG+1. Minni has been playing aggressively, often raising, but has also folded to 3-bets recently. However, A6o is a weak hand to play out of position, easily dominated by Minni's opening range (better Aces, pairs). Calling is generally a losing play due to reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage. A 3-bet bluff is risky against an early position open without a stronger read or better blockers/playability. With a healthy stack of 88bb, there is no need to force action in a marginal spot. The standard and most prudent decision is to fold.

I am in the big blind with 7h 3h. Minni, who has been quite active and currently has the largest stack at the table, raised from early position to 300. While the pot odds are roughly 3:1 to call, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against a strong opponent. With 105 hands remaining and a healthy stack, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand.

Short-handed (5 players) I should open up my UTG range — K♣T♦ is a standard open in 5‑max. Table norm is a ~3x BB open and my stack is deep (≈198bb), so I want enough sizing to get heads‑up and to exert fold equity without committing too much. Raising to 300 (3bb) matches prior open sizes at this table, discourages limp‑calls, and gives me position leverage if folded to the button/ blinds. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold KTo out of position; versus a single call I’ll c‑bet on dry flops and play for value when I hit top pair or good draws. I won’t limp; calling 100 is too passive and misses opportunity to steal the blinds and define ranges.

We’re facing a UTG open to 3bb from Minni, who has shown a fairly active raising strategy. From UTG+1, 3c2h has extremely poor playability (dominated when it makes a pair, no real high-card value, weak straight potential) and performs badly versus an UTG range. Flatting is -EV and a 3-bet bluff from this early configuration is unnecessarily high risk with no relevant blockers and decent stacks behind. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with Tc 7s, a weak offsuit hand. Minni, the chip leader, has opened to 300 (3bb) from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. My hand has poor playability and is likely dominated by Minni's opening range. With no prior investment in the pot and facing an early position raise, the correct play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Minni opened from UTG to 3BB (300). J8o is a weak hand to play out of position against an UTG open, which typically represents a stronger range. Calling would put me in a difficult spot for the rest of the hand, and raising is not advisable with this hand against an early position raiser. Therefore, the standard professional play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Ad3h in the BB facing a min-raise from UTG (Minni) to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, getting about 2.25:1 odds. A3o is marginal against an UTG open, but the pot odds are decent and I have an ace. I'll call and see a flop - if I don't connect well, I can fold to aggression.

I flopped the nut flush draw with Ad on 8d 2s 9d. Minni checked to me. With 9 outs to the nuts (~36% equity by river), this is a good semi-bluff spot. I can win if they fold or improve to the best hand if called. Betting around half pot to build the pot with my draw while applying pressure.

I'm the preflop raiser (UTG) with K♣T♦ on a fairly dry, two‑diamond flop 8♦‑2♠‑9♦. Claude (BB, ~31bb) defended my open and now leads for 325 into a 650 pot (pot now 975). Calling 325 requires ~25% equity to be +EV. With two overcards and 6 clean cards to pair (Ks/Ts) KTo has roughly that order of equity vs a plausible defending range of pairs, broadways and floats. Raising here would force a decision that can easily turn into a shove from a short‑stack and I have no pair or reliable draw to justify committing chips; folding would be too nitty given the pot odds. Plan: call to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn — if I pair (K or T) I’ll bet for value; if the turn bricks and he fires again big, I can fold and control loss; if he checks I can take a free card or apply pressure on later streets. Therefore I call the 325.

Turn brought 4s, a blank. I still have the nut flush draw with Ad (9 outs, ~18% to hit on river). Minni called my flop bet, indicating some hand strength - likely a pair or their own draw. With my short stack (3125) and only one card to come, checking to see a free river makes sense. If I hit my flush, I can value bet; if I miss, I can reassess.

I’m the preflop aggressor with KcTd on 8♦-2♠-9♦-4♠. I called Claude’s small flop raise to realize equity; on the turn he checks and the pot is 1300 while he’s relatively short (~31bb). I have two overcards but no pair and no meaningful draw. Betting here risks either getting shoved by a short stack (which I can’t profitably call with just overcards) or getting called by any pair/9/8 that already beats me. Pot‑control is the prudent route: check back, see a free river and preserve stack depth to extract value if I hit a K/T or bluff later if he shows further weakness. If he bets the river big I can fold; if he checks I can consider a delayed bluff or a value bet when I improve. Therefore: check.

Missed my flush draw completely. I have just Ace high on a board of 8d 2s 9d 4s 7h. Minni called my flop bet showing some strength. Bluffing here is risky - if they called with any pair, they're not folding. The 7h completed some straights (65, T6) but also helped nothing for me. Checking is best - I might win at showdown against busted draws, but betting would just light chips on fire.

Board: 8♦-2♠-9♦-4♠-7♥. I’m holding Kc Td after calling Claude’s small flop stab (325). Turn checked by both and river is checked back — pot is 1,300 and Claude is relatively short (~3,125). I have no pair and no meaningful draw; Claude’s flop lead suggests he often has a made hand (pair or better) or at least good showdown value. Betting here would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity and exposes me to a shove from his short stack that I can’t profitably call with just K‑high. There’s also little value to extract since worse hands that would call my bet still beat me (pairs of 2/4/7/9/8), and hands that fold now already conceded the pot. Pot control and preserving my deep stack are preferable. Therefore I check and go to showdown.

4♣2♦ is the weakest offsuit holding with terrible playability and equity realization from UTG, especially 5-handed where we’ll face frequent 3-bets (Flash on the button) and defenses (Minni in the BB). Limping creates dominated multiway spots; opening as a steal is too loose given likely resistance and poor postflop prospects. Preserve stack for better steal spots in later position.

I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with Ace-Three offsuit (A3o) in a 5-handed game. While the table is short-handed, A3o is a marginal hand to open, especially with two large stacks (Flash and Minni) behind me who can apply pressure. My stack is healthy at 88 BBs, so there is no need to force a steal with a weak ace that plays poorly post-flop and is dominated by most calling ranges. Folding is the prudent long-term play.

I am on the button with 6h 3s, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is a potential stealing opportunity since the UTG and UTG+1 players have folded, the hand strength is too low to justify a raise, especially with a short-stacked player in the Small Blind who may be looking to shove and an active Big Blind. The optimal play here is to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads up vs BB with 8h5d. Very weak hand but I'm getting 3:1 odds to complete. With only needing to call 50 more into a 150 pot, completing is reasonable to see a flop cheaply. Folding just gives up equity, and raising with this hand seems too aggressive against Minni who has shown willingness to 3-bet.

Claude (SB) just limped — likely a wide, relatively weak range. In the big blind with A♠2♥ I want to isolate and take the initiative rather than play a marginal spot multiway. A standard isolation raise here earns fold equity and leverages the ace-blocker; I’ll make a firm but non-committal size to keep the pot manageable and induce folds/calls rather than a big shove. Raising to 700 (total) accomplishes that: it pressures the limp, gives Claude tough odds to call with many junk hands, and keeps postflop play straightforward (c-bet favorable A-high or dry boards, proceed cautiously on coordinated boards). If Claude 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold unless it’s a small-sized 3-bet), but versus a single call I can play for value and fold equity.

Holding 8h5d - a very weak offsuit hand. Minni raised to 700 after my SB completion. I need to call 600 into an 800 pot, getting about 1.33:1 odds. Against a BB raising range, 85o has poor equity (~30%) and I'm out of position. This is an easy fold - the hand is too weak to continue.

I am in UTG position at a 5-handed table with Queen-Six offsuit (Qc 6s). This is a very weak hand that falls well outside of a standard opening range, even in a short-handed game. My stack is healthy at 88 big blinds, so there is no pressure to make loose aggressive moves. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I have been dealt pocket Kings, the second-strongest starting hand in poker. I am in UTG+1. Pro has folded. I need to raise to build the pot and extract value from worse hands while limiting the field. A standard open raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to balance my range and induce action from opponents with wide ranges in the blinds or the button.

Q3o on the button facing a 3x raise from UTG+1. This is a weak hand that doesn't play well against an early position raise. Q3o lacks connectivity and is dominated by many hands in Flash's opening range. With 43BB stack, no need to get involved with marginal holdings. Easy fold.

I’m in the small blind with 6♣3♥ facing an early-position open to 300 (3x). This is a very weak, easily-dominated hand with poor playability out of position. Calling 250 to see a flop puts me OOP against a competent raiser and likely leads to difficult multi-street decisions; a light 3-bet bluff isn’t necessary here given the opener’s stack and table dynamics. Preserve my deep stack for a better spot — fold.

We’re getting a good price: calling 200 more to play a pot that will be 650 gives ~31% required equity. K4 suited is weak but playable as a BB defend versus a late-position/5-handed open; it has decent equity, can make nut-ish flushes, and is easier to realize in a single-raised pot than by bloating OOP with a marginal 3-bet bluff. Plan is to check most flops and continue mainly with pairs, strong draws, or good backdoors.

We defended BB and hit this A-high, Broadway-heavy flop very poorly: no pair, no gutshot, and only a backdoor club draw (need two clubs). Against the preflop raiser, this board favors their range and they will c-bet frequently; we don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with near-zero equity. Best is to check and evaluate—mostly folding to meaningful pressure, continuing only if we pick up real equity on later streets.

I have pocket Kings, which was a very strong hand pre-flop, but the flop has come with an Ace. As the pre-flop aggressor, I am in a position where the Big Blind (FiveTwo) has checked to me. While my hand is still strong as an overpair to the J and 8, the presence of the Ace is a major concern as it is well within the Big Blind's defending range. Betting here might value-cut me against an Ace or a set of Jacks or Eights, while checking allows me to control the size of the pot and see how the opponent reacts on the turn. This check also serves to protect my checking range with a hand that has significant showdown value.

We picked up a real hand on the turn: K♣4♣ now has a strong club flush draw (9 outs) on a board where the flop checked through. The 7♣ is a good card for the BB defending range (more 7x/two-pair/straight combos), so we can credibly represent improved hands and put immediate pressure on Flash’s missed broadways/pocket pairs while also building a pot for when we hit our flush. A medium donk size also charges heart draws and denies a free river when Flash may check back too often after checking flop.

On the turn, the Seven of Clubs appears, and FiveTwo leads out with a bet of 400 into a 650 pot after the flop went check-check. My pocket Kings are still a very strong hand, though the Ace on the board is the primary concern. However, checking the flop often induces bluffs or 'protection' bets from the Big Blind with hands like a Jack, an Eight, or various draws (straight draws like T9 or flush draws). Folding here would be too weak given the strength of my hand, and raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (Ax, sets, or two pair). Calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable and evaluate the river, while still getting to showdown or potentially catching a bluff.

River is a complete brick for our actual hand (Kc4c misses clubs; no pair/straight), so checking gives us almost no way to win at showdown and risks facing a value/protection bet from Flash. The 9s is, however, a very credible barrel card for our turn donk range: we can represent QT (made straight), some T6s/Tx that improved, and two-pair/sets that continue betting for value. Flash’s line (check flop, call turn) contains a lot of medium-strength one-pair hands (Jx/8x/7x, underpairs) and missed heart draws that should fold to real pressure. I’ll fire a strong but not insane sizing (~3/4 pot) to maximize fold equity while risking a manageable amount; if raised, we just fold.

I started with pocket Kings, a premium hand, but the board has become extremely unfavorable. An Ace appeared on the flop, which is a major part of the Big Blind's (FiveTwo) defending range. After both of us checked the flop, FiveTwo led out on the turn and then increased their bet to 1100 on the river. The board texture is now [Ac, 8h, Jh, 7c, 9s], which is highly coordinated; any Ten now makes a straight, and the Ace remains a constant threat. FiveTwo's line (leading turn, large river bet) suggests a strong value hand like an Ace, a set, or a straight. While my check on the flop might have induced some bluffs, the river bet is too large to comfortably call with what is now essentially a bluff-catcher. I will preserve my stack and fold.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with a very weak hand (3d 5h). In a 5-handed game, this hand has almost no value and is not worth playing, especially from early position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

K8o from UTG+1 is a marginal hand. With 4 players left to act and being one of the shorter stacks at 43BB, this hand is below my opening range from early position. K8o has poor playability and is often dominated by better kings. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I’m on the button with 8h4s — a weak, marginal hand, but I have position and both UTG players folded, so I should attempt a standard button steal. The min-raise to 200 is allowed, but opening to 3x (300) is the table-standard sizing here and gives better fold equity versus the SB/BB while still being a small risk relative to my ~192bb stack. Plan: raise to 300 to take the initiative; fold to a sizable 3-bet given my poor holding; if called I’ll play postflop in position and proceed cautiously, c-betting only favorable textures or checking back when behind. This maximizes EV from late position while keeping downside limited.

We’re 5-handed facing a BTN open, getting a reasonable price (250 to win 450 already in there) and 97o has enough raw equity/straight potential to defend sometimes. A 3-bet bluff here isn’t attractive (no blockers, poor playability if called, and we’d bloat the pot OOP). Folding is fine at higher open sizes, but at this price I’ll defend and play cautiously postflop, especially avoiding paying off dominated one-pair spots.

I hold Pocket Eights (8c, 8d) in the Big Blind. The Button (Minni) raised to 300 and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) called. I need to call 200 chips into a pot of 700, giving me 3.5:1 pot odds. This is an easy call based on implied odds for set mining (my stack is 87bb) and the fact that 88 is ahead of a wide Button opening range. A 3-bet squeeze is an option but risks bloating the pot out of position against a deep-stacked aggressive opener and a caller. Flat calling closes the action and allows me to realize my equity relatively cheaply.

We’re 3‑way and out of position with a medium-strength made hand (top pair, weak kicker) on a board that can generate pressure (hearts, overcards). Leading tends to get called/raised by better 9x/overpairs/sets while folding out a lot of the hands we’d like to keep in (overcards and some backdoors). Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets the button continuation-bet bluffs and worse holdings, and allows us to check-call reasonable sizing while avoiding building a big pot when dominated.

I hold Pocket Eights (8c 8d) on a 9h-2h-5d flop. The pot is 900. The Small Blind has checked. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser (Minni) who is on the Button. Leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal here as it folds out worse hands and builds a pot out of position with a medium-strength hand. My hand is vulnerable to overcards on future streets and the flush draw, but currently likely has good showdown value. I will check and evaluate Minni's action.

I raised preflop and both blinds called, so the pot is 900 and both opponents have checked to me on a 9h‑2h‑5d flop. I hold 8h4s — no pair and only a runner‑runner heart backdoor (need both turn and river hearts) so my equity is essentially zero right now. Against two callers my fold equity with a c‑bet is low and betting would bloat the pot with almost no chance to improve. I'll control the pot and keep my range wide by checking — I can call a small probe on the flop to see a cheap turn, or if checked to on the turn I’ll re‑evaluate and only apply pressure on genuinely favorable runouts (or fold to strong resistance).

We’re 3‑way OOP with a marginal made hand (pair of 9s with a weak kicker). After the flop checked through, both opponents’ ranges are somewhat capped, but the K on the turn is a strong card for the BTN opener (lots of Kx) and also improves some BB continues. If we lead, we risk getting called mostly by better hands (Kx, stronger 9x) while folding out a lot of the hands we’d like to keep in (missed overcards/heart-draw floats) and we open ourselves up to a raise that forces us to fold too often. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets opponents stab with missed hands/draws, and we can consider a call versus reasonable sizing while folding to heavy pressure.

The turn brings a King of Clubs (Kc), making the board 9h-2h-5d-Kc. This is a scare card that hits the Button's (Minni) perceived broadway range better than mine or the SB's. My pocket eights (88) are now 3rd pair on the board (behind K and 9). Since the flop checked through, it is possible no one has a strong hand, but betting here turns my hand into a bluff and likely only folds out hands I am already beating (like A-high or small pairs) while getting called by any K or 9. Checking is the best option to control the pot size and try to get to showdown cheaply. I will check and evaluate based on the Button's action.

I have 8♥4♠ — no pair and essentially zero equity on the turn. I raised preflop but both blinds called and both have checked twice; the K♣ on the turn actually helps many of their cold-call ranges (Kx, Ax, pocket pairs). With two opponents in, a bluff has very poor fold equity and would only bloat the pot with a hand that has no playability. Pot control is the right play: check back the turn, take a free river, and re-evaluate there. If either opponent fires a meaningful bet I will fold (only considering a very tiny probe-call if the price is trivial and the bettor is known to stab light). If checked through to the river I’ll look to the river texture and opponent actions before choosing whether to bluff or concede the pot.

River gives us 9♣7♠ for two pair on a previously very low-action line (flop/turn checked through 3‑way). That caps opponents’ ranges toward a lot of one-pair and ace-high hands that will often check back if we check, so we should lead for value. At the same time, the 3rd heart completes the flush, so we don’t want to use a big sizing that only gets action from flushes/stronger two-pair (K9/K7/sets). A small-to-medium value/block bet targets calls from Kx, 9x, 7x, 5x, and some pocket pairs, while allowing us to comfortably fold if we face a big raise (which is heavily weighted to a flush or better).

The river brings the 7h, putting a third heart and a possible straight (6-8) on the board, alongside the King and Nine. FiveTwo leads out for 400 into a pot of 900. FiveTwo's line (Check-Check-Bet) into two opponents often indicates a made hand, such as a rivered flush, a straight, a King that checked the turn, or a Nine betting for thin value. My pocket eights are effectively a bluff catcher, beating only pure bluffs or smaller pairs like 66/55/22 (though 5 and 2 are on the board). With Minni still to act behind me and my hand losing to a significant portion of FiveTwo's value range (Any K, Any 9, Flushes, Straights), the risk outweighs the reward. I am not getting sufficient odds to call purely as a bluff catcher in a multi-way pot.

I fold. On 9♥‑2♥‑5♦‑K♣‑7♥ my 8♥4♠ has no pair and does NOT make a flush (only four hearts including my 8♥). FiveTwo bets 400 into a 1,300 pot — calling 400 requires ~19% equity to break even, but against their cold‑call/preflop caller range (Kx, 9x, pocket pairs, and any heart for a flush) I have effectively zero showdown equity. The line (checks on flop/turn, small river bet) is consistent with thin value or a disguised made hand rather than a high frequency bluff. I previously planned to control the pot and fold to meaningful river pressure with this holding. No reason to risk chips on a speculative call or bluff‑raise here, so fold and preserve the stack for better spots.
J4o is a very weak hand, especially from UTG position. With 43 BBs, I need to play tight from early position. This is a clear fold - no reason to open raise with such a weak holding.

Fold. J♥8♣ is a weak, unsuited hand in early position (UTG+1) with four players still to act (button + blinds). It plays poorly post‑flop and is likely dominated; limp/calling invites multi‑way pots with little equity and raises from later positions. Preserve my healthy 18950 stack and wait for a better spot or position to open-raise.

Folds to us on the button in a 5-handed game: this is a prime steal spot. Kh6h is comfortably within a button opening range (suited Kx with some postflop playability), and we benefit from position against both blinds. I’ll use a small-but-not-min sizing to pick up the dead money often while keeping risk controlled if we face resistance.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o facing a raise to 250 from the Button (FiveTwo). While K7o has some raw equity against a wide button range, playing this hand out of position is difficult. I would be first to act post-flop against an aggressive opponent. The pot odds (needing to call 200 to win a pot of roughly 600 if BB calls, or 400 current) require decent equity realization, which is hard to achieve OOP with a weak off-suit King. Additionally, the Big Blind is still left to act and could squeeze. Folding is the standard and disciplined play to preserve my stack of ~84 BBs for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 3s, which is a low suited connector. FiveTwo (Button) raised to 250. It costs me 150 more to call into a total pot of 400, giving me pot odds of approximately 2.67 to 1. Against a standard Button opening range, 4s 3s has sufficient equity to justify a call, especially given the stack depths. I will call to see a flop and look for two pair, trips, or draw opportunities, while remaining prepared to fold if the flop doesn't connect.

I have 4s 3s on a 7h 5d 8s flop. This gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 to complete a 3-4-5-6-7 or 4-5-6-7-8 straight) and a backdoor flush draw. As the big blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) and see how they react to this connected board, which can be tricky for a Button opening range. I will check and decide my next move based on their bet sizing.

BB’s check caps him a bit and gives us a great spot to apply pressure in position. This 7-5-8 board hits some of his defending range, but with Kh6h we have a strong open-ended straight draw (4 or 9), plus an overcard and backdoor hearts. A small c-bet will fold out a lot of his pure air/overcards, deny equity, and lets us comfortably continue on many turns (barrel on good cards, take free cards when appropriate).

On a flop of 7h-5d-8s, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 to make a 4-5-6-7-8 straight) with 4s 3s. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor from the button, has made a small continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 550. While the price to call is relatively cheap (250 to win 1050), my equity is low (roughly 8.5% to hit on the turn), and I am out of position. Furthermore, a 6 on the turn would create a very connected board where my straight would not be the 'nut' straight (anyone with a 9 would have a higher straight). Given the lack of additional backdoor equity or showdown value with 4-high, I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

I have Ace of hearts + King of hearts (Ah Kh) — a premium, suited broadway. From UTG in a 5-handed game I want to open with a standard 3x BB to build the pot, get fold equity and narrow the field. The table's typical open is 300, so I raise to 300. Postflop plan: continuation bet on most flops, play aggressively for value when I connect (top pair or nut draws), and be prepared to call a reasonable 3-bet to see a flop given our deep stack; fold to an over-aggressive 4-bet shove without favorable pot odds.

Minni opens UTG to 3bb in a 5-handed game. With A3o from UTG+1 we’re dominated by Minni’s value range (better aces) and have weak playability postflop, especially if we just call and invite players behind to enter. A bluff 3-bet has an ace blocker, but we’d be out of position versus the opener with a hand that realizes equity poorly when called, and our stack depth doesn’t demand high-variance spots here. Best EV is to fold and wait for stronger suited/connected holdings to attack opens.

I am on the Button with 6s 7d (offsuit connector). Minni (UTG) has raised to 300 (3BB). An UTG opening range is typically strong, often containing pairs, high aces, and strong broadways. My hand, while having some connectivity, is offsuit and generally too weak to call a 3x raise profitably, even in position, against an UTG range. The likelihood of being dominated or missing the flop is high, and the reverse implied odds if I hit a pair are poor. 3-betting as a bluff is risky against a deep-stacked UTG opener. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. Minni, who is UTG and holds a similar stack size to mine, has opened with a 3x raise. 10-9 offsuit is too weak to defend from the Small Blind against an early position raise, as I would be playing out of position for the remainder of the hand with a marginal starting hand. The most disciplined and profitable play here is to fold.

Holding 54o in the BB against a UTG raise. This is a very weak hand - offsuit, low cards, minimal playability. Minni opened from UTG which typically indicates a stronger range. I'm out of position for the rest of the hand and getting about 2.25:1 pot odds (call 200 to win 450). With my short stack of 4225, I shouldn't be defending weak hands OOP against early position opens. Easy fold.

5-handed and effectively first to act, AQs is a clear open for value and initiative. It plays well against the blinds’ and button’s calling ranges, dominates many worse Ax/Qx continues, and benefits from fold equity preflop. I’ll use a standard small-variance sizing to keep my range wide while still pressuring the blinds and setting up manageable SPR postflop.

I am in UTG+1 holding A6o. The player in UTG (FiveTwo) has opened to 250 (2.5bb). A6o is a weak hand that is often dominated by a UTG opening range (which typically includes strong Aces like AK-AT and pocket pairs). Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, likely dominated or out of position against 3-bettors behind. 3-betting is also not advisable given the strength of a UTG open and the stack depths (approx 84BB effective). The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold.

I am holding 6c 2d on the button. FiveTwo has opened from UTG with a raise to 250. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop, and even with positional advantage, it is not worth defending against an early position raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

J9o in the SB facing UTG raise. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against an UTG opening range. With Minni still to act behind who could squeeze, and being OOP for the rest of the hand, this is a clear fold.

UTG opened to 250 in a 5‑handed game — that range is fairly strong (pocket pairs, broadways, Axs, suited connectors). Ten‑Five offsuit is weak and often dominated, and I’ll be out of position postflop with poor playability. Calling 150 (total 250 committed) gives only ~27% pot equity required, and I don’t have the equity or blockers to justify a light 3‑bet either. Better to preserve my deep stack for a cleaner spot. Fold and concede the BB.

I am in UTG position (first to act in a 5-handed game) with Jd 7d. While suited, this is a weak hand (one-gapper) that is dominated by many calling ranges (AJ, KJ, QJ, JTs, etc.). In a 5-handed table, UTG is effectively the cutoff/hijack range, but J7s is still too loose to open profitably, especially with 84.5 BBs where I want to avoid reverse implied odds situations out of position. Standard play is to fold.

I am in the UTG+1 position with 98o. This is a very weak hand to play from early position, especially in a five-handed game where opening ranges should remain somewhat disciplined. Looking at the stack sizes, Claude is quite short at about 42BBs, while others are fairly deep. There is no reason to risk chips with a marginal hand like 98o here. Folding is the standard and most profitable play in the long run from this position.

JhTh is a strong suited connector on the button with only blinds left. This is a standard raise spot - good playability with straight and flush potential. With 42 BBs, I'll raise to 250 as a steal attempt.

I’m in the SB with A♣Q♣ facing a button open to 250. AQs is strong enough to 3-bet here: calling 200 keeps me out of position and allows other players to come along, so I prefer to take the initiative and apply pressure to Claude (stack ~3925) while discouraging loose callers from the BB. A raise to 1000 (≈4x the open) is a healthy isolating size that charges Ax/Kx and weaker broadways, gains fold equity, and still leaves plenty of stack behind to play postflop. If Claude folds I take the pot preflop; if the BB calls we play a sizable heads-up pot OOP where AQs has good equity. I’ll re-evaluate if I face a polarized 4-bet shove, but 3-betting to 1000 maximizes EV here.

We’re in the big blind with J3o facing a button open and a small blind 3-bet to 1000. This is a dominated, unplayable hand multiway and out of position in a 3-bet pot. Calling 900 gives poor realization and high reverse implied odds (we make weak jacks/3s that can be crushed), and 4-betting would be burning chips with no relevant blockers or postflop playability. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

JhTh is a playable suited connector facing a 3-bet. I have position on Minni throughout the hand. Pot odds: 750 to win 1350 (36% needed). With implied odds and good playability postflop, calling is reasonable. Folding is too tight, and 4-betting with ~39BB effective doesn't make sense - I'd be committing a large portion of my stack with a speculative hand. Calling to see a flop in position is the best line.

I 3-bet preflop to isolate Claude and keep the initiative. On this flop (8♥ 6♥ 6♣) I have A♣Q♣ — one overcard and no heart, out of position. As the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet a size that folds out missed broadways and charges heart draws while keeping the pot controllable against Claude’s ~3.2k stack. A ~1/2-pot c-bet (1000 into 2100) does that: it puts pressure on his calling range (Kx/Qx, single pairs, some draws) and gets value from worse hands that will continue. If Claude jams over this bet I will give it up — his shove will be heavily value-heavy (sets/overpairs) and AQs has poor equity — so I’m sizing to get folds without overcommitting. If he simply calls I’ll proceed cautiously and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have JhTh with 8h6h6c on board - a flush draw with 9 outs (~35% to hit by river). Pot odds: 1000 to win 3100 = need ~24% equity. My flush draw gives me enough equity to call. With only 3175 stack, jamming as a semi-bluff is too thin - Minni likely has a strong range after 3-betting pre and c-betting a paired board. Calling maintains flexibility and I have direct odds to continue.

I 3-bet pre and c-bet the flop to take the initiative, but on the turn (8♥ 6♥ 6♣ 3♠) with A♣Q♣ out of position I have only two overcards and no heart — roughly 6 outs to improve (~12–13% equity against a single pair). The pot is 4,100 and Claude's stack is only 2,175, so any bet here is likely to induce an all‑in. If he jams for his 2,175 I would need ~25.7% equity to call (2,175 to win ~8,450), which AQs does not have versus a value-heavy calling range (sets/overpairs/one‑pair). Better to control the pot and realize equity: check the turn, fold to a shove, and evaluate/call small river bluffs or thin-value bets only if I pair up. This preserves stack and avoids a negative‑EV shove decision out of position.

Minni checked the turn after betting flop, showing weakness. I have JhTh - a flush draw with 9 outs (~18% to hit river). With only 2175 left and a 4100 pot, betting is essentially all-in. Since I'm getting a free card, I should take it. If I hit my flush, I can extract value. If I miss, I can reevaluate on the river. No need to turn my flush draw into a bluff when Minni could easily have trips or be trapping.

Board: 8♥ 6♥ 6♣ 3♠ 8♠. I have A♣Q♣ — no pair, just Ace‑high. Claude called my preflop 3‑bet and a large flop c‑bet, then checked the turn; that line is consistent with either a very strong locked hand (66/88/full house) or a float/heart draw that missed. Betting the river would be a pure bluff that might fold out some marginal hands, but it also risks getting jammed or called by every hand that beats Ace‑high. I planned on the turn to check and avoid committing with only overcards; nothing changed on the river to improve my hand. With a deep stack and no urgent need to gamble, the lowest‑variance +EV play is to check and go to showdown rather than bluff into Claude’s calling range.

Missed my flush draw - I have JhTh for just Jack-high on a double-paired board (8866). Minni checked turn and river after betting flop, suggesting medium strength. Bluffing with a 2175 shove into 4100 needs ~35% fold equity, but Minni's preflop 3-bet range likely contains pairs that won't fold on this board. Check back and accept the loss.

I am UTG with 6h 4h, which is a marginal speculative hand. With Claude behind me on a very short stack of approximately 21 BBs, there is a high likelihood of him shoving over any open. Additionally, the table has several active stacks that could make playing this low-suited gapper out of position difficult. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my healthy stack.
7-3 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. In UTG+1 position with 22BB, I need to fold this trash hand and wait for better spots.

On the button with 2♠3♣ — a weak hand but huge positional advantage and a deep stack. With UTG and UTG+1 folded this is a standard steal opportunity: raise to 3x the BB to take the initiative and pressure the blinds. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet, but if called use position to c-bet on dry flops or check/control on coordinated boards and keep the pot manageable.

Button open to 3bb is a fairly strong/wide range, but 9♠4♥ offsuit has poor playability and is heavily dominated. Calling from the small blind puts us out of position with a hand that rarely realizes equity and can bleed chips postflop; it’s also not a good 3-bet bluff candidate (no blockers, no connectivity/suitedness). Best stack-preserving play is to fold and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ks Jc. Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Button. A Button raising range is typically wide, and KJo performs well against it, often dominating weaker Kings and Jacks. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650, roughly 31% equity required), and my hand has significantly more equity than that against a standard opening range. While a 3-bet is an option, calling is the lower-variance play that keeps the opponent's range wide and avoids bloating the pot while out of position. I will defend by calling.

The flop comes down 7s 2d 2c, a very dry, paired board. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor (Minni). This board likely missed both of our ranges, but Minni retains the range advantage with potential overpairs. Leading out (donk betting) would accomplish little here, as worse hands would fold and better hands would call or raise. My King-high has some showdown value and two overcards to improve on the turn. I will check and evaluate based on her action.

I have trip deuces (2♠ in hand + 2♦2♣ on board) on a very dry, paired flop. As the preflop aggressor in position this is an ideal spot to c-bet for value and to charge any one-pair hands or worse pocket pairs that will call (overcards and most single-pair hands have little fold-equity here). The pot is 650 — I’ll bet a moderate ~300 (roughly 1/2 pot) to extract value while keeping the pot controllable against the BB’s deeper stack. Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn and continue extracting vs single-pair ranges; if I face a sizable check-raise, assume a very narrow strong range (likely 7x/set) and proceed cautiously (call small/medium raises to see another card, fold or proceed depending on raise size and my read).

The board is 7s 2d 2c, which is very dry and paired. I have King-high with no backdoor flush draw. While I might be ahead of Minni's pure air bluffs (like Q-high or connectors), I am out of position against the chip leader who likely has a range advantage (pairs, Ax). Without a pair or a draw (not even a backdoor flush draw), my hand is too weak to profitably call a c-bet of roughly half-pot. Floating here out of position would lead to difficult decisions on later streets if I don't improve immediately. Folding is the prudent, low-variance play to preserve my stack.

JTo in UTG with ~22BB. This is a borderline hand for UTG but it has good playability with straight potential. With my short stack, I should be looking for spots to accumulate chips. A standard 2.5x open to 250 is reasonable - it keeps my range balanced and gives me fold equity if I face aggression.

Claude opened UTG to 250 with only ~19BB behind. I have A♦8♥ UTG+1 — strong enough to defend but not a premium 3-bet candidate in this spot. A tiny reraise to 400 buys little fold equity and I remain out of position vs the opener; a larger 3-bet risks getting shoved or put in a tricky dominated spot. I’ll just call 250 to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, realize equity with top‑pair/ace kicker potential, and react to any squeeze or shove from the button/blinds on a case-by-case basis.

We’re on the button getting a good price (250 to win 650) with a suited hand that can realize equity well in position. Claude is short (likely opening fairly wide), but Q7s is too dominated to 3-bet for value and a 3-bet risks getting jammed on by Claude or put in tougher spots vs Minni’s continue range. Calling keeps the pot manageable while retaining positional advantage and implied odds when we make strong flush/straight hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 6c, facing a UTG raise to 250 and two callers. Pot odds are approximately 4.5 to 1 (calling 200 to win 900), which looks mathematically tempting, but K6o plays extremely poorly in a multi-way pot from out of position. If I hit a King, I am often dominated by better kickers, and I will have difficulty realizing my equity. Given the strength shown by a UTG raise and the presence of multiple opponents, the reverse implied odds make this a clear fold despite the immediate price.

I am in the Big Blind with J3o. Claude raised to 250 from UTG, and there have been two callers (Minni and FiveTwo). Although the pot odds are roughly 7:1 (150 to call into a 900 pot), J3o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots and out of position. The reverse implied odds are significant, as hitting a Jack will often leave me dominated by better kickers, and the hand has virtually no straight or flush potential. Given the tournament context and my healthy stack, there is no need to enter a bloated pot with such a marginal hand.

Missed the flop completely with JTs on Q63 rainbow. No pair, no flush draw, only a weak backdoor straight draw. With two callers behind and a Queen on board that connects with their calling ranges, a c-bet here is unlikely to succeed often enough to be profitable. With my short stack (~19BB), I should preserve chips and check, potentially giving up or taking a free card.

Flop: 6♦ Q♣ 3♥. I hold A♦8♥ — I’ve missed the Q but have Ace-high plus a backdoor diamond draw and two overcards to the 6 and 3. This is a multiway pot (UTG opener is short, button called) so my continuation bet will have poor fold equity and would commit chips OOP with a marginal hand. Pot control and equity realization are the priorities: check. Plan is to see how the button reacts; I’ll call small barrels to realize my equity but fold to heavy pressure or an all‑in from the short stack unless I improve to top pair or a strong draw. If action checks through I’ll reevaluate on the turn for value or a bluff-catch.

Flop goes check/check to us in a 3-way pot and we’ve flopped top pair with a weak kicker (Qs7s) on Qc-6d-3h rainbow. This is likely best often enough to bet for thin value versus worse Qx (Q2–QJ), 6x/3x, and pocket pairs, while also denying two overcards a free look at the turn. At the same time, we don’t want to bloat the pot and get put in a miserable spot versus Minni’s stronger continuing range, so we use a small, non-polar sizing in position. If we face major aggression from Minni we can comfortably fold; versus Claude (short stack) we can reassess based on jam dynamics and Minni’s response.

I have JTo on a Q63 rainbow board with no pair, no flush draw, and only very weak backdoor straight possibilities. FiveTwo's 1/3 pot bet is small but I have virtually no equity here - maybe 10% against a Queen, less against stronger hands. With only ~19BB and needing to preserve chips for better spots, calling 300 (15% of my stack) with no draw and no showdown value is -EV. Easy fold.

Call 300. The flop raise is tiny (300 into a 1,200 pot) so the pot odds (~20%) justify a call with A♦8♥. I’m OOP, out of position, and holding only Ace‑high with a very weak backdoor diamond/runner possibility and just three A outs to make top pair by river (~12–13%), so a raise here would be thin and unnecessarily bloating the pot. Calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize equity against what is likely a mixed (wide) bluff/value button range, and preserves stack depth to re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: call the 300, fold to significant turn shove or heavy pressure if I don’t improve, and extract value or apply pressure only if I pair the Ace/8 or pick up a strong draw.

I’m out of position heads‑up with A♦8♥ on a dry 6♦ Q♣ 3♥ 2♠ board and I have no pair. I called the tiny flop raise to keep the pot controlled and the turn didn’t improve me — I only have 6 clean outs to pair (three Aces, three Eights) ≈13% on the river. With a marginal hand and deep stacks, I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP or turn this into a bluff that can be easily shoved back at, so I will check and see what FiveTwo does. If he bets small I may call for pot odds to realize equity; I’ll fold to heavy pressure or a shove unless I hit the river. Checking preserves flexibility and pot control while keeping my weak Ace‑high alive as a river bluff‑catcher or one‑pair candidate.

Turn is a total brick (Qc-6d-3h-2s) and Minni checks to us. With Q7s we have top pair but close to the bottom of our Qx, and after Minni called the flop our value targets (6x/3x/small pairs) are relatively narrow while a lot of continuing Qx is actually better (Q8–QJ, etc.). Since there are essentially no meaningful draws to deny on this texture, I don’t need protection, and betting risks getting check-raised or just building a pot where I’m often dominated. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our showdown value, and lets Minni bluff rivers with missed floats / turn-check lines that we can evaluate.

Plan: check back; on most rivers call reasonable bets and value-bet only if checked to on very safe runouts.

Check. I missed everything with A♦8♥ on 6♦ Q♣ 3♥ 2♠ T♣ and I only have Ace‑high. FiveTwo was the flop aggressor (small raise to 300) then checked the turn — a line consistent with a mostly made/showdown range (Qx, small pocket pairs, occasional weak bluffs). OOP I have almost no value to extract and limited fold equity, so bluffing the river would be a thin, high‑variance play that can be shoved or called by most of his continuing range. Pot control and saving chips is the priority: check to showdown. If FiveTwo leads into me I will fold to any sizable bet (~>1/3–1/2 pot) and only consider calling a very tiny probe that gives correct pot odds.

River goes check to us in a heads-up pot (1500) and we hold Q7 on Q-6-3-2-T. While the runout is relatively “safe” in that it doesn’t complete obvious draws, our kicker is weak and Minni’s flop check-call range contains a lot of better Qx (Q8–QJ/AQ), plus some slowplayed traps (22 turning a set, occasional 33/66). The hands we realistically get thin value from (6x/3x and small pocket pairs) are fewer and will often fold to a river bet after two streets of checking. Since there’s no protection needed and betting just risks paying off stronger hands (or getting check-raised), the highest-EV line is to take the showdown.

Checking back keeps the pot controlled and realizes our equity versus Minni’s medium-strength pairs and occasional missed floats.

5♦7♦ UTG in a 5‑hand game is a marginal, speculative hand — suited but low and easily dominated. There are four players to act behind, including very deep stacks who can 3‑bet or call and a short big blind (≈18BB) who can shove and put me to a difficult decision. Calling 100 to see a flop creates a likely multiway pot out of position where my equity and playability drop; raising to 200 is too thin — I’d frequently face squeezes or shoves and have poor reverse implied odds. With a 209BB stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better opening spot. Fold.

5-handed from UTG+1, 85o is too weak and dominated to open profitably against opponents who have shown willingness to 3-bet/raise. Claude in the BB is only ~18bb and can jam over a light open, forcing us into a bad spot. With plenty of playable hands ahead and no need to gamble here, I’ll pass on this marginal steal spot.

I am on the Button with Pocket Sixes (6c 6d), a strong starting hand for this position. The action has folded to me. My stack is healthy (81 BB). The Big Blind (Claude) is somewhat short-stacked (18 BB), while the Small Blind is deep. A standard open-raise is the correct play here to steal the blinds or play the pot in position. Limping is weak and folding is too tight. I will raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit (Ah9d). The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. This is a standard late-position open. My hand is definitely strong enough to continue against a button raise in a 5-handed game. I have two primary options: calling or 3-betting. Given the relatively shallow effective stacks (especially Claude in the Big Blind with only 1825), 3-betting might force me into an uncomfortable spot if Claude shoves or Pro 4-bets. However, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good high-card value. I will call the additional 150 to see the flop.

T4o is a weak hand. Getting 4:1 odds to call 150, but I'll be out of position against two players with a hand that's easily dominated. With only ~18BB stack, I need to preserve chips for better spots rather than play marginal hands OOP.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Nines) on a 2d-9h-Ad board. This is an extremely strong hand in this situation. My opponent, Pro, was the pre-flop aggressor from the button. Standard play here is to check to the aggressor to allow them to continuation bet with their wide range of hands, including bluffs or weaker Aces that I dominate. I will then look to either call to keep their bluffs in or raise to build a pot against their value hands.

The flop comes Ad 9h 2d. This board favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, but it also hits the SB's defending range (Ax hands). With pocket sixes, I have a weak made hand with limited improvement potential. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff catcher if raised, or puts me in a difficult spot if called, as I am likely behind an Ace or a 9. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs from missed broadway cards on later streets. I will check behind to keep the pot small.

I have top two pair (Aces and Nines) on a board of Ad-9h-2d-Qh. In the previous round (flop), I checked and Pro (the pre-flop aggressor) checked back. This check-back on an Ace-high flop often indicates either a marginal hand looking for showdown or total air. The Queen of Hearts on the turn adds some straight draws (KJ, JT) and a heart flush draw to the board. Since the flop went check-check, I want to start building the pot now to get value from hands like weaker Aces, Queens, or draws that Pro might have. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot (2/3 pot) to charge draws and extract value from any 'showdown value' hands Pro might be holding.

The turn brings a Queen, making the board Ad 9h 2d Qh. There are now three overcards to my pocket sixes. The opponent leads out for 400 into a pot of 600. Given that I checked back the flop, this bet could be a stab, but the Queen interacts well with the Small Blind's defending range (QJ, QT, KQ) as well as Ax hands they might have checked. My hand is currently beating only pure bluffs or lower pocket pairs, which are unlikely to lead this size. With extremely low equity against any made hand and difficult playability on the river, the prudent play is to fold.

5-handed but UTG is still the first position with 3 players behind who can 3-bet/iso. 9♠4♣ offsuit has poor playability, dominated top-pair prospects, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position. With aggressive opponents in the blinds/button and no compelling reason to limp, folding preserves stack and avoids tough postflop spots for a small EV gain at best.

I am in UTG+1 with Jd 2h, which is a very weak unsuited hand. With 78 big blinds, there is no pressure to make loose moves, and opening this hand from early position is statistically a losing play. The standard and correct action is to fold.

I am on the Button with King-Seven offsuit. In a 5-handed game, this is a standard opening hand when the action folds to me. My goal is to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Claude in the Small Blind is short-stacked (approx. 18 BBs), so I must be prepared for a potential shove, but K7o is strong enough to justify an open-raise here to apply pressure.
K2o is a weak hand OOP against a button raise. With only 17.75 BB, I should be selective. K2o doesn't play well postflop and I'll be out of position. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I’m in the big blind (20800) with 8♦5♥ facing a button raise to 300 (pot 450, to call 200). This is a weak, unsuited, out-of-position hand with lots of dominated matchups vs a button opening range. Calling 200 into a 650 pot after I call requires ~30.8% equity to break even; 8‑5o’s equity vs a button opener is marginal and its postflop playability OOP is poor. 3-betting to 500 with this hand is overly aggressive and not profitable long-term. With deep stacks I can wait for better spots in position. Fold and preserve the stack for higher equity situations.

I am holding 5d6d in the UTG position at a 5-handed table. While suited connectors are playable hands, UTG is the most difficult position to play from, especially with speculative holdings. There is a short stack (Claude) on the button with ~17bb who might shove over an open, forcing me to fold and lose chips. Additionally, the blinds are deep-stacked and may defend aggressively or 3-bet. Playing a low suited connector out of position against multiple potentially strong ranges is not ideal. A fold is the standard, disciplined decision here to preserve my stack of ~78bb for better spots.

I am holding 7h 4d in UTG+1 after the UTG player folded. This is a very weak starting hand that does not warrant an open, especially from early position. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for better opportunities.

A7o on the button with folds to me. This is a standard open from the button, especially heads-up against the blinds with position. With 17.75BB, I should raise to around 2.5x. A7o plays well in position and can flop strong top pairs or use the ace as a blocker.

Fold. Tc6c is a weak, low-card suited hand with poor playability out of position. Claude opened to 250 from the button and the big blind (FiveTwo) still acts — this will often be a multiway or I’ll face post‑flop pressure from better hands. 3‑betting with this holding is too loose and a call commits me to a difficult out‑of‑position flop against a wide but well‑disguised range. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot in position.

Claude is opening the button for a small size and is only ~15bb deep, so my best option is to defend wide getting a great price (150 to win 400 immediate; ~27% required). 7c4c has enough raw equity versus a likely wide BTN range and plays better as a call than a 3-bet bluff here: if I 3-bet, he can profitably jam/call with a short stack and I’m forced into a bad spot. By calling I keep his range wide and can realize equity on favorable boards (flush/straight/2-pair potential) while largely playing straightforward with the low SPR.

We defended wide preflop getting a great price, but this flop is very high and connected (Qh Jh 3d), which strongly favors the button’s raising/c-betting range. With 7c4c we have no pair, no real straight draw, and only a backdoor club flush, so there’s not enough equity to start building a pot out of position—especially versus a short stack who can respond by jamming over any lead. Best is to check our range, see if he gives up, and only continue versus a very small bet or on favorable turns (club/4/7) where our equity/bluffing improves.

Flop is 3d Qh Jh - coordinated with two hearts and broadway cards. I have Ac7s, just ace-high with no hearts. FiveTwo checked. I should c-bet small (~1/3 pot) as the preflop aggressor to represent strength and potentially take it down. If called, I'll likely shut down unless I improve. A small sizing of around 175-200 risks little while applying pressure.

Claude’s flop c-bet is extremely small (175 into 725), giving me ~19% immediate pot odds. Even though 7c4c is mostly air on Qh Jh 3d, I do have some backdoor paths (backdoor clubs, and turns like 4/7 for a pair or 5/6 to pick up a straight draw) and, importantly, this sizing is often a range/probing bet from the short-stacked button. Raising is poor because his ~15bb stack can just jam over it and I’d be forced to fold after lighting extra chips on fire. I’ll take the great price, see a turn, and continue only on improving/scare turns or if he shows weakness.

Turn brings Kh completing the front-door heart flush and further connecting the broadway region (KQJ) that heavily favors BTN’s range. With 7c4c we still have no pair and essentially no meaningful draw, so betting is spew—Claude’s short stack can jam over any lead and we’d be forced to fold after investing extra. Best is to check our range, give him a chance to check back, and if he bets/jams we can comfortably fold.

Turn brought Kh completing a flush for anyone with two hearts. Board is now 3d-Qh-Jh-Kh - extremely dangerous with three hearts. I have Ac7s - just ace-high with no hearts and only a gutshot to a straight (need T). FiveTwo called my flop c-bet showing interest, and this card likely hit their range hard if they have any heart. Betting here is very risky into a potential flush. Best play is to check and potentially see a free river - if a non-heart Ten comes I make a straight.

River is checked to me (I’m OOP) and 7c4c has essentially zero showdown value on Qh Jh Kh 8d 3d—Claude’s range contains tons of hands that beat 7-high even when he “gives up” (A-high, random broadways, small pairs). His line (tiny flop c-bet, then check back the Kh turn) is consistent with a capped/medium-strength range that wants to realize equity and get to showdown (Qx/Jx, some Kx, plenty of air). From the BB I can credibly have turned hands that want to value bet now (two-heart flushes, T9 straights, some KQ/KJ slowplays) after he checked turn. Since I’m almost never winning by checking, the best way to realize equity is a polar bluff that maximizes fold equity against his bluff-catchers, especially given his short stack. I’ll apply maximum pressure by putting him all-in and happily fold if he snaps with a flush/straight.

Board shows three hearts (Qh-Jh-Kh) and I have Ac7s - just ace-high with no hearts. FiveTwo's line (check-call flop, check turn, lead river for 1350) screams value. They likely have a flush, or at minimum a strong made hand. I only beat pure bluffs here. Getting ~37% pot odds but ace-high can't realistically beat any value hands in their range. Clear fold.

I am dealt 8c 5h in the UTG position. This is a very weak starting hand and does not warrant a raise or even a call in the earliest position, especially with a short stack (Claude) and aggressive players behind me. Folding is the only sensible play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
T4o is a very weak hand from UTG+1. With only 13.5 BBs, I need to be selective. This hand has poor playability and is easily dominated. Easy fold.

I'm on the button with pocket tens and both UTG players folded — great position. With deep stacks and two players to act (SB FiveTwo and BB Pro), I want to build the pot, gain fold equity, and avoid the passive limp/call spot that invites multiway action. Standard open to ~3x the BB (300) is appropriate here: it isolates the blinds, prices out speculative hands, and gives me a clear postflop plan. I'll re-evaluate if I face a 3-bet (stack depths and opponent tendencies will dictate calling vs 4-betting).

Minni’s button open is likely wide, and T9s plays well as a pressure 3-bet candidate. Flat-calling from the small blind invites the big blind in and leaves us out of position without initiative. A 3-bet here can win the pot pre often, isolate Minni, and gives us strong postflop playability when called (good equity on many boards, strong semi-bluffing potential). I’ll use a solid OOP sizing that discourages the BB from coming along while keeping my risk controlled versus a 4-bet.

I am holding 6h 3c in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand (trash). The action facing me is a raise from the Button (Minni) to 300, followed by a 3-bet from the Small Blind (FiveTwo) to 1200. The pot is currently 1600. To continue, I would need to call 1100, which offers poor pot odds (roughly 1.45:1) for a hand with very low equity against a 3-betting range. Furthermore, I would be playing a large pot with a garbage hand potentially sandwiched between two players or heads-up against a strong range. This is a trivial fold to preserve my stack of 77.5 BBs for better spots.

I opened to 300 on the button with TcTd and SB (FiveTwo) 3‑bet to 1200. With deep stacks (I have ~204bb) and position, TT is strong but not a hand I want to polarize my range with by 4‑betting large — calling keeps their bluffs in, gives me post‑flop leverage, and avoids bloating the pot out of line. Calling 900 more (1200 total) is cheap relative to the pot (≈36% equity required) and lets me realize equity vs a wide SB 3‑bet range. Postflop plan: use position to c‑bet favorable textures, pot‑control or fold to heavy action on ace/king heavy boards, and raise/extract when I flop sets or strong two‑pair/overpair improvements.

As the preflop 3-bettor OOP, I retain a meaningful range advantage on Q-7-2 (I have all the big overpairs and strong Qx that Minni has less of after just calling). This is a good board to apply pressure with a small c-bet and fold out a lot of her overcards/air and some weak pairs, while keeping my risk low when I’m called (T9s still has backdoor straight potential and can pick up equity on many turns like J/8/spades). If raised, I can comfortably let it go given my lack of immediate equity.

Board is 7c 2d Qd and I hold TT after calling a SB 3-bet preflop. FiveTwo's flop stab is small (800 into a 3300 pot) — calling 800 gives me ~19.5% pot odds (win ~4100 for an 800 call). Versus a SB 3-bet range this is a cheap way to realize equity: TT is behind any Qx but ahead of many bluffs and weaker pairs/overcards. I want to keep the pot controllable in position, deny the 3-bettor an easy fold, and re-evaluate on the turn. I will call the 800 now; fold only to a substantial raise or turn action that clearly shows Q/A/K/flush commitment, and if the turn is a blank and villain checks I can consider a delayed c-bet for value/protection. Total committed after this call will be 2000 chips.

Turn 9d gives us a real hand (middle pair) in a 3-bet pot, and it also brings in/strengthens a lot of diamond-draw continues for Minni. As the preflop aggressor I still have the range advantage (overpairs/AQ/KQ), so I want to keep applying pressure with a controlled sizing that (1) charges diamond draws and overcards, (2) can get thin value from worse pairs (7x/88-JJ sometimes), and (3) lets me comfortably bet/fold versus a raise since raises here skew to sets/two-pair/strong Qx+draw. I’ll use a small-to-medium barrel to avoid overinflating the pot with a marginal made hand while still denying equity.

I have TcTd on a 7c 2d Qd 9d board. I currently have a pair of tens and one diamond in hand, giving me 9 diamond outs to a flush plus 2 remaining tens to make trips — about 11 clean outs (~24% to hit on the river). The pot is 5600 and it costs 1500 to call, so the break‑even equity is ~1500/(5600+1500) ≈ 21.1%. Given that my raw draw+improvement equity (~24%) already clears the break‑even threshold, plus I can still win at showdown versus some of his bluffs, a call is the correct, lowest-variance play. FiveTwo’s line (SB 3‑bet pre, small flop stab, turn bet) can contain Qx, single‑diamond hands, bluffs and some two‑diamond flushes — raising now would bloat the pot when I’m often behind to made Qx/flushes and would commit a lot with only a draw. I’ll call the 1500 to keep the pot manageable, realize equity in position, and re-evaluate on the river: if a diamond hits I’ll extract value; if the river is a blank I’ll proceed cautiously (check/call or fold to large aggression).

River 2h pairs the deuce, which is slightly better for my perceived 3-bet range (overpairs/AQ), but my actual hand (9sTs) is a thin bluff-catcher. After Minni calls flop and turn, her range is heavily weighted toward Qx and some slowplayed sets (77/22) plus some missed diamond draws. If I bet, worse hands that called twice (many Qx) don’t fold and better hands call/raise, while the hands I target (missed diamonds/air) are exactly the hands I can often pick off if I check and induce. Checking keeps the pot controlled and lets Minni bluff missed draws; I can then decide based on sizing.

I have TcTd (pair of tens) on 7c 2d Qd 9d 2h. Villain (FiveTwo) 3‑bet pre, made a small flop stab and a turn bet — a line heavy with Qx/overcards, some medium pairs, and also missed diamond draws. The river 2h didn’t complete my flush (I missed) and they checked, which reads as pot‑control with a marginal made hand or a fold to big aggression if I fire. Betting here to get thin value risks getting called by all the hands that beat me (Qx, any made flush if they held two diamonds, or a boat/set), while only extracting value from a narrow slice of worse hands (9x, 7x, small pairs, bluffs). With significant showdown value and no additional outs, the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check back and go to showdown. If Villain barrels small I’ll call reasonable sizing; I’ll fold to a large jam that polarizes to full houses/flushes.

I have QQ in UTG with only 13.5 BB (1350 chips). This is a premium hand and I'm short-stacked. Standard play is to raise and be willing to get it all in. Raising to 300 (3x) - if I get 3-bet, I'm committed and will shove.

Fold. Q♦9♠ from UTG+1 is a marginal, often dominated hand facing an UTG open in a 5‑handed game. Calling 300 into a ~450 pot out of position invites multiway action and poor post‑flop prospects, and a small 3‑bet won’t isolate reliably against the button/SB/BB — Claude is short (1,050) so his raise could be wide, but I don’t want to gamble out of position with this holding. Preserve the deep stack and wait for a better spot or a hand in position.

Claude’s UTG open is coming from a very short ~10.5bb stack, which tends to be tighter (often strong broadways/pairs) and he can easily shove over any isolate. 8♣5♦ offsuit plays poorly vs that range, is frequently dominated, and has limited implied odds given his stack size. Calling also invites the blinds in and puts us in tough postflop spots with a weak hand. Best decision is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with K9o. Claude (UTG) has a very short stack of 10.5 BBs (1050 chips) and has opened for 3 BBs (300). This large sizing relative to his stack indicates he is likely committed to the hand and has a strong range (pairs, strong Aces, strong Broadways). My hand K9o is dominated by most of his UTG opening range (KQ, KJ, AK, A9, pairs). Even though the price to call is decent, post-flop playability is low and I would essentially be gambling against a stronger range for his remaining stack. Flash is also behind me in the BB. The prudent play is to fold and preserve my healthy stack of 77 BBs.

I am in the Big Blind with As7h. Claude, who is sitting on a very short stack of 10.5 BB (1050 chips), has raised to 300 from UTG. This represents nearly 30% of his remaining stack, indicating he is likely committed to the hand. To call, I only need to commit 200 more chips into a pot of 450, giving me roughly 2.25:1 odds (about 31% equity needed). Against a desperate short stack's opening range, As7h is likely to have sufficient equity to justify a call. While I could shove to put him all-in, A7o is not strong enough to want to induce a call from his better hands, and he is unlikely to fold given his pot commitment. I will call to see a flop and evaluate, keeping in mind he only has 750 chips left.

I am first to act on a very dry, paired board (4-4-6). Claude is a very short stack (7.5 BB remaining) who raised from UTG, which usually indicates strength, but his range could also include broadway cards that missed this flop. By checking, I allow him to continue with his semi-bluffs or shove his remaining chips. Given the pot odds and his stack size, I will likely be calling a shove with my Ace-high, but checking is the best way to see how he proceeds.

I have QQ on a 4-4-6 rainbow board - excellent flop for my overpair. Very dry texture, unlikely Flash has a 4 or hit this board hard. With 1050 chips behind and 650 in pot, I should bet for value. Betting around 300-350 (roughly half pot) to extract value and set up a potential river shove if called.

I am holding Ace-high (As7h) on a very dry, paired board of 4-4-6. Claude, who started the hand with roughly 13-14 BB, raised from UTG and has now fired a continuation bet of 325 into a pot of 650. I am getting 3:1 direct odds to call (325 to win a total pot of 1300). Claude's range for an UTG raise at this stack depth includes many pocket pairs that I am losing to, but it also includes several high-card hands like AK, AQ, and AJ that I am currently tied with or beating. Given the board texture, it is unlikely he has connected with a 4, and my Ace-high has significant equity against his range and the price being offered. I will call the bet and look to reach a showdown or evaluate on the turn, as Claude only has 725 chips remaining behind.

On the Turn, the board is 4d-4s-6c-Js. I am out of position against Claude, who is a very short stack with only 725 chips remaining (roughly half the pot). My Ace-high (As7h) has some showdown value but is vulnerable. If I bet, I am only likely to be called by hands that beat me (pocket pairs or a Jack). By checking, I allow Claude to decide how to play his remaining stack. If he checks behind with his overcards (AK, AQ), I maintain my lead.
