If he shoves, I will receive excellent pot odds (calling 725 to win 2025) and will need to decide if his range contains enough bluffs or inferior Ace-highs to justify the call. For now, checking is the most prudent action.

I have QQ on 4-4-6-J board - still a strong overpair. Jack doesn't change much. With only 725 behind and 1300 in pot, I'm committed. Shoving now maximizes value and puts pressure on Flash. If they have a J or better, so be it, but QQ is too strong to slow down here when short-stacked.

Claude has gone all-in for his remaining 725 chips on the turn. The board is 4d-4s-6c-Js. My current hand is Ace-high (As7h). With the pot at 2025 and 725 to call, I am getting odds of roughly 3.8:1, meaning I need approximately 26.4% equity to make a profitable call. Claude's short-stack UTG opening range includes many pocket pairs and broadway cards. While the Jack on the turn is a dangerous card that likely connects with hands like AJ, KJ, or QJ, the pot odds are significant. Given that Claude is a short stack and might be shoving with a wider range of high cards (like AK, AQ, or AT) to capitalize on fold equity or simply because he is pot-committed, my Ace-high has sufficient equity to close out the action. Calling here is a standard decision based on pot odds and the relatively small impact on my overall stack.

2♠8♥ offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands and I'm first to act (UTG). Out of position against 4 opponents and with players behind who raise frequently, calling 100 or risking a raise with this hand is negative EV — I’ll preserve my healthy 24k stack and wait for a much better spot. Fold and avoid playing dominated, marginal hands from early position.

5-handed with UTG already folded, we’re still relatively early (UTG+1) and out of position versus Button/SB/BB. T4o is too weak to open profitably here and limping creates tough multiway, dominated-top-pair situations with poor playability. With no dead-money incentive beyond blinds, best EV is to release and preserve stack for better spots.

I hold Pocket Jacks [Js, Jh] on the Button. The action has folded to me. This is a premium hand and a clear value raise situation. I want to build the pot and play in position against the blinds, or simply take down the blinds and antes now. The Big Blind (Claude) has a relatively short stack (26.5 BBs), and the Small Blind (Flash) has a deep stack. A standard open raise size of 2.5x BB (250) is consistent with my previous play and appropriate to exert pressure while allowing me to play comfortably in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc7h. Pro has raised from the Button to 250. My hand is weak and out of position, and with Claude still to act in the Big Blind, this is a clear fold. I want to conserve my chips for better spots rather than defending with a marginal offsuit hand against a button open.

K3o in the BB facing a button min-raise. Getting 2.7:1 pot odds but K3o is a weak hand that plays poorly OOP. With 26.5bb effective, I'd rather not defend this marginal holding against a button open. Fold and wait for a better spot.

5-handed UTG plays much wider than full ring, but we’ll still avoid limping. K9o is near the bottom of an open range, yet it benefits from taking initiative and has decent blocker/value vs blinds. I’ll open small to apply pressure on the blinds, keep my risk controlled against the aggressive players behind, and comfortably fold if I face a strong 3-bet/jam from the short SB or pressure from Button/BB.

I am holding 6s 2s in UTG+1 facing an opening raise to 250 from the UTG player (FiveTwo). This is a very weak starting hand (low suited disconnected cards) that performs poorly against a standard UTG opening range. Although I have a reasonable stack (78.5 BB), calling here would be a negative expected value play due to the low hand strength and potential to be dominated or squeezed by players behind. Conserving chips for a better spot is the correct strategy.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has raised from UTG, and although I have position, this hand does not play well against a standard UTG opening range. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
8-3 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing an UTG raise, I'm out of position with poor equity and playability. Clear fold.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 2.5bb in a short-handed (5-handed) game. My hand 7♠6♣ is a marginal offsuit connector — playable but weak OOP. The pot is 400 and I need to call 150 more; with my very deep stack (≈242bb) and only one opponent, the implied odds + chance to realize equity on connected boards justify a defend. I don’t want to 3-bet light here (I have no high-card/blockers and would face difficult decisions OOP), and folding surrenders good pot odds. I'll call to see a flop, look for straights/paired boards, and play postflop with position disadvantage in mind.

Heads-up after FiveTwo opened UTG and checked the flop. I hold 7♠6♣: a made pair of 7s plus a very strong open‑ended straight draw (need a 5 or 9) and a backdoor club possibility. Out of position I don’t want to give a free card to many turn cards that beat my pair (A/K/Q/J or a club that pairs with a club draw), so I’ll take the initiative and bet for value and protection. A half‑pot bet (≈275 into 550) charges overcards and pure draws, extracts value from worse pairs/draws, but keeps the pot manageable if FiveTwo raises. I will reassess on the turn based on their reaction (call/raise/fold).

BB’s small donk (275 into 825) looks like a probing stab with a wide range on a board that hits their defend (8x/7x, pair+draws, some Jx). With K9o I’m not strong enough to raise for value, and a bluff-raise will often get called by a lot of one-pair/draw hands and sometimes run into strong Jx/two pair/sets on this connected texture. But I’m getting a good price in position with two overcards (K) plus a gutshot to the T, so calling keeps the pot controlled while realizing equity and lets me apply pressure on favorable turns if Minni slows down.

The turn (5♦) completed my straight: I now have 5-6-7-8-9. That beats virtually all of FiveTwo’s likely continuing hands (Jx, weaker pairs, 8x, missed draws) though it can still be behind the rare T9 combos that made a higher straight on the flop. FiveTwo’s line (UTG open, check-call on the flop) looks like a lot of one-pair / draw hands and some medium connectors — good hands to extract from. I should be getting value and denying a free river card, so I’ll bet for value and protection. Pot is 1,100; I’ll bet 800 (~70% pot) to charge worse pairs and draws while keeping the pot controllable if I face aggression. If he check‑raises very large I’ll re-evaluate (that line often polarizes to the few hands that beat me), but versus a normal call or small raise I expect to be ahead and will proceed to river.

Minni’s second donk (800 into 1100) on this very connected texture is much more consistent with real value (Jx/two pair/sets/straights) and strong combo-draws than with pure air. With Ks9d I only have a gutshot to the T (4 outs) plus a few thin K outs that are often not clean vs Jx/two pair and can still face a big river bet. Calling 800 to win 2700 needs ~30% equity; I’m nowhere close in practice. Turning it into a bluff-raise also isn’t attractive because this runout hits BB’s defend/donk range hard and they won’t fold enough (lots of pairs + draws). Best is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Six offsuit (T6o), which is a statistically very weak hand with poor playability. Given the early position, the presence of active players with large stacks behind me, and no strategic reason to loosen my range significantly at this stage (Hand 63/150) with 78 BBs, the only correct play is to fold immediately.

I am holding Ace-Trey offsuit in UTG+1 (5-handed). While an Ace is often worth playing in short-handed situations, A3o is quite weak from early position. With Claude sitting on a short stack of 2,600 and Minni holding a massive stack of nearly 25,000 behind me, I risk being shoved on or outplayed post-flop. Given my healthy stack of 15,275, there is no need to enter the pot with a marginal hand that has poor playability.

On the button with Q7 suited. Two players folded to me, and I have 26 BBs. Q7s is a good hand to open from the button - suited with decent high card. Standard open to 250 (2.5x) to steal the blinds or play in position if called.

Facing a button open to 250. I’m in the small blind with Kc 3s (weak, unsuited) and would need to call 200 into a 400 pot. Although the pot odds look tempting, K3o is heavily dominated by a normal BTN opening range and I’ll be out of position versus both the button and the big blind who still has to act. A light 3-bet to isolate is possible, but Claude has ~23 BB so he can call or shove and my hand has poor equity in those spots. With a healthy stack and many hands left, the +EV line is to preserve chips and fold this marginal holding.

Claude is opening the Button very wide and we’re getting an excellent price: 150 to win 400 (need ~27% equity). 65o has reasonable playability (straight potential, some wheel/2-pair equity) and folding would be too tight versus this small sizing. I don’t want to 3-bet bluff here because Claude is only ~23.5bb effective and can respond by jamming or calling, forcing us into an inflated pot OOP with a marginal offsuit connector. Best is to defend and realize equity postflop.

We flop trips on a paired board in a heads-up pot versus a very wide button opener. This is a spot where Claude will c-bet frequently with a lot of air/overcards and some 9x and diamond draws. Leading would often fold out his bluffs and reduce our ability to win his stack, while checking keeps his range wide and lets us spring a check-raise for value (or take a delayed lead on the turn if he checks back). Even though our kicker is weak, we’re still ahead of the vast majority of his continuation range and want to maximize EV by inducing.

Paired board with 5-5-9, two diamonds. I have Qh7h - no pair, no draw. As the preflop aggressor, this is a good spot to c-bet. The board is dry and unlikely to have hit FiveTwo's BB calling range hard. A small c-bet of around 1/3 pot should take this down often.

We flopped trips in a heads-up pot versus a very wide BTN range, and Claude’s tiny c-bet is consistent with range-betting (lots of overcards/air, some 9x, pocket pairs, and diamond draws). With Claude only ~2.2k effective, we want to start building a pot that can comfortably get stacks in by the turn/river, while also charging his draws and denying cheap equity. A check-raise is higher EV than a flat because many hands will shut down on later streets if we just call this small sizing. I’ll raise to a size that keeps in 9x, pocket pairs, and diamond draws without instantly folding out everything.

FiveTwo check-raised my c-bet on a 5-5-9 board. I have Qh7h - complete air with no pair and no diamond draws. A check-raise on a paired board is typically very strong (trips, 9x, or pocket pair). With only queen-high and no backdoor equity, continuing would be burning chips. Easy fold.

I am dealt Jack-Nine offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position in a 5-handed game. This hand is too weak to open from early position as it has poor playability post-flop and frequently finds itself dominated by the ranges of the players behind me. I will fold to maintain my stack for better opportunities.

K6o from UTG+1 with 22BB stack. This is a marginal hand in early position. With several players left to act behind me including aggressive opponents, K6o doesn't have enough equity to profitably open here. Better to wait for a stronger spot.

6♣3♣ is a weak hand, but on the button versus only the blinds it's worth opening because of position and fold equity. Limping surrenders initiative and invites multiway pots where this hand has poor equity. I’ll open to a standard size (≈3x BB) — big enough to pick up the blinds often and keep the pot manageable if called. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold; if called I’ll use positional advantage to c‑bet on favourable flops and realise equity on low, connected boards.

Minni is opening the button frequently and to a relatively large 3bb size. From the SB, flat-calling with T8o is tough (dominated, poor realization OOP, and invites the BB in), so I prefer turning it into a light 3-bet to leverage fold equity and isolate. The larger OOP sizing also pressures the BB from coming along and lets me win the pot immediately a good chunk of the time.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 3s. The action facing me is a Button open to 300 followed by a Small Blind 3-bet to 1200. My hand, while suited, is very weak (low one-gapper). Calling 1100 chips (12BB total) into a 3-bet pot with the original raiser still left to act is a losing play. I am out of position against the aggressors and likely dominated. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I opened to 300 on the button with 6♣3♣ intending to use position, but FiveTwo’s 3‑bet to 1,200 is too large for this hand. 6‑3s is a weak, poorly-connected suited holding that is dominated by most 3‑betting ranges and has far less equity than the ~36% required (900 into a 1,600 pot). 4‑betting as a bluff is unnecessary with no read and I don’t want to inflate the pot with such a marginal hand. Fold and wait for a better, more playable spot in position.
52 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. In UTG position with a short stack of ~22bb, I need to be very selective. This is a clear fold.

UTG+1 with K♠5♦: a weak, offsuit king that’s easily dominated by typical opening/3‑betting ranges. This is early position in a 5‑handed game — I should be opening a much stronger range. Calling 100 (or making a small open) just puts me in marginal multiway or 3‑bet spots against deep, aggressive opponents (FiveTwo and Flash) where K5o has poor equity and bad implied odds. My stack is deep enough to wait for a better spot in position with a stronger hand. Fold and conserve chips for higher EV situations.

Folded to me on the Button with blinds posted (150 in pot). Even with a weak offsuit hand like 7-5, the Button is the best spot to apply pressure and pick up dead money, especially against two blinds who must defend out of position. I’ll use a small raise size to risk little while still leveraging position; if faced with a 3-bet I can comfortably fold, and if called I can play a low-cost positional pot and apply selective c-bets on favorable textures.

I am in the Small Blind with A6o facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (FiveTwo). While FiveTwo is an active player and likely opening a wide range from the Button, A6o plays poorly out of position. It lacks the connectivity of suited connectors or the wheel potential of A2-A5, making it difficult to realize equity post-flop. Calling invites a squeeze from the Big Blind and leaves me playing a marginal hand OOP. A 3-bet is an option but A6o is not the best candidate for a bluff compared to wheel aces. The standard and disciplined play at this stack depth (77 BB) is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9c 3c. FiveTwo raised to 250 from the Button. The Small Blind has folded, and I need to call 150 more to see the flop. The pot is currently 400, giving me pot odds of nearly 2.7 to 1 (requiring about 27% equity). While 93s is a weak hand, it is suited, and in a 5-handed game, defending the Big Blind against a Button open with these odds is generally acceptable to prevent being over-folded. I will call and see the flop out of position.

The flop [Ac Kd 8d] is very strong for the pre-flop raiser's range, which likely includes many Aces, Kings, and Broadways. My hand (9c 3c) has almost no equity here, offering only a backdoor club draw. I will check and prepare to fold to most bets, as this board is difficult to bluff or defend out of position.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on A♣ K♦ 8♦, this board strongly favors my perceived Button opening range (lots of Ax/Kx), while Flash’s BB defend contains many hands that miss or are marginal (broadways without an ace, small pairs, suited connectors). Even though my actual hand has almost no showdown value, a small c-bet will often take it down immediately and keeps my risk low when I’m bluffing. If I get raised, I can comfortably fold; if called, I’ll re-evaluate turn cards (good barrels on some high cards/diamonds; otherwise I can shut down).

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 3c. The flop is Ac Kd 8d, which is a very unfavorable board for my hand and highly favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range (FiveTwo on the Button). FiveTwo has bet 200 into a pot of 550. While the pot odds are roughly 3.75 to 1, I have no pair, no straight draw, and only a weak backdoor flush draw. Given that this board hits almost all of a Button's opening range (Aces, Kings, and Broadways), it is extremely unlikely I can win this hand by calling. I will fold and preserve my stack.

UTG with 8h6c is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with very poor playability out of position in a 5‑handed game. Pot is 150 and it would cost 100 to see the flop; opening or calling risks building a multi‑way pot where I’ll be dominated and face difficult postflop decisions (and Claude in the BB is short and could shove). I’ll preserve my healthy stack and wait for a better spot in position rather than limp/raise with this junk.

5-handed but still effectively early position (UTG+1). 54o has poor playability and domination issues, and opening invites squeezes/reshoves from the short BB (Claude ~20bb) and pressure from bigger stacks behind. Limping also realizes equity poorly multiway. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for better spots.

Action is folded to me on the Button with King-Seven offsuit. My stack is healthy at 77 BBs. The Big Blind (Claude) is somewhat short-stacked (20 BBs). K7o is within the standard Button opening range (top 40-50% of hands) to steal the blinds and antes. I will make a standard raise to 2.5 BBs (250 chips) to put pressure on the remaining players. If I face significant aggression (like a 3-bet or shove), I can easily fold.

I'm in the small blind with J2o. Pro has raised from the button to 250. My hand is weak and out of position. While Claude in the big blind is short-stacked and might be looking to shove, there is no value in calling or raising with this hand against a button open. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

8-2 offsuit is a trash hand. Even getting ~2.67:1 pot odds, this hand is too weak to defend from the BB against a button raise. I'm out of position with a hand that has very little playability. With a short stack of ~20BB, I need to preserve chips for better spots.

5-handed UTG with 7♠2♠ is at the very bottom of the range. Even suited, it makes weak pairs and dominated flush/straight situations, and opening it from worst position invites 3-bets and tough postflop play. With no dead money beyond blinds and plenty of better spots, preserving stack and avoiding negative-EV marginal spots is best.

I hold King-Jack suited (Kd Jd) in UTG+1 (Middle Position in 5-handed play). This is a premium broadway hand that is well within the standard opening range for this position. My stack is roughly 78 big blinds, allowing plenty of room for post-flop play. I have been opening to 250 (2.5x BB) consistently with my playable hands (e.g., Hand #61, Hand #66). Raising builds the pot and allows me to take the initiative, potentially stealing the blinds or playing a heads-up pot in position against the blinds. Folding is too passive for this hand strength, and limping is suboptimal.

I am on the button with 2h 4h. Pro has opened with a raise to 250 from UTG+1. While I have the benefit of position, 2h 4h is an extremely weak hand that does not play well against an early position opening range. Additionally, the small blind (Claude) is short-stacked and may look to jam, which would force me to fold anyway. It is best to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.
9h3h is a weak suited hand. Out of position against a UTG+1 raise with only ~20BB stack. Minni still to act behind could squeeze. Poor odds to call with this marginal hand - folding is standard here.

UTG+1 (Pro) opened to 250 and I’m in the BB with Q♥6♦. I’d face the raiser out of position postflop and Pro’s early-position open is likely weighted toward hands that dominate Q6o (Qs, Qx, broadways, pairs). Calling 150 (to 250 total) yields ~2.7:1 pot odds (~27% equity needed), but Q6o’s equity versus an early opener is marginal and the hand plays poorly OOP — I’ll frequently face tough decisions on later streets. 3-betting to 400 as a light bluff is possible but thin and unnecessary with so many better spots ahead and deep stacks to pick from. Prefer to conserve chips and fold this marginal holding.

I am in UTG position with 3c 6h, which is a very weak hand (unsuited, low cards, gapped). In a 5-handed game, opening from UTG requires a stronger range. There is no strategic merit to playing this hand. I will fold immediately.

I am holding Jack-Four offsuit in UTG+1. This is a very weak hand that does not warrant an opening raise or a call, even in a short-handed game. My position is still relatively early with four players left to act behind me. Conserving chips for better opportunities is the standard play here.

I have AKo on the button - a premium hand. With ~20BB stack and position, I should raise for value. Standard 2.5x raise to 250 to build the pot and potentially take down blinds or play in position post-flop.

I have 9♠7♣ in the small blind facing a button open to 250. Calling costs me 200 into a 400 pot (cheap but leaves me out of position postflop and potentially in a 3‑way pot if the BB calls). 9‑7 offsuit is a weak, awkward hand OOP with poor playability against a button raise. A light 3‑bet to the min 400 wouldn’t fold out many better hands and risks getting committed or facing a shove from the short-stacked opener. With a healthy stack I should conserve chips and avoid marginal, difficult postflop spots — fold this preflop raise.

Claude is opening on the button with a short ~18bb stack. We’re getting a good price in the BB (150 to win 550 ≈ 27% immediate odds) and 6-5o has reasonable equity versus a typical button opening range plus some straight-making playability. 3-betting as a bluff is awkward here because his stack depth incentivizes him to jam over a 3-bet, and 6-5o performs poorly versus a jam/call range—so we’d often be forced into a -EV 3-bet/fold. Calling keeps his range wider and lets us realize equity in a low-to-moderate SPR pot.

We flop a marginal but real made hand: second pair (5s) with a weak kicker and no immediate straight/flush draw. Against Claude’s wide button opening range we’re ahead of his pure overcards, but we don’t want to bloat the pot OOP at a low SPR (~3) and get put in a tough spot versus a jam. Checking keeps his range wide, lets him c-bet bluffs/value worse, and allows us to respond based on sizing (likely check-call versus small c-bets, fold versus very large pressure). If he checks back, we can often value/protection bet many turns.

I have AKo on a low 8s 5c 2s board - completely missed but have two overcards. FiveTwo checked to me. This is a standard c-bet spot with position. The board is low and drawy (spade flush draw). I should make a small c-bet around 1/3 pot to take it down or represent strength. If I get raised, I can fold.

Claude’s 200 c-bet into 550 is a small range/protection bet from a wide button opening range. With 5h6s on 8s-5c-2s we have a real made hand (middle pair) that’s ahead of all his pure overcards and has decent equity even versus some higher-card floats. Calling 200 to win a pot that will be 950 gives us ~21% required equity, which we comfortably have versus his likely c-bet range. Raising is awkward at this SPR because his remaining stack is shallow and he can jam over a check-raise, forcing us into a poor raise/fold with a marginal hand. We’ll call and re-evaluate turns: continue on low/bricky turns and improve cards (5/6/8/2), but be more cautious versus big overcards and heavy turn pressure.

Turn Kc is a meaningful overcard that improves Claude’s wide BTN range (a lot of Kx) and is also a strong barrel card for him. With his very short remaining stack (1575 into a 950 pot), leading doesn’t accomplish much: if we bet for “protection,” we’ll frequently get jammed on and be put into a thin, awkward call with just a weak second pair. By checking, we keep his bluffs in, allow him to size himself (so we can continue versus small bets), and avoid unnecessarily committing chips when his value-jam range is likely Kx/8x+.

Turn brought the Kc, giving me top pair top kicker - excellent! FiveTwo has checked again. I need to bet for value and protection against the spade flush draw. With pot at 950 and my remaining stack at 1575, I should bet around 55-65% pot (~550) to get value from worse hands and charge draws.

We’re getting a very good price: call 550 to win 2050 (need ~27% equity). Even though Kc is a strong overcard for Claude’s BTN range (many Kx), his line can still contain plenty of barrels with missed overcards and spade draws (Ax♠, QJ♠, JT♠, etc.), and we still beat all those hands with our pair of 5s. Raising all-in is poor here because it mostly folds out the hands we beat (bluffs/draws) and gets called by Kx/8x+ that have us in bad shape. So we realize equity and keep his range widest by calling, then re-evaluate river versus his likely small remaining jam (475).

River is a bad value-betting spot with 5h6s: our pair mostly gets called by better (Kx/Tx/8x, some slowplayed overpairs) and folds out the hands we beat (missed spades/overcards), so betting is negative/thin and can even level us into a bad call-off versus a jam. With Claude’s ~0.5x pot stack left, checking keeps his bluff region intact, lets us realize showdown value when he checks back, and gives us the option to bluff-catch if he jams (we’ll then decide based on his sizing/line).

I have top pair top kicker on Ks Ac. River brought Tc completing potential club flush draws (3 clubs on board). FiveTwo has been check-calling passively throughout. With pot at 2050 and only 1025 behind, SPR is very low. I should value bet - FiveTwo could have worse Kx, pairs of T/8, or missed draws. A bet around 700-800 extracts value from worse hands. If they have a flush, I lose the minimum by not shoving. Betting ~750 for thin value.

River spot after we’ve check-called twice: we have a weak showdown hand (5h6s = one pair of 5s) on 8s-5c-2s-Kc-Tc. Claude’s 750 bet into 2800 is a smallish sizing and he’s extremely short behind (only 275), so there’s no practical fold equity or benefit to raising—raising would mostly just get snapped by all Kx/Tx/8x/flushes and fold out the bluffs we want to keep in.

For the call, we’re getting strong pot odds: calling 750 to win 3550 means we need only ~21% equity. While this runout favors his value region (lots of Kx and some Tx/8x), his line and sizing can still include missed spade draws and other triple-barrel bluffs that use K and T as scare cards. Given the price and our stack safety versus his tiny remaining stack, this is a reasonable bluff-catch.

Plan: call and take showdown; we can’t profitably turn this into a raise.

In a 5-handed game, King-Eight offsuit (K8o) is far too weak to open from Under the Gun (UTG). It lacks significant post-flop playability and is frequently dominated by the calling ranges of the remaining players. Given the stack sizes and my position, folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play.
K2o from UTG+1 is too weak to open. With 38BB stack, I need to play tight from early position. This hand has poor playability and will often be dominated by better kings.

On the button with Ad8d (suited Ace) and both UTG players folded, this is a clear spot to open for value and fold equity. Pot is 150 and the cost to see the flop is only 100, but I want to take the initiative and isolate the blinds in position. A standard 3x BB open to 300 is the right size — big enough to pressure the small blind and big blind and to earn fold equity, but not so big as to commit excessive chips out of position vs a 3-bet. Post-flop plan: c-bet favorable textures and use positional advantage to fold out weaker holdings or extract value from worse Aces and diamond draws. If faced with a large 3-bet, re-evaluate and likely fold to preserve my deep stack; can call small-ish 3-bets and play postflop in position.

Minni’s button open is likely wide, but 9♢3♣ offsuit is too far down the range to defend from the small blind: we’re out of position, frequently dominated when we make a pair, and it has poor playability (no suitedness/connectivity). Cold-calling also invites the big blind to come along, worsening our equity realization. A 3-bet bluff with this hand is unnecessarily loose given stack depths and the risk of getting called/4-bet. Best EV is to release and preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 6c facing a 3x raise (300) from Minni on the Button. The pot is currently 450, and I need to call 200. This gives me pot odds of roughly 31%. While 76o has some connectivity, it plays poorly post-flop out of position, making it difficult to realize equity. Against a typical button opening range, my hand has about 35-36% equity, but with realization factors considered, this is likely a losing call. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.
Q5o in UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand from the earliest position with 4 players yet to act. Not worth opening, especially with my shorter stack of ~38bb.

UTG+1 with 9♠3♣ — a very weak offsuit hand in early position. With five players, UTG+1 is essentially an early seat and this hand is often dominated; it has poor playability and little equity multiway or OOP. Calling 100 (limping) just invites raises and creates difficult postflop decisions, while opening to 200 with this holding would be an unnecessary bluff against aggressive opponents. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot.

Button is the best spot to apply pressure 5-handed, especially after two folds. Even though 45o is weak, both blinds have shown plenty of folding (Pro especially), and we have position to realize equity and control pot size postflop. I’ll open small to risk less and keep my range wide; if I face a 3-bet I can comfortably fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Eight offsuit facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (FiveTwo). While the Button's range is wide, Q8o plays poorly out of position. I am dominated by many hands in their calling range (KQ, QJ, QT, A8+). Calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or come along, leaving me sandwich-ed or heads-up out of position with a marginal hand. The chip cost to call is relatively small compared to my stack, but the expected value of playing this hand OOP is negative. Preserving my 78BB stack for better spots is the optimal strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The Button (FiveTwo) has opened for a standard raise to 250. While the pot odds to call are approximately 2.6:1, Queen-Trey is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially when out of position. It is easily dominated by the Button's wide opening range and lacks significant straight or flush potential. At this stage of the tournament, it is better to fold trash hands and wait for better spots rather than defending with marginal equity and a high likelihood of being outplayed on the later streets.

UTG in a 5-handed game with A♠3♣ — a weak but playable ace. Standard plan is to open-raise for fold equity rather than limp: I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the 100 BB) to seize initiative, isolate the blinds, and avoid playing multiway pots out of position. Table stacks are deep (my stack ~246 BB) and several players have shown aggression, so I won’t overcommit with a weak kicker — if I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold; versus a small 3-bet from a wide player I may consider a call, but default is to fold to heavy aggression. If called, I’ll C-bet favorable runouts and proceed cautiously postflop, folding to strong resistance when my kicker is likely dominated.

We’re facing a UTG open from the table chip leader with an offsuit T2, which is far too weak to continue profitably from UTG+1. Calling puts us in a multiway, out-of-position spot with dominated top-pair risk and very poor playability; 3-betting would be pure spew versus an UTG range. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold 6c 3h on the Button, which is an extremely weak hand with poor playability. Minni, the chip leader, has opened with a raise to 300 from UTG, signaling a strong range. Although I have position, my hand has very low equity against a standard UTG opening range, and the pot odds do not justify a call. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with K2s. Minni, the chip leader, opened from UTG with a 3x raise. While suited Kings have some potential, playing out of position against an UTG range is generally a losing proposition, especially with a short stack (Claude) still to act in the Big Blind who could potentially jam. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my healthy stack for better opportunities.

QhTh is a strong suited broadway hand. Facing a min-raise from Minni, I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds to call 200. This hand plays well post-flop with flush and straight potential. Calling is standard here rather than 3-betting against an active player with a large stack.

I flopped middle pair with QhTh on 6d-Qs-Ah. I have second pair with decent kicker plus a backdoor heart flush draw. However, the ace is concerning as Minni's preflop raising range likely contains many Ax hands. Out of position, checking is standard here to control pot size and see what Minni does. If they check back, my hand has showdown value. If they bet small, I can call with my pair + backdoor draws.

Heads-up on the flop vs the BB who just defended my 3x UTG open. I hold A♠3♣ on 6♦ Q♠ A♥ — top pair but a very weak kicker. This is a textbook c-bet spot: my hand has decent showdown value, I block some stronger A combos, and a bet charges Qx, worse Ax, middle pairs and draws while keeping the pot manageable against the hands that currently beat me (AQ/AK/sets). I’ll bet roughly half the pot to get value and deny equity to draws without bloating the pot with a marginal kicker. If Claude check-raises or shoves big I’ll re-evaluate aggressively but be willing to fold to heavy pressure since my kicker is weak.

I have second pair (Queens) with a decent kicker and a backdoor heart flush draw. Minni's half-pot c-bet of 325 gives me 3:1 pot odds (needing ~25% equity). While the Ace is concerning since Minni could have Ax, my hand has enough equity with second pair plus backdoor draws to continue. Folding is too tight here with a made hand, and raising accomplishes little since better hands call and worse hands fold. Calling to see the turn is the best play.

Turn brought Jd giving me a gutshot straight draw (need K for Broadway) in addition to my second pair Queens. With 0 to call, I can check. My hand has showdown value but isn't strong enough to build a big pot against Minni's range which likely contains Ax hands. Checking to control pot size and potentially induce a bluff or get to showdown cheaply is the best line here.

I have top pair (A♠3♣) but a very weak kicker on A–Q–6–J. Claude (BB) check‑called my flop c‑bet, which looks like a medium/weak calling range (Qx, worse Axs, some middle pairs and draws) and his check on the turn suggests pot control/weakness. The turn J is a dangerous card (adds some straight/paired-J combos) but I still want to extract value from worse A’s and charge Qx/Jx while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll bet about half the pot to get value and fold equity; if Claude shows heavy aggression (large raise or shove) I’m willing to fold because my kicker is poor and several hands beat me. Bet ~650 chips (≈½ pot).

I have second pair (Queens) with decent kicker plus a gutshot straight draw to Broadway (need K). Pot is 1950, calling 650 gives me 3:1 odds (~25% needed). My ~9 outs (4 Kings for straight, 2 Queens, 3 Tens for improvement) plus showdown value makes this a call. Folding is too tight with this much equity, raising would only get called by better hands.

The river Queen gave me trip Queens with a Ten kicker - a very strong hand. I was worried about Minni having an Ace, but now even Ax hands are behind me (they'd have two pair at best). I should bet for value. With pot of 2600 and 2550 behind, a bet around half pot (1200-1300) should get called by Ax hands, Jx hands, and possibly bluff-catchers. Going too big might fold out hands I beat.

The river Q pairs the board and Claude, who has passively check‑called my flop and turn bets, now raises — a line strongly indicative of a value hand (Qx turning trips, AQ turning a full house, or A‑X with a better kicker). My A♠3♣ is top pair but with a very weak 3‑kicker and is dominated by many Ax combos and crushed by any Q. Calling 1,300 requires ~25% equity; against Claude’s value‑heavy polarised river range my actual equity is well below that. I block only a few strong combos (one Ace) and shouldn’t hero‑call here. Fold and preserve a big stack for better spots.

5-handed we can open wider, but Jh7d offsuit from UTG is still a dominated, low-connectivity hand that realizes equity poorly—especially when called by the big stacks in the blinds and when facing frequent 3-bets. With plenty of playable spots ahead and no dead money beyond the blinds, the EV of limping or opening is low compared to simply preserving our stack and avoiding tough postflop situations out of position.

Current hand is 9c Td in UTG+1 (Hijack) position 5-handed. This is a weak offsuit connector. While playable from the Button or sometimes Cutoff in an unopened pot, it is generally too loose to open from the Hijack, especially with deep-stacked opponents (Flash on BTN, Minni in BB) remaining who can apply pressure. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

I am on the button with Qh6h, which is a decent suited hand for an opening raise after two players have folded. My goal is to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against the small or big blind. Following my established pattern of opening for 3x the big blind, I will raise to 300.
32 suited is too weak to defend from the SB against a button raise. I'm out of position with poor playability postflop. Easy fold.

Facing a button open to 300 heads-up with 6♦4♠ in the big blind. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (need ≈31% equity) — with deep stacks the implied odds and the button’s frequently wide opening range make a call reasonable. 6-4o is too weak a hand to 3-bet for value or to isolate out of position, but it has enough showdown/backdoor potential to justify a defend here. Plan: call 200 more (total commit 300), see the flop and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy action if I completely miss, realize equity with pairs/draws, and avoid bloating the pot out of position with marginal holdings.

Check. I have a single pair (6s) on A-6-7 with one spade in hand (a weak backdoor flush/straight possibility), but the Ace on board makes my pair very vulnerable to any A‑x in the preflop raiser’s range. Flash opened from the button so his range contains a lot of Aces and broadways and he will c‑bet this texture often. Out of position I want to control the pot and avoid bloating it with a hand that is frequently dominated. Plan: check and call a small–medium continuation bet to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to large aggression or an overbet shove. Avoid donk‑betting or thin check‑raises here.

I have middle pair (6s) on an Ace-high flop after raising pre-flop from the button. Minni checked, which is expected. A continuation bet of 325 (half pot) is a standard play here to represent the Ace and put pressure on her range, which likely consists of mid-pairs or draws. If I am called, I can look to check back on later streets to reach showdown cheaply unless I improve.

Call 325. I have a vulnerable middle pair (6♦) with a weak 4 kicker on A♠‑6♠‑7♣ — often behind any A‑x in the button’s wide opening range but ahead of many missed broadway hands and bluffs. Flash’s bet is small (~1/3 pot), so calling gives ~25% pot odds to see a turn and realize equity with deep stacks. Raising out of position with this hand (weak kicker, frequently dominated) would be spewy; folding now would give up too much equity vs a small c‑bet. Plan: call to control the pot and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression or an overbet shove, otherwise continue only with improvement or when the board becomes safe for showdown.

I improved to two pair on the turn (6s & 4s). This is a strong hand vs non‑Ace holdings but is dominated by many A‑x combos (A6, A4, A7) and loses to sets (66,77). Flash checked the turn, which looks like weakness or pot control. Betting here extracts value from worse Kx/Qx/Jx and small pocket pairs, charges any backdoor draws, and denies a free river card to hands that can improve. I’ll bet about half pot to balance value and protection without overcommitting — big enough to get value and fold equity, but small enough to fold to extreme aggression. If Flash check‑raises large, I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a heavily polarized shove; call smaller raises to keep worse hands in).

I have middle pair (Sixes) on a board of As-6s-7c-4d. After I continuation bet the flop and Minni called, she has now led into me on the turn for 650 (half pot). This 'donk bet' can represent a few things: a turned straight draw (like 5x), a weak Ace looking to define its strength, or a slow-played big hand. However, it can also be a blocking bet with a draw or a Seven. Given the pot odds (3:1), my middle pair is too strong to fold against a range that includes draws and smaller pairs, but it is not strong enough to raise for value. I will call to see the river and evaluate the final action.

I have two pair (6♦4♠) on As 6s 7c 4d Ac. That’s a decent hand but it’s very vulnerable now — the river paired the Ace, so any Ax in Flash’s button range is now trips/full house and beats me. Flash’s line (button open, flop c‑bet, turn check‑call of my raise) is consistent with a lot of A‑x and medium pairs. Betting for thin value risks a check‑raise from trips/full houses or forcing me to call a large river overbet. Checking controls the pot and lets me realize showdown value versus his non‑Ace hands; if he fires a small river probe (≈≤½ pot) I’ll call for value, but I’ll fold to a large overbet/shove that strongly represents Ax/full house. Given the board and opponent’s line, the safest +EV line is to check and go to showdown.

On the river, the board pairs the Ace, giving me two pair: Aces and Sixes with a Queen kicker. Minni led on the turn but checked the river. This check often signifies a medium-strength hand (like a 7) that is now wary of the Ace, or a missed draw (like spades or a straight draw). Given that several hands in her range (any Ace, any Seven, or a 5-high straight) beat me, and I am unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most logical and safest play.

I am in UTG position with Ten of Hearts and Deuce of Diamonds (T2o). This is a very weak starting hand, unconnected and unsuited, with poor playability and low equity against any reasonable range. In a 5-handed game, opening from UTG requires a stronger range. There are no exploitative reasons to play this hand given the stack depths and position. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

I am holding 9s 3s in UTG+1. This is a very weak starting hand with poor connectivity and low high-card strength. Even though it is suited, the potential for making a strong hand is low, and playing it out of position against the remaining three players (Button, SB, BB) is not strategically sound. Following standard opening ranges, this is a clear fold.

I have 3h8c on the button - a very weak hand. While button is a good stealing position with only blinds left to act, 83o is too weak to profitably open even here. Minni in the SB has a massive stack (21975) and FiveTwo has 8775 in the BB - both can easily defend. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

Folds to me in the small blind with 8♦7♥ and big stacks (my stack ~21975, BB FiveTwo ~8775). This is a good spot to seize the initiative: 8-7o is marginal but plays well heads-up and I want to deny the BB correct odds to call with wide, speculative hands. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to isolate and pick up the pot or go heads-up with initiative. If called I’ll proceed cautiously out of position — c-bet on favorable low/connected flops and with equity, check/control on ace/king-high boards, and fold to heavy aggression without significant equity. I will release to a sizable 3-bet since 8-7o has poor equity vs a polarized 3-bet range.

Minni is opening very wide from the SB, so our BB defend range can be wide, but 7♠5♦ offsuit is near the bottom of playable holdings: it has poor high-card value, weak playability OOP, and significant reverse-implied-odds when we make a pair. Even getting 2:1 (200 to win 400 now), our equity realization out of position vs an aggressive bigger stack will be poor. I’ll pass on the marginal defend and preserve stack for better spots (especially 3-bet/call candidates with better blockers or suited connectivity).

I am in the UTG position with Td 2c, which is a very weak hand. In a 5-handed game, this hand lacks any significant post-flop potential or raw equity. Given the stack sizes and the early position, folding is the standard and most profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K8o from UTG+1 is a marginal hand that doesn't play well in early position. It's easily dominated by better kings and doesn't have great postflop playability. With 51BB stack, I should be selective with my opening range. Folding is the disciplined play here.

3♣6♦ is a very weak, easily dominated hand. I'm on the button facing only the blinds with 150 in the pot and 100 to call, but with deep stacks and competent opponents (FiveTwo and Pro) a call or a light open-raise will often get me into marginal, costly post‑flop spots or into 3‑bets. Stealing with garbage is unnecessary — I can preserve my 22125 stack and wait for a better spot to exploit position. Folding keeps my range strong and avoids needless variance.

Folds to me in the small blind heads-up vs a likely fairly tight BB. 9c5c is easily inside a profitable SB opening range: it has decent playability when called (suited, can make strong draws) and benefits a lot from taking it down pre-flop. I’ll use a small-but-standard SB steal size to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal holding.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, facing a 2.5x raise from the Small Blind, I am getting good pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 500, requiring ~30% equity). Q8o is a marginal hand but strong enough to defend in position against a likely wide stealing range from the SB. My positional advantage post-flop allows me to realize equity better. Folding would be too tight given the circumstances, and 3-betting is unnecessary with this specific holding. Therefore, calling is the most standard and effective play.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on a low, fairly dry flop, I want to keep initiative and deny equity to Pro’s many overcard hands (A-high/K-high, etc.). 9c5c has a gutshot to the 3 plus a backdoor club draw, making it a reasonable low-risk c-bet/semi-bluff. I’ll use a small sizing to fold out air and some overcards while keeping the pot manageable; if I get raised I can comfortably fold, and if called I can continue on good turns (3, club, some high cards) and often shut down on bad runouts.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, the Small Blind c-bets 175 into a pot of 500 (approx. 35%) on a dry, disconnected low board (6h 2d 4s). I hold Qh 8d, giving me two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. Given the wide ranges in BvB play, the small bet size, and my positional advantage, I am getting excellent pot odds (roughly 4.8:1) to call. Folding here would be over-folding my range, as Q-high often has sufficient equity against the opponent's bluffs or allows me to realize equity if I hit a pair on the turn. A call is the standard defensive play.

Turn brings Jc, which is a good barrel card for my perceived range (as the preflop raiser I have plenty of Jx/overpairs) and it materially improves my actual hand: 9c5c picks up a real flush draw (any club river) plus the gutshot to a 3 (some overlap), giving me strong semi-bluff equity. Pro’s flop call range contains a lot of medium pairs/6x that can be pressured on this overcard, and a larger sizing forces tougher decisions while still giving me a reasonable price when called. If I get check-raised, a tight BB is heavily weighted to value on this texture and I can fold comfortably unless sizing is unusually small.

On the turn, the board is [6h 2d 4s Jc]. The opponent (SB) fires a second barrel of 575 into a pot of 850 (roughly 68% pot), significantly increasing their sizing from the small flop c-bet. The Jack connects well with the SB's opening range (e.g., Jx, AJ, KJ, QJ). My hand, [Qh 8d], remains just Queen-high with no draw. While I have two overcards to the low flop cards, the Jack is an overcard to my 8 but under my Queen, making it likely the opponent has improved or still holds a better high card (Ax, Kx) or a pair. The pot odds required are roughly 29%, and I likely have significantly less equity than that against a value-heavy double barrel range. With no pair and no draw, and facing a larger bet, this is a clear spot to release the hand.
62 offsuit from UTG is an easy fold. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker, and from early position I need to play a tight range. No reason to invest chips here.

9♦6♠ in UTG+1 is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and lots of players left to act (button, SB, BB). It costs 100 to call into a 150 pot (you'd need ~40% equity to justify a call), and 9-6o rarely has that vs typical preflop ranges — plus history shows aggressive players who will often raise. With a healthy 22k stack in early position, preserve chips for better spots and avoid playing dominated, marginal hands out of position. Fold.

Folded to us on the button 5-handed, Qc9c is well within a profitable steal/open range. We have position and decent playability (suited, some straight potential), and both blinds can be pressured to fold a lot preflop. I’ll raise rather than limp to maximize fold equity and take initiative, using a standard ~2.5x sizing that keeps risk low while still applying pressure.

I hold Qs 7h in the Small Blind. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250 (2.5bb). Q7 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, often being dominated by better Queen holdings or Ace highs. While Button opening ranges are wide, defending this hand from the Small Blind against a 2.5x raise is generally -EV due to the positional disadvantage and lack of playability. Folding is the standard and correct decision here.

I am in the Big Blind with J-Ten offsuit. FiveTwo has opened from the Button for 250. It is 150 more for me to call into a pot that will be 550 total. This gives me excellent pot odds (over 3.6 to 1 on the immediate call). JTo is a standard defending hand against a Button opening range. FiveTwo has been active and aggressive, but my hand is strong enough to see a flop and play post-flop, especially given my chip lead. I will call and see the flop.

On this King-high flop of Kd-6d-7s, my JTo has missed completely and has very little equity, as I lack even a backdoor flush draw. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo), the standard play is to check and see how they react. I do not want to bloat the pot with a weak hand, and I'll likely fold if faced with a standard continuation bet.

BB’s defend range connects well with K♦6♦7♠ (lots of 6x/7x, 87/65, pairs+draws, and some Kx), so a small c-bet with Q♣9♣ is likely to get called frequently and we have weak equity/no strong backdoors (only runner-runner clubs; no diamond/spade help). In position, checking back keeps the pot under control, realizes equity, and lets us fire on favorable turns (club, Q, 9, A, or cards that weaken his mid-pair region) while protecting our checking range.

On the turn 5c, the board texture hasn't changed much for my Jack-high hand. FiveTwo checked back on the flop, which suggests they either have a weak made hand (like a small pair or a 7) or they missed the board entirely. While a lead here could potentially take the pot down, I'm out of position against an aggressive player and have zero equity. Checking is the most prudent play. If FiveTwo checks behind again, I get a free look at the river. If they bet, I can comfortably fold knowing I've invested the minimum with a weak hand.

Flop checked through, so BB’s range is capped toward a lot of 7x/6x/5x, weak Kx, and some total whiffs. On this turn (5c) our Qc9c has little showdown value, but we’ve picked up additional equity: a gutshot to the 8 for a straight plus live overcards that can beat a one-pair 6x/7x type hand. In position, a delayed c-bet pressures his air and some marginal pairs, denies equity, and lets us potentially follow through on good rivers (8/A/T) while folding comfortably if we face a check-raise.

On the turn, the board is Kd-6d-7s-5c. I have Jack-high with no draws. After checking the flop, FiveTwo has bet 275 into a 550 pot. While the pot odds are 3:1, I have virtually no equity in this hand. I don't have a pair, a flush draw, or even a straight draw. Calling would be a pure float out of position with the hope of hitting a Jack or Ten on the river, which still might not be enough to win. Raising is too risky against an aggressive opponent who might have a medium pair or a King they checked on the flop for pot control. The most disciplined move is to fold and preserve my chips.

2♦8♦ (suited) from UTG in a 5-handed table is a bottom-tier hand and is heavily dominated by normal opening ranges. Limping or opening here is asking for action out of position against several aggressive opponents who raise frequently; calling 100 to see a flop is speculative and ineffective from UTG. With a healthy stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot or a hand I can play in position. Fold.

5-handed and effectively in the cutoff after UTG folds, A4o is near the bottom of an opening range but still benefits from decent fold equity and the ace-blocker (reduces AA/AK/AQ in opponents’ ranges). I prefer raising over limping to take initiative, win the blinds uncontested often, and keep my range uncapped. Use a small/standard size to limit risk; if Pro (BTN) or the blinds 3-bet, this is an easy fold.

I hold 3d 6h on the Button, which is a very weak unsuited hand with poor playability. Facing a standard open raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG+1, I have no strategic incentive to enter the pot. Calling would be losing chips in the long run, and bluffing (3-betting) with this hand is unnecessary given my stack depth (over 70 BBs) and better opportunities to come. The correct play is a straightforward fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. FiveTwo has raised from an early position (UTG+1 in a 5-handed game). Q9o is a weak hand to play out of position against a raise. While I have a healthy stack, there is no need to defend with a marginal hand like this, especially with Claude in the Big Blind still to act. I will fold to preserve my chips for a more favorable situation.
94o is a very weak hand. Even getting 2.67:1 pot odds, this hand has poor playability out of position. It's not worth defending - easy fold.

5-handed from UTG we need to open wider than full ring. 9♠8♣ is a connected hand with decent playability (can make strong straights) and benefits from taking initiative; folding it would be a bit too tight given the short-handed dynamic. I’ll open small to pressure the blinds and keep my risk controlled versus a 3-bet from the button/big stack, with a plan to fold to sizable 3-bets and continue versus calls on favorable boards.

I hold [Kh 6s] in UTG+1 facing a raise to 2.5BB from the UTG player (FiveTwo). K6 offsuit is a very weak hand that is often dominated by the opener's range (better Kings, Ace-King, pocket pairs). Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me. Despite FiveTwo being active recently, K6o does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff from early position. Preserving my 74BB stack for better opportunities is the optimal strategy.

FiveTwo has raised from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. While I am on the button with a suited gapper (9d 6d), this hand is too weak to call a raise from an early position player. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

JTs is a solid suited connector. Facing a min-raise from UTG with Minni still to act behind, calling is reasonable. The hand plays well postflop and has good equity against typical opening ranges. Position is bad (SB) but the price is good at 200 to call into a 400 pot. Not strong enough to 3-bet here against UTG.

Facing an UTG open to 250 and the small blind calling, I’m up against at least one strong opening range plus a caller — a multiway pot where I’ll be out of position postflop. 7♦3♦ is a very weak holding: poorly connected, often dominated by UTG’s range, and it doesn’t realize equity well multiway. Calling 150 to win a 600 pot requires ~20% equity; against two opponents my realistic equity with 7-3s is below that and the hand plays poorly on later streets. A min-raise to 400 would be a marginal bluff that likely gets called or 4-bet and leaves me OOP. With a big stack and many hands left, preserving chips for better spots is the best long-term play. Fold.

Flopped middle pair (tens) with a jack kicker on Qd Ts 3c. I'm first to act with 600 in the pot. This is a decent spot to bet for value and protection against overcards. FiveTwo opened UTG, so their range includes hands like AK, AJ, KJ that could catch up. A small bet around 1/3 pot keeps the pot manageable while extracting value from worse hands and protecting against draws.

Claude leads very small (200 into 800) on a board where my UTG range has a clear advantage (AQ/KQ/QQ/TT), which makes this sizing look like a probe/block with a lot of marginal Qx/Tx and some air. With 9♠8♣ I have only a gutshot to the J (and no flush backdoor), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a raise that will often get called and leave me with poor equity. The price is good (200 to win ~1000), I’m in position, and calling lets me realize equity and potentially take it away on turns where he checks (A/K/J and some low bricks) while comfortably folding if he starts applying real pressure.

Turn Jc gives me two pair (jacks and tens). This is a strong hand. The board now has some straight potential (K9 makes a straight) but that's unlikely from UTG. FiveTwo's calling range on flop includes AQ, KQ, QJ, AK, KJ. I'm ahead of most of this range. Should bet for value and charge draws. Betting around 60% pot.

Turn J♣ completes our 8-9-T-J-Q straight. Claude’s flop/turn lead line contains plenty of value hands we beat that will continue (two pair like QJ/JT/QT, sets, pair+draws, club draws), and we also want to charge hands that can boat up on a river pair. At the same time, his range can include higher straights (AK, K9s), so I prefer a value-raise that keeps worse hands in rather than an immediate overjam that may fold out dominated pairs/two pair. Raising also sets up a clean river shove on blanks if he calls.

I have two pair (JT) on QTJ3 board. FiveTwo's raise to 1800 is concerning - could be QJ (better two pair), K9/98 (straights), or sets. However, I block JJ and TT heavily. Getting 3.8:1 pot odds (1200 to win 3400). My hand is too strong to fold here with blockers to sets. Could be overplaying AQ/KQ or semi-bluffing with AK. Calling is correct - can reassess on river.

River is a blank 4d. I have two pair (JT) on Q-T-3-J-4 board. FiveTwo showed strength with turn raise to 1800. Their range includes QJ (better two pair), AK/K9 (straights), or possibly overplaying AQ/KQ. With 2750 behind into 4600 pot, checking makes sense - if I bet I only get called by hands that beat me. If FiveTwo checks back, I win against one pair hands. If they bet, I can evaluate sizing - a small bet I can call, a big bet suggests I'm beat.

River is a brick and we still have a Q-high straight. After Claude’s flop probe/turn bet then call of my turn raise, his range is heavily weighted toward made hands that can pay off (QJ/JT/QT, sets like QQ/TT/33, strong Qx) plus some missed draws. The only realistic hands that beat me are K9 or AK (higher straights), and while they exist in his preflop calling range, they’re not so dominant that we should check back given how much value we gain from worse. With Claude only having ~2750 behind into a 4600 pot, the cleanest play is to put him all-in and maximize value from his strongest non-nut hands.

FiveTwo bets 2750 (my remaining stack) into 4600 pot. I'm getting 3.67:1 odds, needing ~27% equity to call. I have two pair (JT) but facing a concerning line: UTG open, call flop, raise turn big, bet river. Hands that beat me: AK (straight), K9 (straight), QJ (better two pair), sets. However, I block JJ/TT significantly, and at these pot odds I only need to be right 27% of the time. FiveTwo could be overplaying AQ/KQ or value-betting thinner. The pot odds make this a call despite the scary line.

I am in UTG position with Eight-Five offsuit, which is a very weak hand. In a 4-handed game, while ranges are wider, 85o is still well outside of a standard opening range. I should fold this hand to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

Button with A♠6♣ vs SB and BB — ace-high with position is worth applying pressure. A standard 2.5–3x open (300 total) exploits my position and the likelihood both blinds will fold or call with worse hands, while keeping the raise small enough to avoid bloating the pot with a weak kicker. Deep stack lets me play postflop if called; plan is to c-bet favorable A-high or dry boards, control pot on dangerous textures, and fold to a large 3-bet. Raise to 300 to take the initiative and maximize fold equity.

BTN open to 3bb from Minni is likely wide, but T5o plays very poorly from the small blind: it’s dominated by a large portion of a wide opening range and we’ll be out of position postflop. Flat-calling also invites the BB in at a great price, creating a multiway pot where our reverse implied odds are high. A light 3-bet is possible versus a wide opener, but with Pro’s shorter stack in the BB (7.3k) the squeeze/jam risk is meaningful and T5o is a weak candidate to defend. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9h 6d. The action folds to the Button (Minni), who raises to 300 (3 BB). The Small Blind folds. I need to call 200 chips to win a pot of 450, offering me pot odds of roughly 2.25:1 (requires ~31% equity). While 96o has about 37% raw equity against a wide 4-handed Button range, realizing this equity out of position is difficult, especially against the chip leader who has shown aggression post-flop. The hand has poor playability and negative implied odds. Given I have a healthy stack of over 70 BB, I can afford to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I have A♦K♠ — a top-tier, playable hand preflop. In this 4-handed game UTG open-raising is standard and ranges are wider than at a full table, so I want to raise to take the initiative, isolate the button/blinds, and deny good odds to callers. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x the BB) rather than the min-raise 200 to discourage multiway callers and to build a pot with a strong hand while keeping plenty of stack behind (I have 22,175). If I face a small 3-bet I’ll generally continue (call or 4-bet depending on sizing); if I face a large shove from the short stack I’ll evaluate pot odds and ICM — but for now raise to 300 to open the action.

4-handed, Minni’s UTG open is effectively a CO/HJ range and should be fairly wide. On the button we realize equity well, and Kh5h has decent playability (suited, some straight potential, can make strong flushes) while being too marginal to bloat the pot with a pure 3-bet line at high frequency this deep. Calling keeps weaker hands in, maintains positional advantage, and avoids getting put in tough spots versus a 4-bet or blind squeeze with a dominated king.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 3d, a very weak offsuit hand. Minni raised from UTG and FiveTwo called on the Button. Although the pot odds are tempting (calling 250 to win a pot that will be at least 1000), playing 43o out of position against two ranges that likely dominate me is a losing play. The risk of the Big Blind squeezing is also present. This is a standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 2d. Minni raised to 300 from UTG, and FiveTwo called on the button. The pot is currently 750, and I need to call 200 more to see the flop. This gives me pot odds of 3.75:1. Although 8-2 suited is a weak starting hand, the price is attractive enough to defend and see a flop, especially as I am closing the action. I will need to play carefully out of position post-flop.

I am first to act on a flop of 9h-9s-7c holding 8d-2d. I have no pair and no significant draw on this paired board. In a three-way pot against the UTG raiser (Minni) and a button caller (FiveTwo), the most disciplined play is to check and see how the action develops. Betting into two opponents on this board with 8-high is unlikely to be profitable.

I was the preflop raiser with A♦K♠ and we’re on a relatively dry, paired flop 9h 9s 7c in a 3‑way pot (pot = 950). I have two overcards and good equity against wide calling ranges, and as the aggressor I should use fold equity to take the pot down or charge weaker holdings/draws. I don’t want to overcommit multiway on a board that can beat me (pocket 9s/sets), so I’ll make a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) to fold out unpaired hands and get value from worse overcards while keeping the pot controllable. If I get called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if I face a large raise I’ll be willing to fold since I currently have only overcards and limited showdown value.

Minni’s 300 into 1250 is a small multiway c-bet on a paired board that heavily reduces the strength of a lot of her opening range (many broadways/A-highs whiff here). With Kh5h we have K-high with some improvement paths (backdoor heart flush + occasional backdoor straight/runouts) and, importantly, position to apply pressure on later streets when she checks or on scare cards (K/A/hearts/8/6) that favor our perceived range. Raising as a bluff doesn’t make much sense multiway: Minni can continue with any 9x, 7x, overpairs, and some floats, and we risk running into BB slowplays. So we take the cheap peel and re-evaluate turn.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-high on a flop of 9h-9s-7c. The initial raiser, Minni, has bet 300, and FiveTwo has called. Although the pot odds are roughly 5:1, my hand has virtually no equity and no draws. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Continuing in this hand against two opponents with such a weak holding would be a waste of chips. I will fold.

I have A♦K♠ and was the preflop raiser and the flop aggressor (small c‑bet). The turn (T♦) doesn’t improve me — I still only have two overcards — and it brings more combos that beat me (T9/Tx, sets, pair+kicker). I don’t want to build a bigger pot with marginal equity against a calling range that likely includes made hands, so I’ll control the pot and check. If the opponent fires a small probe bet (~≤1/3 pot) I may consider a call as a bluff‑catcher with two overcards and the chance to hit on the river; I will fold to larger turn aggression or a check‑raise that represents a set/top pair.

Turn goes check from Minni after a tiny flop c-bet in a pot that has now become heads-up. With Kh5h we have essentially no equity to improve (the backdoor flush is dead and we’re just K-high), so our best way to win is to apply pressure when she shows weakness. This Td is also a card that hits a lot of our perceived floats/calls (Tx, 98, T9) and we can credibly represent trips (A9/K9 suited), 99/77, or a turned T that now wants value/protection. A medium-big stab should fold out a large chunk of her range that checked (A-high/broadways and some small pairs), while we’re happy to just fold if she check-raises, since we have no real draw.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the paired flop to fold out air and control the pot. I checked the turn (T♦) to keep the pot manageable because A♦K♠ has only overcards here. FiveTwo called the flop and now fires a sizable turn bet (1050 into a 1550 pot → pot now 2600). Calling 1050 would require roughly 1050/(2600+1050) ≈ 28.8% equity to break even. I only have ~6 clean outs (A or K) to make a pair on the river (~12–13% equity) and the board is otherwise very unfavorable — his line is weighted to value (T, Tx, 9x, sets/two‑pair) rather than pure bluffs. A raise to 2100 as a bluff is too risky here: his range contains many hands that will continue, and my blockers are weak. Per my turn plan I fold to larger turn aggression and preserve my stack for better spots.

4-handed UTG with KK is a mandatory value open. I want to build a pot while keeping my sizing consistent with my overall opening range (so I’m not telegraphing strength) and also give the button (shorter stack) room to 3-bet/jam with worse. A standard ~3bb raise pressures blinds, denies cheap realization, and sets up clean stacks-to-pot ratios for stacking top pairs postflop.

I am on the Button with Q4o. The UTG player (FiveTwo) has opened for a raise to 300 (3BB). Q4o is a very weak hand with poor playability and is easily dominated by an UTG opening range. Despite having position, the hand strength is insufficient to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff in this spot. Preserving chips for better opportunities is the standard play here.

I have a medium-small pocket pair (6c 6d) in the Small Blind. FiveTwo raised from UTG in a 4-handed game. While out of position, pocket sixes are strong enough to see a flop, primarily for set-mining value. The stack depths (over 150 BB) provide excellent implied odds if I hit a set. I will call the additional 250 to see the flop, rather than raising and bloating the pot with a hand that is difficult to play out of position on most textures.

T2o is a very marginal hand here. FiveTwo opened UTG to 300 and Flash cold‑called, so this is already a multiway pot vs early‑position opener — my Ten is easily dominated and has poor playability (weak kicker, offsuit). Pot is 700 and I must call 200 (need ~22% equity), but multiway action and UTG’s range make that break‑even equity unrealistic. A small squeeze to 500 won’t get enough fold equity and would commit me out of position with a weak holding. Preserve the deep stack and fold.

On a flop of 5s-2d-9d, I have an overpair to the bottom two cards but am behind a 9. Given that I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) in a heads-up pot, the standard and most balanced play is to check. This allows FiveTwo to continue with their range, and I can evaluate based on their bet sizing. My hand is a marginal made hand that serves well as a bluff-catcher on this texture.

We’re heads-up in a single-raised pot with an overpair on a low, drawy board (9d-5s-2d). Flash’s SB defending range contains plenty of 9x/5x, smaller pairs (TT-66), and diamond draws that can all continue versus a c-bet. With no diamond in hand, I want to deny equity and charge the flush draws while still getting called by worse made hands, so I’ll use a medium sizing rather than a tiny range-bet.

FiveTwo's half-pot continuation bet is standard for the pre-flop aggressor on this texture. My pocket sixes are an overpair to the 5 and 2, and while I am behind a 9 or a larger pocket pair, I am ahead of all unpaired overcards like AK or AQ. Raising here would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws, so calling is the best way to keep the pot manageable and allow FiveTwo to continue bluffing with overcards. I will call the 350 to see how the turn develops.

The turn 4d is a significant card as it completes a potential flush and introduces several straight possibilities. My pocket sixes are now a marginal hand on this three-flush texture. FiveTwo, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked back after betting the flop. This check could indicate a variety of hands: a 9 that is now wary of the flush, a flush draw that has hit and is looking to trap, or overcards that have missed. Since my hand has some showdown value but won't be called by many worse hands if I bet, checking is the most effective play to control the pot size and see the river for free.

Turn brings the 3rd diamond, which is a major range-shifter: a big chunk of Flash’s flop continuing range was diamond draws that are now made flushes, plus some 44/55/99 that improved/are still strong. With KsKc (no diamond), our overpair loses a lot of its clean value and is a poor candidate to bet-call or bet-fold if we get check-raised.

In position, the highest-EV line is to check back and realize our showdown equity, while also keeping in Flash’s missed/non-diamond hands that may bluff river when we show caution. We can then bluff-catch reasonable river sizes and avoid inflating the pot on a card that heavily favors the caller’s range.

I have improved to a set of sixes on the river. The board is somewhat dangerous with three diamonds and a potential straight (any 3), but FiveTwo's decision to check back on the turn significantly discounts the likelihood that they have a completed flush. Given their turn check, it's probable they have a marginal made hand like a 9 or a middle pair (77, 88) that they are looking to take to showdown. If I check, they will likely check back for the free showdown. By betting approximately 60% of the pot, I can extract value from those hands. If I am raised, I will have to carefully reconsider the possibility of a flush or straight, but for now, a value bet is the most profitable line.

We checked back turn on the third diamond, so our range is capped and Flash has incentive to stab river with both (1) thin value (9x/5x, some improved hands like 66) and (2) missed one-diamond holdings that picked up a 4-to-a-flush on the turn and now have to bluff. The 850 sizing into 2250 is also very “blocker-ish” and gives us strong pot odds (~27%).

Even though flushes/straights exist and we don’t hold a diamond blocker, folding KK here is too tight versus a range that should still contain enough thin value we beat and some bluffs. Raising would isolate ourselves against better hands, so the best EV line is a straightforward bluff-catch.

I am in UTG position with J6 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Given my position, I have all opponents left to act behind me, significantly increasing the risk of facing a stronger hand or a 3-bet. With a stack of 72 BBs, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand from early position to conserve chips and avoid difficult post-flop situations.

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit. In a 4-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. Pro has already folded, so I only have to contend with the blinds. I will make a standard 3x raise to 300 to either take the blinds now or play a pot in position.

Pocket nines in the SB vs a Button open to 300 is a clear value/isolation spot. With deep stacks (my ~21.4k vs Button ~16.9k and BB ~14.1k) I want to take the initiative, get folds from the weakest part of the Button’s wide opening range, and extract value from Axs, Kx, broadways and weaker pairs. Calling keeps me out of position and often invites the BB to limp/call and make the hand multiway where 99 is less profitable. Folding is too tight. I’ll 3-bet to a size (~1000) that’s large enough to generate fold equity but still reasonable to continue against calls. If I face a large 4-bet shove I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to an extreme 4-bet), but if called I plan to c-bet/protect on most flops and look to set-mine when appropriate.

We’re in the BB facing a BTN open and a SB 3-bet to 1000. With 63o we’re extremely low equity, dominated when we make a pair, and we’ll be out of position in a bloated pot against strong ranges. The price (900 to win ~1400) isn’t enough given how poorly this hand realizes equity versus a 3-bet range, and there’s no credible squeeze/4-bet option. Best decision is to surrender and preserve stack.

I opened with a standard 3x raise from the button, but Minni has responded with a significant 3-bet to 1,000 from the small blind. While I would have position post-flop if I called, Ace-Four offsuit is a weak hand to take into a 3-bet pot. It is easily dominated by Minni's likely 3-betting range (stronger Aces, pocket pairs, and high Broadways). Given that I have only committed 300 chips so far, folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable play to avoid being caught in a difficult spot post-flop with a weak kicker.

In a 4-handed game, A7o is a standard opening hand from the UTG position. My goal is to take control of the pot early or win the blinds. I will raise to 300, which has been a consistent opening size in this tournament.

Fold. J♣4♦ is a weak, poorly coordinated offsuit hand that is routinely dominated by an UTG open range in a 4‑hand game. Calling 300 into a 450 pot gives me only ~40% pot equity (300/(450+300)=40%) to justify a call, and J4o has far less equity versus a typical UTG raising range. A 3‑bet bluff from the button is possible in some dynamics, but with both the small blind (14k) and big blind (7.1k) able to defend or 4‑bet, I don’t have the blockers or reliable fold equity to make that profitable here. I’ll preserve my healthy stack (21.9k) and wait for a better spot in position with a hand that plays well postflop.

Facing an UTG open in 4-handed from the small blind, 84o is too weak and has poor playability out of position (dominated by many of villain’s continues, no suitedness, weak straight potential). The small price doesn’t compensate for the positional disadvantage and high reverse implied odds. No good 3-bet bluff properties either (no blockers), so we just let it go.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 8h facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the UTG player, Flash. In a 4-handed game, UTG is arguably looser, but Flash has been active. The pot odds are approximately 30.7% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650). However, 98o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, often dominated by better nines or eights, or simply behind broadway cards. Given the 3x sizing rather than a min-raise, and the fact that I would be playing the entire hand out of position against a chip leader, the expected value of calling is likely negative due to difficulty in equity realization. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

UTG with A♣6♥ in a 4-max table: borderline but playable — I want initiative rather than limp. Open-raising is standard here to pick up the blinds and avoid multiway pots where my weak kicker is more likely to be dominated.
