I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB): big enough to generate fold equity and simplify postflop decisions, small enough to keep the field from committing huge stacks. Plan: c-bet favorable flops; be cautious vs heavy resistance (fold to large 3-bets from the short stack, consider calling small 3-bets to play postflop heads-up).

Minni is opening from UTG 4-handed, which can be relatively wide, but J5o is still a very poor defend: dominated by her Jx, weak equity realization even in position, and it performs badly against any squeeze from the blinds. A 3-bet bluff with this exact hand is also too low-quality given reverse implied odds when called. Best is to pass and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 2c, a very weak suited hand. Minni raised from UTG to 300 (3bb). With 4 players left, UTG is effectively the CO/UTG position, so their range is likely decent, though Minni has been active. I am getting pot odds of 250 to call into a pot of 450 (roughly 1.8:1 or 35% equity needed), but I will be playing out of position against the aggressor and have the Big Blind left to act behind me who could squeeze. 62s does not have enough playability or equity to justify a call here, and 3-betting as a bluff is ill-advised with such poor card quality. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 4d, a very weak starting hand. Minni raised to 300 from the UTG position (4-handed). To call, I need to commit 200 more into a pot of 450. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, this hand has poor playability and realization of equity out of position, especially against an active opener like Minni. Given my healthy stack size, there is no reason to defend such a marginal hand.

4-handed UTG ranges can be fairly wide, but 6♠5♥ offsuit is still a low-equity hand that plays poorly out of position (dominated by higher connected hands and easily forced to fold versus 3-bets/isolations). With aggressive blinds behind and deep stacks, limping invites punishment and opening risks getting played back without strong realizability. Best to pass and preserve stack for higher EV opens.

I am on the Button with a strong suited connector (QJs). The UTG player has folded. With a stack of over 70 BBs, I should open-raise to build the pot and utilize my positional advantage. My standard open size in this tournament has been 2.5 BB (250 chips), which has been effective in stealing blinds or setting up a manageable pot in position. QJs plays well post-flop. Therefore, I will raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce suited, which is a strong hand in 4-handed play. The Button has opened with a standard raise. In this position, I prefer a 3-bet strategy to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately, especially since playing out of position post-flop is difficult. The Ace blocker makes this a good candidate for a raise. I will 3-bet to 850 to put pressure on the Button's wide opening range and the Big Blind.

UTG (Pro) opened to 250 and SB (Flash) 3‑bet to 850. I’m in the big blind with K♣3♠ — a weak, unconnected, off‑suit hand that fares poorly multi‑way and out of position. Calling 750 into a 1200 pot would require ~38.5% equity (750/(1200+750)) to be +EV, which K3o doesn’t have vs an opener + 3‑bet range. A squeeze bluff is possible in theory but K3o has no blockers, no suit, and deep stacks mean callers or a 4‑bet could put me in a very difficult spot. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am facing a 3-bet to 850 from the Small Blind after opening from the Button. The pot is currently 1200, and I need to call 600 more, giving me pot odds of roughly 33% (600/1800). With QhJh, I have a strong suited connector that plays very well in position. My equity against a standard SB 3-betting range is sufficient to call, especially given the positional advantage which will help me realize that equity. We are fairly deep (68 BB effective), allowing room for post-flop play. Folding is too tight given the price and position, and 4-betting is unnecessary. Calling is the most +EV decision.

I have top pair with an Ace on a relatively dry board. As the pre-flop 3-better, I have a significant range advantage on this Ace-high texture, as my range contains many strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) and big pocket pairs. Pro's range for calling a 3-bet likely includes mid-to-low pocket pairs and some suited connectors or weaker Aces. By betting here, I can extract value from those mid-pairs or draws while protecting my hand and maintaining the initiative. A standard continuation bet of roughly 1/3 the pot is effective for this board texture to keep the pot manageable and allow for further action on later streets.

I am facing a continuation bet of 600 (1/3 pot) on a board of [4h 7s As]. The Ace-high texture heavily favors the pre-flop 3-bettor's range (AK, AQ, AA), giving the opponent a significant range advantage. My hand, JhQh, has missed the flop completely, holding only Queen-high with a backdoor flush draw. While the price is enticing (getting 4:1 odds), my equity against a strong range containing an Ace is very low (likely <15%), and it will be difficult to continue on future streets without immediate improvement. Floating here with just backdoors and no immediate draw is a losing play in the long run against a range that connects so well with the board. Preserving my 62BB stack is prioritized.

I am in the UTG position (which is effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 9s6s. My stack is roughly 62 BBs, while all my opponents have significantly larger stacks (139bb+). 9s6s is a suited one-gapper but is generally too weak to open profitably from the first position, even short-handed. It plays poorly against 3-bets, which the deep-stacked opponents might employ aggressively. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand to enter the pot.

I am on the Button with Qd 9h in a 4-handed game. Since the action folded to me, this is a standard opening spot. Q9o is a solid hand to play in position against the blinds. I will open for 300 (3x the big blind) to attempt to take down the blinds or play a pot in position.

I’m in the SB with T9o facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need to commit 250 more (300 total including my 50 SB). With deep stacks (~219 BB) T9o has good post‑flop playability (pair+straight/runner possibilities) so I prefer to flat-call rather than 3‑bet out of position or fold. A minimal 3‑bet to 500 is too small to fold out hands and leaves me OOP; calling keeps the pot controllable and preserves implied odds. Plan: see the flop, continue aggressively with top pair/strong draws, and fold to heavy action on dry/unfavorable boards.

We’re getting a good price (200 to win 700), but 43o realizes equity extremely poorly in a 3-way pot: dominated when we make a pair, weak straight potential, and we’ll be out of position versus the preflop raiser with a caller behind. With deep stacks, the reverse-implied-odds risk is high and there’s no credible squeeze/bluff option with this holding. Preserve stack and wait for better defend/squeeze candidates.

Out of position on a very bad texture for T9o: flop A♠ K♦ 6♦ with two diamonds. I have no pair and only weak backdoor equity (one diamond and runner-runner straight possibilities). The button’s raising range contains a lot of A/K and many hands that currently beat me, so I don’t want to build the pot out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot and see how Flash reacts. Plan: fold to a sizable c-bet (≈>1/2 pot) since my equity is low; I may call a very small probe (<=~1/3 pot) to realize backdoor outs, and if Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn card and re-evaluate (look to barrel only if I pick up real equity such as a T/9/diamond).

I raised pre-flop and was called by the Small Blind. The flop comes A-K-6, which is a very strong board for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Although I have missed the flop entirely with Queen-high, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. My opponent checked, indicating weakness or a marginal hand. By betting here, I represent a strong range including Aces, Kings, and broadway draws. A standard half-pot bet should be sufficient to force a fold from many of my opponent's marginal holdings that missed this board.

Flash's flop raise (350 into a ~1050 pot ≈ 1/3 pot) is a small probe. On A♠ K♦ 6♦ my T♠9♦ has no pair and only weak backdoor outs (one diamond plus runner‑runner straight possibilities). I don't want to build a big pot out of position or bluff/raise with such marginal equity, but the bet is small enough that calling is justified to realize my backdoor equity and use implied odds with deep stacks. Plan: call the 350, keep the pot under control, and fold to significant turn aggression (> ~1/2 pot) unless I pair a T/9 or pick up a strong diamond or clear equity.

Turn (2♦) improves my hand into a four‑to‑a‑diamond: with 9♦ in hand and K♦/6♦/2♦ on board I now have 9 diamond outs (~19.6% to hit the flush on the river) and additional pair outs with tens/nines. Combined chance to hit a pair or the flush on the river is roughly 14/46 ≈ 30.4%. Pot is 1400 and I’m out of position against the button who was the last aggressor. Betting out here as a semi‑bluff risks a raise/shove from lots of made hands (Ax, Kx, sets) and builds a pot when I only have a draw; checking preserves pot control, induces bluffs, and lets me realize my equity for a single card. Plan: check. If Flash leads a small–medium bet (small fraction of pot up to ~1/4–1/2 pot) I’ll call to realize my ~30% combined equity; I’ll fold to very large turn/river aggression that gives poor pot odds or clearly represents a made hand.

On the turn, the board has become increasingly coordinated with three diamonds (6d, Kd, 2d) along with an Ace and a King. I hold the Queen of Diamonds, giving me a flush draw. After my continuation bet on the flop was called by Minni, her check on the turn suggests she likely has a piece of the board, such as an Ace or a King, and is looking to reach a showdown or is wary of the flush possibility. While I could continue to represent a strong hand with a second barrel, checking back is a more prudent play here. It allows me to realize my equity by seeing a free river card and keeps the pot size manageable. If a diamond hits the river, I will have a very strong hand; if not, I can decide based on her action whether to attempt a bluff or fold.

I rivered a diamond flush (9♦ + K♦6♦2♦7♦ on board). Button (Flash) was the preflop raiser, c‑bet small on the flop and then checked the turn — a line consistent with a lot of Ax/Kx and some non‑nut diamond combos. My hand is strong vs most of that range (beats A♠/Kx and single‑pair hands) but is vulnerable to the rare A♦ (nut flush) or other higher diamond holdings. I want to extract value from worse hands while not bloating the pot where I can be crushed. Bet about half the pot (≈700 into 1400) is a good balance — it gets called by top pairs and K/A second‑pairs but is small enough to fold to a large raise (which would represent a superior flush). Action: value bet 700, fold or strongly re‑evaluate only to a large river raise.

On the river, the board shows 6d-Kd-As-2d-7d, which completes a four-flush. Holding the Qd, I have the second-highest possible flush because the Kd is on the board and only the Ad beats me. Minni's lead of 700 into a 1400 pot suggests she has a made hand, likely a flush. While I have the second nuts, raising on a four-flush board often only gets action from the nuts (the Ad) and folds out the hands I beat (like a Jack-high or Ten-high flush). Calling is the most prudent play to ensure I win the pot while protecting myself against the only hand that has me beat.

I am dealt 3h 4c in the UTG position. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker. In a 4-handed game, opening from UTG requires a reasonably strong range to defend against the button and blinds. Playing such a low-equity hand out of position is a losing strategy in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I’m on the button with Q♦9♥ — a reasonable hand to open for a steal in 3-handed action (UTG folded). Both blinds are live: FiveTwo (SB) is capable of defending, Pro (BB) will defend some but is a middling stack. I want a standard aggressive play to capitalize on position and fold equity while keeping a readable sizing. A 3x open (300 total) is the conventional size here — it applies pressure, denies good odds to the blinds, and gives me position post‑flop if called. Plan: if both fold I take the blinds; if I face a single call I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and play pot control with Q9o; if I face a 3-bet I’ll fold to a large reraise (or consider calling a small 3-bet depending on sizing and opponent).

BTN opens to 3x with a wide range, but 7♣6♦ offsuit is a weak defend from the small blind: it’s dominated often, plays poorly multiway if BB comes along, and we’ll realize equity badly out of position without the suitedness to generate strong draws. With no ante and a relatively large open size, this is a marginal/negative-EV spot compared to waiting for better 3-bet bluffs (with blockers) or better call candidates (suited/connected).

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 3s, a very weak off-suit hand (trash). Minni raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. The Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 chips into a pot of 450, offering pot odds of roughly 30.7%. While I am getting decent odds, 83o has poor playability and equity, especially out of position against an aggressive opener. Defending here would likely result in check-folding most flops or being dominated. Preserving my stack of roughly 61 BB is preferable to bleeding chips with a bottom-tier hand.

4-handed UTG with Tc9s (offsuit). With deep stacks (~207bb) and only three opponents, this is a playable, connected hand with good postflop playability. I want initiative and to avoid a multiway pot where T9o loses value, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp. A standard 3x BB to 300 builds the pot, gains fold equity, and keeps my range balanced. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold or re-evaluate vs the specific opponent; if called I’ll play for position and look to c-bet favorable flops.

Minni’s 3bb UTG open is fairly large for 4-handed, which tightens my button continue range. K7o is dominated too often by her continuing range (all better Kx, many Ax, broadways) and has poor playability when called or when the blinds come along/squeeze. With no strong blocker structure beyond a single K and plenty of better spots to apply pressure, I avoid the reverse-implied-odds spot and preserve stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s3s, facing a raise to 300 from Minni in UTG. My hand is very weak, despite being suited. Playing 63s out of position against a UTG opening range is not profitable due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. I need to call 250 chips, which is a significant portion of the blind level relative to the strength of my hand. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. Minni opened to 3 big blinds (300) from UTG in a 4-handed game. My hand is strong enough to defend against a standard opening range in a short-handed setup, especially given the pot odds of 3.25:1 (calling 200 to win a pot of 450). While 3-betting is an option to try and take the pot down immediately, Minni has been active and has a deep stack, so calling to see a flop is a more prudent way to utilize my position and hand strength without bloating the pot unnecessarily out of position.

I have middle pair (Eights) with an Ace kicker on a J-8-3 board with a flush draw possible. In a 4-handed game, this hand has significant showdown value. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to see how they evaluate the board. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands or draws, whereas checking allows Minni to bluff or bet with a wider range that I currently beat. I am planning to call a standard continuation bet to see how the turn develops.

I opened preflop and am now heads‑up on a Jc‑8c‑3h flop holding Tc9s. This is a very playable flop for me: I have an open‑ended straight draw (7 or Q completes — 8 outs → ~17% to hit the turn, ~31.5% by the river) plus a tiny backdoor club flush possibility. As the preflop aggressor and in position, I want to apply pressure to win the pot now and build a pot when I have fold equity and good equity if called. A standard continuation bet around half the pot charges weaker pairs and random broadway/club floats while keeping my range balanced. If I face a large raise I will generally fold (I’m a draw-heavy hand), but if I get a call I can realize my equity and decide on the turn based on the card. Bet half pot (325).

I have middle pair (Eights) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a board of J-8-3. In a 4-handed game, middle pair is a strong hand that should not be folded to a single continuation bet, especially one of half-pot (325 into 650). Raising here might isolate me against better hands like a Jack or an overpair, and it could drive out Minni's bluffs or weaker draws. Calling allows me to control the size of the pot, keep Minni's range wide, and use my hand's showdown value. I will call to see how the turn card changes the board texture.

On the turn, the Ace of Clubs is a complex card. It improves my hand to two pair (Aces and Eights), which is extremely strong in a 4-handed game. However, it also completes potential flushes. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), checking is the most strategic move. It allows Minni to continue bluffing with hands that I now beat or to bet for value with a weaker Ace. If Minni has already made a flush, checking helps me control the size of the pot and evaluate based on their bet sizing. Lead-betting here would likely fold out the bluffs I want to keep in and isolate me against better hands like flushes or sets.

On the flop I c‑bet half pot with T♣9♠ and got called. The turn A♣ is a double‑edged card: it gives me a big one‑card flush draw (Tc + Jc 8c Ac = 4 clubs; 9 clubs left) and I still have my straight outs (7s and Qs), but it also heavily improves the caller’s range (any Ax or many pairs). Counting unique river outs: 9 clubs + (four 7s + four Qs) minus the two club‑outs already counted (7♣, Q♣) = 15 unique outs ≈ 15/46 ≈ 32.6% to make at least a straight or flush on the river. With the Ace on board and the villain checking, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a draw versus a range that often has me crushed. I’d prefer pot control and to realize equity — check the turn and see the river. If Villain leads the river I’ll fold to very large bets and call reasonable ones (or value‑raise if I complete). So: check and proceed to the river with ~33% equity rather than risking chips now as a semi‑bluff.

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Eights) on the turn. The board currently shows three clubs (8c, Jc, Ac), which makes a flush a possibility for Minni. However, Minni's decision to check behind on the turn after the Ace of Clubs appeared is a strong indicator that she likely does not have a flush or a strong Ace, as she would typically bet those for value or protection against further draws. The Queen of Diamonds on the river doesn't change much, though it does complete a very unlikely straight (KT). Since I am out of position and Minni has shown weakness by checking the turn, I will lead out with a value bet. A bet of 650 (exactly half the pot) is designed to get called by a Jack or a Queen, or perhaps a weaker Ace that was pot-controlling. If she has a flush and was slow-playing, I can evaluate my next move based on her raise, but a lead-out here maximizes value against her marginal holding range.

I made a straight on the river (8-9-10-J-Q) with Tc9s. Flash's line — check-call flop, check-turn, then a small 650 into a 1950 pot on the river — is consistent with thin value (Qx, Jx, A with a queen kicker) or a small blocking bet/bluff. The only hands that beat my straight are club flushes (villain would have needed two clubs), which are possible but not the majority of his calling/leading range. Calling 650 gives ~4:1 pot odds and extracts value from pairs and bluffs while avoiding enlarging the pot against the flush-heavy part of his range. Raising risks getting called by the exact hands that beat me, so the +EV play is to call and realize my equity.

4-handed from UTG our opening range should be fairly wide, and ATh suited is comfortably ahead of typical blind/BTN defending ranges. Limping gives up initiative and invites multiway pots against aggressive opponents; raising lets us win the blinds often and play in position postflop versus the blinds when called. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open to apply pressure without bloating the pot too much against potential 3-bets.

I am holding 7d 3s on the Button, which is 7-3 offsuit, statistically one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. FiveTwo has opened with a raise to 250 from Under the Gun (UTG), indicating a potentially strong range. Given my hand's lack of playability and equity, combined with the positional disadvantage of calling a UTG raise with trash, the only correct decision is to fold immediately.

I have been dealt pocket tens (Td, Tc) in the small blind. This is a very strong hand in a four-handed game. FiveTwo, who has been an active and aggressive player, opened for 250 from UTG. I want to build a pot and potentially take the initiative or win the hand pre-flop. A 3-bet is standard here to protect my equity and define FiveTwo's range. I will raise to 900, which is approximately 3.6x the opening raise, a standard sizing from out of position.

I have 9♣3♣ in the big blind facing an UTG open to 250 and a SB 3-bet to 900. This is a weak, low suited hand with poor playability out of position in a multiway pot. Calling 800 for marginal pot odds will put me in tough spots postflop with low equity and likely dominated. A light 3-bet to 1550 is also unattractive — the SB's sizing shows strength and UTG can still continue; I don't want to bloat a pot out of position with junk. With a healthy stack and many hands left, folding and waiting for a better spot is the +EV play.

Flash’s SB 3-bet to 900 is fairly large, but AThh has plenty of equity and plays very well in position. Calling keeps the SPR healthy and avoids building a low-SPR 4-bet pot where we can end up over-committing with dominated top pair versus AK/AQ/overpairs. We also keep in his bluffs and weaker suited broadways that will continuation-bet too much postflop, letting us realize our equity and leverage position.

I 3-bet pre-flop with pocket tens, but the flop comes with an Ace, which is the most significant scare card for my hand. Since I was the aggressor pre-flop, I could represent the Ace with a continuation bet, but FiveTwo's range for calling a 3-bet from UTG often includes several suited and unsuited Aces (like AQ, AJ, or ATs). By checking, I am exercising pot control and looking to see how FiveTwo reacts to the board. If he checks back, I likely have the best hand against his middle pairs (66-99) or broadway cards that missed. If he bets, I can evaluate the sizing to determine if he's trying to push me off the pot or betting for value with an Ace.

Flash 3-bet pre and then checks on an A-high board that normally favors the 3-bettor, which often caps him toward KK/QQ/JJ, weaker Ax that wants pot control, or give-ups with broadways (sometimes with a spade). With AhTh we have top pair with a medium kicker: strong enough to bet for thin value and protection, but not strong enough to play a big pot versus a check-raise. A small c-bet pressures his underpairs and unpaired hands, denies equity to overcards/spade floats, and still gets calls from hands like KK/QQ/JJ and some Ax. If raised, we can comfortably fold and avoid getting stacked by AK/AQ/sets.

I have pocket tens and 3-bet pre-flop. The flop contains an Ace, which is in FiveTwo's range, but his bet of 600 into a 1,900 pot (roughly 31%) is small and could be a probe bet with a wide range of hands, including lower pairs or spade draws. I will call to see a turn card and evaluate his further aggression, as folding now would be too exploitable given my pre-flop strength and the small bet sizing.

I have pocket tens on an Ace-high board that now also contains a Jack. I checked and called a small bet on the flop to see where I stood. The Jack of Hearts on the turn is another overcard that could have improved FiveTwo's range (specifically hands like AJ or JJ). Given that I am out of position and my hand has moderate showdown value but is unlikely to get called by worse if I bet, I will check. This allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially reach a cheap showdown or evaluate a river bet.

Flash’s check-call on the A-high flop after 3-betting pre keeps a lot of his range as medium strength (KK/QQ/TT), some trapped Ax (AK/AQ/AJ), and some spade floats/draws. The J♣/J♥ turn is a card that can improve parts of his continuing range (AJ, JJ), so we don’t want to bloat the pot with a big sizing or put ourselves in a tough spot versus a check-raise. However, giving a free card is also costly versus his spade draws and overcard + spade combos. I’ll take the middle road: a small-to-medium turn bet for protection and thin value, sized to keep our range credible while allowing an easy fold if he springs a check-raise (which will be heavily weighted to AJ+/sets).

I am currently in a tough spot with pocket tens on a board that contains two overcards (Ace and Jack). However, FiveTwo's betting behavior is somewhat confusing; his flop bet was small (600 into 1,900) and his turn bet is even smaller relative to the pot (900 into 4,000, which is 22.5%). This sizing often indicates either a marginal made hand looking for a cheap showdown, a blocking bet with a draw like spades, or an attempt to induce a raise. Given that I am getting nearly 4.5-to-1 odds on a call, folding here would be too exploitable. My tens still beat all spade draws (like KQs, QTs) and smaller pocket pairs he might be turning into a bluff. I will call this small bet to see the river and evaluate his final action, as I only need to be right about 18% of the time to justify the call.

On this river, the board texture remains relatively static with the Seven of Hearts. I have pocket tens, which is currently a mid-strength hand behind the Ace and the Jack on the board. Throughout the hand, FiveTwo has consistently bet with small, non-standard sizing, which could indicate either a marginal made hand (like a weak Ace or a Jack) or a missed draw (specifically the spade draw that failed to materialize). Leading out with a bet here doesn't serve much purpose, as I'm unlikely to be called by weaker hands and will only be called or raised by hands that beat me (Aces or Jacks). By checking, I allow FiveTwo to potentially bluff with missed draws or check back with hands that have similar or slightly less showdown value, like smaller pocket pairs. This check-call line is the most effective way to reach a showdown while controlling the pot size.

River (A♠5♣4♠J♥7♥) after SB 3-bet pre and then check-calling flop and turn, Flash’s range is heavily weighted toward Ax (AK/AQ/AJ/AT) plus some stubborn underpairs (KK/QQ) and missed spade draws. With A♥T♥ we have solid showdown value, but a third value bet is very thin: most worse hands either fold (missed spades) or may even fold underpairs facing three streets, while better Ax will happily call and we also open ourselves up to an annoying check-raise spot. Taking the free showdown keeps our EV high and variance low in this medium-strength top-pair spot.

I am in UTG (which acts as the Cutoff in 4-handed play) with Qd 9c. My stack is 60.75 BB. In a 4-handed game, opening ranges are wider, but Q9o is still on the weaker side of a Cutoff opening range. It is easily dominated by hands like KQ, AQ, AJ, QJ, A9, etc., which opponents in position or in the blinds might defend with. Being the shortest stack at the table (though still relatively deep), I should avoid opening marginal hands that can lead to difficult post-flop spots or bleeding chips to 3-bets. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

I am on the button with 7c 6c, a solid speculative hand that plays well in position. The UTG player has folded. Opening this hand from the button is standard in 4-max play to either take down the blinds or play a pot with positional advantage. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind).

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind with J♣4♥ (J4o). This is a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position. Calling 250 commits me to play OOP against the button (and possibly the big blind), often in multiway pots where J4o has very little equity. A 3‑bet bluff is too speculative with the big blind still to act and stacks deep enough to call or 4‑bet. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position — fold.

Button is opening wide and we’re getting a solid price to defend (200 to win 450). A7 offsuit plays reasonably as a call but is a bit too dominated to bloat the pot OOP with a 3-bet at these stack depths, especially versus an opponent who has shown willingness to continue versus 3-bets. I’ll defend by calling and proceed cautiously on ace-high flops versus sustained aggression.

We’re OOP in a single-raised pot on a board (J♦ T♠ 2♦) that favors the Button’s opening range (lots of broadways, Jx/Tx, and strong draws). Our A♠7♥ is just ace-high with no pair and only weak backdoor potential, so a donk-bet doesn’t apply much pressure to better hands and risks bloating the pot with little equity. Best is to check our range and respond based on sizing—generally folding to normal c-bets, only considering continuing versus very small bets.

On this board of Jd Ts 2d, my hand (7c 6c) has missed completely. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture hits the Big Blind's defending range quite well, as it contains many broadway cards and diamond draws. Given that I have zero equity and the board is highly coordinated, a continuation bet is unlikely to succeed often enough to be profitable. I will check back to see a free turn card and potentially realize some very slim equity if the board improves my hand.

Flash’s flop check-back caps his range a bit (more ace-highs, medium pairs, some marginal Jx/Tx that didn’t want to build a big pot). The Q♥ turn is a strong card for our BB defending range (we have plenty of Kx, Qx, two-pair/straight combos), and we also pick up a gutshot to the K for Broadway. With A♠7♥ we don’t win often at showdown if we check twice, so I’ll probe as a bluff to fold out his air/underpairs and deny equity, while keeping a plan to fold to a raise and mostly slow down unless we improve on the river.

I started the hand with 7c 6c and raised pre-flop, but I have completely missed this board. The turn card is the Queen of Hearts, making the board Jd-Ts-2d-Qh. This board is very dangerous as it connects with many cards in the Big Blind's range (Kings, Queens, Jacks, Tens, and various straight draws). After I checked the flop, the opponent (FiveTwo) has led out for 400 into a 650 pot. With only 7-high and no draws to improve my hand, there is no justification for calling or attempting a bluff on such a coordinated board. I will fold.

I am holding 94 offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position in a 4-handed game. This is a very weak starting hand with poor equity and post-flop playability. Even in a short-handed environment, 94o is not a hand that warrants an opening raise or a call. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

On the button with JTo — a standard open-get-it-in spot to use position and fold equity. Both opponents are deep enough to defend but not so deep that we need to be ultra-conservative; raising gives us initiative and often wins the blinds outright. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) — big enough to make the SB/BB pay to see a flop and to avoid giving good pot odds, but small enough to keep postflop play manageable. Plan: take the lead with continuation bets on dry/favorable flops, play cautiously on very wet boards, and fold to a large, committed 3-bet (JTo is too often dominated); consider calling a small squeeze depending on opponent sizing and tendencies.

Minni is opening the Button frequently and has shown she can fold to 3-bets. A2o is too weak and dominated to defend profitably as a flat from the SB (poor playability OOP, reverse implied odds when we flop an ace), but it has good blocker value (removes AA/AK/AQ combos) to use in a polarized 3-bet bluff. By 3-betting we also deny the BB a cheap realization and give ourselves initiative to win many flops with a c-bet.

I hold 4h 3c in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand (offsuit low connectors). The action before me involves a raise from the Button to 300 and a 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1100. Facing a 3-bet with this holding makes this an automatic fold. My equity is extremely low against their ranges, and the price to call is too high relative to the pot odds and implied odds. I will fold immediately to preserve my stack of nearly 60bb.

I opened to 300 on the button with TcJh and got 3‑bet to 1,100 from the SB. I’ll call to 1,100. JTo is too good to fold here — it has decent equity and excellent post‑flop playability in position — and calling keeps the pot manageable while preserving our positional advantage. A 4‑bet bluff (min to 1,900 or larger) is too thin versus this sizing and would bloat the pot without sufficient fold equity against a likely fairly strong/linear 3‑bet range. Plan: use position postflop to realize equity, float or defend against c‑bets on favorable boards, and fold to heavy action (large 4‑bet shove or clear value lines) if villain shows strong commitment.

As the 3-bettor I keep a high c-bet frequency on this dry, paired flop (K55) where my range has lots of strong Kx/overpairs and Minni has plenty of hands that miss (broadways, suited connectors, some small pairs). With A2o I don’t want to build a big pot when called, but a small range c-bet pressures her air/under-realizers and often wins immediately while keeping my risk low and preserving barreling options on favorable turns (A/Q/J/T, etc.).

Board: Ks 5d 5h. I’m on the button with Tc Jh. FiveTwo (SB) 3‑bet preflop and now makes a small c‑bet 600 into a 2900 pot (~20% pot) — a probe bet that can include a lot of hands (Kx, small/medium pairs, broadway combos and some bluffs). Calling 600 is cheap (call 600 to win ~3500 → ~17% immediate pot odds) and keeps the initiative in reach since I’m in position. My hand has limited immediate showdown value but has backdoor straight possibilities and two overcards to the 5s, plus excellent post‑flop playability in position. A raise to 1200 would be a thin semi‑bluff vs a 3‑bettor’s range that likely contains many Ks and strong pairs — I don’t want to bloat the pot without a pair or clear equity. Plan: call to float and evaluate the turn — if FiveTwo shows big aggression I can fold; if they check I can take the lead, and I’ll value/defend if I pair or pick up real equity. Therefore I will call the 600.

Turn pairs my hand (2s) giving me some real showdown value, but it’s still very thin in a 3-bet pot on K55: when Minni continues strongly she’ll often have Kx/5x or a pocket pair that won’t fold to another small barrel. Betting again mainly folds out the exact hands I’m already ahead of (A-high/broadway floats), while bloating the pot versus her stronger continuing range. By checking I control pot size, keep my range protected (I can also check strong hands here), and give her room to bluff with missed overcards—hands that I can often pick off with this pair depending on sizing.

Pot 3500, I’m on the button with TcJh (J‑T offsuit) after calling a SB 3‑bet preflop and calling a small flop c‑bet. Turn checked around (Ks 5d 5h 2d) and I have no pair and only 6 clean outs (3 Js + 3 Ts) to improve on the river — 6/46 ≈ 13% equity. FiveTwo’s line (3‑bet pre, small flop c‑bet, check turn) looks weak or medium strength rather than a locking value hand, but they still can have Kx, a 5, or a set. Betting the turn would be a thin bluff that bloats the pot with very little equity and invites calls from Kx/5x; checking keeps the pot controlled and preserves my ability to see a cheap river in position. I’ll check and plan to re‑evaluate on the river: bluff if they check again and the card is favorable, or fold/call depending on sizing if they show aggression.

River gives us 5-5-2-2-A (two pair), which is decent showdown value but not a hand that wants to bet for value in this 3-bet pot. After Minni calls flop on K55 and checks back turn, her range contains plenty of Kx, pocket pairs (66–QQ), and occasional 5x/slowplays that all beat our two pair. The hands we beat (missed AQ/AJ/QJ-type floats) mostly won’t call a river bet, so betting risks getting called by better and folding out worse. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity, and can induce a bluff from her missed overcards.

The river (Td) pairs my Ten and gives me two pair — Tens and Fives. Opponent (FiveTwo) 3‑bet preflop then used a small flop probe-bet and checked the turn; that line is ambiguous (can contain Kx, medium pocket pairs, and some bluffs). Many hands in a 3‑bet/CB range actually beat my two pair (Kx, QQ+/some pocket pairs that make two‑pair or better), but there will also be a meaningful fraction of worse hands (lower pocket pairs, missed broadways, some turn/river freebies) that will pay a modest river bet. I want to extract thin value now and fold out pure air — a half‑pot river bet is the right balance: large enough to get called by worse and small enough not to overcommit if I’m behind. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (1750). If I face a sizable raise I’ll assume I’m beat (Kx/full house/trips) and fold; if called I’ll go to showdown and evaluate.

We arrive at the river with Ac2s on Ks-5d-5h-2d-Td, giving us two pair (5s and 2s) with an Ace kicker. This is not a hand that can raise for value (worse hands rarely call), and if we raise as a bluff we risk running into a range that contains plenty of Kx, pocket pairs 66–QQ, and some 5x that can continue.

Facing Minni’s 1/3-pot bet (1750 into 5250), we’re getting a good price: we need about 25% equity to call. Her turn check after calling the flop c-bet caps her range somewhat (fewer strong, multi-street value hands), and she still has missed overcards/broadway floats and some diamond-based hands that can take this sizing as a bluff. Even though most of her pure value hands beat our two pair, the small sizing and her likely missed-high-card region make calling the best way to realize equity and avoid over-folding versus river stabs.

So we bluff-catch and call.

Q9s (queen-nine of diamonds) is a reasonable open in a 4‑hand game — UTG in 4‑max is much looser than full ring. I want to take the initiative, build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop and has flush/straight potential. I’ll open‑raise to 3×BB (300) — big enough to get fold equity and discourage limp multi‑way pots, but small enough to keep weaker callers. Plan: c‑bet favorable dry flops, control pot size on highly connected boards, and fold to large 3‑bets/shoves unless pot odds and position make a call + postflop play realistic.

Minni’s 3bb UTG open (4-handed) is still weighted toward stronger broadways/aces and pairs. Td6c is offsuit, poorly connected, and heavily dominated when we make a one-pair hand (T-x or 6-x), which creates reverse implied odds even with position. Calling also invites squeezes from the SB (shorter stack) and BB, and we don’t have good coverage to continue versus aggression. With plenty of playable chips behind, this is an easy spot to preserve stack and wait for higher-EV button defenses (suited/connectors, better broadways).

I hold 9h 3s in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em. Facing a raise to 300 from the UTG player (Minni), who is the chip leader, I have no reason to get involved. I am out of position and the shortest stack at the table (approx 60 BBs). Conserving chips for a playable hand is the only logical move here. Folding is standard.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. Minni, who has been quite active and has a large stack, has raised to 300 from the UTG position (which is effectively the cutoff in this 4-handed game). While the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 450, Ten-Deuce offsuit is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially when out of position against a strong opponent. There is no reason to defend such a marginal hand here.

4-handed widens ranges, but UTG is still the tightest seat and Q3s is near the bottom: it’s frequently dominated by better queens (Qx) and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus the blinds, who can defend/3-bet aggressively. Limping is especially unattractive (invites isolation, plays poorly multiway). With no dead money beyond blinds, I preserve stack and wait for cleaner opens/spots.

I hold Pocket Tens (Ts Td) on the Button in a 4-handed game, which is a premium starting hand. Action has folded to me. I should raise to build the pot for value and deny free equity to the blinds. A standard open-raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is appropriate here to balance my range and put pressure on the blinds while playing in position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 2s. Pro has raised to 2.5x from the button. While 92s is suited, it is an extremely weak hand to play out of position against a button open. Calling would leave me out of position for the rest of the hand with poor playability, and raising as a bluff is unnecessary given my current stack size and the fact that the chip leader (Minni) is still to act in the Big Blind. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips.

Faced with a button open to 250 heads-up: pot is 400 and I must call 150 (≈27% immediate pot odds). My hand 6♦3♥ is weak, unsuited, poorly connected and is heavily dominated by a typical button opening range. I'll be out-of-position postflop, so realization and bluff opportunities are limited. A 3-bet bluff is too speculative with this holding and calling leads to difficult, low-expected-value postflop decisions. With a deep stack and plenty of better spots to pick up chips, I fold and conserve my stack.

In a 4-handed game, UTG acts as the Cutoff. A7o is a borderline hand to open from this position. While I have successfully stolen blinds with marginal hands recently (e.g., K7o in Hand #66), I am currently the shortest stack at the table (approx 60bb) facing three deep-stacked opponents who are capable of applying pressure. A7o plays poorly post-flop and is easily dominated by the calling ranges of the deep stacks (A8+, broadways). Opening invites 3-bets which would force me to fold, or leads to difficult out-of-position play. Given the stack dynamics and the marginal nature of the hand, folding is the disciplined, lower-variance choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey offsuit. While it is a potential stealing position, T3o is a very weak hand to attempt a steal against active blinds like Minni and FiveTwo, who have shown a willingness to defend or re-raise. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up against FiveTwo (only the big blind left). 7♣5♥ is a weak but playable hand in this spot — I want to take the initiative rather than play OOP passively. Standard exploitative play from the small blind is an open to ~2.5–3x BB to apply pressure and pick up the pot or play the flop with fold equity. I'll raise to 300 total (I’ve posted 50, so +250) — a size consistent with my opening frequency and big enough to discourage easy floats. If FiveTwo 3-bets large I can fold and preserve most of my stack; if he calls I can c-bet favorable flops and continue aggression.

Facing Minni’s 3x SB open, we’re getting a workable price but 43o has very poor playability and realization out of position (dominated often, makes weak pairs, and we’ll be forced to overfold vs c-bets). This is also a bad 3-bet bluff candidate because when called we have low equity and awkward postflop. Best EV is to concede the blind and preserve stack for better defend/3-bet combos.

I am dealt 10-5 offsuit in the UTG position. This is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially when out of position. Given the table dynamics and my current stack size, there is no strategic reason to open this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

On the button with K♦4♥ I have a marginal but positionally valuable hand. I want to apply pressure and avoid giving cheap flops to the blinds, so I’ll open-raise a standard amount (3x BB) to try to steal the blinds. If I get a large 3-bet from either blind I will fold (bad kicker, poor equity); if I get called I’ll use position to continuation-bet favorable flops or control pot size when checked to. The raise is a small, low-risk way to stay aggressive and accumulate chips from the blinds.

Button is opening to 3bb and we’re getting a solid price (250 to win 450) with a suited connector that realizes well when it flops equity (pairs + straight/flush draws). 3-betting 6♠5♠ would bloat the pot out of position against a deeper, likely wide opener and can force us into tougher postflop spots; flatting keeps our range protected and lets us play a multi-street hand when we connect. If BB squeezes large or jams, we can comfortably fold this hand.

I am in the Big Blind with KJo. The Button raised to 300 and the Small Blind called. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 into a pot of 700, requiring ~22% equity). KJo performs well enough against a wide Button opening range and a capped Small Blind calling range to justify a call. A squeeze raise is possible but risky given the stack depths and being out of position against the aggressor. Closing the action with a call is the standard, low-variance play.

On a paired Q-Q-T flop in a 3-way pot, the preflop raiser retains the strongest range advantage (Qx, overpairs, strong Tx), and our 6♠5♠ has essentially no made hand equity and no meaningful backdoor flush potential. Leading as a bluff into two players is low-EV and risks getting raised or called by sticky Tx/underpairs. Best is to check and proceed cautiously, continuing only if we pick up real equity on later streets.

I hold KJo on a Tc-Qd-Qc board, giving me an open-ended straight draw (needs an Ace or a 9). It is a multi-way pot with the pre-flop aggressor (Button) yet to act. The board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range or the caller in the Small Blind. Leading out (donk betting) is ill-advised as I have no showdown value yet and am out of position. The standard play is to check to the raiser to see their action. If they bet, I have sufficient equity to consider calling depending on the size, but for now, checking maximizes my options and minimizes risk.

I raised preflop from the button and both blinds checked to me on a relatively dry, paired flop T♣ Q♦ Q♣. I have K♦4♥ — no pair but a decent blocker (the king reduces KQ combos) and strong positional leverage. My preflop range credibly contains many Qs/Ts and broadway hands, so a small continuation bet can often take this pot down and keeps the pot controllable if I get called. I’ll make a small c-bet of ~1/3 pot (300). If I face a raise I’ll fold — my hand has poor showdown value against likely ranges (Qx, TT, overpairs). If called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check and fold to significant aggression unless a very favorable runout appears).

Minni’s 1/4-pot c-bet in a 3-way pot is going to be very wide, but our 6♠5♠ has essentially no real equity on Q♣Q♦T♣: no pair, no straight draw, and only a very thin runner-runner spade flush possibility (and even that can be crushed by full houses). Calling 300 needs ~20% equity (300 to win ~1500), which we don’t have, and turning this hand into a bluff-raise is low fold-equity multiway and sets us up to get continued on by any Qx/Tx/overpairs. Best is to just release and preserve stack.

I hold JcKh on a TcQdQc board. This flop gives me a massive combo draw: an open-ended straight draw (outs: A, 9) and a flush draw (I hold Jc, board has two clubs). This totals approximately 15-16 outs (counting straight cards, flush cards, and overlaps correctly), giving me tremendous equity (over 30% to hit on the turn and over 50% by the river). The opponent's bet is small (300 into 900), offering me excellent pot odds (20% required vs >30% actual equity). Raising is a valid aggressive option to fold out weaker hands, but calling is the lower-variance play that guarantees I realize my equity without risking a re-raise/shove from a trip Queen or full house. I will call and evaluate the turn.

I have turned a King-high straight with [Jc Kh] on a [Tc Qd Qc 9s] board. While this is a very strong hand, the board is paired (Queens), meaning I lose to full houses (Q9, QT, QQ, TT, 99). Given Minni is the pre-flop aggressor and c-bet the flop, checking is the standard play to keep her range wide. If she has a Queen (trips) or an overpair, she will likely bet for value, and if she has air, she may bluff. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out her bluffs and only get action from hands that have good equity or beat me (like boats). I will check and look to call a bet, evaluating the sizing.

I c‑bet a small 1/3 pot on the paired T‑Q‑Q flop and was flat‑called by the BB. The turn 9♠ changes nothing for my K♦4♥ — I still have no pair and only a king blocker to some KQ combos. Pro’s call on the flop indicates a range heavy on Qx, some Tx and medium pocket pairs; he checked the turn which is consistent with showdown or pot‑control hands. Betting the turn would be a pure bluff into a caller that likely has at least some showdown value and would often call or raise; I don’t want to bloat a pot with essentially no equity. I’ll check for pot control, preserve my stack, and re‑evaluate on the river (look to fold to meaningful aggression; consider a river bluff only on a favourable card or if he shows weakness).

I hold a King-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K) on a board of [Tc Qd Qc 9s 3c]. While my hand is very strong, the river card [3c] completes the club flush draw (there are three clubs on the board: Tc, Qc, 3c, and I hold the Jc, so I do not have a flush). The board is also paired (Queens), making full houses possible, though unlikely given the action. Minni's check-back on the Turn suggests she likely does not have a strong Queen or a full house, as she would likely bet those for value/protection on such a wet board. However, checking back a flush draw is a standard line, meaning the river card hits that part of her range. Betting here is risky because worse hands (like Tx, JJ, or air) will likely fold, while better hands (Flushes, Boats) will call or raise. Checking allows me to potentially induce a bluff from missed straight draws or catch a thin value bet from a Queen, while controlling the size of the pot if I am beaten.

Check. The river (T♣ Q♦ Q♣ 9♠ 3♣) is a complete blank for K♦4♥ — I have no pair and only a king blocker. Villain (the BB who flat‑called my small flop c‑bet) is weighted toward hands with real showdown value (Qx, some T combos, medium pairs), and his passive line (call flop, check turn) suggests he’s content to see showdown rather than fold to a delayed river bluff. My fold equity is very low here and a bluff risks chips unnecessarily; I also can’t get value with K‑4. With a small pot (1,500) relative to my stack (25,000) the +EV play is to check and go to showdown (and fold to any meaningful raise).

I’m UTG in a 4-handed game with A♠4♥ and a deep 250bb stack. In short‑handed play you should open a wider range; A4o has blocker value to the Ace and reasonable postflop playability. The min raise is 200 but a standard 3xBB open to 300 gives me initiative, fold equity and a chance to isolate the button. Plan: open-raise to 300; if I face a large, out-of-position 3-bet I’ll usually fold A4o, I’ll call small-ish 3-bets in position, and postflop I’ll c-bet dry boards, bet for value when I pair my Ace, and control the pot when I miss and multiple opponents remain.

4-handed, Minni’s UTG is effectively a cutoff open and she’s shown an active raising frequency, so her range should be wide enough that Q9o has reasonable equity on the button. I don’t love 3-betting this offsuit hand into the chip leader because when she continues/4-bets we’re often dominated and forced to overfold; calling keeps the pot small and lets us realize equity with position. If either blind squeezes to a meaningful size, we can comfortably fold and avoid a dominated spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s6s, facing a raise from Minni (UTG) and a call from FiveTwo (Button). While Minni has been playing very loosely, opening a wide range of hands, playing a low suited connector from the Small Blind in a multi-way pot is structurally disadvantageous. I would be playing the entire post-flop hand out of position against potentially stronger ranges. Although the pot odds are tempting (calling 250 into a likely pot of ~1000+), the realization of equity is difficult OOP. The disciplined play is to fold and look for spots where I have position or initiative, avoiding the risk of a squeeze from the Big Blind or bleeding chips in a difficult post-flop scenario.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh 3d. Minni raised to 300 from UTG (effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) and FiveTwo called on the Button. The pot is 750 and it costs me 200 to call, giving me pot odds of 4.75 to 1. Despite the favorable odds, Jh 3d is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. My stack is healthy (over 150 BB), and there is no reason to commit chips with such a low-equity hand against an UTG raiser and a caller. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I opened preflop and hold A♠4♥ on A♥ K♣ 2♣ — top pair but a thin kicker. The button’s cold‑call indicates a wide range (Kx, pocket pairs, suited connectors and some two‑club combos). I have initiative and the board is relatively dry, but two clubs give a small subset of strong club draws (villain holding two clubs). A value/protection continuation bet is correct: it extracts from worse made hands (Kx, medium pairs), charges the club combos, and preserves fold equity. I size to ~1/2 pot to get value while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: bet 375; if I face a large, polarized raise I’ll fold or proceed very cautiously, if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn.

We’re facing a small flop c-bet (375 into 1125), but Q9o on Ah Kc 2c has essentially no real equity: no pair, no flush backdoor (we don’t hold a club), and only very thin/backdoor straight potential. When we improve with a Q or 9 we’re often still behind (Ax/Kx and a lot of better Qx/9x), so our equity realization is poor even in position. Bluff-raising here is also unattractive versus the chip leader’s UTG range and tendency to continue with any Ax/Kx. Best is to just release and avoid spewing chips.

4-handed, UTG is effectively the hijack and AcTc suited is comfortably in our opening range. Limping invites the blinds to realize equity cheaply and creates multiway spots out of position; folding is too tight. I’ll raise for value and initiative, targeting folds from weaker broadways/suited trash while still being able to continue versus 3-bets at reasonable frequencies given stack depths.

I hold J2o on the Button, facing an opening raise to 300 from the UTG player (FiveTwo). J2o is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em with very poor playability and equity. Even in a 4-handed game where UTG ranges are wider, this hand is nowhere near strong enough to call or 3-bet bluff. With a stack of ~69 BB, I need to preserve chips for better opportunities. The correct and standard play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit. FiveTwo has raised to 300 from the Under-the-Gun position (which is also the Cutoff in this 4-handed game). Ten-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a raise from an early position player. Given the stack sizes and the need to maintain a solid image, there is no reason to defend this hand or attempt a 3-bet bluff here. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play in this situation.

Call 300. Facing an UTG open to 300 from the big blind with 9♣8♦, this hand is marginal but playable heads‑up. Pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (300 total), which requires ~31% equity to break even (200/(450+200)≈30.8%). With deep stacks I have good implied odds and postflop playability (straight possibilities, decent connectivity), so defending is correct. I won't 3‑bet — out of position with a weak/off‑suit connector that's thin and could get me into trouble against stronger UTG ranges or a 4‑bet. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds. I call to see a flop and plan to proceed cautiously if faced with strong aggression.

Board is Qd Qs Ks and I hold 9c8d — I have no pair, no spade, and only a remote backdoor straight possibility. The preflop opener (FiveTwo, UTG) has a range that hits this texture very hard (Qx, Kx, KK, QQ, strong broadways), so leading out as a bluff from out of position is a losing line most of the time. Pot is 650 and there is nothing to call, so I check to control the pot and see how the aggressor reacts. Plan: fold to any meaningful bet (give up to reasonable pressure), only entertain calling very small probe bets to try and realize a tiny amount of equity, and look for better spots later.

Heads-up vs the BB on a high, paired board (QQK) where our UTG range has a big advantage (lots of Kx/Qx/AK/AQ) and BB has many misses/pairs that hate pressure. With AcTc we also have a gutshot to Broadway (J) plus ace-high showdown value, so a small range c-bet is ideal to fold out weaker hands while keeping our risk low if we get played back at.

Board Qd Qs Ks crushes my range — FiveTwo opened UTG so his continuing range contains many Qx/Kx combos (trips, full houses, overpairs) that beat 9♣8♦. Calling 200 into the 850‑chip pot requires ≈200/(850+200) ≈ 19% equity; my actual equity with 9♣8♦ on Q Q K is essentially zero (only remote two‑card runner outs). A raise would be an unblockable, high‑variance bluff versus a strong preflop raiser and is not credible here. I preserve a big stack for better spots and fold to this flop bet.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with A5o. While having an Ace is decent, A5 offsuit is a weak hand to open from early position, even 4-handed. My stack is 69 BBs, which is comfortable, so there is no need to force action with marginal holdings out of position. Opening here risks domination by better Aces or being forced to play a tricky post-flop pot out of position if called. The standard and disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with a small pocket pair (3s 3h). In a 4-handed game, pocket pairs carry significant value pre-flop. Folding would be too tight, and just calling would be too passive. A standard 3x raise to 300 allows me to take control of the hand, potentially take down the blinds immediately, or set myself up to play a pot with position if called. My stack is healthy at 150.25 big blinds, so I can afford to play this speculatively.

Fold. T2s is a very weak holding from the small blind facing a BTN open — dominated by most of the button’s calling/raising range and playable only as a rare defend. Calling 250 commits me out of position to a multiway pot (FiveTwo still to act) with poor kicker and only a backdoor advantage (suitedness). A tiny squeeze to 500 is an option as a light bluff sometimes, but with a competent big blind behind and deep stacks I prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Losing the 50 SB is cheaper than playing a large, difficult pot out of position.

Button opens wide 4-handed, and we’re getting a good price: 200 to win 450 (need ~31% equity). Kh4h has solid realization potential with suitedness (flushes/backdoors) and reasonable top-pair coverage versus a wide BTN range. A 3-bet would bloat the pot OOP with a marginal Kx that can make dominated top pairs, so the higher-EV line is to defend by calling and play postflop with a protected range.

We’re out of position versus the Button preflop raiser on a board that strongly favors their range to c-bet at high frequency. With Kh4h we have a strong combo draw (K-high flush draw + gutshot to the 5, plus an overcard) that plays best as a check/raise candidate rather than a donk lead: checking lets Flash continue with all their air and medium hands, giving us the chance to win the pot immediately via a raise or realize our equity cheaply if they check back. Leading also risks getting raised off our equity or only getting action from stronger hands.

I have flopped a set of threes on a low, coordinated board (3d-6h-7h). This is a very strong hand, but the board presents several draw possibilities, including a flush draw and straight draws (4-5, 8-9, 5-8). FiveTwo checked to me, and I need to start building the pot and charging any draws he might be holding. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 70% of the pot to get value from 6x, 7x, overpairs, or draws.

We have a very strong semi-bluff: K-high flush draw (9 outs) plus a gutshot to the 5 (3 additional non-heart outs) and some K-overcard equity versus Flash’s wide BTN c-bet range. This board is one where BB can credibly have sets (33/66/77) and two-pair/strong pairs (76s/65s/7x), so a check-raise generates meaningful fold equity against his air/overcards and even some marginal one-pair hands, while also building a pot for when we complete. Calling is fine, but raising maximizes EV by pressuring his high-frequency c-bets and denying equity.

I have flopped bottom set (3s 3h) on a very coordinated board of 3d-6h-7h. While I have a monster hand, this board is extremely wet, featuring both a flush draw and several straight draws (4-5, 5-8, 8-9). FiveTwo's check-raise to 1500 indicates significant strength, likely a strong draw, two pair (like 67), or a higher set (66 or 77). Given the high stack-to-pot ratio and the number of 'scare cards' that could appear on the turn to either kill the action or improve my opponent's draws, I will choose to 3-bet the flop. This builds the pot immediately, charges the draws, and allows me to get value from hands like 67 or even an overpair that is playing aggressively. I will raise to 3800, which is approximately 2.5 times his raise amount.

We’re facing a flop 3-bet after check-raising on 3d-6h-7h with Kh4h. Pot odds are excellent: 2300 to win 5950 (~28% needed), and we have a very robust draw (K-high flush draw + gutshot to the 5, with some occasional K-overcard equity vs air). 

While a shove is viable with this SPR, our draw is not the nut flush draw and Flash’s 3-bet range can contain many AhXh combos that dominate us and will never fold. Since we’re already getting a great price and the SPR will be ~1 on the turn, the higher-EV/safer line is to call, keep his bluffs/semi-bluffs in, and be prepared to continue aggressively on heart/5 turns (and often to stack off versus a turn jam given our equity).

Turn is a relative brick (2d) that doesn’t improve our hand; we still have a strong but non-nut draw (Kh4h: K-high FD + gutshot to the 5). With the pot already huge (8250) and stacks ~1 pot behind, leading/jamming has very low fold equity against Flash’s flop 3-bet range (sets/overpairs/two-pair + many strong heart draws, including some AhXh that dominate our flush outs). Checking maximizes EV by (1) letting his bluffs/semi-bluffs continue, (2) sometimes realizing our equity for free if he checks back, and (3) avoiding torching chips by value-owning ourselves into a range that rarely folds.

I have bottom set on a coordinated board with straight and flush draws. My opponent, FiveTwo, has been aggressive and has now checked the turn after calling my flop 3-bet. The turn 2d is relatively safe but adds a backdoor diamond draw and doesn't change the board state significantly. I must bet for value and to charge any remaining draws (like hearts or a straight draw). A bet of 4500 (approximately 55% of the pot) maintains pressure on his range, including hands like 67, 7x, and high draws, while setting up a potential shove on the river.

We’re getting a very good price: calling 4,500 to win 12,750 means we need ~26% equity. With Kh4h on 3d-6h-7h-2d we still have a strong draw: 9 heart outs for a flush plus 3 non-heart fives for a straight (12 clean-ish outs ≈ 27% to improve on the river), and sometimes a made hand is still possible if Flash is over-bluffing this line.

Shoving has essentially zero fold equity because Flash bet 4,500 leaving only 1,925 behind; they’d be getting ~8% to call and can continue with almost any hand/draw. Calling maximizes EV by realizing our equity and keeping any bluffs/semi-bluffs in.

Plan: call now; if we hit a heart or any 5, we’re happily continuing versus the likely river jam. If we miss, we can reassess based on river card/action, but the turn call is mandatory given pot odds and draw strength.

River 5d gives us a made straight (3-4-5-6-7) with Kh4h. On this runout there is no higher straight possible, so we’re essentially at the top of the non-flush hands; the main concern is villain having a backdoor diamond flush (two diamonds in hand), which is possible but not a large portion of his flop 3-bet/turn barrel range.

Given the huge pot (17,250) and our short remaining stack (4,300), checking risks Flash checking back hands we crush (sets/two pair/overpairs) because the 5d is a scary card, and if Flash bets we’re functionally committed anyway. Best EV is to lead shove: we maximize value versus his strong made hands that will feel priced-in, and we don’t leave money on the table when he would otherwise take a free showdown.

So we bet all-in for value.

On the river, the board is 3d-6h-7h-2d-5d. I have a set of threes (bottom set from the flop). The 5d on the river is a scare card because any hand with a 4 now makes a straight (A-2-3-4-5, 3-4-5-6-7, or 4-5-6-7-8). FiveTwo has led all-in for 4300 into a pot of 17250. This gives me pot odds of roughly 5:1. While I am losing to any 4, as well as higher sets like 66 or 77, my hand beats all two-pair combinations (such as 67, 75, or 65) and any potential missed heart draws or bluffs. Given the strength of my hand and the excellent price, a fold would be too exploitable. FiveTwo's aggressive line could easily be a value bet with two-pair or a desperate attempt to pick up the pot with a missed draw.

I am currently the short stack with 2125 chips (21.25 BB) following a major loss in the previous hand. In a 4-handed game, Ace-Jack Offsuit (AJo) is a very strong starting hand. While I am in a 'danger zone' stack-wise, 21 BB is still enough to play a standard opening raise rather than an open shove. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and pick up the blinds, while remaining committed to most post-flop scenarios or a 3-bet shove from my opponents given the hand's strength in a short-handed environment.

Flash opened UTG to 300 but is very short (~18 BB). I'm on the button with pocket 4s (4d4c). Folding surrenders a playable hand with position; 3‑betting to isolate risks a shove from the short stack or a cold 4‑bet/call from the big stacks and would needlessly escalate the pot with a low pair. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves position, and allows set‑mining and post‑flop play — if I hit a set I can extract maximum value from the deep SB/BB, and if I miss I can fold to heavy action. Immediate pot odds are poor for pure set‑mining, but implied odds + position justify a flat call here. I'll call and re-evaluate on the flop.

T6o is too weak and dominated to defend from the small blind multiway versus an UTG open plus a cold call. Even getting a good price (250 to win 750), we’ll be out of position against at least one deep stack (Minni) and a short stack (Flash) who can jam over any squeeze, making a 3-bet unattractive. Best EV is to preserve chips and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 4h, a very weak off-suit hand. Flash raised from UTG and Minni called on the Button. Although the pot odds are attractive (calling 200 into a pot of 750, offering roughly 4.75 to 1), my hand has very poor equity and playability, especially out of position in a multi-way pot. Flash is also short-stacked, making post-flop maneuverability limited. Defending with 74o is not profitable in this spot. I will fold.

I have flopped trip Aces with a Jack kicker on an extremely dry board (Ac-5d-Ad). Given my short stack of 1825 chips (approx. 18 BB) and the nature of the board, I want to extract value from pocket pairs (66-KK) or high-card hands that might float. A small bet of roughly 1/3 of the pot (250 chips) looks like a standard continuation bet and is most likely to get called by a wider range than a check-raise or a large bet, which would appear too strong on such a static board.

Flop is A♣‑5♦‑A♦ and I hold 4♦4♣ — I missed my set and have an underpair to a paired Ace board. Villain (Flash) opened UTG preflop and is very short (~1.5k), and sized the c‑bet tiny (250 into a ~1000 pot). That sizing is consistent with either a value Ace (which beats me) or a probe/bluff from a wide short‑stack range. Raising here to isolate risks an immediate shove or a call with an Ace and needlessly escalates a pot where I’m usually behind. Folding now surrenders too much equity for only 250. Calling is cheap, keeps the pot controlled and preserves position so I can fold to real strength on a later shove or extract value from bluffs. Plan: call 250 and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold to significant raise/shove unless the turn meaningfully improves my relative equity or villain shows clear weakness.

I have trip Aces with a Jack kicker on a very dry and static board (Ac-5d-Ad-7h). My current stack of 1575 is quite small relative to the 1250 pot (SPR ~1.25). Minni called a small flop bet, suggesting she has a marginal hand like a pocket pair (22-44, 66-KK) or is floating. Since there are almost no draws on this board, my goal is to extract value from her 'made' hands that are currently behind. A bet of 500 chips (40% of the pot) is large enough to build the pot for a river shove while still being a size that a pocket pair might call, thinking I'm continuation-betting with air. Checking risks her checking behind with hands she would have called with.

Flash is very short (~1,075) and opened UTG, c‑bet small on the flop (250) and now bets 500 on the turn. My 4♦4♣ is an underpair to a paired Ace board (A♣ 5♦ A♦ 7♥) and is behind any A or 5; I have no meaningful draws. However the turn bet is modest relative to the pot (500 into 1,750) — calling requires ~22% equity (500/2,250) and is cheap relative to my deep stack. Raising here would be a mistake: I’m likely behind and would either fold out worse hands or induce a shove from the short stack when I’m dominated. Folding surrenders equity for a small bet. I’ll call 500 to keep the pot controlled, retain position, and reevaluate on the river — folding to a shove or continuing only if I improve (set) or villain shows clear weakness.

I have trip Aces with a Jack kicker on a board that has not changed significantly. My remaining stack is 1075 chips and the pot is 2250, resulting in a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of approximately 0.48. At this point, I am committed to the hand. Betting all-in is the most effective way to extract value from Minni's likely range, which includes pocket pairs (66-KK) or perhaps a weaker Ace. Given the size of the pot relative to my remaining chips, she is likely to call with any marginal made hand thinking I could be bluffing after a failed triple-barrel.

Board: A♣ 5♦ A♦ 7♥ 2♣. I hold 4♦4♣ — an underpair; my best current hand is A A 4 4 7 (two pair). Flash (UTG) is very short and has acted every street: opened UTG, tiny flop c‑bet, a modest turn bet, then shoved the river for ~1,075 (all‑in). Pot = 3,325; calling 1,075 gives ~24% pot odds (1,075 / 4,400 ≈ 24.4%).

Range/read: UTG open from a short stack plus this line is heavily weighted toward value on this particular board — any Ace (trips), 5/7/2 (sets → full houses), and pocket pairs >4 (making a better two‑pair on the Ace‑paired board) all beat my 44. The hands I beat are mainly pure missed holdings (broadways) and very small pocket pairs (33) — not enough frequency to justify a call. My pre‑turn plan was explicit: call the turn but fold to a shove unless I improved or saw clear weakness. This river shove is exactly the “significant raise/shove” I planned to fold to.

Decision: fold. Preserve a healthy stack and wait for a better, +EV spot rather than calling with sharply negative equity vs Flash’s likely value‑heavy all‑in range.

UTG with Q7 offsuit — a weak, easily dominated hand in a short-handed 4-player game. There are three players to act behind (button, SB, BB) and both the button and blinds have shown frequent aggression; limping or min-raising here will likely get isolation/3-bet pressure and I have poor equity multiway. With a healthy stack I can wait for a much better spot to pick up chips; folding preserves my stack and position. Fold.

Folded to us on the button 4-handed, we should be opening very wide. Ks2s has good playability (suited) and blocks strong Kx continues from the blinds. With position and a big stack edge versus both blinds (especially Flash at ~32bb), we can apply pressure while keeping our risk controlled with a smaller-than-2.5x sizing that still generates folds and keeps their shove sizes manageable.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9c 7s, a weak off-suit hand. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the massive chip leader, has opened for a standard raise to 225. Although the price to call is relatively cheap (175 chips), playing 9-7 offsuit out of position against an aggressive chip leader is a losing long-term strategy. The hand has poor playability, making it difficult to realize equity post-flop, and I risk being squeezed by the Big Blind. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack of 6775 (approx. 68 BBs).

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 7h. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has raised to 225. Minni and Pro have folded. The raise is relatively small (2.25x), and I am getting 3:1 odds to call (125 to call into a pot of 375). While 87o is a marginal hand and I will be out of position, the pot odds are favorable enough to see a flop in a 4-handed game. My stack is currently 3225 (approx. 32 BB), which gives me enough room to play a post-flop game without being forced to commit my entire stack immediately. I will call and evaluate the flop.

I have 8-high on a board of K-J-3 after calling a raise from the button. This board texture significantly favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which likely includes many Kings, Jacks, and high cards. I have no pair and no straight or flush draws. The standard play in this position is to check and likely fold to any continuation bet from FiveTwo.

We have top pair with a weak kicker (K2) on a fairly dry K-J-3 rainbow board. As the preflop raiser on the button, we retain a strong range advantage (lots of Kx, KJ, overpairs) and can c-bet at a high frequency. A small sizing accomplishes three things: (1) gets thin value from worse Kx/Jx and pocket pairs, (2) denies equity to hands like QT/T9/Ax that peeled pre, and (3) keeps the pot manageable versus a ~31bb stack that could check-jam and put us in a tough spot if we bloat it with a bigger bet.

I have 8-high on a board of King-Jack-Trey. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. The button's small continuation bet (175 into 500) is standard for a chip leader on a board that favors their range. While the price to call is relatively cheap, I have zero equity and no realistic way to win the hand without a massive bluff, which is ill-advised against the table's dominant stack. Folding is the correct disciplined play.

7-3 offsuit has terrible playability and reverse implied odds, especially out of position against the big stack in the BB who can defend widely and put pressure on later streets. With an ICM-ish dynamic (two shorter stacks behind), preserving our chip lead and avoiding marginal spots is higher EV than trying to steal with the absolute bottom of range.

I am on the Button with King-Jack offsuit, which is a strong hand to open when folded to. The UTG player has folded. My stack is roughly 68 big blinds. The Small Blind (Flash) has about 30 big blinds, and the Big Blind (Minni) is deep with ~240 big blinds. A standard open raise is appropriate here to steal the blinds or play the hand in position. I have consistently used a 2.5x raise size (250 chips) in previous hands, so I will continue with that sizing to balance my range.

I am holding Ten of Spades and Deuce of Hearts in the small blind. This is a very weak hand. Pro has raised to 2.5x from the button, which is a standard opening range. Calling out of position with such a weak hand is a losing play in the long run, and my stack size of 30.5 big blinds is best preserved for more favorable spots. Folding is the correct strategic decision here.

Facing a button open to 250 while I’m in the big blind with Q♦3♠. Pot is 400 and I must call 150 more (need ~27% equity to break even). Q3o is a weak, poorly playable hand out of position that will be dominated a lot of the time by the button’s opening range and has minimal postflop playability (only weak Q or backdoor spade). Pro has a medium stack and will c-bet frequently; calling invites difficult postflop decisions OOP and likely loss of chips. A 3-bet with this hand is too speculative. With a healthy stack and many better spots to apply pressure, the correct play is to conserve chips and fold.

I am in UTG (effectively the Cutoff in 4-handed play) with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o). This is a very weak hand with poor playability and reverse implied odds if I hit a King due to the weak kicker. With 69 big blinds, I have plenty of stack depth to wait for better spots. There is no strategic merit in opening this hand into three opponents, two of whom are deep-stacked and one who is short-stacked and might shove. Folding is the standard and correct play.

On the button in a 4-handed game, a 10-9 offsuit is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. My stack is currently the shortest at the table (approximately 30 BBs), so I need to pick up blinds and antes where possible without over-committing. Raising to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the pot while maintaining flexibility if one of the deep-stacked blinds chooses to re-raise.

I’m in the small blind with pocket 4s facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, I must add 200). I have a very deep stack (23900) while the opener is ~2800 (≈28bb), so this is a classic set-mining spot — the call is cheap relative to my stack and can be profitable if I hit a set. 3-betting to isolate isn’t appealing with such a small pair (would bloat the pot out of position and risk getting committed without much equity), and folding would forfeit too much implied value.
