I’ll call 250 total to see the flop, fold to heavy action if I miss, and look to extract value if I connect.

We’re getting a very good price in the big blind: 150 to win a 600 pot (4:1), and we’ll often realize enough equity multiway even with a weak hand because we can make disguised two-pair/straights and win bigger pots vs Minni’s deep stack. A squeeze/raise with 7-5 offsuit is too spewy given poor playability and being out of position against two players, and folding is too tight at this price.

I called preflop as a set-mining spot and the flop (4d 3c 3h) gives me a full house with 4h4s (44433) — essentially the near-nut hand on this board. Only quads (the lone 4c or an opponent holding pocket 3s) can beat me, and those are very unlikely. Two players remain: the button (aggressive opener) and a sticky big blind who calls lots of action — both have many hands that will call a sizable value bet (pocket pairs, Ax, worse full houses if they have a 3). There are essentially no realistic draws to fear, so I should bet for value rather than check and risk giving a free turn card or missing value. A pot-sized bet is appropriate to extract calls from worse hands without overfolding them. I will bet 750 into the 750 pot. If I face a large raise, I’ll re-evaluate (a big raise heavily suggests quads), but my baseline is to extract value with a pot-sized bet now.

On 4-3-3 rainbow with 7♣5♠ we only have a gutshot to the 6 (4 outs) plus some very thin backdoor ways to win. Facing a large 750 bet into a 1500 pot (and with Flash still to act behind with a short stack), we’re unlikely to realize enough equity: we’ll often be forced into tough spots versus strong ranges (3x/4x/overpairs) and can’t profitably float multiway with such poor playability. This is not a good candidate to bluff-raise either (no relevant blockers, low fold equity given sizing/line), so preserving stack is best.

Minni has led out for a full-pot bet on a paired board of 4-3-3. My 10-high (9h Ts) has no connectivity with this board and no draw. While a donk bet on this texture can sometimes be a bluff or a 'probe' bet, calling 750 chips (more than 25% of my remaining stack) with only 10-high is a losing play in the long run. I have no equity to continue, and with my current stack size, I must preserve my chips for a better spot rather than engaging in a high-variance bluff-catching scenario.

I am currently UTG with 2,800 chips (28 BBs). I have King-Trey offsuit (K3o), which is a very weak hand, even in a four-handed game. Opening from UTG with such a wide and weak range is generally unprofitable, especially with two very large stacks (Minni and FiveTwo) acting behind me. Given my stack size, I have enough chips to wait for a more favorable hand or a better position to attempt a steal. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

On the button with A♣T♦ — a strong stealing hand with an Ace blocker and good postflop playability. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call; opening to 3x BB (300 total) is standard here to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and isolate heads-up where ATo performs well. I avoid the tiny min-raise (200) because it gives poor fold equity and invites limp/calls; I also don’t want to overinflate the pot and invite big 3-bets from the deep SB. Plan: open to 300; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to big sizing from the deep stacks, call smaller 3-bets occasionally), and play postflop aggressively in position.

Minni’s button open to 3bb is likely quite wide, but 2♠3♠ is still at the very bottom of a SB defend. Calling creates a tough-to-realize equity spot out of position, often multiway with the BB getting a good price, and our suited hand makes dominated flushes plus lots of reverse-implied-odds pairs. As a 3-bet bluff it has poor blocker value and plays badly when called. Preserving EV and avoiding marginal OOP spots is best here.

Opponent Minni (Button) raises to 300 (3bb). I am in the Big Blind with K7o. Without antes, pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 450 (30.7%). While K7o has decent raw equity against a wide button range, playing out of position against a large stack (Minni is a chip leader) with an offsuit marginal hand is difficult. The 3x raise sizing makes the price worse compared to a min-raise. Additionally, with Flash as a short stack (2800), I should prioritize preserving my medium stack (6825) and avoiding marginal -EV spots OOP. I fold.

9c3s offsuit in UTG is a very weak, easily dominated hand. In early position with three players yet to act (including a shorter BB who can 3-bet or shove and a big stack on the button), limp or raise are both poor choices — limp hands get you outplayed postflop and raises with garbage are reckless. With a deep stack and many hands left, preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

Minni folds, so this is a prime spot to attack the blinds. With A♠4♠ I have a suited ace (good blocker to strong Ax and some nut-flush/straight playability) and I cover both blinds, especially the short-stacked BB, creating strong fold equity preflop and on many flops. I’ll use a small steal sizing to risk little while still applying pressure; if either blind shoves, I’m not pot-committed and can comfortably fold versus their likely jam ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with A2o facing a 2.25x raise from the Button (FiveTwo). While I have raw equity against a wide button opening range, A2o plays poorly out of position due to weak kicker issues and the lack of connectivity. Additionally, just calling invites the Big Blind (Flash) to call or squeeze, putting me in a difficult spot. Given my stack depth (67 BB) and the stage of the tournament, I prefer to preserve chips and avoid marginal spots out of position. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

In a 4-handed game, Kh Qc is a premium hand, especially when facing a Button open. FiveTwo likely has a wide range here, and with a stack of 27 BB (2700 chips), I have enough leverage to 3-bet for value and protection. Raising to 700 allows me to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand immediately, while still leaving room to navigate post-flop if called.

Flash is very short (2100) and has 3-bet to 700 leaving only 1400 behind, so any continuation is close to a preflop all-in spot. With A♠4♠ I have solid equity and good blockers (removing some strong Ax), plus strong playability (nut-flush potential and wheel possibilities) in position.

4-betting to put him in would give him great pot odds (he’d call 1400 to win a big pot) and likely gets called very wide, reducing fold equity and turning it into a marginal/gamble. Folding is too tight given price and his likely wide BB 3-bet range.

Calling keeps his bluffs and worse hands in, lets me realize equity in position, and I can make clean decisions versus his likely flop jam: continue with any A, most flopped pairs on favorable textures, nut flush draws/combo draws; fold the total whiffs.

On an Ace-high board, as the pre-flop 3-bettor, I technically have the range advantage, but my specific hand (Kh Qc) has completely missed. FiveTwo is the significant chip leader and has shown a willingness to be sticky and aggressive in previous hands. Given my relatively short stack of 21BB, a continuation bet here is risky because if I'm called, I'll be left with a very awkward stack-to-pot ratio on the turn with almost no equity. Checking allows me to see how FiveTwo reacts; if he checks back, I get a free turn to potentially improve, and if he bets, I can fold and preserve my 2100 chips for a better spot in this 4-handed game.

Flop comes A♣4♦3♦ and Flash (only ~1400 behind into a 1450 pot) checks. With A♠4♠ I have top two pair on a fairly dynamic board (diamond draws, 5x/2x straight draws). At this SPR, the cleanest and most profitable line is to put the rest in now: we get called by plenty of worse Ax, combo/flush draws, and some stubborn pairs, while denying equity to hands that would like a free turn. Any smaller bet essentially commits him anyway, so I prefer maximizing immediate value and avoiding tough turn cards.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Kh Qc and was called by FiveTwo. The flop came Ace-high (Ac 4d 3d), which I checked. FiveTwo then bet nearly the size of the pot (1400 into 1450). My hand has no showdown value and very little equity against his range, which likely includes Aces, flush draws, or middle pairs. Since I only have 2100 chips remaining, calling 1400 would leave me with just 700, effectively committing my stack with a hand that has almost no chance of winning unless I hit a runner-runner. Folding preserves my 21 BB stack for a more favorable situation.

4-handed from UTG, A4o is within a reasonable opening range: we have an ace blocker, can win preflop often, and can make top pair plus some wheel equity on low boards. With a short stack in the SB (likely to shove or fold) and a deep BB who will defend, I’ll use a standard but not oversized open to apply pressure while keeping my risk controlled. If faced with a shove from the SB or a strong 3-bet from the BTN/BB, I’m generally folding this offsuit wheel ace.

I am on the Button with pocket Fours (4h 4d). The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has opened to 250 (2.5x BB). With a stack of over 67 BBs, I have sufficient depth to 'set mine' (call hoping to flop a three-of-a-kind). The implied odds are favorable since the effective stack with the opener is my entire stack (6775), which is well over the 15-20x the calling price usually required for profitable set mining. Having position on the opener is a significant advantage, allowing me to potentially take the pot away if checked to or control the pot size. While there is a risk of a squeeze from the aggressive big blind or a shove from the short-stacked small blind, the potential reward justifies the low cost of calling.

I am currently holding a pocket pair of Eights (8s 8h) in the Small Blind. My stack is quite short at 2,050 chips (20.5 BB). FiveTwo (UTG) opened with a raise to 250 and Pro (Button) called. In this situation, flat-calling would be suboptimal because it leaves me out of position with a very low stack-to-pot ratio on the flop, making it hard to play if overcards appear. Given my stack size, this is a prime opportunity to shove all-in. This move maximizes fold equity against the two active players and allows me to realize the full equity of my pair if called, usually resulting in a coin-flip against overcards. Shoving for 2,050 is the most effective way to either win the 650 currently in the pot or double up.

Fold. Flash’s 2050 shove after FiveTwo opened to 250 and Pro called is essentially a short‑stack all‑in. To call I must put 1950 more into a pot of 2650 (4600 total) — I need ~42% equity just to break even immediately. A♣6♦ is easily dominated by the shover’s and opener’s ranges (better Aces, broadway, pairs) and my equity collapses in a multiway pot if UTG or the button wake up and call. With a healthy 24,500 stack there’s no reason to risk ~2k with a marginal, kicker‑vulnerable hand; folding and waiting for a cleaner spot is the +EV play.

Flash’s squeeze to 2050 with only ~50 behind is effectively a 21bb 3-bet/near-shove into an UTG open plus a BTN cold call, which strongly concentrates his range (pairs/big broadways) and increases the chance Pro continues or back-raises. A4o plays poorly versus that range (often dominated when we make an ace) and we don’t realize equity well multiway, so calling for pot odds is not enough and 4-betting would be spew. Preserve stack and fold.

I originally called the UTG open with 4h4d to set mine. However, the Small Blind (Flash) has squeezed/shoved for 2050 chips (effectively 21 BBs, leaving 50 behind). FiveTwo folded. The pot is 2650, and I need to call 1800. This offers me pot odds of roughly 1800/4450 = 40%. With pocket Fours, I am set mining no longer feasible as the opponent is all-in. I must evaluate my equity against Flash's shoving range. A squeeze over an UTG open and a flat call usually represents a strong range (99+, AQ+). Against this range, 4h4d has only ~33% equity, which is insufficient given the 40% odds required. Even against a wider range, 44 is often flipping at best or dominated by higher pairs. Preserving my remaining ~6500 stack is better than taking a marginal to losing spot.

In a 4-handed game, UTG acts as the Cutoff, making 7c8c (suited connectors) a reasonable candidate for an opening raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Although the Button (Flash) is short-stacked (27 BB) and showed aggression recently (Hand #104), opening to 2.5 BB is an acceptable risk. My stack of 65 BB allows for post-flop maneuverability against the deep-stacked blinds if called. Folding is too passive for this hand strength in this position given the need to accumulate chips against the chip leaders.

I am on the button with a very weak hand (J4o). Pro has opened with a raise from UTG. Given my relatively small stack (27bb) and the poor quality of my hand, there is no reason to contest this pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

UTG (Pro) opened to 250 in a 4-handed game. I’m in the small blind with 5♦8♠ — a weak, poorly coordinated hand that plays badly OOP against an UTG opening range and is frequently dominated. Calling another 200 to see a flop will very likely put me in a multiway pot (Big Blind likely to defend) with poor postflop equity and little implied odds. 3-betting for fold equity is inappropriate with such a hand. With a healthy stack and many better spots to pick, the correct, low-variance play is to fold and conserve chips.

We’re getting a very good price (150 to win a pot that will be 550), and A4o has enough raw equity vs a 4-handed UTG open to defend the big blind. A light 3-bet is possible with an ace blocker, but offsuit A4 plays poorly in a bloated pot out of position, and Pro’s stack depth makes continuing vs a 4-bet awkward. Best EV is to call and realize equity, then play fit-or-fold/marginal continues depending on flop texture (A-high, wheel/gutshot boards, etc.).

We flop trips on a very dry, paired A-A-6 board. With As in hand there’s only one ace left (Ad), so Pro rarely has an ace, and he’ll often continuation-bet a lot of non-ace hands (broadways/pairs) if checked to. Even though our kicker is weak, the best way to maximize EV is to keep his range wide and induce bluffs rather than lead and narrow him to mostly value. Plan: check, mostly call reasonable flop bets; consider raising later (or vs bigger sizing) when ranges are clearer / if he keeps firing.

The board texture is paired Aces (6s Ah Ac). While this texture favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor allowing for frequent c-bets, my specific hand (7c8c) has very little equity (no pair, no immediate draw). The opponent (FiveTwo) is the massive chip leader and has shown capability to play aggressively on Ace-high boards (e.g., Hand #103). Betting here folds out worse hands (King/Queen high) but isolates me against better hands (any pair, any Ace). Given the opponent's stack size and potential to check-raise or float, attempting to bluff with 7-high on this texture is risky. Checking back controls the pot size and allows me to see a free turn card where I might pick up equity (clubs or straight draws).

Pro’s flop check-back on A-A-6 heavily discounts him having an ace (only Ad remains) and shifts his range toward underpairs (77–KK), some slow-played 6x, and broadway air. If we check again, he’ll often take the free river with those underpairs/air, and we miss a street of value. The J turn is great for us because it gives him more hands that can continue (Jx that picked up a pair, plus stubborn pocket pairs). With his ~6.3k stack, we also want to start building a pot that can be pressured for value by the river. I’ll lead a solid value size that still gets called by pairs/Jx and sets up a bigger river bet.

On the turn, the board is [6s Ah Ac Jc]. I checked back the flop with [7c 8c] and have now picked up a club flush draw. The opponent (FiveTwo) leads out for 350 into a pot of 550. My pot odds are roughly 28% (350 to call into a total pot of 1250). My direct equity with the flush draw (9 outs) is approximately 19.6%. The difference is small enough to be covered by implied odds; I only need to extract roughly 150 additional chips on the river if I hit my flush to make this a break-even call. Given I am in position and playing against the chip leader who might be probing with a wide range, calling is the most prudent play to realize my equity without bloating the pot prematurely.

River pairs the 6, giving us Aces full of sixes (AAA66), which is essentially the nuts here (only 66 makes quads; AdX just chops). Pro’s line (check flop, call small turn lead) weights him heavily to bluff-catchers like 77–KK and Jx, plus the occasional 6x that will never fold. If we check, he’ll often check back these hands and we lose a full street of value. I’ll value bet a solid size that pressures his bluff-catchers but is still very callable given the runout gives him AA66J a lot of the time.

I hold 7c8c on a board of 6s Ah Ac Jc 6h. My flush draw from the turn has missed, leaving me with 8-high (playing the board's two pair with a Jack kicker). The opponent, who is the massive chip leader, has led out for 900 into a pot of 1250. My hand has zero showdown value, as even most bluffs would beat 8-high. Attempting to bluff-raise here is ill-advised given my passive line (checking back the flop and calling the turn) which heavily signals a draw that missed, and the opponent's deep stack allows them to call light. Conserving my remaining 59 BB stack is the priority.

I am UTG (which is effectively the Cutoff in a 4-handed game) with 8-6 offsuit. This hand is significantly below the opening range for this position, especially with a stack of 27 big blinds. Given the strength of the remaining players and my current position, folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

7♥4♦ is a garbage hand, but I'm on the button with only the blinds to act behind me — this is exactly where to open a very wide stealing range. I don't want to limp and give the blinds good odds to see a flop, so I open to a standard 3x BB (300) to take the initiative. The SB is deep and can defend wide, the BB (Pro) is relatively short (~58bb) and will defend more narrowly or 3‑bet/shove polarized; I will fold to a large 3‑bet or shove, and if called I’ll play post‑flop in position, c‑betting on favorable/dry boards and folding to heavy aggression without improvement. Raising to 300 maximizes fold equity while keeping risk reasonable with a weak holding.

We’re in the SB facing a BTN min-open. Q7o is heavily dominated by Minni’s continuing range (Qx, broadways, better offsuit hands) and plays poorly out of position, especially with the BB still to act and capable of coming along or even jamming over a 3-bet with his ~58bb stack. A light 3-bet bluff here is unnecessary and risks getting played back at; calling realizes equity poorly and creates tough postflop spots. Best EV is to just let it go and preserve stack for better defend/3-bet candidates.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 2c, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I am getting reasonable pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 450), 9-2 offsuit has extremely poor playability and equity realization, especially out of position against a large stack. Defending here would be negative EV. The correct play is to fold.

I'm UTG with pocket Jacks in a 4-handed game and deep stacks (my stack 24,600). JJ is a premium hand — I want to build the pot and narrow the field rather than limp/call and allow multiple players to see a flop. A standard 3x open is appropriate here: it gives fold equity, extracts value from worse hands, and still keeps the pot manageable against deeper stacks. Min-raising to 200 is too small and invites multiway action; the BB (Flash, ~26BB) may shove but I can comfortably fold or call depending on the shove size and reads, and the SB (Pro, ~57BB) and Button (FiveTwo, deep) will have to decide. I open to 300 to get value and isolate when possible, and will re-evaluate if I face a large 3-bet or shove.

Minni opens UTG to 3bb and we’re on the button with 9c2h offsuit—one of the weakest hands with poor playability (dominated 9x, no suitedness, no connectedness). Calling invites squeeze pressure from the short/medium stacks in the blinds (especially BB at 26bb) and offers very low implied odds. A 3-bet bluff has no blockers and is unnecessarily high variance when we can wait for better spots in position versus a likely wide opener. Best decision is to fold and preserve stack.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ace-Five offsuit (A5o) with a stack of 57.75 BB. Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 3 BB from UTG. While A5o has an Ace blocker, it is generally too weak to call out of position against an UTG opening range, as it is often dominated by better Aces (AT+). Additionally, playing post-flop out of position is difficult, and I risk being squeezed by the Big Blind. A 3-bet bluff is risky given the stack depths and position. Folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for a better spot.

In this 4-handed situation, Minni has raised to 3BB from UTG. I am in the Big Blind with 4d 3d and a stack of 2600 (26BB). While the pot odds are roughly 3.25:1, calling with such a weak hand out of position against a UTG range is generally a losing play, especially with a shorter stack where I have less room to maneuver post-flop. I'd rather preserve my chips for a more advantageous spot to either 3-bet shove or call with a hand that has better equity and playability.

4-handed UTG plays more like a cutoff range, and Q8 suited is a reasonable open with good playability and blocker value against Qx. With two shorter stacks in the blinds/button, I want to apply pressure but keep the sizing modest so I can comfortably fold to a shove/3-bet without burning chips. A small open also gives me a chance to win the blinds uncontested and take initiative versus Minni in the BB.

I am holding 5h 2c on the Button, which is a very weak unsuited hand. The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has raised to 250. Given the poor quality of my hand and the strength indicated by an UTG open, there is no strategic justification for calling or raising. Folding is the only correct play here to preserve my stack of roughly 58 BBs for better opportunities.

I am holding King-Seven offsuit in the small blind. FiveTwo, who has a massive stack and has been playing aggressively, raised to 2.5bb from the cutoff/UTG position. K7o is a weak hand to play out of position against a strong stack, especially when I only have 25.5 big blinds left. While I am getting a decent price to call, the hand performs poorly post-flop and calling would likely lead to losing more chips or being forced to fold to later aggression. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack for a better opening.

FiveTwo opened to 250 from UTG (2.5x). I’m in the big blind with A♣5♠ and the SB and Button folded, so it’s heads-up. A5o is worth defending heads-up against a standard open — it has showdown value and good blocker value to Broadway Aces, and with the stacks this deep I don’t want to inflate the pot with a light 3-bet and risk a big 4-bet. Calling 150 more (250 total) keeps the pot controllable and leaves me in position postflop to realize equity or fold to heavy aggression. If I pair my Ace or pick up strong draw equity I’ll proceed; otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously.

I have top pair (A♣5♠) on A♠ K♠ 6♦ vs an UTG opener. Their range is weighted to Ax, Kx and strong pairs, but heads‑up my ace still has good value and it blocks some of their Ace combinations. Pot is small (550) and stacks are deep, so I want a thin value bet and to deny free cards to any two‑spade draws. A modest bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) extracts from worse A‑x, Kx and medium pairs while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll bet 200; if they raise big I’ll heavily discount my kicker and re‑evaluate (likely fold to large polarized raises), but call/continue against single calls or small resistance.

Minni’s 200 donk into a 550 pot is a very small, often range-y probe from the BB (can be weak Ax/Kx, spade draws, or just trying to set a price). The flop heavily favors my UTG range, and I have position plus some backdoor equity (diamonds, potential overcard improvements). Raising here would mostly be a pure bluff that risks building a bigger pot versus the other big stack (bad with two short stacks still alive), while folding is unnecessarily tight given the price and positional leverage. I’ll peel once and look to apply pressure on many turns if Minni checks or sizing stays weak.

Board is As Ks 6d Qs — three spades on board. I hold A♣5♠: top pair but a very weak 5‑kicker and only one spade. FiveTwo was the preflop aggressor and simply called my small flop bet, so his continuing range contains Kx, Ax, Qx, pocket pairs and some suited spade combos. The Qs on the turn both pairs and completes any two‑spade holdings, so a sizable portion of his continuing range now either beats me (KQ, Qx, two pair) or has a made flush. I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal top pair into those hands. Pot‑control: check and see what he does. Plan: if he bets a small amount (thin value/bluff, ~<=1/3–1/2 pot) I will call and reevaluate river; if he fires a large pot‑size/overbet or raises I’ll strongly discount my kicker and fold (likely behind to two pair/flush). If he checks back I keep the pot small and go to showdown or take a cheap river stab if a safe card arrives.

Turn gives me a marginal but real showdown hand (pair of queens with a weak kicker) on an A-K-Q texture where Minni’s continuing range still contains a lot of Ax/Kx that has me crushed, plus a bunch of hands with a spade that can improve on the river. After the flop probe and now a check, I could stab small, but betting mostly builds a pot versus the other big stack and risks getting check-raised into a miserable spot while not getting called by many worse hands often enough. With two short stacks still in, I prefer pot control, take the free card, and be ready to bluff-catch small or fold to big river pressure depending on runout (especially spade rivers).

River: As Ks 6d Qs 4s. My hand A♣5♠ gives me a five‑card spade flush (As,Ks,Qs,5s,4s) — very strong but not the absolute nut because any opponent holding a spade higher than 5 (6s,7s,8s,9s,Ts,Js) now has a better flush. FiveTwo opened UTG, called my small flop bet and checked the turn; that line is weighted toward Ax/Kx/pocket pairs and is less consistent with a turn‑checked two‑spade slowplay (which would already have had a made flush on the turn). Given the river completed single‑spade flushes, a small value bet (~1/3 pot) is the best play: it extracts from worse one‑pair hands (Kx, Qx, A with non‑spade kicker) and medium pairs while keeping the pot controllable against the portion of his range that now beats me. I’ll bet thinly for value: 300 chips. If he raises big/overbets I will strongly discount my 5‑kicker and fold; if he calls I’m happy to go to showdown.

River brings the 4th spade, meaning any single spade in Minni’s hand is now a made flush. Given the line (small flop donk, turn check, then small river stab), this sizing looks very much like a blocker/value bet with a spade rather than a bluff, and we have no spade ourselves (so we don’t block those flushes). Our Qd8d is just a weak pair in a spot where Minni’s betting range is heavily spade-weighted, and raising as a bluff would be bad without blockers versus the other big stack. Even getting a good price, I don’t expect enough pure no-spade bluffs to justify a call, so I’ll preserve chips and fold.
I am in UTG position holding Jd 3s. This is a very weak hand (J3 offsuit) and is not suitable for opening from early position, especially with active players behind. The standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with 25.5 big blinds. Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand. While the button is a good position to attempt a steal, the players in the blinds (Minni and FiveTwo) have very large stacks and have shown a willingness to defend and play aggressively. With my stack size, I should wait for a better hand or a more favorable situation to commit chips rather than splashing with a marginal hand.

2♠4♠ is a weak, low suited hand — playable only cheaply and for implied odds. With only the big blind left and a tiny extra 50 to call (100 total committed), I’ll take the cheap flop rather than inflate the pot with a raise that can be 3‑bet or put me in tough spots. Stack depths are deep but opponent (FiveTwo) defends widely; calling preserves pot control and keeps options post‑flop (fold to strong aggression, float or barre l on favorable boards). Call 50 more (100 total) to see a flop and re‑evaluate.

Small blind limp creates a low-risk spot, but 8-5 offsuit plays poorly out of position in an inflated pot. With two short stacks still in (Flash/Pro), there’s no need to take a thin preflop steal line against the only other big stack and potentially face limp-raises or tough postflop decisions. I’ll take the free flop and realize equity.

I have 2♠4♠ on a dry, rainbow 10♣-8♥-6♦ flop — essentially air with no backdoor spade draw. I'm in position against one opponent (FiveTwo) who defends fairly wide. This is a classic heads‑up c‑bet spot: small, low‑risk probe to fold out unpaired hands and pick up the 200 chip pot. I'll bet half‑pot (100). If I'm called I'll proceed very cautiously on the turn and fold to meaningful aggression — I don't want to build a big pot with nothing.

Minni limp-called pre and now leads small (100 into 300), which is often a cheap stab with a very wide range (overcards, gutshots, random pairs). We have top pair, but a weak kicker and are in a deep, big-stack-vs-big-stack dynamic with shorter stacks still at the table, so there’s no need to inflate the pot and get forced into big decisions when Minni can easily have better 8x/Tx. Calling keeps her bluffs and worse hands in, controls variance, and lets us re-evaluate on the turn (we can raise later on cleaner cards or versus bigger pressure).

I hold 2♠4♠ and have no pair and no spade on the turn (board: 8♥ 6♦ T♣ A♥). I bet the flop (100) and was called, so Villain’s calling range contains lots of hands that improve with an A (any A, tens, eights, sixes, pocket pairs, strong draws). The turn Ace destroys most of my fold equity — barreling again with pure air into a caller is a losing line. With deep stacks I want to control the pot and avoid getting raised or bloated with zero equity. I’ll check to keep the pot small and fold to meaningful aggression; if Villain checks back I can re-evaluate the river and attempt a small bluff or take a free card. Action: check.

Minni’s flop lead was tiny and her turn check on the Ace is very consistent with a wide range that includes give-ups, marginal pairs (6x/8x/Tx), and some Ax that now feels comfortable. With 8♠5♠ (pair of 8s, weak kicker) we have decent showdown value but don’t want to bloat a deep SB-vs-BB pot where her continue range (Ax, better 8x, Tx) dominates the hands that will call. Betting also exposes us to an awkward check-raise from the other big stack. Checking back keeps variance low, realizes equity, and lets us bluff-catch or value-thinly on the river depending on her sizing and the runout.

I have 2♠4♠ on 8♥-6♦-T♣-A♥-6♣ — no showdown value and no meaningful blockers to A or 6. I bet the flop and was called, then checked the turn; Villain’s calling range includes many hands that beat me (A‑x, T‑x, 8x, pocket pairs, and now any single 6 makes trips). The pot is small (~400) and this opponent defends/calls widely, so my fold equity on a river bluff is thin. Betting risks getting called by any pair (or worse) and is not +EV enough here. I’ll preserve chips and check to fold to a bet.

We improved to two pair (8s and 6s) on a very small pot where Minni has taken a passive check/check line on turn and river after a tiny flop lead. That line contains a lot of marginal made hands (Tx, 8x, small pairs) and give-ups that will often either call a small bet or fold without punishing us.

I don’t want to polarize and bloat a deep-stacked pot versus the only other big stack, especially since some trap hands exist (A6/86/T6 turning into boats) that could check-raise. So I’ll go for a thin, low-risk value bet and plan to bet/fold versus meaningful aggression.

Sizing: ~1/2 pot targets crying calls from Tx/8x while keeping my loss small if she springs a trap.

I have 2♠4♠ with no showdown value or meaningful blockers. I bet the flop and was called, then checked the turn — Villain’s calling line contains many hands that beat me (A‑x, T‑x, 8x, pocket pairs) and the river pairs the 6. The 200 river bet into this pot is small, but calling requires ~25% equity; I have essentially none and am only calling to catch a bluff. Raising as a bluff is thin and unnecessarily risky versus a player who defends widely. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

I am holding K7o in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a weak hand to open with, particularly from early position. With a stack of 25.5 big blinds, I am the shortest stack at the table, but I still have enough fold equity to wait for a more premium hand or a better situation to commit chips. Raising into three opponents, two of whom have very large stacks, is unnecessarily risky with this holding.

UTG folded and I’m on the button with Q6o — a marginal hand but well within a reasonable open-raise range from the button. I want to take the initiative and steal the blinds or play postflop in position rather than limp and give free flops. I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300) — big enough to generate fold equity and discourage very wide defends, but not so big that I bloat the pot with a weak holding. Plan: if both blinds fold I take the pot; if I get called I’ll use position to continue carefully; if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack. Total committed amount shown is 300.

Button is opening fairly wide to 3bb and we’re getting a good price in the SB (250 more to win 450). J6s has reasonable playability (flush potential, some straight/2p possibilities) and is too strong to just fold at this price. I don’t want to 3-bet this hand deep-stacked without good blockers (and risk getting 4-bet), especially being OOP; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets us realize equity. If BB comes along, we’ll play straightforwardly postflop without overvaluing dominated jacks.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3bb raise from the Button (Minni) and a call from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). Pot odds are approximately 4.5 to 1 (calling 200 to win 900), requiring about 22% equity. While 94s has raw equity close to this against wide ranges, it suffers from poor playability (disconnected ranks, weak flush draw potential often dominated) and being out of position in a 3-way pot against two deep stacks. Realizing equity will be difficult. GTO ranges typically fold 94s in this specific spot (vs 3bb open and call) due to these factors. Conserving chips is preferable to bleeding them in marginal multi-way spots.

Heads-up vs the button raiser on an A-K-9 flop, we’re at a significant range/nut disadvantage from the SB. Our hand (Jc6c) is currently just J-high with only backdoor club potential and very thin ways to improve, so there’s no incentive to build the pot OOP. Checking keeps our range protected, lets Villain c-bet their air/value as expected, and preserves our ability to respond (fold most bets, occasionally continue vs small sizing when we pick up equity on later streets).

Flop is A♣ K♠ 9♣ and I have Q♦6♠ — completely missed. Heads-up vs the small blind who called my 3x button open. As the preflop aggressor I have range and positional advantage, so a small continuation bet is the standard +EV play to fold out unpaired hands and weaker holdings while keeping the pot manageable if called. The SB’s calling range contains many Ax/Kx and some pairs that will continue, but plenty of hands will fold to a modest c-bet. I’ll bet ~1/3–35% pot (250 into 700) to apply pressure; plan is to fold to significant raise, and if called use position to control the turn and re-evaluate — check back on blanks and only barrel again if I pick up equity or see clear weakness.

We picked up a real club flush draw (9 outs) on a board where we’re otherwise behind Villain’s button range. Versus this very small c-bet (250 into 950), we’re getting excellent immediate odds (~21% needed) and our draw has sufficient equity plus some future bluff/semi-bluff possibilities on favorable turns (clubs and some broadway runouts). Raising is less appealing because it’s a non-nut flush draw (Ac on board) and we’re OOP into a strong A/K-heavy range; calling keeps the pot controlled and lets us realize equity cheaply.

We’re still OOP versus the button’s A/K-heavy range on A♣K♠9♣3♥. While we do have a real flush draw (any club), it’s a non-nut draw because the A♣ is on board and our best flush will be J-high, which can be dominated by Villain’s higher club holdings (K♣X♣/Q♣X♣, etc.).

Leading (donking) here on a blank turn tends to bloat the pot in a spot where we’re usually behind when called and sometimes crushed when the club comes. The higher-EV, lower-risk line is to check and let Minni continue with their value and bluffs; then we can comfortably continue versus reasonable sizing given our ~18–20% river equity.

So we check, keep the pot controlled, and react to Villain’s turn bet sizing.

I completely missed with Q6o. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the flop; villain called and then checked the turn (3♥), which is a blank that didn’t improve my hand or add meaningful equity. Stacking off with no showdown value against a wide but often-strong calling range (A‑x, K‑x, 9x, pairs) is unnecessary with ~24.5k effective stacks and a 1.2k pot. I’ll exercise pot control and keep the initiative to the river — checking behind preserves chips, lets me see villain’s river action in position, and keeps bluffing/creeping options open if the river favors me or villain shows weakness.

River is a complete brick for us: we missed the club flush and have J-high with essentially zero showdown value. Checking just concedes the pot almost always.

Given Minni’s line (small flop c-bet, then checking back turn), their range contains a meaningful chunk of air and medium hands (missed broadways, small pairs, some 9x) that will often fold to a straightforward river stab, while our river lead is still credible as thin value (Ax/Kx that checked turn for pot control, or some 9x). 

I’ll use a small-to-medium sizing to maximize fold equity versus that capped portion of their range while keeping downside limited versus Ax that will call. If raised, we fold.

I have Q6o with no pair and no meaningful showdown equity on A♣ K♠ 9♣ 3♥ 4♠. Villain called my 3x open and a modest 250 c-bet on the flop, checked the turn, and now fires a small 500 into a 1700 pot on the river. That line is much more consistent with thin-value hands (Ax, Kx, 9x) than a large bluff; calling 500 to win 2200 requires ~22.7% equity, which Q6 simply doesn't have versus a value-heavy calling range. A raise as a bluff would be risky (I have poor blockers and would bloat the pot with no equity). Fold — preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

UTG with J♥5♦ facing a raise to 200 (100 to call). J5o is a weak, easily dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position against the opener and players behind (SB and BB). Calling 100 for pot odds (~2.5:1) still requires too much equity vs a typical raising range, and 3-betting as a bluff here is too thin. With deep stacks and many hands left, this is not a spot to spew chips — fold and wait for a better situation.

We’re on the button 4-handed after UTG folds, holding AcQh (strong top-of-range open in this spot). With position and a big stack advantage, we want to raise for value and to apply pressure on the blinds—especially the BB who is very short (~25bb) and can reshove wide. A raise also denies the SB good immediate odds to peel. I’ll use a clean 3x open to 300, which keeps worse hands in, sets up profitable calls versus a BB shove, and maximizes fold equity without overcommitting.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9d Ts. The player on the Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has opened for a raise to 300. Although T9o has some playability, it is a marginal hand to play out of position against a likely wide button opening range, especially with the Big Blind still to act behind me. Calling would invite a squeeze or leave me playing a tricky hand OOP. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my stack of roughly 56 big blinds for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 93o, which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo, the chip leader, has raised from the Button. While the price to call is 200 chips to win a pot of 450, 93o performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against a loose and aggressive chip leader. With 24.5 big blinds remaining, I should preserve my stack and wait for a better spot to double up rather than defending with junk.

4-handed from UTG with A9o we’re well ahead of the random hands in the blinds and can profitably take initiative. As chip leader versus two much shorter stacks (especially SB), a small open applies pressure, keeps our risk controlled, and allows us to play in position postflop when called by the BB. I’ll use a standard small sizing to 2.5bb to deny cheap realization while not overcommitting versus potential reshoves.

I hold J9o on the Button. The player in UTG (FiveTwo) has opened to 250. UTG opening ranges are typically strong, consisting of pocket pairs, strong aces, and strong broadways. J9o is dominated by much of this range (AJ, KJ, QJ, etc.) and performs poorly post-flop against strong hands. Additionally, there are players in the blinds behind me who could squeeze, including a short stack in the Small Blind. Calling is -EV against a standard UTG range, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary with this specific holding. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Hearts and Trey of Clubs, which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo has opened for 250 from UTG (which is the cutoff in a 4-handed game). My stack is currently the shortest at the table with 2400 chips (24 BB). Calling 200 more out of position with a trash hand like T3o is a losing play, especially given the stack dynamics and the strength of the opponent's range relative to mine. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my remaining chips.

UTG (FiveTwo) opened to 250 and I’m in the big blind with T♦6♠. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 150 more (250 total) — break-even equity ≈ 27%. FiveTwo opens a lot of pots and has been fairly aggressive, so his range is probably wider than a nit’s UTG range. T6o is marginal but has reasonable showdown and implied-odds potential in these deep stacks and will pick up enough equity vs a wide open to justify defending. 3-betting out of position with this hand is thin and will force difficult postflop decisions; folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call 250 total (add 150), see the flop, fold to heavy, non-connecting aggression when I miss, and extract value or continue with tens or strong draws.

Flop: 4♣ 8♦ Q♠ — I have T♦6♠. Out of position with no pair and essentially no draw (only 3 tens + 3 sixes = 6 outs). FiveTwo opens a lot and will c‑bet frequently, but I have no fold equity and don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Pot control is correct: check and see how the raiser reacts. Plan: if FiveTwo bets small (~≤1/3 pot ≈180–200) I’ll call to realize equity and evaluate the turn; if he fires a larger C‑bet (>~1/2 pot) I’ll fold. If he checks back I’ll take the free turn card and re‑evaluate (look to bet only if I pair or pick up a clear draw).

Heads-up vs BB on a dry Q84r flop, our UTG opening range has a clear range advantage (AQ/KQ/QQ+/overpairs) while BB has a lot of misses and weak pairs. With A9o we don’t have showdown value to happily check down, so I’ll use a small c-bet to fold out BB’s air (broadway whiffs, suited connectors, low cards) and deny cheap realization, while keeping risk controlled versus a check-raise (which we can comfortably fold to).

FiveTwo fired a small flop c‑bet (175 into the ~550 pre‑bet pot) — roughly 1/3 pot. I have T♦6♠ with no pair and 6 clean outs (three tens + three sixes). Pot math: calling 175 to make the pot ~900 gives break‑even equity ≈ 19.4%; my chance to hit by the river ≈ 24.1%, so calling is +EV. FiveTwo has been opening wide and is aggressive, so many c‑bets are wide — I can realize equity cheaply and have implied odds in these deep stacks. Plan: call to keep the pot small and see the turn, fold to large, non‑connecting aggression if I miss, and if I pair a T or 6 (or pick up a clear draw) I’ll look to extract value on the turn/river. No 3‑bet or bluff‑raise OOP with this marginal holding. Total committed after this call = 250 (preflop) + 175 (flop) = 425.

Check. The turn paired the Queen (board: 4♣ 8♦ Q♠ Q♥) and I hold T♦6♠ — no pair and essentially no fold equity. FiveTwo flatted my preflop and c‑bet small on the flop, then checked the turn; that line can be either weakness or a trap, but with his wide opening range he will call a thin OOP turn-bluff with many one-pair and pocket-pair hands. Pot control is the priority: the pot is only 900 against my ~24k stack, so I should avoid bloating a pot with a marginal hand. Checking keeps options open — I can fold cheaply to river aggression, or target a river bluff/value bet only if I pair or hit a very favorable scare card (A/K or a blank that credibly represents strength).

Turn pairs the top card (QQ on board), which is a good card for our preflop range, but after BB check-calls flop their range is already weighted to Qx, 8x, and pocket pairs that won’t fold often to a second small barrel. With Ad9s we have little fold equity against that continuing range and almost no equity when they do have a queen, so firing again mostly risks lighting chips on fire versus the only stack that can hurt us. Checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes our 6 clean outs vs 8x/pocket pairs, and protects our checking range (we can have strong Qx/boats that also check back sometimes).

Check. River is 4c 8d Qs Qh 5d and I hold T♦6♠ — I missed entirely and have effectively no showdown value or credible bluffs. FiveTwo opened wide preflop, c‑bet small on the flop then checked the turn; that line is ambiguous (could be a trap with Qx or just pot‑control with medium hands), so leading OOP or bluffing here is thin — my ten doesn't block the hands I need to fold out and a value bet will only get called by better. The pot is small (~900) relative to the stacks, so pot control and preserving chips is the right course. Plan: check to avoid bloating the pot; if FiveTwo fires a meaningful river bet I’ll fold (only consider calling an absurdly small probe).

River after BB check-calls flop on Q84r, their range is heavily weighted toward some made hand (Qx, 8x, 4x, pocket pairs) rather than pure air. The turn check-check makes it even more likely they have a marginal pair that will often bluff-catch, and Minni has shown a tendency to look us up rather than overfold. With A9 high we have no real showdown value, but the pot is only 900 and this is the one opponent whose stack can meaningfully hurt ours; forcing a bluff here needs unrealistically high fold equity. I’ll take the low-variance line, give up, and preserve stack leverage vs the two short stacks.

I am holding 4d Tc in UTG position. This is a statistically very weak hand with poor connectivity and no suit coordination. Being first to act with three opponents behind, including two deep stacks in the blinds, the likelihood of dominating anyone is near zero, and the risk of being dominated or facing a 3-bet is high. There is no strategic merit in playing this hand. The correct play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

I am on the button with 6-2 offsuit, which is widely considered one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of 2,400 (24 big blinds), I need to be selective about the hands I play to avoid bleeding chips. There is no reason to limp or raise with this hand from any position. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

We're effectively heads-up vs the big blind with A♠9♠ — a strong, suited Ace with good blocker value. Calling 50 gives the BB a cheap look and concedes initiative; the hand plays well postflop in position in a heads-up pot, so I want to both build value and get fold equity. I'll raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) — big enough to charge draws and loose hands but small relative to both stacks so I'm not overcommitting. Plan: see how FiveTwo responds; continue for value/initiative on favorable flops, and re-evaluate vs a large 3-bet (fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing).

Minni opens from the SB to 300 (3x), and we’re in the BB with 8♠3♦ offsuit, out of position. We’d be calling 200 to play a pot of 600, needing ~33% raw equity; while 83o can have enough equity versus a wide SB opening range, it realizes very poorly OOP and creates lots of dominated/low-equity postflop situations, especially versus a capable big-stack opponent. With a big stack and multiple short stacks in play, I’d rather avoid thin, low-realization defenses and keep my stack for higher EV spots and leveraging pressure elsewhere. No good 3-bet blockers either, so folding is best.

I have Pocket Queens, which is an elite premium hand. With a stack of 2400 (24 big blinds), I am the shortest stack at the table and need to look for opportunities to double up. Raising to 300 is a standard open that builds the pot and invites action from weaker hands. Given my stack depth, I am prepared to go all-in if any opponent re-raises.

Button facing an UTG open to 300 with Q♠2♣. UTG's open in a 4-handed game is likely fairly strong and my hand is very weak and easily dominated. Calling 300 would create a multiway pot where Q2o has poor equity and little postflop playability; 3-betting as a bluff is undesirable because the opener (Flash) is short-stacked (~21bb) and can shove, and the SB/BB behind can complicate isolation. With a healthy stack (24k+) there's no need to gamble with a marginal, dominated holding — fold and wait for a better spot.

J5o plays very poorly out of position, and flat-calling invites the big blind in with great odds, creating a tough multiway spot with lots of domination/reverse implied odds. A squeeze bluff is also unattractive because Flash is short (~21bb) and can jam over a 3-bet at a high frequency, forcing us to 3-bet/fold and torch chips. Best stack-preserving decision is to let this one go.

I am in the Big Blind with T4o, facing a raise to 300 from Flash in UTG. Flash is the short stack with about 21BB. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650, requiring ~31% equity), T4o performs poorly against a UTG opening range and will be difficult to play out of position. It is likely dominated by many hands in Flash's range. Preserving my stack of 55BB is better than defending a marginal hand in this spot.

UTG with 8♥5♣ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability out of position. There are only the blinds in the pot (150) so calling 100 to limp OOP against the button and two blinds is marginal and will put me in tough spots postflop. Raising to 200 as a steal is appropriate with a much stronger UTG range, but not with this garbage holding — I can be 3-bet or face shoves from the short stacks (Pro 5,475; Flash 2,450). Preserve my 24,175 stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Minni folds and we’re on the button with position and a large stack versus two shorter blinds (SB ~55bb, BB ~25bb). K8o is plenty strong to open on the BTN, and the main goal is to apply max pressure and pick up the dead blinds/antes-equivalent while risking little. I’ll use a small-but-standard open size to keep my steal cheap and maintain raise/fold flexibility if either blind shoves or 3-bets big.

I am in the Small Blind with K6o facing a 2.5x raise from the massive chip leader on the Button. While K6o has reasonable raw equity against a wide Button opening range, my equity realization will be poor playing out of position. I need to call 200 to win a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% realized equity. Given the positional disadvantage, the risk of domination by better Kings, and the threat of the short-stacked Big Blind squeezing behind me, calling is marginally -EV. Folding preserves my 55bb stack for better spots.

I am holding Jc 6h in the big blind. FiveTwo, who has a massive chip lead, raised to 250 from the button. Although it costs me only 150 more to call into a pot of 400, J6o is a weak hand and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the table captain. Given my short stack of 24.5 big blinds, I should preserve my chips for better opportunities rather than defending with marginal hands.

4-handed from UTG, 7c5c is a profitable open: it has good playability (suited connectivity) and benefits from fold equity in the blinds. I want to apply pressure as the big stack while keeping the risk controlled versus the two shorter stacks (SB 24bb, BTN 55bb) who can 3-bet jam; a small open lets me fold comfortably to a shove/large 3-bet and still realize strong EV when they defend/pass.

I am on the Button with Ten-Six offsuit (6s Tc). The player 'FiveTwo' has opened from UTG to 250 (2.5x BB). T6o is a very weak hand with poor playability and is easily dominated by an UTG opening range. Even in a 4-handed game, this hand is below the threshold for calling or 3-bet bluffing, especially against the chip leader who has covered me significantly. Folding is the only prudent decision to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am currently the short stack at the table with 24 big blinds (2400 chips). In a 4-max game, Ace-Queen offsuit is a premium hand. The chip leader (FiveTwo) has opened from the cutoff/UTG position to 2.5x the big blind. Calling out of position with my stack size is suboptimal as it leaves me in a difficult spot post-flop. A 3-bet is the standard play here to either take down the pot immediately or set up a shove on most flops. Raising to 800 (about 3x the initial raise) is a strong size that leaves me with 1600 behind, creating a pot of approximately 1700 if called, allowing for a comfortable shove on the flop. This also puts pressure on the big blind and the initial raiser.

I’m on the big blind with 10♥5♥ facing an UTG open to 250 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 800. Pot is 1,150 and it costs me 700 to call (min raise to 1,350). This is a multiway, out‑of‑position spot against two likely stronger ranges (UTG open + SB squeeze). My hand is marginal/speculative and will be heavily dominated; required equity to call (~700/(1150+700) ≈ 38%) is higher than my realistic equity vs these combined ranges. Flash’s stack is short but not locked in and FiveTwo (UTG) can continue — raising or calling is spewy with T5s. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

Flash’s 3-bet to 800 leaves him extremely shallow (effectively ~850 behind), and I’m getting a good price: 550 to win 1150 (~32% immediate equity needed). 7c5c has decent equity versus a SB 3-bet range in 4-handed play, and—importantly—I’ll have position with a low SPR, letting me realize equity well and potentially fold on the very worst flops if he jams and I’m drawing thin. I don’t want to 4-bet jam because it likely tightens his continuing range (reducing my equity) and I don’t need to force variance when I’m already priced in with position.

I 3-bet pre-flop to 800, and the chip leader FiveTwo called. The flop comes King-high, which is a very good board for my perceived 3-betting range (which includes AK, AA, KK). With a stack of 1650 and a pot of 1700, my stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is nearly 1. On this board, I have significant fold equity against middle pocket pairs (88-QQ) and air. My Ace of Diamonds provides a valuable blocker and a backdoor nut flush draw. Given my short stack, checking would likely induce a bet that I couldn't comfortably call without a pair. Shoving (betting my remaining stack) is the most aggressive and theoretically sound play to either take the pot down immediately or realize my equity if called.

Flop is Kh-2d-7d and Flash has effectively shoved for 1650 into a pot that’s 3350 already (SPR ~1). I’m being laid ~2:1 (need ~33% equity), and while 7c5c is only bottom pair, against a short-stack SB 3-bet range that’s likely to jam this flop very wide (all overpairs/Kx but also lots of A-high broadways and diamond semi-bluffs), this hand has enough equity to continue.

If his range were only strong made hands (Kx/overpairs), this is a fold, but with the low SPR it’s common for him to just put the rest in with unpaired hands to realize equity + fold equity. Since I cover and don’t face future streets, I’ll take the priced-in call.

I’m on the button with A♠8♥ in a 3‑handed game — a strong, positionally powerful hand. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to play. I want initiative and fold equity from the blinds while keeping good post‑flop playability. The minimum open is 200, but I’ll open to 3x the BB (300 total) to deny cheap flops, discourage callers, and isolate heads‑up where A8 plays very well. Stack dynamics: I’m deep (≈240 BB), FiveTwo is very deep and can call wide, Pro is shorter (~53 BB) and can 3‑bet or shove — I’ll re‑evaluate if I face a large reraise (fold to extreme shove unless the price/reads justify a call). If called, I’ll c‑bet favorable ace/high and dry boards and use position to pressure. Raise to 300 to open the pot and take control.

We’re in the small blind versus a 3x button open, deep effective against Minni, and this is the very bottom of the range (52o) with no blockers and terrible postflop playability out of position. Calling creates a low-EV spot and often invites the short-stacked BB along, while turning this into a 3-bet bluff is unnecessary given we can apply pressure with better candidates. Preserve chips and wait for a stronger/cleaner squeeze/3-bet opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with T3o facing a 3x raise from the Button (Minni). The pot odds are roughly 31% (calling 200 to win a pot of 650), but T3o is one of the worst hands in the deck with very poor playability post-flop, especially out of position against a deep-stacked aggressive opponent. With a stack of ~54BB, I am not desperate and should wait for a better spot to invest chips. Defending here is negative EV.

3-handed on the button we can profitably open very wide because position plus fold equity is valuable, and both blinds have to defend honestly to avoid bleeding. 6♠3♠ is weak but has playable postflop equity (suited, can make straights/flushes) and benefits a lot from having position. I’ll raise small to apply pressure, keep the pot manageable when called, and be ready to fold to significant 3-bet pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 5c, facing a standard 2.5x open raise from the Button (FiveTwo). With 53 BBs, I am the shortest stack but still relatively deep. However, Q5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, often dominated by the opener's range. Although I'm getting decent pot odds, the reverse implied odds and lack of playability make this a clear fold. I will wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I have premium pocket Aces in the big blind facing a single button open to 250. This is the best hand preflop and I want to build the pot and isolate the opener while charging speculative hands. A 3-bet to 1,000 (about 3.5–4x the open) is a standard, value-oriented size here — large enough to fold out weaker holdings and extract value from broadways and medium pairs, but small enough to keep stacks deep for postflop play. If FiveTwo 4-bets, I can re-evaluate (likely call or shove depending on sizing), but for now I raise to build value and take control of the hand.

We opened very wide on the button, but Minni’s 3-bet from the big blind is a range that’s much stronger than our 6♠3♠. This hand has poor playability when called (dominated low flushes, weak straights) and no useful blockers to justify a 4-bet bluff. With a comfortable stack lead and a shorter third stack in play, there’s no need to take a marginal, high-variance defend here. Fold and continue opening wide while respecting the 3-bet.

On the Button with Ace-Eight offsuit in a 3-handed game. This hand is well within the standard opening range (top ~30% of hands) given the position and reduced number of players. My stack is roughly 53BB, which is healthy but significantly smaller than the opponents', so accumulating blinds is valuable. I will open-raise to 2.5x the big blind to apply pressure and attempt to steal the pot or play in position post-flop.

Pro (button) opened to 250. I hold 4♦5♣ — a weak, offsuit small connector. In 3‑handed play I need to defend wider than in a full ring, but this hand is still marginal and will be difficult to play out of position if I inflate the pot. Calling (putting me to a total of 250) costs 200 more into a 400 pot, giving reasonable pot odds to see a flop and realize the hand’s straight/paired outs. I’ll avoid a reraise with such a marginal holding (don’t want to build a bloated pot OOP or get isolated by the big blind/shorter-stacked button). Plan: call to see the flop, fold to a substantial 3‑bet, and play straightforward postflop — look to realize equity and bluff/attack only on favorable textures.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action: 150 to win a pot that will be 750 after we call (~20% required). Jc4c is weak and often dominated, but suitedness plus deep stacks versus Minni give enough implied odds to defend, especially multiway. A squeeze doesn’t make sense with this hand against a button open and a cold call (we’ll get called too often and play a bloated pot OOP), so the best EV line is to realize equity cheaply.

Flop: 4h 6d 6s and I hold 4d5c — I have two pair (6s & 4s). This is a strong but vulnerable holding: any 6 in an opponent’s hand, pocket 4s, or a 6+4 combo beats me, but many hands in their calling ranges are single pairs or overcards that I can get value from. It checked to me, so I should bet for value and protection while keeping the pot manageable OOP. A half‑pot bet (~375 into the 750 pot) charges worse pairs and weaker holdings, denies free cards that could give opponents trips/full houses, and keeps options open if I face a raise. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if I face a large/committing raise (or shove) I will fold or call depending on sizing and reads (short‑stack shove from Pro would require careful consideration).

Minni’s 1/3-pot lead into two players on a paired board is often a wide, range/protection stab (overcards, small pairs, some 6x, occasional slowplays). With Jc4c we have a pair plus some backdoor equity (BDFD) and, importantly, we’re getting a good price: 375 to win 1125 (~25%). Raising would bloat the pot OOP with a weak made hand and risks getting jammed on by Pro’s short stack; folding is too tight given the price and the likelihood we’re ahead of a portion of Minni’s stab range. Best line is to call and re-evaluate versus Pro’s action and future barrels.

Pre-flop, I raised with A8o on the button and got two callers from the blinds. The flop comes 4-6-6 rainbow. The Small Blind (Minni) leads out for half-pot (375) and the Big Blind (FiveTwo) calls. This action sequence is concerning for Ace-high. The lead into the pre-flop raiser often indicates a pair (like a 4 or pocket pair) or a 6. The call by the Big Blind suggests they also have a piece of the board or a straight draw (5-7). In a multi-way pot where one player shows aggression and another shows willingness to continue, my Ace-high is unlikely to be the best hand, and my outs (3 Aces and 3 Eights) might not even be clean or sufficient if I am drawing dead against a 6. Given I am the short stack, preserving chips is crucial. I'm getting 4:1 odds, but my equity against a range of pairs and trips is poor, and reverse implied odds are high if I hit an Ace. Folding is the disciplined play.

Turn comes 10c — my hand (4♦5♣) is two pair (6s & 4s). That’s a decent showdown hand but very vulnerable: any 6, any 4 with a higher kicker, or any pocket pair 7+ that was in FiveTwo’s cold‑call range preflop now beats me. I bet the flop for protection/value and got called, so FiveTwo’s range likely contains many pocket pairs and some hands that dominate my two pair. Betting OOP on the turn here risks bloating the pot when I’m often behind and invites turn raises that I don’t want to face out of position. I’ll check to control the pot and get to showdown cheaply — plan to call reasonable river bets but fold to large, committed action (raise/shove) that polarizes to full houses/quads/top two pair. Checking keeps options open and preserves stack equity against a calling‑station/caller range.

Heads-up now, and Minni’s turn check on 4h 6d 6s Tc after leading flop is often either (1) give-up with overcards/air, or (2) pot-control/trap with some 6x/Tx. Our hand (Jc4c) is a very thin made hand: we beat unpaired overcards and 22–33, but we’re behind any T, any 4x with better kicker, and most pairs 77–99 that could have stabbed flop. Betting for “value” is awkward because worse hands mostly fold, while better hands call/raise, and we don’t want to bloat the pot or get put in a tough spot versus a check-raise. Best is to take the free river, realize our showdown value, and be ready to bluff-catch small or fold to heavy pressure depending on sizing/line.

I have two pair (6s and 4s) on 4♥ 6♦ 6♠ 10♣ J♦ with 4♦5♣. This is a good showdown hand but very vulnerable — the only hands that beat me are trips/quads/full houses (any 6, pocket 4s, 4+6 combos). FiveTwo cold‑called my half‑pot flop bet then checked the turn, a relatively passive line more consistent with single pairs, medium pocket pairs or floats than with monsters that would often raise. Betting the river would bloat the pot and invite a raise from the few hands that beat me while extracting little from worse holdings. With deep stacks and a single opponent, pot control is correct: check and take it to showdown. If FiveTwo bets a small/medium size I will call for value; I will fold to a large polarized shove/raise that represents a full house/quads.

River checks to us in a heads-up pot after Minni led small on the flop and then checked turn+river. That line is very often a one-and-done stab with overcards/air or a medium-strength hand (77–99, some 4x, some Tx) trying to get to showdown.

Our hand is actually fairly strong here: with Jc4c on 4h 6d 6s Tc Jd we have two pair (Jacks and Sixes) and we beat all one-pair hands, all 4x, and Tx. The main hands that beat us (6x, JT, TT, JJ, 44) are a smaller portion of the range given the double-check.

So we should go for thin value with a small sizing that gets called by worse (Tx, 4x, stubborn pocket pairs) while keeping the loss small if Minni springs a check-raise (which we can comfortably fold to at this stack depth).

I have two pair (6s and 4s) — a good showdown hand but vulnerable to trips/quads or pocket 4s. Villain (FiveTwo) cold‑called my flop bet, checked the turn and now makes a small river probe (550 into a 2050 pot). That passive line is more consistent with single pairs / medium pocket pairs or a thin value bet than with a polarized full house/quad line. The sizing gives me ~20–22% pot odds to call, and my hand beats most of the calling range (one-pair hands and missed draws). Raising OOP would bloats the pot and only gets action from hands that already beat me. I’ll call the small river bet to get to showdown and fold to any large raise that clearly polarizes to full houses/quads.

I’m on the button with A♠4♥ (an ace with a weak kicker — offsuit). It’s folded to me and I have strong positional advantage vs the blinds. With deep stacks and heads-up, open-raising a wide range from the button is correct to (1) pick up the blinds, (2) isolate the short/mid BB, and (3) give me post‑flop initiative. Table sizing has been around 3x opens, so I’ll raise to 3x (300) to be standard — large enough to deny cheap flops and discourage light 3-bets from the big blind (Pro has ~49bb). If I face a large 3-bet I’ll proceed cautiously (likely fold unless it’s small or I have a read). If called, I’ll use position to c-bet favorable ace/low boards and extract value when I connect.

6♠3♣ offsuit is near the bottom of our defend range and plays very poorly out of position, especially with the big blind still to act who will often come along getting a great price. Calling creates a low-EV, multiway spot where we realize equity badly and get dominated when we make a pair. A bluff 3-bet has no blockers and risks inflating a pot with a hand that can’t continue well versus a button continue range. Preserve stack and avoid the marginal spot.

I am in the Big Blind with A5o facing a standard 3x raise from the Button (Minni). My stack is approximately 50 big blinds. A5o is a standard defense hand against a wide button opening range due to card removal (Ace blocker) and decent equity. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 450), and 3-betting is a bit risky/aggressive for this stack depth without a specific read to exploit. Calling allows me to see a flop and evaluate, keeping the pot manageable while out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with A5o. The flop comes 2-3-7 rainbow. I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). This is a relatively dry board that misses a lot of high-card ranges, though the Button raiser (Minni) still has range advantage. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal here as it denies me the chance to induce bluffs or see a free turn if the opponent checks back. My hand has decent equity to call a standard continuation bet, but checking is the first step.

I opened preflop to 300 from the button with A♥4♠ and was called by the BB. Flop 2♣-3♠-7♦ is very dry and largely misses a lot of the BB’s calling range (broadways, many suited connectors, small pairs). I have Ace-high plus a backdoor wheel draw (need a 5 — ~16% to hit by river) and one spade for a remote runner-runner flush. Heads-up and in position this is a textbook spot to continuation-bet to take down the pot or charge worse hands. I’ll size about 1/2 the pot to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal holding: bet 325 into 650. If I face a large raise I’ll give this up with the weak kicker; versus a small raise I can call and reevaluate on the turn.

I hold A5o on a 7-3-2 rainbow flop. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4) and an Ace overcard, giving me reasonable equity. Minni (Button) bets 325 into a pot of 650 (half-pot). I am getting 3:1 pot odds. My Ace-high is currently ahead of Minni's pure bluffs (like KQ, JTs that missed), and I have outs to improve against their value range. Folding A-high with a gutshot on this dry texture would be over-folding. Raising is too risky as it isolates me against better hands. Calling is the standard play to see a turn card while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

I am out of position on the turn with Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (A-2-3-4-5). The Jack of Clubs is a card that hits the opponent's range reasonably well (broadway hands). Leading out (donk betting) here serves little purpose as I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor who also bet the flop. If they check back, I realize my equity for free. If they bet, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing, although facing a double barrel would make continuing difficult.

Preflop I opened from the button and c‑bet the dry 2♣-3♠-7♦ flop to take the pot or charge worse hands. Pro’s check‑call on the flop shows a range of medium strength hands: pairs (2s/3s/7s), small pocket pairs, broadway combos and some floats/overcards. The J♣ on the turn improves a lot of those hands (gives Jx/top pair outs) and Pro now checks — which is an ambiguous but not necessarily weak action (could be pot control with a pair or a slow/cautious caller).

With A♥4♠ I have only Ace‑high (weak kicker) and no clean turn improvement. Betting the turn would be a thin bluff/value‑bet: I don’t have much value to extract and my fold equity is limited because Pro already called the flop. A bet also risks a shove from a shorter stack that I don’t want to face with a marginal hand. Checking is the prudent line — it controls the pot, realizes my showdown value, and keeps weaker hands and bluffs in the pot so I can evaluate the river. If the river improves me (a 4 or A) I can extract; if Pro bets the river I’ll re‑evaluate (call small/medium bets with Ace‑high but fold to big aggression unless I improve).

I arrive at the river with Ace-high (A5) on a board of 2-3-7-J-6. The opponent (Minni) raised pre-flop, c-bet the flop, but checked back the turn. This check-back on the turn caps her range, suggesting she likely holds a marginal made hand (like a weak pair, e.g., 7x, 88-TT) or air (missed broadways). The river 6 completes a possible straight for 4-5, but is otherwise a blank. My Ace-high has showdown value against her missed broadway bluffs (KQ, QTs, etc.). Betting here would be turning my hand into a bluff, but it is difficult to represent a strong hand that would take this line, and she is unlikely to fold a pair given her stack advantage. Checking allows me to realize my showdown equity against her bluffs. If she bets, I can easily fold knowing I am likely beaten.

I check. I only have Ace‑high (A4) on 2♣‑3♠‑7♦‑J♣‑6♦ — a marginal showdown hand. Villain cold‑called my flop c‑bet, then pot‑controlled by checking the J on the turn and checked the river as well. That line is consistent with made small pairs (2/3/7), cautious Jx, some 6x/7x turns into pair, or a sticky float — not a lot of pure air. Betting the river would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity (he already called the flop), and is likely to get called by pairs that beat me or induce a shove I can’t profitably call. I’d only bluff with a much stronger read or a blocker-heavy holding; while my Ace removes some Ax bluffs it doesn’t create enough fold equity to justify risking chips here. Plan: check to showdown; if he fires a small/medium bet I’ll consider a call for pot odds against potential bluffs but fold to large aggression since my hand has almost no value.

3-handed with deep stacks versus BB and a short SB, we should be opening very wide from the button/UTG to pick up blinds and leverage position postflop. 9h6h is a solid suited connector that plays well in position, can make strong draws, and benefits from fold equity. I’ll use a standard small open size to keep my range wide and avoid bloating the pot versus the short stack’s potential shove range.

FiveTwo is the chip leader and is opening frequently from the button (2.5x raises). [7d 6d] is a strong suited connector that plays well against a wide opening range, offering good post-flop playability and implied odds. With a stack of 57 BB, I am deep enough to speculatively call. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to play for a pot of at least 450), and 3-betting might force a fold from worse hands or face a 4-bet that forces me to fold equity. Defending the Small Blind by calling is the most standard and EV+ line here.

I’m in the big blind with Ad7h facing a standard button open to 250 and the small blind already called — a 3-way pot. Pot is 600 and I only have to call 150 (total committed 250). Ad7o is a playable hand here: it blocks some of the button’s strongest A combinations and has decent showdown value, but the 7 kicker is weak so I don’t want to bloat the pot or get isolated by a stronger Ace. Calling gives me ~20% pot‑odds (150 into a 750 pot) to see a flop and realize equity with position against the button postflop; it keeps the pot under control against a potentially stronger multiway range. Plan: call to see the flop, play cautiously if I miss, and extract value or pot‑control if I connect (bet value with top pair, fold or pot‑control with weak Ace).

I have flopped middle pair (7s) combined with a flush draw on a [9d 7s 2d] board. This is a very strong semi-bluffing hand or even a value hand depending on the opponent's holdings. In a multi-way pot out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo), the standard and most prudent play is to check. This conceals the strength of my hand and gives the aggressor a chance to make a continuation bet, which I can then decide to call or raise. Leading out (donk betting) typically weakens my checking range and might fold out bluffs that I want to keep in the hand. I will check and evaluate based on the action behind me.

I have A♦7♥ on 9♦ 7♠ 2♦ — middle pair with a top-kicker Ace and I’m in a 3‑way pot. This is a marginal but showdown‑worthy hand: it beats lots of missed hands and some weaker pairs, but it loses to 9x, sets and many stronger Ax hands. With two opponents still to act and the SB already checked, I want to control the pot size and keep options open rather than bloating the pot out of position. A bet here would expose me to raises from the preflop raiser and charge hands that have decent equity (9x, sets, and diamond draws) — I’d rather see how the button reacts and call a reasonable c‑bet. Plan: check to pot‑control; call small/medium continuation bets and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold only to a large raise that strongly represents a made hand.

Flop checks to me multiway on 9d7s2d. I have top pair but a weak kicker and this board is fairly dynamic (diamond draws, straight draws like T8/86, plus lots of overcards that can realize for free). In position, I want to bet for thin value/protection and to deny equity, while keeping sizing modest so I’m not overcommitting versus the SB’s short-stack check-jam range. If I face a big raise/jam, especially from Pro, I can comfortably fold this kicker multiway.

I hold [7d 6d] on a [9d 7s 2d] board, giving me middle pair and a flush draw. The pot is 750, and the chip leader (FiveTwo) has bet 300 (40% pot). My hand has significant equity and plays well as a check-call. Raising is an option to deny equity and exert pressure, but check-calling keeps the pot manageable, keeps the opponent's bluffing range wide, and protects my checking range. With Minni yet to act behind me, calling is also safer than re-opening the action to a potential shove. The price is good to see a turn card.

I have A♦7♥ on 9♦‑7♠‑2♦: middle pair with the best possible Ace kicker but vulnerable to 9x and sets. This is a 3‑way pot and the button’s flop stab is small (300 into a ~1350 pot). I prefer pot control with this marginal made hand — raising here is thin and risks isolation against stronger, polarized hands (or a shove from the short SB). Calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in, gives me cheap equity to improve on the turn, and lets me re‑evaluate with more information. Plan: call the 300 to see the turn; if I improve (A or 7) I’ll extract value, if the turn is blank I’ll protect my stack and fold to significant aggression or a polarized shove.
