The turn is the Jack of Hearts [Jh], making the board [9d 7s 2d Jh]. This card likely connects with the Button's range (e.g., QJ, JT, KJ) or potentially the Big Blind's calling range. My hand, a pair of Sevens with a flush draw, has likely dropped in relative value as a made hand, now sitting as third pair. Leading out (donk betting) into two opponents is inadvisable as it likely only gets action from better hands. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser and flop bettor (FiveTwo). I still hold significant equity with the flush draw and pair outs, so I intend to check-call a reasonable bet, but checking is the clear first step to realize equity efficiently.

I have A♦7♥ on 9♦‑7♠‑2♦‑J♥: middle pair with the best Ace‑kicker but very vulnerable to 9x, Jx and sets. This is a multiway pot (button initiated, SB called), so I want to control the pot size rather than inflate it. Betting here will often get raised/isolated by better hands (or shove by the short SB) and I’m not willing to commit with just middle pair. Checking keeps worse hands and bluffs in, allows me to see the river for free, and lets me fold to clear, strong aggression. Plan: check and call a reasonable river bet for value, fold to large raises/shoves that clearly represent two‑pair or better.

The Jh is a fairly bad turn for my specific hand: I go from top pair to a marginal middle pair (lots of Jx now beats me), and in a 3-way pot a second barrel is likely to get called by better (Jx, stronger 9x) or by strong draws that have good equity. Since both players checked, their ranges contain plenty of medium-strength hands and draws, but a check-raise (especially from the short SB) will be very value-heavy and force me to fold anyway, making a protection bet awkward. In position, I’ll take the pot-control line, realize my equity, and be prepared to call reasonable river bets on safe cards / fold to big aggression or bad runouts (diamonds/overcards).

On the river, the board reads [9d 7s 2d Jh 4c]. I hold a pair of Sevens (third pair) with a missed flush draw. The turn checked through, suggesting neither opponent holds a very strong hand like a set or strong two pair. FiveTwo's check-back on the turn caps their range, likely holding a weak Jack, a Nine, a Seven, or air. Minni's range is likely capped at one pair or missed draws. 

Betting here achieves little: worse hands (like Ace-high or a Deuce) will fold, and better hands (Jacks, Nines, better Sevens) will call. Turning my hand into a bluff is risky in a 3-way pot. The most EV+ play is to check and hope to get to showdown cheaply. My hand has enough showdown value to beat missed draws and some Ace-highs, but not enough to withstand a bet. I will check.

This is a 3‑way pot and I hold A♦7♥ — second pair with the best Ace kicker on 9♦-7♠-2♦-J♥-4♣. Both opponents checked the turn and river, which strongly suggests missed or medium-strength holdings; thin value-betting here is likely to get called by better pairs (9x, Jx, sets) and will cost chips unnecessarily. My priority is pot control and a cheap showdown with a vulnerable but showdown-worthy hand. I'll check to go to showdown; if I face a small river bet I'll call to catch bluffs/weak pair showdowns but I will fold to a large shove that clearly represents two-pair or better.

River goes check/check to me in a 3-way pot on 9d7s2dJh4c with 9h6h (one pair, weak kicker). A thin value bet can sometimes get looked up by worse (7x/2x/4x/88-55), but in multiway this runout also contains a meaningful amount of better hands that will check to me (any Jx, stronger 9x like T9/98s, slowplayed sets/straights), and the downside is getting put in a terrible spot versus a check-raise (especially from the short SB who can jam). Since the pot is modest, I don’t need to force thin value; taking the free showdown maximizes realized EV and avoids donating 1 bet into ranges that are more value-dense when they spring aggression on the river.

I am on the Button with Ace-Jack suited (AsJs) in a 3-handed game. My stack is 52 BBs, while opponents have significantly more. AJs is a premium hand, especially short-handed. I should open-raise to build the pot and capitalize on my position. A standard open size of 2.5x BB (250) is appropriate here to balance my range and offer a good price while retaining fold equity.

Pro (button) opened to 250. I hold K♦5♦ in the small blind — a suited king with reasonable playability but not strong enough to 3‑bet for value out of position. Calling 200 more (250 total committed) is cheap relative to my 22,125 stack and gives good pot odds against a wide BTN opening range in a 3‑handed game. I’ll flat-call to see a flop and re-evaluate: fold to a large squeeze/3‑bet preflop, and if I see the flop plan to proceed aggressively only with top pair, strong draws, or good board texture.

Pocket 5s are easily strong enough to continue getting ~5:1 immediate odds (150 to win 600) and a multiway pot where we can realize big implied odds by flopping a set. A squeeze here risks inducing a shove from the short-stacked button and/or getting called by Minni, creating a bloated pot OOP with a hand that doesn’t play great as a big 3-bet bluff/value mix. Calling keeps Pro’s wide opening range and Minni’s flats in, and lets us play straightforwardly postflop (set value / disciplined folds on high, coordinated boards).

Flop: 8♣ 4♣ 3♥. I hold K♦5♦ — I completely missed. There are two clubs on board (possible club draws for opponents) and I have no pair, no meaningful straight draw (only an extremely remote runner-runner A+2 possibility) and no backdoor diamond flush (with zero diamonds on the flop I cannot make a 5-card diamond flush with only two cards left). This is a three-way pot with the button’s wide open and the BB defending; leading out OOP as the small blind into two opponents with no equity is a losing line. I’ll check to keep the pot small and see how the other players act. Plan: fold to a standard bet (I have no real equity/clear outs), only continue if checked through (then consider a small delayed bluff on a very favorable turn or bet for value if I pair a King), or call only an extremely tiny probe that gives good odds (unlikely).

Multiway flop on 8c-4c-3h, our 55 is just an underpair with modest showdown value and only weak backdoors (one club, no real straight equity). Betting doesn’t accomplish much: we get called by any 8x/4x/3x and club draws, and we risk getting raised (especially by the button preflop raiser) and being forced to fold the best hand. Checking keeps the pot controlled OOP and lets Pro c-bet his air; we can then consider a check-call versus a small sizing and fold versus larger pressure or multiway action.

I raised pre-flop with AsJs and got two callers. The flop comes 8c 4c 3h. This low, connected board with a flush draw hits the blind calling ranges fairly well (sets, two pairs, straight draws, flush draws). With two opponents and only overcards (plus a backdoor spade draw), my equity is vulnerable. C-betting into two players on this texture is risky as I likely have little fold equity against pairs or draws, and I face the risk of a check-raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see a turn card, and potentially hit top pair or evaluate the situation with a smaller pot.

Board is 8♣‑4♣‑3♥‑3♣ and I hold K♦5♦. I have no pair and no club — the turn both pairs the board and completes a lot of club flushes. The Button opened wide pre and BB defends a lot, so both ranges contain many clubs and hands that now beat any lone pair I might make on the river (flushes, trips, 8s/4s, two‑pair combos). With virtually no equity and two opponents in the pot, bluffing the turn is low EV and betting invites calls from made hands. Pot control is the correct line: check and fold to meaningful pressure. If checked to the river I’ll re‑evaluate and only bluff on a safe blank (non‑club, non‑3) or bet for value if I pair. For now I check to keep the pot small and avoid committing with nothing.

Turn brings the 3c, pairing the board and giving us a club (8c-4c-3h-3c). With 5s5c we’ve picked up a flush draw, but it’s a very weak 5-high draw and the paired board reduces our implied odds (flush can still lose to boats/quads). In a 3-way pot, betting doesn’t fold out the hands that matter (any 8x/4x/3x, decent pairs, higher club draws) and, importantly, can induce an awkward jam from Pro’s short stack that we can’t profitably continue against. Best is to keep the pot controlled, realize our equity, and respond: call small bets with correct price, fold to big pressure/shoves.

The turn card is the 3c, making the board 8c 4c 3h 3c. This card completes the flush draw and pairs the board. With two opponents checking to me again, it signals they likely do not have a flush or a strong 3, or they are trapping. My Ace-Jack high has some showdown value against missed straight draws (like 56, 67) or worse Ace-highs. Betting here is risky because I represent very little strength given my flop check, and I likely only get called by better hands (flushes, trips, pairs) while folding out worse hands. Checking back allows me to see a free river, potentially hitting an Ace or Jack, or winning at showdown with A-high.

I have K♦5♦ on a final board 8♣-4♣-3♥-3♣-7♣. I missed completely — no pair, no club, no backdoor. The river is a club (7♣) and there are four clubs on board, so any opponent holding a single club now has a made flush; with Pro (button) opening wide and BB defending a lot, both ranges contain many club combos and hands that beat K-high (flushes, any 3x/7x two‑pair, and high pairs). This was my plan from the turn: check for pot control and only bluff on a safe blank (non‑club, non‑3) or value bet if I’d paired — the river is neither. Betting into two opponents in a three‑way pot here is very low EV (they’ll call with clubs, pairs, or even some river-showdown hands) and I have no value to extract. I’ll check and go to showdown; if someone leads out after my check I will fold to meaningful pressure.

River puts the 4th club out on a paired board. With 5c we do have a flush, but it’s the absolute bottom-end flush (only 2c is worse) and any single higher club beats us, while all 3x/8x/4x/7x boats beat all flushes. After flop/turn checking and Minni checking river, we’re best off taking the free showdown: betting isn’t for value (worse rarely calls) and as a bluff it risks getting looked up by any flush and always losing more to boats. Check and realize our equity.

The river brings a fourth club to the board (8c 4c 3h 3c 7c). I hold the Ace of Spades but no club. My hand strength is technically a pair of Threes (from the board) with an Ace kicker. Given the four clubs on the board, it is highly probable that one of the two opponents holds a club, giving them a flush. Even if they don't, any pair (8, 7, 4, or pocket pair) beats me. However, since the action checked down on the flop and turn, their ranges are weak, likely lacking strong made hands. I might win at showdown against other unpaired hands without a club (like KQ, QJ, T9 offsuit). Betting would likely only fold out hands I am already beating (worse high cards) and get called by any flush or pair. Bluffing is ill-advised into two opponents on a 4-flush board without the Ace of Clubs blocker. The best play is to check back and hope my Ace kicker is sufficient to win the pot.

I’m on the button with Ac6h (219 BB) and two opponents: SB (FiveTwo, deep) and BB (Pro, ~48 BB). This is a classic spot to open a wider range — A6o is marginal but plays well in position and has blocker value. I’ll open-raise rather than limp or min-raise to 200 to gain fold equity, avoid a multiway limp pot, and try to isolate the shorter big blind. A standard 3x-ish open to 300 achieves that while keeping the pot manageable deep-stacked. If FiveTwo 3-bets large I’ll fold non-premiums; against small 3-bets or calls I’m comfortable playing postflop in position.

3-handed, BTN is opening very wide and we’re out of position in the SB. Kh9o is too strong to just fold getting a good price, but flat-calling invites the BB to come along (especially with a shorter stack) and puts us in a tough multiway OOP spot. A light 3-bet here leverages our big stack, applies pressure to Minni’s wide range, often wins preflop, and when called we play with initiative and a useful blocker (Kh) to strong Kx.

Sizing to 1100 is big enough to discourage the BB from over-calling and forces Minni to continue with a more defined range, while still keeping our risk controlled.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 9d. The Button (Minni) opened to 300 (3BB) and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 1100 (11BB). My hand is an offsuit connector gap that is easily dominated by the ranges of both a Button opener and especially a Small Blind 3-bettor. Calling would require investing about 20% of my remaining stack (4850 chips) with a marginal hand, out of position, and with the original raiser still to act behind me. This is a clear fold to preserve chips for a better spot.

I opened to 300 on the button with Ac6h and FiveTwo (SB) 3‑bet to 1100; BB folded. Ac6o is marginal but has an ace blocker and plays very well in position. Both stacks are deep (I’m ~216 BB, FiveTwo very deep), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a non‑premium. A minimum 4‑bet to 1900 is an awkward small bluff that can be exploited or face a 5‑bet; folding is too tight. Calling preserves positional advantage, realizes equity, and keeps postflop options open. I’ll call the extra 800 (1100 total) and use position to c‑bet on ace‑type boards, control the pot on dry boards, and fold to large 5‑bets or very aggressive lines.

Heads-up 3-bet pot, I have top pair on KQ8 with a weak kicker. This is strong enough to value-bet versus Minni’s wide preflop calling range (Qx, worse Kx, straight/flush draws) while also denying equity to hands like AJ/JT/T9 and diamond draws. I don’t want to bloat the pot with K9o OOP, so I’ll use a small c-bet that keeps my range protected (still consistent with AA/AK/KQ) and lets me comfortably fold if I face significant aggression.

I called the 3‑bet preflop to use position, but this flop (8c Kd Qd) completely misses my A6o — no pair, no meaningful draws (Ac blocks some A‑combos but I’ve got essentially Ace‑high only). FiveTwo is a deep, aggressive 3‑bettor and a small 650 into a 2950 pot is a standard continuation bet; many of his continuing combos (Kx/Qx, diamond draws, sets) beat me. Calling 650 requires ~18% equity; with only three remaining Aces and no backdoor flush/straight I don’t have that. Bluff‑raising is thin here — he’s unlikely to fold enough and I’ll be in a tough spot if he pushes back. Preserve the stack for better spots and fold.

3-handed on the button we should be opening very wide, and J2o is still within a profitable steal range given position and our big stack advantage. A small 2x keeps risk low, pressures both blinds (especially the SB who can’t comfortably defend/3-bet too wide versus a big stack), and lets us realize our positional edge postflop when called. We’ll comfortably fold to significant 3-bet pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 2d, which is a very weak hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has min-raised to 200. Although the price to call is cheap (150 chips), playing 82o out of position against the chip leader and with the Big Blind still to act is a losing strategy. As the short stack, I need to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

I’m in the big blind with 3c6c facing a standard 2BB open to 200 from the button (SB folded). Pot is 350 and it costs me 100 more to call (200 total). 3-6 suited is speculative but playable heads‑up and I get good pot odds (~4.5:1) to see a flop. A tiny 3-bet to 300 would have little fold equity and would bloat the pot out of position with a weak hand; folding is too conservative given the price. I’ll call 200 to realize equity and evaluate the flop (look to continue with a pair/flush/strong draw, otherwise proceed cautiously OOP).

I have a marginal made hand: middle pair (6s) with a 3 kicker (3c6c) on Qd Ks 6h, out of position versus the button’s open. Betting OOP would bloat the pot with a hand that is vulnerable to most K/Q combos, overpairs and sets; checking controls the pot and lets the opener c‑bet wide so I can react. Plan: check and call small continuation bets (roughly up to ~1/2 pot ≈225) to realize equity, but fold to a large raise since my kicker is weak and I’m often behind. If villain checks back I take a free turn card and re-evaluate (bet turns only when I improve or have a clear fold equity line).

We have total air but strong range advantage on KQx as the button opener (we credibly have AK/KQ/QQ+), while BB’s check contains a lot of middling hands that can’t continue versus even a small stab. A low c-bet risks little, folds out random lows/A-high, and sets up turn barrels on good cards (A/T/diamond) while we can shut down when called on bad runouts.

I call 175. I have middle pair (6) with a very weak 3‑kicker, out of position vs the button. Villain’s 175 into a 625 pot is a very small c‑bet (~28% pot) and is consistent with a wide, often bluffing/airy range. Calling is cheap — 175 to win 800 after my call (≈21.9% of the pot), so I only need ~22% equity to justify a call. Folding here surrenders too much value; raising is thin and will often isolate me against Kx/Qx/sets that continue and bloat the pot OOP with a marginal holding. Also note there is no realistic flush possibility (no clubs on the flop and only two cards remain), so my main improvement route is trips. Plan: call to see the turn, then re‑evaluate — proceed if I improve or if villain shows clear weakness, but fold to substantial turn aggression while still holding just one pair with a poor kicker.

I check. I have middle pair (6) with a very weak 3‑kicker on Qd Ks 6h 4s and I'm out of position vs the button. Villain (FiveTwo) opened and made a very small flop c‑bet (175) that I called — that line is wide and often includes Kx/Qx, some bluffs and many hands that still beat or dominate my kicker. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and mostly get called by hands that dominate me (K/Q/sets) or get raised by stronger holdings. With deep stacks and only one pair, pot control is correct: check and take a free river card, then re‑evaluate. Plan: fold to large river aggression if I don’t improve; call small‑to‑medium river bets only very selectively; if I improve to trips/two‑pair on the river I will value bet.

Turn is a complete brick (no draws complete), and after we c-bet small on KQ6 and get called, BB still has a lot of one-and-done floats (A-high, JT/T9 gutters), underpairs, and 6x that can’t comfortably continue versus a real second barrel. Even though we have no equity, we retain a strong perceived range advantage as the button opener (AK/KQ/QQ+), and a medium sizing should generate enough folds without risking much against the 2nd stack. If we get called, we’ll usually shut down on most rivers and avoid lighting money on fire into obvious Kx/Qx continues.

Board Qd Ks 6h 4s, I hold 6c3c — just middle pair with a very weak 3‑kicker. Villain opened from the button, made a tiny flop c‑bet (175 into ~450) that I called, then upsized to 550 into the ~800 pot on the turn (~69% pot). Calling 550 to win 1,350 requires ≈40.7% equity. I only have ~5 clean outs to meaningfully improve (two remaining 6s + three 3s ≈ 11% to make two‑pair or trips), so I’m far short of the required equity. The line and turn sizing polarize toward value (Kx/Qx/sets/overpairs) and there aren’t enough bluffs in the range to justify calling. Raising would be thin and will often bloat the pot out of position versus stronger holdings. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the Button with Ten-Eight offsuit (T8o) and a stack of 48 big blinds. While Button opening ranges in 3-handed play are wide, T8o is at the very bottom of a standard opening range and is often a fold. My opponents in the blinds have significantly larger stacks (200+ BBs) and have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet. Given that T8o plays poorly post-flop against calling ranges and cannot withstand a 3-bet, the prudent play is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

5♣3♣ is a speculative, suited hand that gains value with deep effective stacks (I have ~204bb). The price to see a flop is cheap (call 50 more to 100 total) and gives good implied odds to hit a hidden flush or straight heads-up. Raising to 200 would bloat an out-of-position pot with a marginal holding and likely get called; folding would be too tight given the pot odds. Plan: complete to see the flop, fold to heavy aggression if I miss, and pursue pots when I hit pair+ or strong club/straight draws.

We’re 3-handed with Pro extremely short (48bb at 50/100 is 48? actually 4.8k = 48bb) and Minni covering him but well behind us. Versus a small-blind limp, 8♠2♦ is near the bottom of our range and plays poorly postflop OOP—raising just bloats a pot where we’ll often be guessing and exposes us to a limp-raise or stubborn defending range. With no need to take thin, high-variance edges against the only stack that can threaten us while a short stack is present, I’ll take the free flop and realize whatever equity we have.

Flop: A♣ 9♣ 2♥. I hold 5♣3♣ — a strong four‑card club draw (9 outs). That’s ~19% to hit on the turn, ~35% by the river. Pot is 200 and I’m out of position acting first. With deep effective stacks (~200+ BB) this is a good semi‑bluff spot: I want some fold equity to win the pot now and to build it cheaply if I hit. I’ll fire a modest c‑bet (≈½ pot) to pressure hands like Kx/Qx/small pairs and get called by worse equity or floats. If I face significant raise I’ll concede — I’m drawing and will fold to heavy value/raise — but I’ll continue against a call or a small raise to the turn.

Minni’s 100 into 300 is a very small stab on an A-high board from a limped pot, which can contain a lot of air and random backdoors. With bottom pair (2x) we’re not strong enough to raise for value and we don’t want to bloat the pot against the only stack that can hurt us, but we have plenty of equity versus a wide stabbing range and we’re in position to control later streets. We’re getting ~4:1 and can continue, folding later if pressure ramps up on turns/rivers.

Flop c‑bet got a call; on the turn the board paired Aces (Ac 9c 2h Ah). I still have 5♣3♣ — a four‑card club draw with 9 outs (~19% to hit the river). Facing one opponent who called the flop, I want to apply pressure to fold Kx/weak pairs and build the pot if I hit, but I must respect that any Ace in his hand now makes trips and will rarely fold. With deep stacks, a controlled semi‑bluff is best: bet for fold equity but keep the size manageable so I can fold to a strong raise without committing too much. I will bet ~½ pot (200). If he raises substantially I’ll fold (I’m drawing and behind to trips/full house); if he calls I still have ~19% to hit on the river and can proceed accordingly.

We pick up a decent showdown hand: with Ac Ah on board and our 2d, we have two pair (Aces and Deuces). Minni’s sizing is still very small (200 into 600), which keeps a lot of her range wide (random stabs, 9x, weaker 2x, and some Ax). Raising here is high-variance and mostly isolates us versus Ax that beats us (trip Aces) while folding out bluffs/worse, so it’s not a great value/protection spot. Calling realizes our equity cheaply with position and keeps the pot manageable; we can fold rivers to significant pressure on bad runouts.

The river (Tc) completes my club flush: Ac Tc 9c + 5c 3c = Ace‑high flush. This is a very strong hand vs the range that cold‑called my small flop (100) and turn (200) bets — likely Ax/medium pairs/Kx/Qx and some missed draws. The main hands that beat me are full houses/quads (e.g. A9/A2, 99, 22, TT, or pocket AA) or a club with a K/Q kicker (KcQc, etc). Most of those would often have raised earlier or polarised on the river, so they look relatively thin in Villain’s call‑call line. I want to extract thin value from worse hands (Ks/Qs/Tx, single pair, some A‑x that will call) while keeping the price reasonable — half‑ to two‑thirds pot is ideal. I will bet 500 into the 800 pot to get calls from worse hands; if I face a large, polarized raise (river shove / pot‑sized raise) I will fold as that range is almost always full houses/quads or the very few higher flushes.

We’re getting a very good price: calling 500 to win a final pot of 1800 means we only need ~28% equity. In this limped SB pot, Minni can arrive at the river with lots of hands that take this small-bet line for thin value or as a stab (9x, Tx, random 2x, and some bluffs), and our hand (Aces and Deuces) beats all of those. Yes, the river completes a possible club flush and any Ax beats us (trips), but her sizing is still relatively small and doesn’t strongly polarize to only monsters. Raising would be bad since worse hands mostly fold and better hands (Ax/flush/full houses) continue, so we take the low-variance bluff-catch and realize our showdown value while keeping ICM/3-handed dynamics in mind with Pro short.

I’m on the button with 5♣T♠ — a marginal but playable hand in position in a 3-handed pot. With the blinds to act behind and deep stacks, opening a standard button raise steals dead money and lets me play postflop in position if called. I’ll raise to 300 total (a standard 3x-ish open here) to apply pressure to the small blind and either take the pot preflop or see a flop with positional advantage. If the big blind (short stacked) jams over, I’ll fold—he’d be polarizing and my hand has poor equity vs a shove. If called, I plan to use position to control the pot and proceed cautiously on dry boards.

Facing a BTN min-open, we should defend SB with hands that either realize equity well postflop (suited/connectors) or make strong top pairs (broadways/aces). 9♣2♥ is one of the weakest offsuit holdings: it plays poorly out of position, has terrible domination issues when it pairs, and it’s a bad 3-bet candidate (no blockers, low playability). Calling also invites the BB to come along at a good price, creating a tough multiway spot. With plenty of chips, we can pass and wait for better steal/3-bet opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with J3o facing a Button raise from Minni to 300. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win 450, requiring ~31% equity), and my hand technically has about 33% raw equity against a wide button range. However, J3o has very poor playability out of position. It suffers from significant reverse implied odds and will struggle to realize its equity post-flop, often being forced to fold to continuation bets or dominated when hitting a Jack. Given my position as the short stack, conserving chips is better than engaging in a marginal spot out of position with a trash hand.

3-handed on the button with a big chip lead, we should apply pressure and play a wide opening range. Q7o is good enough to open in position, and raising also forces the blinds (especially the short-stacked SB) to make mistakes. I’ll use the minimum raise to keep risk low versus a potential SB shove while still leveraging fold equity and initiative against the BB.

I hold 8c 5h in the Small Blind. The Button (FiveTwo) min-raises to 200. My hand is extremely weak (8-5 offsuit) and poorly playable, especially out of position against the chip leader. While the pot odds are roughly 30%, realizing equity with this hand is difficult, and calling likely leads to losing more chips post-flop. With a stack of 46.5 BBs, I have plenty of time to wait for better spots. Folding is the clear best option.

I have KK in the big blind facing a button open to 200 (pot 350, 100 to call). This is a premium hand in a 3-handed game — I want to take the initiative, build the pot and isolate the opener rather than flat-call and give them position and free equity. Against FiveTwo’s likely wide BTN raising range a standard 3-bet for value is correct. I’ll 3-bet to a size that prices out speculative hands but leaves room to maneuver post‑flop: raise to 1000 total (I’ll be committing an extra 900 chips). If FiveTwo 4-bets very large I’ll reassess (KK is strong vs almost all ranges; respond based on the 4‑bet sizing), but preflop this is the value/isolation play.

Minni’s 3-bet sizing (to 1000 over my 200) is large for 3-handed and creates poor defending conditions: I’d need ~39% equity to call (800 into what would become ~2050), and Q7o is too dominated versus any reasonable 3-bet range. A 4-bet bluff here also performs poorly because this hand has bad blockers and plays terribly if called, while risking unnecessary chips despite my chip lead. Preserve stack and continue pressuring in better spots.

Current tournament situation: 3 players left. I am on the Button with QTo. My stack is roughly 46.5 BB, which is healthy but I am the shortest stack by a significant margin. To maximize my stack over the remaining 22 hands, I need to accumulate chips and cannot play too passively. QTo is a standard opening hand from the Button in 3-handed play due to position and reasonable high-card strength. While the opponents (Minni and FiveTwo) are aggressive big stacks capable of 3-betting (as seen in Hand 127), I cannot fold standard opens purely out of fear. A raise to 2.5x BB is a standard sizing to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. If faced with a 3-bet, I can easily fold without committing too much.

I have pocket Aces (Ad Ac) in the small blind facing a standard button open to 250. Calling 200 is passive — keeps too many players in and forces me to play out of position postflop. With the best possible starting hand I should 3-bet for value and to isolate the raiser. I want a size big enough to charge calling pairs and speculative hands and to discourage light squeezes from the big blind, but not so large that I unnecessarily commit my deep stack. Pro (button) has ~44bb (4400) and can still call or shove; that’s fine — I’ll re-evaluate if he jams. Raising to 1200 (~12bb) balances value and protection: it builds the pot with the best hand, gives fold equity, and keeps stacks manageable if I’m called. Action: 3-bet to 1200.

We’re in the BB with J2o facing a button open and a small blind 3-bet to 1200. This is a dominated, offsuit trash hand with very poor playability multiway and out of position. The pot odds (1100 to win 1550 now) aren’t enough because our equity realization vs a tight-ish 3-bet range is awful, and any squeeze/bluff is especially bad with Pro’s short 4400 stack likely to jam over the top. Best EV is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I opened to 2.5BB from the Button with QTo. The Small Blind (Minni) has put in a large 3-bet to 1200 (12BB). This is nearly 5x my open. The pot odds are unfavorable (calling 950 to win 1550, requiring ~38% equity), and the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) would be very low (~1.4) if I call, forcing me to commit my stack with a marginal hand often dominated by their value range (AQ, KQ, TT+). Minni has shown a tendency for large 3-bets previously (e.g., Hand 127). Given my status as the short stack and the poor playability of QTo against a large 3-bet range, the disciplined play is to fold and preserve my ~44BB stack for a better spot.

9♠3♦ is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with almost no playability. Calling 100 into a 150 pot (likely multiway) demands a ton of equity (~40%) which this hand doesn’t have, and seeing a flop out of position against two deep, sticky stacks is poor. I could attempt a steal by raising, but the small blind is deep and the big blind (43BB) can reasonably defend or 3-bet — not a spot to bluff with garbage. Preserve my healthy 221BB stack and wait for a much better opportunity. Fold.

Button folded so this is a heads-up blind-vs-blind spot where we should attack very wide. I cover Pro and a small open pressures a lot of his weaker holdings while giving me initiative and ways to win without showdown. 8♠5♣ is not strong but has enough playability (can make straights/two pair) to open at a cheap price. I’ll use a modest sizing to risk little while still picking up the dead blinds frequently and to avoid overinflating pots versus a shorter stack.

In a Blind vs Blind situation, the Small Blind (FiveTwo) raises to 2.5BB. I am in the Big Blind with 7d 8c, a connector that plays reasonably well post-flop. The pot odds are favorable; I need to call 150 chips into a pot of 350 (plus my call makes it 500), giving me direct pot odds of 30%. Against a standard, wide SB opening range, 78o has approximately 40% equity. Additionally, I will have position on the opponent post-flop, which allows me to better realize that equity. Folding would be too tight for this price, and 3-betting turns a playable hand into a bluff unnecessarily. A call is the standard, positive expected value play here.

Blind-vs-blind ranges are wide, but on A♣ J♠ 9♦ I still have a strong range advantage as the preflop raiser (more Ax/Jx value combos). My hand (8♠5♣) has essentially no showdown value and very little equity, so checking just invites a stab and I’ll often have to fold. A small c-bet applies cheap pressure to his missed junk, underpairs, and weak floats, and sets up profitable barrels on favorable turns (T/Q/K/club turns, etc.). If he raises, I can comfortably fold.

The opponent (Small Blind) c-bets 200 into a 500 pot on an Ac Js 9d board. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which contains many Ax, Kx, and broadway hands. I hold 7d 8c, giving me only a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) with no backdoor flush potential. I have approximately 8.5% equity to hit my straight on the turn. The pot odds offered (22%) do not justify a call based on direct odds, and the implied odds are speculative given that a Ten makes the board very coordinated (4-card straight possibilities). Floating with just a low gutshot on an Ace-high board against a range advantage is a losing play long-term. Preserving my 41bb stack for better spots is the correct decision.

3-handed on the button, JTo is well within a profitable opening range. We have position and cover both opponents, so raising pressures the blinds (especially the SB who may be more ICM/stack-conscious) and lets us realize equity with a hand that flops well enough (pairs/straights). I’ll use a small-but-standard open to keep my risk low while still denying BB a free look and setting up good SPR in position.

I hold 3h 2h in the Small Blind. The chip leader, FiveTwo, has raised to 250 from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), my hand is extremely weak (3-high) and I would be playing out of position against an aggressive big stack. The raw equity of 32s is barely enough to cover the pot odds, but my equity realization out of position will be poor. It is better to preserve my stack (41 BB) for a better spot than to bleed chips calling with the bottom of my range.

FiveTwo opened to 250 and Pro folded, so this is heads-up preflop. I’m in the big blind with 8♣3♠ — a very weak, unsuited, poorly connected hand that is heavily dominated by a button opening range. Calling 150 into a 400 pot gives ~27% immediate pot odds, but postflop I’ll be out of position with almost no realizable equity and little chance to extract value. A light 3‑bet bluff is feasible in some spots, but with these cards I lack the blockers and postflop playability to make it profitable. Fold and preserve my 22,000 stack for better spots.

I hold King-Ace offsuit on the button, which is a premium hand in 3-handed play. My stack is 41 big blinds, allowing me to play comfortably post-flop. I should open the pot to build value and take the initiative. A standard raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is consistent with my previous play and appropriate for the stack depths, balancing the desire to steal the blinds with the goal of getting value from worse hands.

I have A♣J♠ in the small blind facing a button open to 250 (pot 400). In a 3‑handed spot AJs is a strong 3‑bet candidate: it has good blocking value to premium Aces/AK and plays very well HU. I want to isolate the opener and deny cheap multiway flops to the big blind, while building a pot with a hand that has good equity and postflop playability. A raise to 900 (total) is a comfortable 3‑bet size — big enough to apply pressure and fold out lots of weaker button opens, but small relative to my 21,950 stack so I’m not overcommitting. Plan: if I get callers we play postflop aggressively; if I face a shove I’ll evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies, but AJs blocks strong Ax combos and has acceptable equity vs a shove range.

We’re facing a button open and a small blind 3-bet, with Pro very short behind (~3.8k) and likely to continue/shove often. 8♣2♥ has terrible equity versus both ranges, plays poorly postflop, and can easily get us put in a bad spot if Pro jams after we call (creating awkward multiway/side-pot dynamics). Even with decent pot odds, our realized equity is very low and the downside (reverse implied odds / getting squeezed) is high. Best is to release and preserve our stack edge.

I hold King-Ace offsuit (AKo) on the button in a 3-handed game, which is a premium hand. I opened to 250 and faced a 3-bet to 900 from Minni in the Small Blind. I am the short stack with 4100 total chips (41 BBs), while Minni covers me significantly. Minni has been active with 3-bets but has also shown the discipline to fold to a 4-bet (Hand #2). Calling would create a pot of 1900 with 3200 behind (SPR ~1.7), which is a bit awkward if I miss the flop. By 4-bet jamming all-in, I maximize fold equity, potentially picking up the 1250 chips in the pot (a +30% stack increase) without a showdown. If called, AKo has excellent equity against most of her range. Given the high value of the dead money and the desire to minimize post-flop variance as the short stack, an all-in raise is the highest EV play.

Call 4100. I 3‑bet to 900 with A♣J♠ to isolate the button; Pro’s 4‑bet to 4100 is big but still small relative to our deep stack and likely contains both value and some bluffs. Calling 3200 more (4100 total) gives ~38.6% break‑even equity (3200 / (5100+3200)), and AJs has reasonable equity vs a realistic 4‑bet range while also blocking many strong A‑combos. Folding here is too nitty in a 3‑handed spot and a 5‑bet shove commits too much with a hand that can play well postflop. Plan: call and play heads‑up OOP — proceed with aggression on favorable textures, pot‑control or fold to heavy action on A‑heavy/overcard boards, and re‑evaluate if Pro jams over my call.

Button with K♦T♣ in a 3-handed pot — deep stacks and I'm last to act preflop. The current bet is only 100 into a 150 pot, so I have good odds to call, but I prefer to take the initiative and isolate in position. FiveTwo (SB) has shown aggression in earlier hands and the BB (Pro) is middling-stacked; raising to a standard 3x-ish size will give me fold equity, keep worse hands in, and let me play postflop with position. I’ll raise to 300 total (min raise is 200), which is a routine open size here — if I face a big 3-bet I can fold KTo without risking too much, but if called I get to play a playable hand in position against ranges I can exploit.

Button opened to a relatively large 3x. T7o has poor equity realization out of position from the small blind, is frequently dominated when we make top pair, and doesn’t provide useful blockers for a profitable light 3-bet. With a big chip lead, we don’t need to take thin/negative-EV spots; preserving stack and choosing better squeeze candidates is higher EV.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h4s, a very weak offsuit hand. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300 (3BB). The Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 to win a pot of 450, offering pot odds of approximately 30.7%. Against a typical wide Button opening range, 94o has roughly 32-33% equity. However, being out of position with a hand that has poor playability (making it hard to realize that equity) makes calling a losing proposition in the long run. I am the shortest stack (though still comfortable at 82BB) and should preserve chips for better spots rather than defending junk OOP.

3-handed on the button we should play very wide, but 6h2h is near the bottom of our playable range. With Pro in the SB sitting on a shorter stack, an open-raise is more likely to get jammed on, forcing us to fold and burn 200–300 chips. Limping keeps our loss small versus a raise/shove, while still allowing us to realize some equity in position when they check (and we can often take it away on many flops with a stab).

I am in the Small Blind with K3o. The Button (FiveTwo) has limped. The pot offers me 5:1 odds to complete the blind (calling 50 more into a pot of 250). Despite being out of position and holding a weak hand, the price is efficient enough to see a flop with King-high. I will complete the bet to 100. If the Big Blind raises significantly, I will fold.

6♠8♥ offsuit is a marginal hand — not strong enough to inflate a multiway pot out of position. Both opponents are deep enough (FiveTwo very deep, Pro mid-stack) and tend to defend/raise; a raise is likely to get called and leave me OOP facing wide ranges. I’ve already invested the BB and there’s nothing to call, so I’ll check and see a cheap flop. Postflop plan: proceed cautiously — check-call small bets with decent equity or a pair/draw, fold to heavy aggression if I miss, and only build the pot with clear equity or a made hand.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o on a flop of A-7-2 rainbow. The pot is 300. This board texture (Ace-high, disconnected) hits limping ranges reasonably well (Ax), and I have missed completely with only King-high and no significant draws. Being out of position against two opponents, one of whom limped the button and the other checked the big blind, leading out would be spewing chips as better hands will call and worse hands might fold but I have little equity to protect. The standard line is to check and fold to any aggression.

Marginal hand (6♠8♥) on a wet-looking multiway flop with A♥ on board. I have almost no showdown value and only backdoor, low-probability improvement (pair of 6s/8s or runner-runner straight), and both opponents have wide ranges that include many Aces and pairs. Pot is multiway (300) and both players have deep stacks — I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with such weak equity. Plan: keep the pot small and see a free turn; check and fold to any meaningful bet unless I hit a pair or pick up a clear draw. If both opponents check through I can evaluate the turn for a possible low-cost bluff or a fold if aggression shows up.

Both blinds checked in a limped 3-way pot on A72r. Our hand (2h6h) has weak showdown value but is very vulnerable and benefits from denying equity to all the random overcards/small pairs they limp with. This is also a great spot to represent the ace after it checks through from the blinds. I’ll use a small stab size to pick up the pot often, keep risk low versus Pro’s shorter stack, and comfortably fold if we get check-raised.

I hold King-Three offsuit on an Ace-Seven-Deuce rainbow flop. I have missed the board completely, holding only King-high. FiveTwo, who limped the button, has bet half the pot (150 into 300). This texture favors the limper's range which contains many Ax hands. I am out of position and have a player behind me yet to act. I have insufficient equity (likely drawing dead or very thin) and poor pot odds to continue. Floating here with King-high is not profitable.

FiveTwo's small c-bet (150 into a 450 pot) gives me ~25% pot odds to call (150/(450+150)). I hold 6♠8♥ on 7c Ah 2s: an open‑ended straight draw to 5 or 9 (8 clean outs) plus pair‑outs (three 6s and three 8s) — roughly 14 outs to improve by the river (~51% to hit by river, acknowledging some pair-outs can be countered by A‑x combos). Stacks are deep so implied odds favor seeing the turn/river. Raising OOP with a marginal draw would needlessly inflate the pot and surrender initiative on later streets; folding is too tight given the pot odds and real equity. I will call the 150 to keep the pot under control, see the turn, and proceed: if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I will fold; if I pair or make the straight (or pick up a strong two‑street plan), I will start building the pot or raise appropriately.

Board: 7c Ah 2s Qh; my hand 6s8h. I have no pair — only a draw. On the turn I have 8 outs to a straight (5 or 9) and 6 outs to pair either card, for 14 raw outs (14/46 ≈ 30% to improve to at least a pair/straight by the river), but only ~17% to make the clean straight. Pot is 600 and there is currently no bet to call. FiveTwo showed a small flop c-bet and called my flop call; they have a wide, deep, and often sticky range that includes many Ax/Qx and pairs that the Q on the turn helps. Betting or raising OOP with only a draw will bloat the pot and will frequently get called or raised by hands that dominate me. A small semi-bluff might fold out some air, but given stack depths and opponent tendencies it's likely to be called enough to make it unprofitable. Checking keeps the pot controlled, preserves fold equity on later streets, and lets me realize my equity cheaply — I can call a small bet on the turn/river to see a card or fold to heavier aggression. Plan: check here, call small river bets or shove/raise only if I complete a clear made hand (straight) or get a very favorable read.

Minni check-called a small flop stab on A72, so their range is a mix of weak Ax (A3–AT), some 7x/2x, pocket pairs, and floats. The Qh is a good barrel card for us: it improves our equity significantly (we pick up a real flush draw with 2h6h) and it’s also a card we can credibly represent (AQ/Qx/Ax continuing). As chip leader, I want to keep applying pressure to fold out their 7x/2x/pairs and deny equity to random overcards, while still having strong backup equity when called. A controlled ~50% pot bet accomplishes that and keeps us from bloating the pot too much versus sticky Ax.

Plan: bet, and if check-raised large we can comfortably fold; if called, we can value-bet hearts on the river and mostly shut down on bricks unless we pick up a strong bluffing opportunity.

FiveTwo leads the turn for 300 into a pot that will be 1,200 if I call (600 pre‑bet + 300 bet + my 300 call). Calling 300 to win 1,200 requires ~25% equity. With 6♠8♥ on 7♣ A♥ 2♠ Q♥ I have ~14 raw outs (four 5s + four 9s for the straight, three 6s + three 8s to pair) → ~30% to improve by the river, so a single-call is +EV. The opponent has used small, consistent bets (wide, sticky range), so a raise OOP with only a draw would bloat the pot and is likely to be called or 3‑bet; I prefer to realize equity cheaply and preserve stack depth. Plan: call the 300 now; if I hit a clean straight (or a strong two‑pair) on the river I will extract value; if I miss I’ll fold to heavy river aggression and only call small river bets for showdown.

Board: 7♣ A♥ 2♠ Q♥ K♥ — I hold 6♠8♥ and missed every meaningful draw: no pair, only a lone heart. FiveTwo’s line (small c-bet on the flop, small bet on the turn) is consistent with a wide but sticky range containing Ax/Qx/Kx, some broadway straight combos (J‑T), and heart combos that now make a flush. I have essentially zero showdown value and very limited fold equity OOP; betting as a bluff risks getting called or raised by worse hands and bloats the pot against a deep, sticky opponent. Pot is 1,200 and there’s no bet to act on, so the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and preserve chips — fold to large river aggression, and only consider calling a very small river stab if it’s clearly a bluff. Decision: check.

We river an A-high flush (AhKhQh6h2h). It’s a real value hand, but the relevant issue is kicker strength: since the board already supplies A-K-Q of hearts, any opponent heart makes a flush and many of those (J/T/9/8/7/5/4/3h) beat our 6h. After Minni check-calls flop and turn and then checks river, they can still have a lot of one-pair Ax/Qx that will hate this third heart and won’t pay a big bet, plus some worse flushes (3h/4h/5h) that will call. The best way to get paid by the non-flush portion while keeping losses controlled versus a check-raise from a better flush is a small blocker/value bet. If raised big, I’m comfortably folding because bluff-raises here are rare and our flush is near the bottom end.

Board: 7♣ A♥ 2♠ Q♥ K♥. I hold 6♠8♥ — no pair and no flush (only four hearts total including my 8♥). FiveTwo’s line (small flop raise/c-bet, small turn bet, modest river bet) is exactly the line a value-heavy, sticky range (Ax, Kx, Qx, two‑pair/trips, or rivered flushes if he held two hearts) would take. Calling 450 into a 1,650 pot gives me ~21.4% pot‑odds (450/(1650+450)); my actual showdown equity versus that range is far lower — I only beat pure air/bluffs, which I expect to occur much less often than 21%. Raising OOP as a bluff with zero showdown value would bloat the pot against deep, sticky opponents and is unnecessarily high variance. With virtually no showdown value and a healthy stack to preserve for better spots, the correct play is to fold.

I am on the button with 97o, which is a weak hand. With 81 BBs, I am deep enough that I don't need to take marginal steal attempts, especially against active opponents in the blinds who are covering me. I recently folded T8o on the button, and 97o is weaker. Folding is the prudent play to preserve my stack for better spots.

Heads-up after Pro folded so it’s me (SB/button) vs the big blind. A♦2♣ is a playable hand heads-up — it has the Ace blocker and some straight potential and I’ll prefer to play it in position postflop. I don’t want to limp and give FiveTwo cheap odds to see a flop; a standard 3x BB open applies pressure, builds the pot when I have position, and keeps my range wide. I’ll raise to 300 total (300 includes my SB). If FiveTwo makes a large 3-bet I’ll fold; if he calls we proceed to the flop with position and roll forward accordingly.

Minni is opening wide from the small blind and I’m getting a good price: calling 200 more to play a pot of 400 (2:1 immediate odds), with a hand that can make strong disguised pairs/straights. 8♣6♥ isn’t a great 3-bet bluff candidate (dominated when called and higher variance), and as the chip leader I’d rather realize equity cheaply and outplay postflop on favorable textures.

Heads-up vs the big blind who called my 3x open preflop — their calling range is wide. The flop (9c 4h 3d) is very dry and misses a lot of calling hands. I hold Ad2c: currently Ace-high, with a wheel (A-2-3-4) draw needing a 5 (4 outs) and three remaining Aces/Deuces to pair (3+3 outs) — reasonable equity but no made hand. Given position and range advantage, I should c-bet to fold out many overcards and small pairs and to charge any backdoor draws. I’ll use a half-pot sizing (300 into a 600 pot) — large enough to have meaningful fold equity but not commit my stack. Plan: if FiveTwo folds I take it down; if he calls I proceed cautiously — continue for value if I pair the Ace/2 or hit a 5, check and control the pot on blanks; fold to a large check-raise since I have no made hand and am out of value/defense.

On 9c-4h-3d rainbow, 8c6h has essentially no immediate equity (no pair, no flush draw, no meaningful one-card straight draw). Calling 300 more OOP just to “see what happens” is a leak, even with decent pot odds, because we’ll be forced to fold too often on turns and can’t profitably realize or bluff enough without better backdoors. As the chip leader with a shorter third stack in play, I don’t want to bloat pots versus Minni with air and risk creating a big swing unnecessarily. Best is to release and keep pressure for better spots.

Three-handed on the button with A♣9♦ (Ah9d) — this is a clear open: good high-card value, positional advantage, and a hand that plays well postflop. Blinds are 50/100 and pot is 150; calling 100 is passive and a min-raise to 200 is too small (invites cheap flops and light 3‑bets). I’ll raise to ~3x the BB (300) to take the initiative, build the pot, and apply pressure to the blinds. Pro (BB) has ~8k (≈80bb) and could 3‑bet; I’ll fold to a large, polarized 3‑bet but may call a small-to-medium 3‑bet to play postflop in position. Overall plan: open to 300, use position to c-bet favorable flops and pressure weaker ranges.

3-handed and out of position in the SB versus a BTN open, AKo is a clear value 3-bet. I want to (1) build a pot versus Minni’s wide opening range, (2) deny Pro (shorter stack in the BB) an attractive price to come along and realize equity, and (3) set up a stack-off line if Minni 4-bets/jams given their 17.5k stack. A larger OOP size also reduces multiway frequency and simplifies postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with [Th 9h]. The action started with Minni (BTN) raising to 300, followed by a large 3-bet from FiveTwo (SB) to 1400. While T9s is a decent suited connector, facing a large 3-bet (14bb) cold makes this a straightforward fold. The price to call is poor (investing ~16% of my stack without closing the action), I would likely be out of position against the opener if they call, and I am currently the shortest stack. The risk/reward ratio does not justify entering this pot.

Three-handed on the button with A♥9♦ facing a 3-bet to 1,400 after my open to 300. I have position and a playable Ace-high hand — folding is too tight and a thin 4‑bet with A9o is risky (we’re often dominated by stronger A‑x/broadways and a small 4‑bet won’t fold out many hands). I’ll call to keep the pot controllable and use position postflop. I will call the extra 1,100 (1400 total committed) and plan to c-bet favorable A/high-card flops, pot-control on marginal boards, and fold to heavy aggression when out of position on scary textures.

This Q98 rainbow flop is quite connected and interacts well with Minni’s BTN 3-bet-calling range (98s/Q9s, 99/88, TJs/JTs, etc.), while my specific hand (AK high, no immediate draw) doesn’t benefit much from a c-bet—many hands won’t fold and I’m exposed to check-raises that force me off my equity. Since I still have strong range advantage overall (AA/KK/QQ/AQ) I can protect a flop-checking range and realize with AK, then continue aggressively on favorable turns (A/K/T/J) or vs small stabs depending on sizing.

Three-handed, I called a 3-bet to see flop in position and now hold A♠9♦ (A9) on Q♦ 9♠ 8♥ — middle pair with the best possible kicker vs many of the villain’s missed hands. FiveTwo 3-bet preflop so his range is weighted to strong hands (QQ+/AK, but also some bluffs), and his check on the flop looks like either a missed overcard or pot-control. Betting here achieves: (1) thin value vs worse nines/eights and some weaker pairs, (2) charges overcards and some turn-straight draws (JT), and (3) uses my positional initiative to fold out many hands that missed. I don’t want to inflate the pot too much with a marginal hand, so I’ll make a medium c-bet ~1/2 pot (1500). Plan if exploited: fold to a large check-raise (villain’s 3-bet range + check-raise = strong), otherwise continue cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate based on villain’s response.

Minni’s 1/3-pot stab into a 3-bet pot on this Q98r texture is very likely range-bet/deny-equity with a lot of one-pair hands (Qx/9x), straight-draws (JT/T7/76), and some traps. Folding AK here is too tight given the price: I’m calling 1500 to win 5900 total (≈25% needed), and I have solid equity with 6 clean-ish outs to top pair (A/K), plus some backdoor runouts. Raising as a bluff is high-variance on a board that connects well with their call-3bet range and risks getting jammed on by sets/two-pair/strong draws, which I don’t want with pure overcards. Call and reassess—apply pressure on favorable turns (A/K/T/J) and proceed cautiously on bricks versus larger barrels.

Turn pairs the top card (Qh), which is generally better for my perceived 3-bet range (QQ/AQ/KQ/overpairs) than for Minni’s flop-stabbing range. With AsKc I still have only ace-high and my equity comes mostly from hitting an A/K on the river; betting now as a “delayed barrel” risks getting shoved on by Qx (or slowplayed boats) and having to give up after investing more. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets Minni continue bluffing/double-barreling their draws and 9x-type hands, and preserves my ability to call reasonable sizing (or fold versus a big polar barrel) based on price.

I opened and then called a 3‑bet; I bet the flop (1500) and was check‑called. On the turn the board paired Q (Qd 9s 8h Qh). I currently have middle pair (9s) with an Ace kicker — a vulnerable holding now that many hands in FiveTwo’s 3‑bet/call range have improved: any Q in his hand becomes trips, JT already made the straight on the flop, and sets/strong pairs are still possible. Betting the turn risks value‑getting check‑raised or building a big pot when I’m likely behind; checking keeps the pot under control and preserves position for river play. Plan: check and evaluate the river — call a reasonable small bet, fold to heavy aggression (turn/river check‑raise or a large river bet), and extract thin value only if the river improves my hand or he checks again.

River pairs none further and we improve to a very strong “board-pair + top kickers” hand: QQ with A-K-T kickers. That has plenty of showdown value versus all of Minni’s missed floats/draws and most non-queen hands (9x/8x/Tx without a J). Turning this into a bluff by leading risks getting called/raised by exactly the hands that beat us (any Qx for trips, any Jx for the straight, or boats), while folding out worse hands that we currently beat at showdown. Best line is to check and realize our equity, and if Minni bets we can respond based on sizing/polarization.

Board: Qd 9s 8h Qh Ts. I hold A♥9♦ — second pair with the best kicker but a very vulnerable holding. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop and then check‑called my half‑pot flop c‑bet; that line fits a lot of strong/flatted hands (QQ/sets, JT straight, strong pairs) as well as some cautious made hands. The turn pairing the Queen greatly increases the combos that beat me, and the river Ten doesn’t meaningfully improve my relative strength but can give straights/two‑pair to parts of his range. Betting risks building a big pot when I’m often behind or folding out the only hands I beat (small aces, inferior nines/8s). With pot control in mind and zero to call, I’ll check and take showdown; if he barrels a small probe I’ll call for thin value but fold to large river aggression.

3-handed on the button with a big stack advantage, I want to apply maximum pressure and pick up blinds frequently. Even though 9♦3♦ is weak, it’s suited and plays fine as part of a wide BTN opening range, especially with the short-stacked SB (who may fold a lot or shove, which we can comfortably fold to) and a BB who has to defend out of position. I’ll use a small raise size to risk little while still generating strong fold equity and isolating the blinds.

I hold Ace-Queen suited (Qd Ad) in the Small Blind, a premium hand. The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and very active, has opened to 250. While flat-calling is a valid option to control pot size out of position, I choose to 3-bet for several reasons: 1) To extract value from the Button's wide opening range. 2) To isolate the Button and discourage the Big Blind from entering, avoiding a multi-way pot out of position. 3) As the short stack with only 14 hands remaining, I need to be aggressive and build pots with my strong hands to accumulate chips. A raise to 900 (3.6x the open) puts pressure on the opponent and charges them to see a flop.

FiveTwo opened to 250 and Pro (SB) 3‑bet to 900. Pot is 1250 and it costs me 800 to call, so I need roughly 800/(1250+800) ≈ 39% equity to justify a call. T6h is a weak, easily‑dominated holding versus a typical SB 3‑bet range (pairs, A/K broadways, strong Ax) and performs well below that threshold. Calling would likely produce a multiway, out‑of‑position pot with limited implied odds (SB only has ~7100 total), and a small 4‑bet bluff to 1550 is unattractive because I lack blockers and postflop playability. Fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I opened very wide on the BTN to apply pressure, but once the SB 3-bets to 900 (and BB folds), this becomes a heads-up pot versus a range that is typically weighted to stronger broadways/pairs plus some suited bluffs. 9♦3♦ is too low-equity and too dominated to defend profitably: it flops poorly, has weak straight potential, and even making a pair (9x/3x) is often second-best versus a 3-bet range. Calling 650 would largely force us into folding to frequent cbets, and 4-bet bluffing with this hand is unnecessary risk against a stack that can shove 71bb. Preserve the chip-lead edge and move on.

I am on the Button with Ace-Six suited (A6s), which is a strong hand to open-raise in a 3-handed game. I have 83.5 big blinds, which is a comfortable stack size. Opening from the button puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to play in position post-flop if called. A standard raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) balances value and risk, consistent with my previous opening sizes.

I have Q♠7♥ in the small blind facing a Button open to 250 with the big blind yet to act. This is a marginal, out-of-position hand — not a good 3-bet candidate — but calling is cheap relative to my 20,650 stack (200 more, total 250 committed) and gives good pot odds against a likely wide button opening range. A small re-raise would invite a 3-bet or multiway action I don't want with Q7o. Folding is too tight given the price. I will call to see a flop and then proceed cautiously OOP, folding to heavy resistance unless I improve or pick up clear equity/positional advantage.

8♣3♦ offsuit is at the very bottom of a defend range, and this spot is especially bad: it’s multiway (BTN open + SB cold call) and we’ll be out of position to both players postflop, so our equity realization is poor and reverse implied odds are high when we make a weak pair. Despite getting a decent price (150 into 600), the combination of domination and low playability makes this a clear pass.

I have Q♠7♥ and missed the flop — I only have Q-high plus a single heart (a backdoor runner-runner heart draw, very unlikely). The board (4♥ 6♥ 4♣) is a low paired texture that reasonably connects with the button’s opening range (pocket pairs, some 6x/5x, and broadways), and I’m out of position. I don’t want to build a pot OOP with a marginal hand, so I’ll check to control the pot and induce bluffs or a free turn. Plan: check and fold to significant pressure, call very small, non-committal bets to realize equity, and re-evaluate on the turn if I pick up pair or stronger draw.

I hold Ac6c on a 4h-6h-4c flop, giving me top pair with an Ace kicker on a paired board. This is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. The presence of a flush draw (hearts) suggests that betting is superior to checking to extract value from draws and lower pocket pairs, as well as to deny equity to random overcards. Checking back risks giving a free card to a flush draw or overcards. A small continuation bet of around 1/3 to 40% of the pot is standard on paired boards to induce calls from worse hands while controlling the pot size. The pot is 600, so a bet of 225 strikes a good balance.

Call 225. The button’s flop bet is small (225 into a ~600 pre-bet pot — ~37.5% of the pot), and calling 225 to potentially win a 1050-chip pot requires only ~21.4% equity (225/1050). My Q♠7♥ has little immediate showdown value but has reasonable absolute improvement chances (≈24% to pair by the river plus a ≈4.2% runner-runner heart flush chance) and decent equity vs a wide button c-bet/continuation range. The cost is tiny relative to my 20,450 stack, so I’ll call to realize equity and see the turn. Plan: fold to significant turn/river aggression if I don’t improve; continue or call small bets if I pair a Q/7 or pick up a heart/clear draw; consider a check-raise only if the turn gives a strong, playable read or real equity.

I have Q♠7♥ and completely missed — only Q-high with a single heart and virtually no realistic draw (would need runner-runner hearts). The board (4♥ 6♥ 4♣ K♠) is a paired low texture that connects well with the button’s opening/c-bet range (pocket pairs, 6x, some 4x, and broadways/hearts). I’m out of position and have no fold-equity and little showdown value, so bluffing the turn would be expensive and unlikely to work often enough. Checking preserves stack and controls the pot size. Plan: check and fold to meaningful turn aggression (villain betting again with Kx/66/etc. or a value range), only consider calling very small bluffs to realize equity, and re-evaluate on the river if I pair or pick up a legitimate draw or a favorable runout. Therefore I will check.

I hold Ac6c on a board of 4h-6h-4c-Ks. I have two pair (Sixes and Fours) with an Ace kicker. The King on the turn is an overcard to my pair. While the King favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, it also connects with some of Minni's SB calling range (KJs, KQs, etc.). My hand has showdown value, but it is not strong enough to comfortably bet for value on the turn, as worse hands (like 55, 77, or draws) might fold, and better hands (Kx, 4x) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs on the river from missed draws. It protects my stack in this late tournament stage.

Check. I missed — Q♠7♥ has no pair and with the final board 4♥ 6♥ 4♣ K♠ 3♥ I only have Q-high (one heart) and no realistic draw. Villain’s line (button open, tiny flop c-bet 225, then checked turn and now checked to me on the river) looks passive and is consistent with marginal pairs, missed broadways, or a small c-bet bluff; it also includes heart combos that now make a flush. I don’t have strong blockers to the top heart/face-card heart combos (I only block small hearts), so my fold equity is limited — a river bluff would have to fold out a large portion of villain’s range to be +EV versus a pot of 1,050, which is unlikely given the passive turn checks. With a big tournament stack relative to the pot I should conserve chips and pick better spots. Plan: check and concede the pot if faced with meaningful river aggression; if villain checks back we go to showdown without further investment.

I hold Ac6c (Two Pair, Sixes and Fours, Ace kicker) on a board of 4h-6h-4c-Ks-3h. The river 3h completes the potential heart flush draw and also brings straight possibilities (5-7, 2-5). Minni has checked to me on all three streets. My hand has showdown value but is unlikely to get called by worse hands (like 55, 77, or a weaker 6) given the scary board texture (flush and King present). Conversely, better hands like any King, trip 4s, straights, or flushes will certainly call or raise. Betting provides little value and opens me up to being check-raised or calling a better hand. Checking back is the optimal play to realize my equity and get to showdown cheaply.

Heads-up (3-handed) on the button with Q♥7♦ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Button open-raising should be wide; I want initiative and fold equity rather than just seeing a flop out of position. Raising also pressures the small blind and the shorter big blind, and gives me postflop position advantage if called. A 3x BB open to 300 is a standard, balanced size: big enough to pick up the blinds and reduce limp/call frequency, but small enough to avoid overcommitting if I run into a 3-bet. Plan: open-raise to take the lead; if I face a large 3-bet I can fold and preserve stack, otherwise play the flop in position.

3-handed ranges are wide, but facing a 3bb button open from the small blind, K2o is still a dominated, low-playability hand. Calling gives marginal pot odds (250 into 450) but we’ll realize equity poorly out of position and often go multiway with the BB, and when we make top pair we’re frequently in kicker trouble. A bluff 3-bet isn’t attractive either: it needs a high fold rate to show immediate profit and plays badly when called. With a large stack, I’d rather conserve chips and apply pressure with better blockers/playability hands.

The opponent on the Button (Minni) has raised to 300 chips (3bb). While 3-handed play typically involves wider ranges, K7o is a marginal hand to defend from the Big Blind, especially against a larger 3x sizing. The pot odds require about 31% equity realization, but being out of position with a hand easily dominated (reverse implied odds) makes realizing equity difficult. Standard defensive ranges against a 3bb open typically cut off around K8o or K9o. Given I am the shortest stack and the spot is at best neutral EV (likely slightly negative due to positional disadvantage), preserving chips for a better opportunity is the correct strategic decision.

3-handed on the button we should be opening very wide to leverage position and our big stack versus the shorter SB. Even though 52o is near the bottom of the range, raising is still profitable if the blinds defend imperfectly and we can often win the pot preflop or with a simple c-bet on favorable boards. Plan: raise small for fold equity and pot control; fold to any meaningful 3-bet and avoid bloating pots when called.

Current Hand 139/150. I am the short stack (8775 chips) significantly trailing the other two players (30k and 20k). With only 11 hands remaining, I need to find spots to win large pots to improve my standing. Pocket Deuces (2s 2c) is a speculative hand primarily played for set value. Facing a standard Button open from the chip leader, calling is an attractive option given the deep effective stacks (87 BB), which provide excellent implied odds to get paid off if I hit a set. The cost to call (200 chips) is minimal (~2.3% of my stack), and the potential reward of doubling up or winning a significant pot aligns with the need to catch up in the final hands of the tournament. Folding is too passive given the tournament situation, and 3-betting is ill-advised with the lowest pair.

Fold. 5♣3♥ is a very weak, uncoordinated offsuit hand. Facing a button open to 250 with the small blind calling, this is a multiway pot out of position — my equity is well below the ~20% I’d need given the effective pot odds (pot ~600, call 150 → ~4:1). The 100 I already posted is sunk; preserve my ~20k stack and wait for a much better spot or position. If this were heads-up or the SB had folded I’d defend wider, but not here.

I hold pocket Deuces on a Queen-Ten-Eight board. This board texture (Q-T-8) hits the pre-flop raiser's range very hard (broadway cards) and misses my low pair completely. Since I did not flop a set, my hand has very little showdown value and almost no draw equity. Leading out (donk betting) would be unwise as I'm likely to be called or raised by a better hand. The standard play is to check and fold to any bet.

Heads-up in position after opening the button, Pro’s check gives us a good spot to apply immediate pressure. On Th-8d-Qc our actual hand has essentially no showdown value and very little equity, but our preflop range still contains plenty of strong Qx/Tx/overpairs that can credibly c-bet. A small c-bet targets Pro’s whiffs and undercards, keeps risk low when we’re pure air, and sets up a straightforward plan to mostly give up if called on bricks (and potentially continue on select scare cards).

I am the short stack with only 11 hands remaining, holding Pocket Deuces on a board of [Th, 8d, Qc]. This board texture is extremely unfavorable for my hand, heavily connecting with the Button raiser's broadway range (sets, two pairs, top pairs, and straights like J9). My underpair is almost certainly behind, and I have virtually no drawing equity. Although the c-bet size is small (200 into 600), calling would be a losing play as I have no clear path to winning the pot at showdown or by bluffing. Preserving my 8,575 chips for a better opportunity to shove or play a pot with equity is critical given the tournament state.

Playing 3-handed, the Button opening range should be quite wide (typically 40-50%+ of hands). A3o, possessing an Ace blocker, falls well within a standard stealing range from the Button, despite being on the weaker end of Ace-x hands. With 85BB effective, I have plenty of depth to fold to a 3-bet if necessary, but stealing the blinds (150 chips) contributes to maximizing the stack. I will open with a standard raise to 2.5bb.

I'm in the small blind with K♥Q♥ facing a Button open to 250 (2.5x). In a three-handed game the opener's range is fairly wide and KQs is strong enough to 3-bet for value and fold equity. Calling would leave me out of position and allow the big blind a cheap call/squeeze; a small reraise (to the min 400) is too weak. I want a sizing that: 1) isolates the opener, 2) prices out speculative hands from the big blind, and 3) gives fold equity vs many worse Broadway hands. I will 3-bet to 1000 (total) — big enough to achieve those goals while keeping stacks deep enough to play postflop. Plan: if I take it down preflop great; if called I'll continuation-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously facing a 4-bet, folding to very large 4-bets but continuing vs smaller ones where pot odds and blocker effects justify it.

J5s is too weak and too easily dominated in this configuration: BTN opens, SB makes a large 3-bet (to 10bb), and we’re out of position to both with a short-stacked BTN still able to back-jam. Calling creates an awkward spot where we’ll often have to fold to a BTN shove and/or play a bloated pot OOP versus a stronger range. A 4-bet bluff is also poor here (no meaningful blockers, SB sizing suggests strength). Preserve chips and wait for a better squeeze/defend spot.

I opened from the button with A3o, a standard steal attempt. The Small Blind (Minni) has 3-bet to 1000 (10bb). Facing a 3-bet, A3o is a clear fold. It is dominated by almost all value 3-bets (better Aces, pairs) and plays poorly post-flop against a polarized bluffing range. The pot odds require about 36% equity, and A3o typically has around 30-32% against a standard SB 3-bet range. With 83bb effective, there is no need to defend this marginal hand. I fold to preserve my stack.

I’m on the button with T♣6♠, blinds 50/100, pot 150 and to-call 100. In a 3-player pot with deep stacks, opening a wide button stealing range is correct — T6o is marginal but playable because of position and fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) — a standard sizing at this table that gets reasonable fold equity, avoids inviting cheap multiway pots that limp/calling would create, and isn’t so large that I overcommit with a weak hand. Plan: C-bet favorable, dry flops; fold to a large 3-bet unless the 3-bet is small/wide and the pot odds + implied odds justify a call.

3-handed versus a Button open, A5o is well within my defend range and plays especially well as a 3-bet due to the ace blocker (reduces opponent’s strongest continues/4-bets) and wheel/straight potential. Calling would invite the BB in and leaves me out of position in a multiway pot with a dominated-ace type hand. By 3-betting I apply pressure while covering both stacks, often win preflop, and when called I retain initiative and can leverage favorable textures. Sizing slightly larger from the SB to discourage the BB from overcalling.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o. The action started with Minni raising to 300 on the Button, followed by FiveTwo 3-betting to 1200 from the Small Blind. K4o is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially out of position and cold-calling. The pot odds (calling 1100 to win a pot that will be around 2700) do not justify playing this hand, as I will likely be dominated or facing difficult post-flop decisions against two aggressive opponents. With a stack of over 80 big blinds, I should preserve chips for better spots.

I opened to 300 on the button with T♣6♠ and FiveTwo (SB) responded with a 3‑bet to 1,200. Calling 900 into the ~1,600 pot requires roughly 36% equity — T6o has poor equity and is frequently dominated by a typical SB 3‑bet range, and it’s a weak hand to continue with even in position. A 4‑bet bluff (min to 2,100 or larger) is also a bad candidate with this holding because it has little fold equity and poor post‑flop playability. Table history shows FiveTwo is aggressive but often 3‑bets for value here, so the prudent + EV decision is to conserve chips and fold.

3-handed on the button we should apply maximum pressure with a wide opening range, especially with Pro in the SB at ~82bb and a bigger blind stack that will still fold a meaningful portion. 8♣6♥ is not premium but is good enough to open on the BTN: we can win the blinds immediately, and when called we still have decent connectivity and can leverage our big stack with position. I’ll use a small, consistent raise size to risk little while keeping fold equity high; if faced with a sizeable 3-bet, we can comfortably fold this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven suited (Td 7d). The Button (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has raised to 250. Although the price to call (200 chips) offers reasonable direct pot odds, playing a suited two-gapper out of position against the chip leader is generally a losing play due to the difficulty of realizing equity. With only 8 hands remaining and being the shortest stack, preserving chips is crucial. I will fold and look for a better spot to enter the pot.

Facing a standard button open to 250 while I’m in the BB with 6♦2♣ — the absolute bottom of the hand spectrum. To call I’d need 150 more into a 400 pot, so I’m getting ~27% pot odds (150 / (400+150)). 6-2o has very poor equity versus a button opening range, is frequently dominated, and plays terribly post‑flop (no straight/flush potential worth pursuing). A 3‑bet as a bluff is too risky/deep-stacked here and I have no blockers. With a healthy 20k+ stack late in the tournament, there’s no reason to gamble multi-street with this junk — fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o) in a 3-handed game. My stack is 81.75 BBs, which is deep enough for standard play. A9o is a strong hand in this position, well ahead of the random ranges of the blinds. I have established a pattern of opening to 2.5x (250) from the button with Ax hands (e.g., Hand 137, 140). This sizing puts pressure on the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if called. Folding is too passive, and limping caps my range and invites aggression. Therefore, I will raise to 250 to steal the blinds or play a pot in position.

I’m in the SB with 9♦3♠ — a very weak, unplayable hand. Pro opened to 250 from the button; I’d have to call 200 into a 400 pot. Although the call offers ~3:1 pot odds, 9-3o has very little equity vs a button open and plays horribly out of position in a 3‑handed pot. A raise here would be a thin bluff facing two opponents and deep stacks — unnecessarily risky with seven hands left. Preserve my healthy stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

3-handed, Pro’s button open to 2.5x is likely very wide. A9o is comfortably ahead of much of that range and the ace blocks a lot of his strongest continue/4-bet hands. With the chip lead, I want to pressure his opens and pick up the pot preflop often; if he continues, I can still realize equity and play aggressively on favorable boards. I’ll 3-bet to a size that applies real pressure but keeps losses contained if he 4-bets (I fold to a 4-bet with A9o).
