I opened to 250 with A9o from the Button, and the Big Blind (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader, has 3-bet to 900. While I am in position, A9o is a marginal hand to defend against a 3-bet. It suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated by AK, AQ, AT) and lacks the playability of suited connectors or suited Aces. The price to call is 650 into a pot of 1200, offering roughly 35% direct odds, but realizing that equity with an offsuit Ace is difficult. Given FiveTwo's large stack advantage and the fact that A9o is toward the bottom of my defense range here, the disciplined play is to fold and preserve my ~79bb stack for better spots.

I’m on the button with a very strong pocket pair (JsJh) in a 3-handed game with deep stacks (≈20k). Preflop I want to take the initiative, build the pot and get value while preserving fold equity against the blinds. A standard 3xBB open (300 total) is the right balance — it’s big enough to discourage speculative calls but small enough to avoid committing too much out of position against a possible 3-bet. Plan: take the lead postflop with continuation bets on favorable boards; re-evaluate if I face a sizable 3-bet (call or 4-bet depending on sizing and opponent tendencies).

3-handed BTN opens very wide, and A7o is ahead of a large chunk of that range while also blocking stronger Ax. Calling from the SB invites the BB to come along (or squeeze) and puts us in a tough multiway spot with a hand that doesn’t realize equity well. I prefer a 3-bet to isolate the opener, deny equity, and take initiative as the big stack. If faced with a big 4-bet, we can comfortably fold given poor playability and domination risk.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 4c, a very weak unsuited hand. The action started with a Button raise to 300 from Minni, followed by a 3-bet to 1100 from FiveTwo in the Small Blind. Facing a 3-bet, my hand has virtually no equity and the pot odds (calling 1000 into a pot of 1500) are poor for such a speculative holdings. My stack is 7825 (78 BBs), and there is no reason to risk a significant portion of it with trash cards in a 3-bet pot. The standard and only correct play here is to fold.

I have JJ on the button facing a 3-bet to 1,100 after opening to 300. In a 3-handed pot with me in position, calling is the standard, +EV line: JJ is often ahead of this 3-bet range except for QQ+/AK, and calling preserves my positional advantage while controlling pot size with deep stacks. A 4-bet is viable but would be more for QQ+/AK — risking bigger commitment and polarizing the pot. I’ll call the extra 800 (1100 total) to see a flop, c-bet on many dry/favorable flops for value and fold equity, and pot-control or check/raise as the board and villain action dictate. If FiveTwo jams or polarizes heavily I’ll re-evaluate based on stack-off odds and their tendencies.

3-bet pot OOP on T♠8♣2♦ is a good board to range c-bet small: my preflop range contains the overpairs/strong Ax and big broadways, while Villain has a lot of unpaired overcards and mid/low pairs that are pressure-sensitive. With A♠7♦ I have no pair, but I do have decent backdoor equity (backdoor spades + backdoor diamonds) and I benefit a lot from denying two overcards a free turn. I’ll use a small sizing to keep my risk low while still generating folds and setting up profitable barrels on favorable turns (A/K/Q/J, spade/diamond, or some low bricks).

I have a clean overpair (J♠J♥) to the 10♠-8♣-2♦ board — very frequently ahead of FiveTwo’s 3‑bet/c-bet range (they have QQ+/AK sometimes, but much of the range and many bluffs are behind). FiveTwo’s flop bet is very small (~650 into ~2950), which looks like a probe/c-bet to realize equity cheaply rather than a polar, large value shove. In position with an overpair I want to both extract value and protect against backdoor/paired-street turn cards, so I’ll raise. A min‑raise to 1,300 achieves that: it charges draws and worse tens/eights/top-pair hands and keeps the raise small enough that I’m not overcommitting if they respond very aggressively. If they call I’ll continue for value on safe turns; if they raise big I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (fold to extreme polarization, call or shove if pot odds and reads justify).

We’re in a 3-bet pot OOP and our small c-bet gets check-raised (even if it’s a small/min clickback). On T♠8♣2♦ there aren’t many natural semi-bluff raises (no real flush/straight draws available), so this action is more weighted to value/protection (Tx, sets, 88/22/TT, sometimes 99–JJ/8x). With A♠7♦ we have no pair and only thin backdoors, and while the pot odds are tempting, we’ll realize equity poorly out of position versus continued pressure. I’d rather preserve chips here and continue versus this line with stronger parts of my range (overpairs/Tx/sets and better backdoor candidates).

3-handed on the button as chip leader, we should play an aggressive steal strategy. 9c6c has good playability (suited, connected, can make strong disguised hands) and benefits from having initiative. Limping gives up fold equity and invites the blinds to realize equity cheaply. A 2.5bb open pressures both blinds while keeping the pot manageable if we face resistance.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen-Three offsuit (Qc 3s), facing a raise to 250 from the Button (FiveTwo). Although the raise is small (2.5x BB) and I'm getting decent pot odds, Q3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. With a stack of 7,775 (approx 78 BB), I have plenty of chips to wait for better spots, especially with only 5 hands remaining after this one. Defending here would likely be a losing play in the long run. I will fold.

Heads-up vs FiveTwo who opened to 250 from the button. I’m in the big blind with Qh‑2c — not great, but against a wide button opening range and with deep stacks my hand has enough equity to defend. Calling 150 into a 400 pot gives ~27% pot odds, so 3-betting a marginal hand is unnecessary and folding is too tight. I’ll call to see a flop and play postflop cautiously: continue with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry, unfavorable boards.

I have Q♥2♣ on a 7♥ T♣ 6♦ flop — no pair and no real draw, just one overcard. Villain opened wide from the button so will c-bet frequently, but I’m out of position and the board is fairly dry. Betting OOP with a marginal hand is a bluff that’s easy to call or raise out of me; better to control the pot and see what FiveTwo does. I’ll check to induce a c-bet and then decide: call small (≈1/3 pot) continuation bets as a bluff-catcher occasionally, but fold to larger bets or heavy aggression since I have little equity. If FiveTwo checks back I can take a free turn card and proceed cautiously.

We have bottom pair plus a gutshot to the 8 (and backdoor clubs). This T76 texture connects with BB’s range, so I don’t want to build a big pot, but as the preflop raiser I can still profitably c-bet small: it folds out a lot of BB’s pure overcards/air, denies equity to hands like QJ/KQ/Ax that whiffed, and gets some thin value/protection. If called, we can often check back many turns and realize our equity; if raised, we can comfortably fold versus a range that’s heavily pairs/two-pair/sets/strong draws.

Call 200. I’m OOP with Q♥2♣ on a pretty dry 7♥‑T♣‑6♦ flop — no pair and only three outs to a Q. Villain opened wide from the button and c-bets frequently; this is a small c‑bet (200 into ~550), so calling is cheap (≈26–27% pot odds). My raw chance to hit a queen by river (~12–13%) is insufficient as a pure draw, but I expect a decent portion of his range to be air or weak overcards that I currently beat. Raising OOP with this hand is too ambitious. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to substantial turn aggression if I don’t improve, and if I pair my queen I’ll look to extract value or apply pressure depending on how he plays the turn.

Turn A♣ completes the board to 7♥‑T♣‑6♦‑A♣. I hold Q♥2♣ — no pair, no real draw and only ~6–7% to hit a queen on the river. Villain was a wide button opener who c‑bet the flop and I called; his turn range now contains many A‑x, Tx, 7x, and strong hands that will continue for value. With huge stacks relative to the pot (950) and being out of position, leading or barreling is a weak bluff with little fold equity and high downside. I should control the pot and avoid committing more chips with air. Action: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river (only consider bluffing if the river is very favorable or I pair my queen).

Turn A♣ is a great card for my perceived range, but it’s also very plausible Minni picked up top pair with Ax after calling the flop. My actual hand is a marginal made hand (pair of 6s) that doesn’t want to face a check-raise, yet I still have strong realization equity: a clean club gives me a flush and an 8 gives me a straight. With position and no need to bloat the pot, the highest-EV line is to take the free card, keep Minni’s worse hands/air in, and decide on the river with more information (value-bet good rivers, often take showdown on blanks).

Board: 7♥ T♣ 6♦ A♣ A♠. I hold Q♥2♣ — completely missed, no pair and no real bluffing blockers. Villain was a wide button opener who c‑bet the flop (small) and then checked the turn; that line contains many Ax, Tx, 7x and some air, but the turn ace heavily improves his continuing range. There is no value to extract on the river and any bet would be a thin bluff with very limited fold equity (many combos — any Ace, any Ten, single pairs — will call or at least not fold enough). With the pot at 950 and deep stacks, risking chips on a marginal river bluff is unnecessary late in the tournament. Plan: exercise pot control — check and fold to a river bet. If checked through we show or go to showdown, but we will not lead out.

River pairs the Ace, improving us to two pair (Aces and Sixes) with the board Ten as kicker. However, this is not a good value-bet spot: any calling range from Minni is heavily weighted toward hands that now beat us (any 7x/TX, strong pocket pairs like 88/99, boats like 77/TT), while most worse hands (missed straight draws like 98/85, random overcards) will usually fold to a bet. Since we already beat the missed-draw/air portion and don’t expect to get paid by worse, the highest-EV play is to check back and realize our showdown value without exposing ourselves to a check-raise or value-owning ourselves.

It is Hand 146 of 150. I am on the Button with [2d 5d] and 78BB deep. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wide, but 5d2d is at the very bottom of playable hands. Given that the Big Blind (FiveTwo) has a massive chip lead and has shown recent aggression with 3-bets (e.g., Hand 143, 144), opening with such a weak hand invites a re-raise that I cannot call, simply bleeding chips. With only a few hands left, preserving the stack for better spots or stronger steal candidates is preferred over forcing action with five-high. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

I have A♣4♠ in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind. This is a playable, blocking ace that performs well heads-up; I want to take the initiative and avoid playing a multi-street pot out of position with a limp/call. I’ll raise to 300 (3× BB) — big enough to generate fold equity against a wide BB defending range and to define the pot, but small relative to our deep stacks so postflop play remains flexible. If FiveTwo 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and tendencies; if called I’ll c-bet favorable boards and use position postflop to extract value or fold when appropriate.

3-handed BB vs SB open, Minni’s raising range should be quite wide. AJo plays well as a value 3-bet here: we’re ahead of many aces/broads she opens, we deny equity to suited connectors/gappers, and we take initiative in position-disadvantage spot. With us covering, we can also apply pressure and often win preflop or on a c-bet. I’ll size around 3–3.5x the open to punish the price but still keep her weaker hands in: raise to 1000 total. If she 4-bets big, we can comfortably fold this dominated Broadway.

I opened to 300 with A♣4♠ to take the initiative, but FiveTwo 3‑bet to 1000. Calling the extra 700 (1000 total committed) is the best option: the call is cheap relative to my 21.3k stack, A4o has an Ace blocker (reduces villain’s AA/AK combos) and reasonable equity against a wide 3‑bet range, and keeping the pot manageable OOP is preferable to thin 4‑bet bluffs that will often be called by better hands. Folding is too nitty given the price, and a 4‑bet bluff is too risky because I’m dominated by typical value continues. Plan: call to see a flop, c‑bet or apply pressure on favorable dry/A‑high flops, control the pot or fold to heavy action when I miss.

I opened and called the 3‑bet with A♣4♠ intending to c‑bet A‑high/dry flops, but this one (J♦7♥5♥) is a miss: two hearts and a jack make it connect well with a 3‑better’s value and draw combos (JJ/77/55, AK/AQ, heart draws, broadways). I have almost no fold equity and no backdoor heart, so firing here OOP would mostly get called by better hands or induce raises I’m not comfortable facing out of position. Pot control and information are superior: check to see how FiveTwo proceeds, fold to a strong check‑raise or big barrel, and only call smaller continuation bets with the plan to float the turn if it’s a blank or pick up equity on an Ace. If he checks back I’ll take a free turn to decide whether to bluff or value‑bet if I improve.

We 3-bet pre and now see Jd-7h-5h. AJo is a clear value/protection c-bet: we get calls from worse Jx, 77/55 are possible but limited, and we charge hands with decent equity (heart draws, 98/T9/86, underpairs like 88-TT). In a 3-bet pot we also maintain range advantage (overpairs/strong broadways), so betting small-to-mid keeps her wide range in while denying equity. If check-raised, we can continue versus reasonable sizing and reevaluate on turns (especially hearts/straight cards).

The flop (5♥ J♦ 7♥) completely misses A♣4♠ but I have an Ace blocker and cheap showdown value. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop and now made a small c‑bet (800 into ~2,000) — a sizing that contains both value (Jx, 77/55, some two‑pairs/sets) and plenty of bluffs/heart draws. Calling 800 to win 2,800 gives ~22% break‑even odds; vs a wide c‑betting range my hand has enough equity to justify a float. Plan: call and keep the pot controllable OOP — if the turn brings an A I can take the lead for value; if it’s a blank I’ll check and fold to large turn aggression. Raising OOP with no backdoor heart/straight is too risky and folding is too nitty given the price and blocker. Therefore I call.

Turn pairs the Jacks and gives the 3‑bettor a lot of stronger hands in his continuing range (trip Jx, two‑pair/set, overpairs, plus heart draws). I have only A♣4♠ — no heart backdoor, no pair — so I haven’t improved and have very little equity. Betting OOP as a bluff or blocker is thin EV: many hands call or raise, and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Best play is pot control and information: check and let him show his hand. If he bets big I’ll fold; if he bets a small/medium size I may call a thin float depending on tendencies; if he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river (ace becomes a potential value bet).

Turn pairs the J, giving us trips (Jx) with the best kicker (A) in a 3-bet pot where Minni can easily have worse Jx, 7x/5x, pocket pairs, and plenty of heart draws from the flop (QThh, T9hh, 98hh, etc.). We don’t want to give a free river to those draws (a heart river can beat our trips), and we also want to start building a pot for value while keeping her calling range wide. A medium sizing accomplishes both: it charges draws and still gets called by many worse made hands.

I’m out of position with A♣4♠ and haven’t connected with the board (5♥ J♦ 7♥ J♣). FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet the flop and now bets 2,000 on the turn (pot 5,600; to call 2,000). I have no heart backdoor and only 3 Aces + 3 Fours = ~6 outs to improve on the river (~6/46 ≈ 13%). Calling 2,000 to win 7,600 requires ~26% equity, so my raw equity is far below the break‑even point. Opponent’s line is consistent with many hands that already beat me (Jx trips/two‑pair, 77/55, sets, some overpairs) or will at least call a raise; a bluff raise here is unlikely to fold enough of that range and risks getting raised/called into a huge pot. Given poor pot odds, weak equity, and the strength signaled by villain’s actions, folding is the correct, low‑variance play to preserve my 19.8k stack for a better spot.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed game with Q♥5♣ — a weak but playable hand in position. With pot 150 and only 100 to call, I want to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds rather than limp and invite multiway play out of position. I’ll open-raise to 300 (≈3x BB) — a standard size that preserves fold equity and gives callers worse odds. If I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll concede (Q5o is often dominated), but if called I can play the flop in position and use aggression or pot control depending on the texture. My deep stack allows this low-risk steal attempt while keeping options postflop.

3-handed, Button opening range is very wide and calling from the SB would invite the BB in, leaving us out of position in a multiway pot with a dominated-ace type hand. A6o plays better as a blocker 3-bet: we block AA/AK/AQ, can win preflop often, and when called we retain initiative and can barrel favorable ace-high/low boards. If Minni 4-bets aggressively we can comfortably fold this combo.

I am in the Big Blind with Ah 7h. The Button (Minni) opened to 300, and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 1300. While A7s is a playable hand, facing a large 3-bet (13bb) puts me in a difficult spot. FiveTwo is the chip leader and has been aggressive, but cold-calling a 3-bet out of position with a dominated Ace is generally -EV. I would need ~41% equity to call, and against a strong 3-betting range (99+, AJ+, KQ), I have significantly less (approx 30-33%). Additionally, Minni is still to act behind me and could 4-bet. Given the tournament stage (Hand 147/150) and the risk of bleeding chips against a dominant range, folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q♥5♣ and FiveTwo 3‑bet to 1,300. The pot is ~1,700 and I must call 1,000 more (to have 1,300 total committed). Calling 1,000 into 1,700 requires roughly 1,000/(1,700+1,000) ≈ 37% equity to break even. Q5o is routinely dominated by a typical 3‑bet range (pairs, A/K broadways, strong suited connectors) and won’t have the needed equity. A 4‑bet bluff isn’t attractive either — I don’t have good blockers, a minimum 4‑bet to 2,300 buys little fold equity, and FiveTwo has a big stack that can comfortably call or shove. I’ll preserve my ~19.5k stack and fold to this sizable 3‑bet.

3-handed on the button with a big chip lead, we should be opening very wide to pressure the blinds and pick up dead money. 9-6 offsuit is not a premium, but it’s playable in position and benefits from initiative. I’ll use a small but not min sizing to reduce how often we give a great price and to keep the risk contained if we face a 3-bet. Plan: fold versus any significant 3-bet/raise; if called, use positional advantage with selective c-bets on favorable/high-card textures and take free cards on poor ones.

I am in the Small Blind with Td 8h, a weak off-suit hand. It is hand 148/150. FiveTwo has raised from the Button to 250. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win 400), playing T8o out of position against an aggressive chip leader is not a profitable spot, especially with a 30BB stack that allows me to wait for better opportunities in the final few hands. The equity realization of T8o OOP is poor. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Facing a standard button open to 2.5x (250) from the Button and I’m in the BB with T♠ J♣ (unsuited). I only need to call 150 more (total commitment 250) into a 400 pot — cheap to see a flop and this hand has decent connectivity and showdown equity heads-up. I’m out of position, so 3‑betting with JTo is marginal (would prefer stronger/ suited broadways or pairs to build a pot OOP). With deep stacks I can realize equity on many flops and fold to heavy aggression when I miss; preserving stack and seeing a flop is the +EV line here. Call to 250.

Heads-up vs FiveTwo (button) with Ts Jc on 4c 3c Th: I have top pair with a very good kicker, but the two clubs on board give many hands a flush draw. Pot is 550 and I’m first to act. Checking lets club draws see a free card and gives up protection; betting is the +EV line to get value from worse tens/jacks/medium pairs and to charge draws. I size to ~50% pot to deny equity while keeping worse hands and draws calling — bet 275 chips. If FiveTwo raises large I’ll re‑evaluate (call smaller raises, but respect strong aggression OOP).

Minni’s donk-lead for ~1/2 pot on T-4-3 two-tone hits their BB defend range reasonably often (Tx/4x/3x, small pairs, club draws), so a pure bluff-raise with 9-high and no club is likely to get called too much and leaves us with poor barrel cards. However, we’re getting good immediate price (need ~25% equity), we have some backdoor potential (5 gives an OESD; 6/9 can pair), and—most importantly—we have position to realize equity and potentially take it away on turns when Minni slows down. Plan: call now; fold to sizable turn barrels on bad cards, and stab when checked on favorable/low turns that don’t improve their range too much.

I have top pair (T♠) with a very strong Jack kicker on 4♣-3♣-T♥-6♠. The turn 6♠ doesn't complete any realistic made flush (only two clubs on board and I hold one club), but there are club draw and overcard combos that can improve on the river. I led the flop and was called, so FiveTwo's range contains worse tens, medium pairs, club draws, and some overcards — plus the occasional slow-played set. Checking now surrenders initiative and lets draws see a free river; betting protects my hand and extracts value from worse tens/weak pairs and charges club draws. With pot = 1100 a bet around 55–65% pot is appropriate: large enough to deny equity to draws and get value, but small enough to keep worse hands calling OOP. I size to 700. If FiveTwo raises big I’ll assume a set/two-pair and fold to heavy aggression; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river and proceed to get value or pot-control depending on the card.

We pick up a pair on the turn (6x) in position versus Minni’s small donk-bet line. Their range still contains plenty of one-and-done bluffs and club draws (AcXc/KcXc), plus some weak pairs/straight-draw type hands that we’re ahead of. Pot odds are good (700 to win 2500 ≈ 28%), and raising would mostly isolate us versus Tx, better pairs, and two-pair/sets while folding out the hands we want to keep betting/bluffing. Best EV is to call, realize position, and re-evaluate on the river—value-check back many rivers when checked to, and bluff-catch selectively versus reasonable sizing on blanks while folding to big polar river bets.

I have T♠J♣ on 4♣-3♣-T♥-6♠-7♦: top pair with a very strong kicker. Villain (Button) called my flop and turn bets, so his range includes worse tens, medium pocket pairs, and club draws that missed. The 7♦ is a blank — it doesn’t complete clubs or an obvious straight — so this is a good river to seek thin value. Checking surrenders value to worse tens/small pairs and gives up fold equity; betting charges the missed draws and gets called by weaker tens/single-pocket pairs and some one-card pairs. Pot = 2500; I’ll size to ~60% pot to extract value while keeping worse hands in (bet 1500). If villain raises large on the river I’ll assume set/two-pair and fold to heavy aggression; call only a small raise if pot odds and reads justify it.

Minni’s triple donk-lead line can certainly be value (Tx, 7x, better 6x, straights like 54/65), but the river sizing (1500 into 4000) is relatively small and consistent with a block/thin-value bet from weaker pairs (4x/3x/5x/65-type that doesn’t want a big pot) and with missed club-draw follow-throughs. With 9♦6♥ we have a straightforward bluff-catcher that’s ahead of all missed clubs and many one-pair hands, and we only need ~27% equity (1500 to win 5500). Raising is bad because it folds out exactly the hands we beat and gets called by better. Given our big stack and cheap price, calling is highest EV.

I hold Pocket Kings [Kc Kd] on the Button, a premium hand. With only 2 hands left (149/150) and being the shortest stack (though still ~76bb deep), my goal is to maximize value. Looking at my history, I have consistently opened to 250 from late position (e.g., Hand 137, 131, 122). Sticking to this standard size of 2.5bb (250 chips) disguises the strength of my hand and encourages the aggressive large stacks in the blinds (Minni and FiveTwo) to either call wide or 3-bet, both of which are favorable outcomes for me.

Pro opened to 250 from the button. I'm in the small blind with 5♠9♠ — a suited, speculative hand that plays well multiway but is weak to build a big pot out of position. A 3-bet here would be out of position with a marginal holding and a small min-raise (to 400) would have poor fold equity versus these stacks. I'll call to 250 to see a flop and realize equity: fold to a large 3-bet (I won't defend a big all-in or sizable 3-bet OOP with 9-5s), and on the flop continue only with strong connections (pair+ or good flush/straight draws); otherwise keep pot control and check/fold to heavy aggression. Calling preserves chips, keeps stronger hands and bluffs in Pro's range, and gives me playable postflop scenarios with implied odds in a 3-handed pot.

We’re getting a very good price (150 to win 600 = 4:1) and we close the action preflop. Even though J7o is weak and plays worse multiway, the pot odds plus BTN’s wide opening range in 3-handed make this a standard BB defend. No incentive to squeeze with a dominated offsuit jack and awkward postflop play; take the cheap flop and proceed cautiously on high-card/heavy-action boards.

I have 5♠9♠ on T♠‑2♣‑4♥ — I missed: no pair and only a runner‑runner spade (very weak backdoor). This is a 3‑way pot and I'm out of position versus the preflop opener (Pro) and the big stack (FiveTwo). Bluffing or barreling here is thin multiway and I risk a costly raise from a deep stack. Pot control is best: check to see how my opponents act, fold to heavy aggression, and only continue if I pick up a pair or a real draw on the turn (or face a very small probe bet I can call cheaply).

Multiway pot versus the preflop raiser, this Ts-2c-4h rainbow board is relatively dry and our J7o has very little real equity (one overcard + weak backdoors) and poor playability if we inflate the pot. With Pro having range/position advantage to c-bet and Minni still behind, a donk-bluff is low EV here. Best is to check and realize whatever equity we have, continuing only versus small bets with the right price/turns and otherwise folding to pressure.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kd) on a dry Ts 2c 4h flop in a 3-way pot. The pot is 750 chips. My opponents, the blinds, have checked to me. With an overpair on this unconnected board, I have a very strong value hand. My goal is to extract value from weaker pairs (Tx, 55-99) or floating hands while protecting against random 2-pair or set luck, though those are unlikely. Checking back is an option to trap, but with only 2 hands remaining in the tournament, I must prioritize getting value immediately. A continuation bet of roughly 40% pot (300 chips) is consistent with my previous sizing (e.g., Hand 137) and small enough to induce calls from worse hands or perhaps a bluff raise, while starting to build a pot I intend to win.

Flop T♠‑2♣‑4♥ and I hold 9♠5♠ — no pair, only a very weak runner‑runner spade draw and a few backdoor pair outs. Pro’s bet is small (300 into a 1050 pot), so the immediate pot odds (~18% required equity) make a cheap call acceptable to see a turn. Raising OOP with this marginal holding multiway would be a mistake; folding to such a small probe is overly tight. I’ll call to realize equity and to potentially float the button on a safe turn card, but I will fold to meaningful turn aggression or a raise behind.

We’re in a multiway flop spot where our J7o has very poor equity and realization. On Ts-2c-4h rainbow we effectively have just one overcard (J) plus very weak backdoor possibilities, and Minni’s call makes it much less likely the pot is simply up for grabs. Even with a small bet giving decent immediate odds, we’re still likely below the equity needed versus two ranges, and calling sets us up for tough turns where we frequently have to fold anyway. Bluff-raising is also unattractive: Pro’s short stack can respond by jamming, and Minni’s range contains plenty of Tx/pairs that won’t fold. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

Turn brought the 8♠, giving me 9♠‑5♠ a four‑card spade flush draw (9 outs, ~36% to hit the river). Pro bet the flop then checked the turn — a sign of weakness or pot control. Pot is only 1,350, so a half‑pot semi‑bluff both applies pressure to Pro’s marginal hands and builds a pot I want to be in if I hit. I’ll bet ~½ pot (675). Plan: if Pro folds I scoop the 1,350 pot; if called I still have ~36% to improve and can proceed on the river (bet for value if I make the flush, check/cut losses if I miss). If Pro check‑raises large I’ll fold — I’m OOP with only a draw and don’t want to call heavy aggression without improved equity.

I hold Pocket Kings on a Ts 2c 4h 8s board. After check-calling the flop, Minni leads out (donk bets) for 675 (half pot) on the turn. This line often indicates a turned set (88), two pair (T8), or a semi-bluff with a picked-up draw (spades or straight draw). Raising here would likely fold out the bluffs I want to keep in and isolate me against hands that beat me (sets/two pair). By calling, I keep Minni's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing or value betting worse hands (like Tx) on the river, while avoiding bloating the pot in a spot where I could be drawing nearly dead against a set.

I missed my flush on the river (board T♠‑2♣‑4♥‑8♠‑3♦). I have nothing (9♠5♠) and Pro called my turn semi‑bluff, which strongly suggests at least a pair or another hand that will call a reasonable river bluff. My earlier plan on the turn was to check/cut losses if I missed — that remains correct: bluffing here has thin fold equity against Pro’s calling range and risks losing more chips for little expected gain. I’ll check to avoid turning a marginal semi‑bluff into a costly bluff; if Pro bets I’ll fold to reasonable sizing, and if he checks we go to showdown.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kd), an overpair to the board of [Ts 2c 4h 8s 3d]. The river card [3d] is relatively safe, completing only a 6-5 straight (unlikely given the action) or A-5 wheel (also unlikely). Minni's line of check-calling the flop, leading the turn, and then checking the river is indicative of either a missed draw (which she is giving up on) or a marginal made hand like Top Pair (Tx) or middle pair (8x) that is trying to get to showdown cheaply. A strong hand like a set or straight would likely continue betting the river for value. Since Minni checked, she has capped her range. I can extract value from her Top Pair hands (AT, KT, QT, JT) which are well within her range as a loose-aggressive big stack. A check-back guarantees the win but forfeits value. I will bet approximately 37% of the pot (1000 chips), a sizing that is easy for a Ten to call but not large enough to commit me if I get check-raised (though a check-raise is unlikely).

I checked the river and Pro bet 1,000 into a 3,700 pot. I hold 9♠5♠ with no pair — I missed the spade on the river and have essentially no showdown value. Calling 1,000 to win 4,700 requires ~21% equity, but Pro’s line (open preflop, small c‑bet on the flop, then check‑call my 675 turn semi‑bluff) is consistent with a made hand (T‑x, 8‑x, small pair) or at least a hand that will call down. A raise as a pure bluff (min to 2,000 or shove) is very thin here because Pro showed willingness to call the turn and is unlikely to fold enough for this to be +EV. Given the weak hand, the opponent’s line, and the tournament situation (three‑handed, preserve a big stack), folding is the lowest‑risk, highest‑EV play.

On the button in a 3-handed spot I should be opening a very wide range — K7o is marginal but valuable as a button open (king blocker, decent post‑flop playability). With deep stacks I prefer a standard isolation size rather than a tiny min‑raise to avoid giving the blinds cheap flops and to apply pressure to the big blind (9000) and the large‑stack SB (29825). I'll open to ~3x the big blind (300) to take the initiative. Plan: if either blind 3‑bets to a large size I’ll fold K7o (hand is dominated and not worth a bloated pot); if I'm called I’ll proceed cautiously post‑flop — continue on favorable king/top pair or good draws, otherwise check/fold to heavy action. Winning the blinds uncontested nets the current 150 pot with minimal risk.

Button is opening fairly wide to 3bb and the BB is shorter/tighter, so a small blind flat would realize equity poorly OOP and invites the BB in. K6 suited has decent playability plus a king blocker, making it a good candidate to 3-bet as a semi-bluff to win the dead money often and isolate the opener. If faced with a 4-bet/jam from either opponent, we can comfortably fold.

It is the final hand (#150/150). I am in the Big Blind with 8-2 offsuit, one of the worst starting hands. The action involves a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 1200. Facing a 3-bet with a garbage hand, my equity is extremely low (likely <25%). Although I want to maximize my stack, calling here has a significantly negative expected value (-EV). Preserving my remaining 9000 chips is better than throwing 1100 away on a low-probability gamble, especially since winning the hand is unlikely to change my 3rd place standing given the large chip deficit to the other players (20k and 28k). The disciplined and correct mathematical play is to fold.

I opened to 300 on the button and FiveTwo (SB) 3‑bet to 1200. Pot = 1600, my call = 900, so after a call the pot would be 2500 — I need roughly 900/2500 ≈ 36% equity to make a pure call +EV. With K♠7♥ vs a typical SB 3‑bet range (AK, AQ, mid‑high pairs, some suited broadways) I am heavily dominated and my equity is well below that threshold. Position helps, but this holding has poor playability against a value‑heavy 3‑bet and a 4‑bet bluff to 2100 is too thin (I don’t have the right blockers and I’d be risking more without reliable fold equity). Also this aligns with my earlier plan to fold marginal kings to large 3‑bets. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.
